However, it remains unclear whether these models capture the semantic structure required for downstream tasks. We find that existing deformable reconstruction approaches, despite producing visually plausible geometry, yield unstable correspondences across instances and perform poorly on semantic correspondence benchmarks. We introduce SEMAGIC, a framework for learning semantically consistent deformable 3D representations from single-view in-the-wild images. Rather than treating reconstruction as the end goal, SEMAGIC uses deformable modeling as a mechanism to discover category-level correspondences. Each category is represented by a canonical template mesh and a learned deformation field, functioning similarly to an autoencoder that reconstructs instance geometry from image features, enabling vertices to maintain consistent semantic meaning across instances. Semantic consistency is enforced during training through (i) a feature-level consistency loss aligning semantic features between canonical and deformed meshes, and (ii) vertex-index-conditioned deformation that preserves semantic correspondence across instances. By explicitly coupling geometric deformation with semantic alignment, SEMAGIC produces representations that maintain stable part correspondences across intra-category variation. Experiments demonstrate that SEMAGIC improves semantic correspondence of deformable models by +14.7 PCK@0.1 on SPair-71k, establishing deformable models as effective semantic 3D representations.

</details>


### [27] [Evaluating the Feasibility of Inferring Dietary Behavior Change Receptivity from Egocentric Images of Eating Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27950)
*Long Li,Yuning Huang,Heather A. Eicher-Miller,J. Graham Thomas,Fengqing Zhu,Edward Sazonov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pilot study evaluating whether egocentric eating images (AIM-2) can infer self-reported behavior change receptivity (awareness, interaction capability, motivation) via a CLIP-based transfer-learning framework with a lightweight transformer on image sequences; preliminary results indicate potential cues but require larger datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-report measures of dietary behavior change receptivity are sparse and delayed, hindering real-time monitoring for JITAIs. Passive sensing via wearables could enable continuous assessment and timely interventions.

Method: Use AIM-2 egocentric eating images with self-reported receptivity dimensions. Apply a transfer-learning pipeline that combines a pre-trained CLIP vision encoder with a lightweight transformer classifier to extract semantic and temporal cues from eating episode image sequences.

Result: Preliminary experiments show improvements over simple baselines in predicting receptivity indicators, suggesting visual cues in egocentric eating images related to receptivity.

Conclusion: Egocentric eating episode images may contain cues related to dietary behavior change receptivity and merit further investigation with larger, more comprehensive datasets.

Abstract: Accurately assessing dietary behavior change receptivity is essential for designing effective just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) that promote healthier eating habits. However, self-report-based assessment of behavior change receptivity is sparse and delayed, limiting its practical use in continuous monitoring. To explore whether passive sensing may help address this challenge, this study conducts a pilot investigation of inferring participants' self-reported behavior change receptivity from egocentric eating images collected by a wearable camera. We use pilot data obtained from free-living eating episodes using the Automatic Ingestion Monitor v2 (AIM-2). The data included egocentric image sequences captured during eating and paired with responses to questions assessing specific dimensions of behavior change receptivity (awareness, interaction capability, and motivation). To examine whether visual information contained any relevancy to these responses, we evaluated a transfer-learning-assisted framework that combines a pre-trained Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training (CLIP) vision encoder with a lightweight transformer classifier. The model processes eating episode image sequences to extract potential semantic and temporal cues related to behavior change receptivity. Preliminary experimental results show promising improvements over simple baseline models for behavior change receptivity indicators. These early findings suggest that egocentric eating episode images may contain cues related to dietary behavior change receptivity, and warrant further investigation with larger and more comprehensive datasets.

</details>


### [28] [Con-DSO: Learning Short-Horizon Consistency Priors for RGB-D Direct Sparse Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27952)
*Haolan Zhang,Thanh Nguyen Canh,Chenghao Li,Ziyan Gao,Xiongwen Jiang,Nak Young Chong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A consistency-aware RGB-D direct sparse odometry (Con-DSO) predicts pixel-level consistency uncertainty from adjacent RGB-D frames and uses it as a quality prior to guide keyframe selection and decoupled photometric-weighting, achieving strong gains over direct VO baselines across multiple RGB-D benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct RGB-D VO suffers in dynamic, occluded, or ill-illuminated scenes where photometric and depth-geometric assumptions break; existing fixes rely on hand-crafted criteria or external modules, limiting flexibility.

Method: Train a consistency network to output pixel-level uncertainty from pairwise frame data using flow-guided photometric errors and projective depth-consistency errors. Convert uncertainties into a host-side quality prior for keyframe tracking, enabling quality-aware support-pixel selection and decoupled weighting of photometric and geometric terms in pose estimation.

Result: Outperforms baselines on five RGB-D benchmarks with 20% TE improvement on ICL-NUIM and 50-80% improvements on RGB-D Scenes V2, TUM/Bonn Dynamic, OpenLORIS sequences.

Conclusion: Incorporating learned consistency uncertainty yields continuous attenuation of unreliable observations, offering a unified, flexible approach to robust RGB-D VO without hard rejection.

Abstract: Visual odometry (VO) is a fundamental component in robotics and augmented reality. RGB-D direct VO benefits from metric depth measurements, but it can degrade in challenging environments, where dynamic objects, occlusions, illumination changes, and unreliable depth violate the short-horizon photometric and depth-geometric consistency assumptions used by direct alignment. Existing approaches mitigate these issues through semantic filtering, explicit occlusion reasoning, illumination adaptation, or hand-crafted geometric criteria, but often rely on external modules or fixed assumptions tailored to individual failure modes, limiting their flexibility and ability to handle diverse challenges in a unified manner. In this work, we propose Con-DSO, a consistency-aware RGB-D direct sparse odometry framework that predicts dense photometric and depth-geometric consistency uncertainty from temporally adjacent RGB-D frame pairs. The consistency network is trained using flow-guided photometric errors and projective depth-consistency errors, allowing consistency violations to be represented as pixel-level uncertainty. These pairwise uncertainty predictions are converted into a host-side quality prior for keyframe-based tracking. The prior is then applied to VO through quality-aware support-pixel selection and decoupled photometric-geometric weighting during pose estimation, enabling continuous attenuation of unreliable observations rather than hard rejection or threshold-based gating. Experiments on five public RGB-D benchmarks show substantial gains over direct RGB-D VO baselines, with over 20\% absolute trajectory error reduction on ICL-NUIM and 50\%--80\% reductions on RGB-D Scenes V2, TUM/Bonn Dynamic, and OpenLORIS sequences.

</details>


### [29] [ROVER: Routing Object-Centric Visual Evidence for Grounded Multi-Image Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27959)
*Guannan Lv,Ren Nie,Hongjian Dou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ROVER introduces a lightweight, learnable plugin for efficient global visual evidence routing in multimodal LLMs, enabling object-centric differential attention and history-aware evidence integration across multiple images, improving grounding and answering accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current grounding methods rely on RoI cropping or heavy feature selection, which can harm holistic scene understanding and inter-object reasoning while increasing decoding costs; a global, adaptive routing mechanism is needed to leverage whole-scene context efficiently.

Method: ROVER injects a step-specific token triplet during each object grounding prediction to (1) aggregate ongoing reasoning context, (2) distill intra-image cues into a visual working space via object-centric differential attention, and (3) route and integrate history-aware evidence across objects and images; implemented as a lightweight plugin integrated into Qwen2.5-VL-7B with an interleaved SFT-to-GRPO training pipeline.

Result: ROVER achieves state-of-the-art performance on MM-GCoT (+4.8% answer accuracy, +14.6% grounding accuracy) and VideoEspresso (+8.6% answer accuracy); the VideoEspresso trained model transfers strongly, outperforming the base model by +4.7% on average across benchmarks.

Conclusion: ROVER provides efficient, scalable global visual evidence routing for grounded multi-image reasoning, mitigating limitations of RoI-based methods, with strong cross-dataset transfer when plugged into a base LLM.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have increasingly localized and interleaved visual evidence for deliberative reasoning. Grounding-based approaches typically focus on regions of interest (RoIs) by injecting cropped image patches or RoI-specific features into the reasoning context. However, such designs can weaken holistic scene understanding and inter-object relations, while incurring decoding costs that scale with the number and size of RoIs. Alternatively, adaptive visual feature selection often requires fine-grained supervision or complex heuristics. To address these limitations, we propose ROVER (Routing Object-centric Visual Evidence for grounded multi-image Reasoning), a lightweight, learnable plugin for efficient global visual evidence routing. Upon each object grounding prediction, ROVER injects a step-specific token triplet to synergistically: (i) aggregate the ongoing reasoning context, (ii) distill intra-image cues into a visual working space via object-centric differential attention, and (iii) route and integrate history-aware evidence across objects and images within this space for subsequent reasoning. We integrate ROVER into Qwen2.5-VL-7B and develop an interleaved SFT-to-GRPO training pipeline. Strictly adhering to the original datasets and evaluation protocols, our method achieves the best performance on MM-GCoT (+4.8% answer accuracy, +14.6% grounding accuracy) and VideoEspresso (+8.6% answer accuracy). The VideoEspresso-trained model demonstrates strong transferability, outperforming the base model by +4.7% on average across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [30] [Mags-RL: Wearing Multimodal LLMs a Magnifying Glass via Agentic Reinforcement Learning For Complex Scene Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27960)
*Xuanzhao Dong,Wenhui Zhu,Peijie Qiu,Xiwen Chen,Xiaobing Yu,Xin Li,Zhipeng Wang,Shao Tang,Gen Li,Yujian Xiong,Hao Wang,Yanxi Chen,Prayag Tiwari,Yalin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Mags-RL: an agentic RL framework that adds an external super-resolution module to MLLMs for high-resolution inspection, enabling two-round reasoning with ROI discovery, data-efficient curriculum RL (~40 samples), and strong results on VSR, TallyQA, and GQA; code/weights to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with accurate image interpretation in dense scenes and clutter; existing approaches rely on bounding-box cues and low-resolution crops that miss fine details, hindering reasoning and grounding.

Method: Two-round reasoning: (1) generate initial rationale and autonomously identify regions of interest without extra annotations; (2) invoke a super-resolution agent to crop and upscale the identified regions and revisit the initial reasoning to produce the final answer. Also introduces a curriculum learning strategy for data-efficient RL training, requiring as few as ~40 training samples.

Result: Empirical evaluation on VSR, TallyQA, and GQA subsets shows superior performance against recent strong baselines, with high-quality reasoning and precise visual grounding.

Conclusion: The Mags-RL framework effectively enhances MLLM reasoning by coupling external high-resolution inspection with agentic RL and a data-efficient training strategy; code and weights will be released.

Abstract: Despite their popularity and success, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often struggle to interpret images accurately, which limits their reasoning capability in complex scenarios (e.g., high object density and complex background clutter). Prior work mainly addresses this limitation by incorporating explicit visual cues like bounding boxes that require extra annotations. In addition, the resulting low-resolution crops often miss fine-grained details that MLLMs require for accurate reasoning. Therefore, we propose Mags-RL, an Agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework that equips MLLMs with an external super-resolution "magnifying glass" agent for high-resolution fine-grained inspection. Specifically, the model performs two-round reasoning: in the first round, it generates an initial rationale and autonomously identifies regions of interest without relying on additional annotations; in the second round, it invokes a super-resolution agent to crop and upscale those regions, then revisits and verifies its earlier reasoning to produce the final answer. We also introduce a novel curriculum learning strategy that enables data-efficient RL training, needing as few as only 40 training samples to achieve reasonable performance. Experiments on VSR, TallyQA, and GQA subsets show its superior performance against recent strong competing methods, demonstrating high-quality reasoning with precise visual grounding. Code and weights will be released soon.

</details>


### [31] [Bridging the Generalization Gap in Adverse Weather Segmentation: A Training Recipe Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27962)
*Cong Xu,Pu Luo,Yumei Li,Boyou Xue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A carefully designed training recipe can close the weather-induced generalization gap in outdoor semantic segmentation, achieving strong test performance with limited architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a severe generalization gap when segmenting outdoor scenes under five weather degradations; models that do well on validation fail on test sets, indicating robustness is not solely a function of model capacity.

Method: Starting from a pre-trained SegMAN-S backbone, the study explores domain-adaptive fine-tuning, multi-source data mixing, scene-balanced sampling, and synthetic degradation augmentation; assesses negative results from architectural changes and loss-function variants.

Result: The proposed pipeline achieves 59.9% mIoU on the official test set with a validation-test gap of 6.5 points, substantially smaller than larger models (e.g., SegFormer-B5).

Conclusion: A well-designed training recipe can outperform larger architectures for weather-robust segmentation under limited data; architectural or loss-function tweaks offer limited gains compared to data-centric strategies.

Abstract: This paper describes our approach for the 8th UG2+ Workshop (CVPR 2026) Track~2, which targets semantic segmentation of outdoor scenes degraded by five weather conditions: blur, darkness, snow, haze, and glare. A central challenge we observe is a severe generalization gap -- models that perform well on the validation set often collapse on the test set. For instance, SegFormer-B5 drops 16.1 mIoU points from validation to test, suggesting that model capacity alone is insufficient for robustness. We investigate whether a carefully designed training recipe, rather than architectural complexity, can address this gap. Starting from a pre-trained SegMAN-S backbone, we systematically study the effects of domain-adaptive fine-tuning, multi-source data mixing, scene-balanced sampling, and synthetic degradation augmentation. Our final system achieves 59.9\% mIoU on the official test set while maintaining a validation-test gap of only 6.5 points -- less than half that of larger models. We analyze negative results from architectural modifications, loss function variants, and model scaling to provide practical insights for weather-robust segmentation under limited data.

</details>


### [32] [ABot-OCR Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27978)
*Kaitao Jiang,Ruiyan Gong,Xiaolong Cheng,Kangning Niu,Tianlun Li,Mu Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ABot-OCR is an end-to-end vision-language model that transcribes page images directly into clean Markdown in one pass, eliminating brittle modular pipelines. It uses a dedicated data engine for large-scale, structurally consistent supervision and a structure-constrained reinforcement learning method (Decoupled Heterogeneous Document Optimization) to improve text accuracy and ensure well-formed markup. It achieves state-of-the-art end-to-end performance on OmniDocBench v1.5/1.6 and demonstrates strong multilingual generalization across ten languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the fragility of modular OCR pipelines and improve transcription fidelity and markup well-formedness in document understanding by producing clean Markdown in a single forward pass.

Method: An end-to-end vision-language model (ABot-OCR) that outputs Markdown directly from page images, a dedicated data engine providing large-scale structurally consistent supervision, and Decoupled Heterogeneous Document Optimization (D-HDO), a structure-constrained reinforcement learning framework to sharpen textual accuracy and enforce well-formed markup beyond supervised fine-tuning.

Result: ABot-OCR achieves state-of-the-art end-to-end scores on OmniDocBench v1.5 (92.81) and v1.6 (93.30), narrowing the gap to strong pipeline baselines; demonstrates robust multilingual recognition across ten languages, indicating strong generalization.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end ABot-OCR framework, reinforced by a specialized data engine and D-HDO training, delivers superior transcription fidelity and markup validity, with strong cross-language generalizability and competitive, if not superior, performance relative to traditional pipelines.

Abstract: We introduce ABot-OCR, an end-to-end vision-language model that transcribes a page image directly into clean Markdown in a single forward pass. By doing so, our approach completely eliminates the need for brittle modular orchestration. To maximize parsing fidelity, we develop a dedicated data engine to provide large-scale, structurally consistent supervision. Furthermore, we propose Decoupled Heterogeneous Document Optimization, a structure-constrained reinforcement learning method that sharpens textual accuracy and strictly enforces markup well-formedness beyond supervised fine-tuning alone. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the superior performance of our framework. On the OmniDocBench v1.5 and v1.6 benchmarks, ABot-OCR achieves state-of-the-art scores of 92.81 and 93.30 among all end-to-end systems, substantially narrowing the performance gap relative to strong pipeline baselines. Finally, comprehensive multilingual text recognition across ten diverse languages further confirms the robust generalizability of ABot-OCR.

</details>


### [33] [Automated Estimation of Impact Time, Impact Location, and Shuttlecock Speed in Badminton Smashes Using Event Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28011)
*Yudai Washida,Yuto Kase,Kai Ishibe,Ryoma Yasuda,Sakiko Hashimoto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two synchronized event cameras estimate impact time, racket-face impact location, and shuttlecock speed in badminton smashes; validated via Bland-Altman against high-speed camera across 125 trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome trade-offs in temporal resolution, data efficiency, and preparation effort in conventional measurement systems; enable integrated performance and equipment assessment in practical settings.

Method: Two synchronized event cameras capture smashes. Swing interval detected from event rate statistics; impact time inferred from shuttlecock trajectory inflection in lateral-view event data; impact location determined by ellipse fitting on the racket face in the rear-view event image; shuttlecock speed computed in the sagittal plane. Validation against high-speed camera reference with Bland-Altman analysis over 125 trials (5 players). 124 analyzable for time and speed; 116/124 for location.

Result: Bland-Altman biases: impact time 1.84 ms (95% CI 1.45–2.23); medio-lateral location 3.45 mm (2.18–4.72); longitudinal location -1.92 mm (-2.97–-0.88); shuttlecock speed -1.00 m/s (-2.46–0.46); no proportional bias observed for any metric. Location estimated in 93.5% of analyzable trials (116/124).

Conclusion: The method provides a practical, integrated measurement tool for badminton smash performance and equipment assessment, suitable for use in real-world settings with acceptable accuracy.

Abstract: Quantifying impact phenomena in badminton smashes is important for evaluating both athletic performance and equipment; however, conventional measurement systems involve trade-offs between temporal resolution, data efficiency, and preparation effort. This study proposes a measurement method using two synchronized event cameras to automatically estimate impact time, impact location on the racket face, and post-impact shuttlecock speed in an integrated manner within the same trial. The swing interval was detected from event rate statistics, impact time was estimated from the shuttlecock trajectory inflection in the lateral-view event data, impact location was determined by ellipse fitting to the racket face in the rear-view event image, and shuttlecock speed was calculated in the sagittal plane. To validate the proposed method, Bland-Altman analysis was performed against a high-speed camera-based reference method using 125 smash trials from five players. Impact time and shuttlecock speed were estimated in all 124 analyzable trials, and impact location was estimated in 93.5% (116/124). The bias (95% CI) for impact time, medio-lateral impact location, longitudinal impact location, and shuttlecock speed were 1.84 ms (1.45 to 2.23), 3.45 mm (2.18 to 4.72), -1.92 mm (-2.97 to -0.88), and -1.00 m/s (-2.46 to 0.46), respectively. No proportional bias was observed for any metric. These results suggest that the proposed method can serve as a useful tool for integrated assessment of badminton smash performance and equipment in practical settings.

</details>


### [34] [Enhancing Ultra-low-field MRI with Segmentation-guided Adversarial Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28016)
*James Grover,Andrew Phair,Michael Ferraro,David E. J. Waddington*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An anatomical-conditioned, ensembling approach synthesizes 3T-like MRIs from 64 mT ULF scans by using Swin UNETR-based tissue priors to condition two models (CycleGAN and T-REX), whose outputs are fused with a weighted average, achieving performance comparable to high-field scans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ULF MRI has low image quality due to low field strength; the goal is to produce high-field-like images to enable practical clinical use, validated in the 2025 ULF-EnC competition.

Method: Generate tissue segmentation priors with a Swin UNETR trained on challenge data. Use priors to condition two enhancement networks—CycleGAN and a transformer-based residual enhancement model (T-REX)—each trained to synthesize 3T-like MRIs. Combine outputs via a weighted average.

Result: Enhanced MRIs achieved quantitative and qualitative similarity to true high-field scans, indicating effective enhancement and successful ensembling approach.

Conclusion: An anatomical-conditioning and ensembling strategy can convert 64 mT ULF MRIs into 3T-like images with competitive accuracy, suggesting practical viability for portable MRI improvements.

Abstract: Ultra-low-field (ULF) MRI offers portable and low-cost imaging but suffers from poor image quality. To address this, we present our submission to the 2025 ULF Enhancement Challenge (ULF-EnC), where the goal is to synthesise high-field-like MRIs from 64 mT scans. Our pipeline enhances ULF MRI through a combination of anatomical conditioning and model ensembling. We first generate tissue segmentation priors using a Swin UNETR trained solely on challenge-provided data. These priors condition two independent enhancement networks - a CycleGAN and a transformer-based residual enhancement model (T-REX) - each trained to synthesise 3 T-like MRIs. Outputs from both models are combined using a weighted average. Our approach produces enhanced MRIs that were comparable to high-field scans both quantitatively and qualitatively.

</details>


### [35] [Dual-branch Distilled Transformer for Efficient Asymmetric UAV Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28018)
*Hongtao Yang,Bineng Zhong,Qihua Liang,Yaozong Zheng,Xiantao Hu,Yuanliang Xue,Shuxiang Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EATrack introduces an efficient teacher-guided distillation framework for UAV tracking, using a dual-branch structure with spatial feature-level and prediction-level distillation, plus target-aware distillation and a temporal adaptation module, achieving a favorable accuracy–speed trade-off on five UAV benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time UAV tracking often uses lightweight backbones to meet speed constraints, but this reduces feature quality and tracking accuracy in complex scenes; there is a need to restore feature expressiveness and robustness without sacrificing speed.

Method: A teacher-guided dual-branch distillation framework: (1) spatially focused feature-level distillation to strengthen target representations in the student, (2) prediction-level distillation to improve localization capability, (3) fine-grained target-aware distillation to adapt the teacher's target modeling to the student, and (4) a temporal adaptation module at inference to improve temporal robustness.

Result: Empirical validation on five UAV benchmarks shows that EATrack achieves a favorable accuracy–speed balance; the authors also provide code for reproducibility (GitHub).

Conclusion: The proposed distillation ensemble and temporal adaptation effectively compensate for the reduced capacity of lightweight backbones, yielding robust, real-time UAV tracking performance.

Abstract: Given the real-time demands of UAV tracking, many methods simplify the backbone to reduce computation, but this often weakens feature representation and degrades performance in complex scenarios. To alleviate this issue, we propose EATrack, an efficient and asymmetric UAV tracking framework centered around a teacher-guided dual-branch distillation strategy that enhances the feature expressiveness of the lightweight student model. Specifically, EATrack investigates two complementary perspectives of knowledge transfer: spatially focused feature-level distillation that compensates for weakened representations by guiding the student to learn strong target representations, and prediction-level distillation that enhances spatial localization by learning the teacher's capability for accurate target localization. Furthermore, to enhance robustness against appearance variations, we introduce a fine-grained target-aware distillation strategy that selectively transfers the teacher's target modeling capacity to the student. A temporal adaptation module is incorporated at inference to enhance robustness over time. Experiments on five UAV benchmarks demonstrate that EATrack achieves a favorable balance between accuracy and speed. Code: https://github.com/GXNU-ZhongLab/EATrack

</details>


### [36] [VCap: Hypergeometric Rewards for Weak-to-Strong Visual Captioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28023)
*Xingyu Lu,Jinpeng Wang,Yi-Fan Zhang,Yankai Yang,Yancheng Long,Yiyang Fan,Xuanyu Zheng,Haonan Fan,Kaiyu Jiang,Tianke Zhang,Changyi Liu,Bin Wen,Fan Yang,Tingting Gao,Han Li,Chun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VCap introduces a Witness-Adjudicator reward for RL-based image/video captioning, using a reference caption (witness) and a visual signal (adjudicator) to verify factual consistency, delivering precise reward signals to improve factual correctness and generalization; an 8B model achieves SOTA on benchmarks; human evaluation confirms alignment; improves perceptual capabilities and challenges RLVR assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the insufficiency of current RL-based captioning rewards for fine-grained factual verification, reducing omission and hallucination, and enabling learning from imperfect references through a visually grounded, adjudicated reward signal.

Method: Pair a reference caption (witness) with a visual signal (adjudicator) to verify factual consistency; provide a reward with hypergeometric-distribution-level precision; enable weak-to-strong generalization in RL training; train an 8B MLLM with VCap; evaluate on image/video captioning benchmarks and through human evaluation; assess cross-task perceptual capability and distillation.

Result: An 8B model trained with VCap outperforms open- and closed-source SOTA models on multiple image and video captioning benchmarks; human evaluation confirms strong factual alignment; VCap improves MLLM perceptual capability, generalizes across tasks, and surpasses best-of-N distillation, challenging prior RLVR assumptions.

Conclusion: VCap offers a more reliable, fine-grained reward signal for factual verification in RL-based captioning, enabling learning from imperfect references and stronger alignment; demonstrates cross-task generalization and potential to reshape RLVR design assumptions.

Abstract: Visual captioning requires models to capture visual content faithfully while minimizing both omission and hallucination. As the dominant paradigm for captioning, MLLMs have achieved strong performance through scaling and high-quality data. Recently, RL has emerged as a key route to driving MLLMs toward higher precision and broader coverage, however, existing reward designs for captioning fail to provide fine-grained and reliable signals for factual verification, limiting their effectiveness. To address this, we propose VCap, a Witness-Adjudicator reward that pairs the reference caption (a witness) with the visual signal (an adjudicator). By explicitly verifying factual consistency between the reference and policy-generated captions grounded in the visual signal, VCap delivers a reward signal with hypergeometric-distribution-level precision for caption quality verification. This design enables effective learning even from imperfect references, facilitating weak-to-strong generalization in RL training. In our experiments, an 8B model trained with VCap outperforms open- and closed-source SOTA models on multiple image and video captioning benchmarks. Human evaluation further confirms its strong alignment with factual correctness. Additionally, VCap improves MLLM perceptual capability, generalizes across tasks, and surpasses best-of-N distillation, challenging prior assumptions about RLVR.

</details>


### [37] [Stay Fair! Ensuring Group Fairness in Diffusion Models Across Guidance Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28036)
*Myeongsoo Kim,Eunji Kim,Minwoo Chae,Sangwoo Mo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Decomposes diffusion-model bias into model and guidance components; introduces StayFair to maintain fairness across guidance scales by equalizing guidance across groups for classifier guidance and offsetting the null embedding for classifier-free guidance; decouples fairness from guidance scale with preserved image quality, applicable to class-conditional and text-to-image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Debiasing techniques optimized for a single guidance scale degrade fairness when users adjust the scale. The authors decompose total bias into model and guidance biases, show guidance bias grows with scale, and extend strong demographic parity to guidance to ensure fair distributions across scales.

Method: Decompose total bias into model bias and guidance bias; derive a condition under which the target distribution preserves group ratios across guidance scales; propose StayFair: (1) classifier guidance—equalize classifier output distributions across groups; (2) classifier-free guidance—shift the null embedding by a prompt-dependent offset. StayFair operates during the guidance step and can layer onto existing fair diffusion models.

Result: StayFair decouples fairness from the guidance scale in both class-conditional and text-to-image diffusion generation, preserving image quality and enabling fairness across a range of guidance strengths; it is orthogonal to model debiasing and can be layered onto existing fair diffusion models.

Conclusion: StayFair provides a scalable, orthogonal approach to maintain group parity across varying guidance scales in diffusion-based generation, with broad applicability to both classifier-guided and classifier-free guided diffusion models.

Abstract: Diffusion models steer conditional generation with a tunable guidance scale to trade off prompt alignment and diversity. However, existing debiasing techniques are optimized for a single scale, degrading fairness when users adjust this parameter. We trace this behavior to a previously overlooked source by decomposing total bias into two components: a model bias and a guidance bias. While prior work primarily targets the former, we show that the guidance bias grows monotonically with the guidance scale, eventually dominating the high-guidance regimes users prefer. To address this, we extend Strong Demographic Parity to guidance and derive a condition under which the target distribution retains its group ratio across guidance scales. We propose StayFair, which leverages this condition to design fair guidance algorithms in both regimes. For classifier guidance, it equalizes the classifier's output distributions across groups; for classifier-free guidance, it shifts the null embedding by a prompt-dependent offset. Because StayFair modifies only the guidance step, it is orthogonal to model debiasing and can be layered onto existing fair diffusion models to extend their fairness across guidance scales. Across class-conditional and text-to-image generation, StayFair decouples fairness from the guidance scale without sacrificing image quality.

</details>


### [38] [Beyond Surrogate Gradients: Fully Differentiable Token Pruning for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28051)
*Landi He,Mingde Yao,Shawn Young,Lijian Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffPrune reframes visual token pruning as continuous information control via an Information Throttler, enabling fully differentiable training and simple hard-threshold pruning at inference, yielding strong accuracy retention with substantial speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address unreliable token-importance learning from surrogate gradients in Gumbel-Softmax-based pruning; seek a differentiable, information-centric approach that directly operates on token representations.

Method: Introduce Information Throttler that applies variance-preserving noise to tokens conditioned on importance scores; higher scores trigger less information suppression; training is fully differentiable; inference uses hard threshold on learned scores to prune tokens.

Result: Reported across ten VLM benchmarks: 96.5% of full-model accuracy retained; LLM prefill accelerated by 2.85x; inference overhead 0.69 ms.

Conclusion: DiffPrune offers a differentiable, information-theoretic approach to token pruning with strong empirical efficiency/accuracy trade-offs and broad applicability across VLMs; future work may explore robustness and hyperparameter sensitivity.

Abstract: Visual token pruning reduces the computational cost of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by removing redundant visual tokens. Existing methods typically rely on Gumbel-Softmax to approximate discrete selection during training. However, the optimization is driven by surrogate gradients rather than the true selection process, leading to unreliable learning of token importance. In this paper, we propose DiffPrune, which reformulates pruning as continuous control of token information instead of discrete selection learning. Specifically, we introduce an Information Throttler that modulates each token using variance-preserving noise conditioned on importance scores, where higher scores induce less information suppression during training. This design directly operates on token representations, naturally providing a fully differentiable optimization path for learning token importance. At inference, tokens are removed via hard thresholding on the learned scores. Across ten VLM benchmarks, DiffPrune retains 96.5% of full-model accuracy while accelerating LLM prefill by 2.85x, with only 0.69 ms of inference overhead.

</details>


### [39] [CogPortrait: Fine-Grained Eye-Region Control in Portrait Animation via Hierarchical Agent Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28056)
*He Feng,Yongjia Ma,Donglin Di,Lei Fan,Tonghua Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CogPortrait: a two-stage portrait animation framework using high-level labels to achieve fine-grained eye-region control via MLLM-driven keypoint planning and DiT-based video synthesis, with dynamic guidance and a new EMH benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a trade-off between input granularity and motion accuracy for eye-region manipulation in portrait animation. Coarse emotion labels or prompts fail to capture subtle ocular dynamics, while AU/driving videos impose input burden. Beyond-emotion states (e.g., thinking) and drowsiness demand a more scalable, high-level labeling approach.

Method: Stage 1: Three chain-of-thought Multimodal LLM agents convert high-level labels into temporal facial keypoints using event planning, prototype retrieval, real-behavior library composition, and semantic-physiological constraints. Stage 2: A DiT-based video generation backbone renders the animation conditioned on keypoints, reference portrait, audio, and text prompts, with dynamic classifier-free guidance that reweights eye regions and KTO-based refinement for boundary cases. An EMH benchmark with AU-level metrics evaluates eye-region and head-motion control.

Result: Empirical evaluation on HDTF and EMH demonstrates CogPortrait achieves more precise eye-region control than existing methods while preserving high visual quality and identity consistency.

Conclusion: A two-stage, high-level-label-driven framework enables precise, flexible eye-region animation in portraits, supported by a new benchmark and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Portrait animation methods have achieved substantial visual quality and lip synchronization, but fine-grained manipulation of the eye region still faces a trade-off between input granularity and motion accuracy. Existing methods using emotion labels or coarse text prompts are insufficient for describing subtle ocular dynamics, whereas approaches based on Action Units or driving videos provide higher fidelity at the cost of a heavier input burden. These limitations are still restrictive for beyond-emotion states (e.g., thinking) and drowsiness. In light of the above, we propose CogPortrait, a two-stage framework that generates portrait animations from high-level labels. In the first stage, three chain-of-thought Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) agents compile high-level labels into facial keypoints through temporal event planning, prototype retrieval, and composition from a real-behavior library, and semantic-physiological constraint enforcement. In the second stage, a DiT-based video generation backbone synthesizes the final animation conditioned on the keypoints, reference portrait, audio, and text prompt, enhanced by a dynamic classifier-free guidance strategy with eye-region-aware reweighting and KTO-based refinement for boundary cases. We further introduce the EMH benchmark covering diverse emotions and beyond-emotion categories with two AU-level metrics for evaluating fine-grained eye-region and head-motion control. Extensive experiments on HDTF and the EMH benchmark demonstrate that CogPortrait achieves more precise eye-region control than existing methods while maintaining supe- rior visual quality and identity consistency

</details>


### [40] [VLA-Hijack: A Transferable Patch Attack against Vision-Language-Action Models via Visual Proprioception Hijacking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28083)
*Jiyuan Fu,Kaixun Jiang,Jingkai Jia,Zhaoyu Chen,Xueyao Chen,Lingyi Hong,Shuyong Gao,Chenzhi Tan,Dingkang Yang,Wenqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLA-Hijack proposes a unified adversarial framework that targets the self-localization step in Vision-Language-Action models to sever the link between embodiment and control, using Attention-Guided Proprioceptive Suppression and Multimodal Proprioceptive Injection to create a phantom embodiment; it achieves superior white-box optimization efficiency and sets new benchmarks in cross-architecture and cross-domain transferability across OpenVLA, UniVLA, and CronusVLA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome severe vulnerability of VLA models to adversarial patches and the poor cross-architecture transferability of patches that overfit to a single action space, enabling robust, cross-architecture attacks in safety-critical contexts.

Method: Identify a common visual self-localization stage in VLA models. Simultaneously optimize two mechanisms: (1) Attention-Guided Proprioceptive Suppression to dampen the real robotic arm's features, and (2) Multimodal Proprioceptive Injection to render the patch as a surrogate phantom embodiment. Alternate between semantic anchoring of concepts and projection of visual prototypes to break the semantic linkage between embodiment and control policy, enabling a transferable adversarial patch.

Result: Demonstrates superior optimization efficiency in white-box settings and achieves new state-of-the-art transferability in cross-architecture and cross-domain black-box scenarios across OpenVLA, UniVLA, and CronusVLA.

Conclusion: The work reveals a fundamental vulnerability in VLA models tied to their self-localization process, offers a transferable, architecture-agnostic attack strategy, and highlights the need for defenses that harden the visual self-localization and embodiment-to-control mappings.

Abstract: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as powerful generalist policies, their severe vulnerability to adversarial patches significantly hinders their deployment in safety-critical domains. Moreover, existing patch attacks primarily focus on white-box settings, heavily overfitting to the specific action output space of the target model, which results in poor cross-architecture transferability. To overcome this limitation, we propose VLA-Hijack, a unified adversarial framework that breaks the transferability bottleneck by exploiting a fundamental vulnerability identified in this work: before planning any motion, a VLA model must first use visual information to locate its own robotic arm within the environment. Targeting this shared visual self-localization process, our approach concurrently optimizes Attention-Guided Proprioceptive Suppression to inhibit the real robotic arm's features, and Multimodal Proprioceptive Injection to establish the patch as a surrogate "phantom embodiment". By alternating between semantic concept anchoring and visual prototype projection, VLA-Hijack effectively severs the semantic relationship between the agent's true embodiment and its control policy. Extensive experiments across diverse architectures (OpenVLA, UniVLA, and CronusVLA) demonstrate that VLA-Hijack achieves superior optimization efficiency in white-box settings and sets a new SOTA for cross-architecture and cross-domain black-box transferability.

</details>


### [41] [Qwen-Image-Bench: From Generation to Creation in Text-to-Image Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28091)
*Niantong Li,Guangzheng Hu,Weixu Qiao,Ying Ba,Qichen Hong,Shijun Shen,Jinlin Wang,Fan Zhou,Jianye Kang,Xin Shang,Ziyi He,Wei Wang,Dalin Li,Jiahao Li,Jie Zhang,Kaiyuan Gao,Kun Yan,Lihan Jiang,Ningyuan Tang,Shengming Yin,Tianhe Wu,Xiao Xu,Xiaoyue Chen,Yuxiang Chen,Yan Shu,Yanran Zhang,Yilei Chen,Yixian Xu,Zekai Zhang,Zhendong Wang,Zihao Liu,Zikai Zhou,Hongzhu Shi,Yi Wang,Bing Zhao,Hu Wei,Lin Qu,Chenfei Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A creator-centric benchmark, Qwen-Image-Bench, evaluates text-to-image models on Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation using a hierarchical taxonomy (5 pillars, 23 sub-capabilities, 56 rubrics) with 1000 stratified prompts. A unified judge model (Q-Judger) trained with 80 professional annotators provides fine-grained rubric-based diagnostics to reliably distinguish state-of-the-art models and guide production development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on basic text-image alignment and fail to capture nuanced capabilities demanded by professional artistic practice, real-world workflows, and faithful reconstruction and creative expression in production settings.

Method: Develop a hierarchical, application-driven evaluation framework with 5 pillars, 23 sub-capabilities, and 56 rubrics. Curate 1000 prompts that test multiple facets. Train Q-Judger (Qwen3.6-27B) with 80 blinded, triple-reviewed professional annotators to score images on all 56 rubrics.

Result: The benchmark reliably differentiates leading T2I models, showing the largest separation along Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation, and provides a robust, rubric-grounded diagnostic signal for production-level model development.

Conclusion: Qwen-Image-Bench offers comprehensive, interpretable evaluation aligned with professional practice, enabling more reliable model comparison and optimization in real-world creative workflows.

Abstract: Text-to-Image generation has evolved from basic image synthesis into a frequently used core capability in professional creative workflows, where simple text-image alignment can no longer satisfy users' pressing demands for faithful real-world reconstruction and genuine creative expression. Existing benchmarks, however, remain anchored in these foundational criteria and do not yet capture the nuanced capabilities that matter in authentic artistic practice, making it difficult to reliably distinguish state-of-the-art T2I models. To address the gap, we introduce Qwen-Image-Bench, a creator-centric benchmark co-designed with professional artists and grounded in real-world creation scenarios. Qwen-Image-Bench enriches conventional evaluation with two application-driven dimensions: Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation. Drawing on the staged reasoning inherent in professional artistic workflows, we organize these five pillars into a top-down hierarchical taxonomy that further decomposes into 23 second-level sub-capabilities and 56 third-level verifiable rubrics. To ensure broad coverage, we curate 1000 stratified prompts with each prompt jointly exercising more than four fine-grained facets across multiple pillars. We train a unified judge model Q-Judger based on Qwen3.6-27B, supervised by 80 professional annotators from global art academies under blind labeling and triple-review protocols, that scores every image across all 56 verifiable facets, producing fine-grained, rubric-grounded, and fully attributable diagnostics rather than a single opaque score. Empirically, Qwen-Image-Bench reliably distinguishes leading T2I models, achieving the greatest separation on the two application-driven dimensions of Real-world Fidelity and Creative Generation where existing benchmarks provide little insight, while also providing a trustworthy optimization signal for production-level T2I development.

</details>


### [42] [Revisiting Change Detection Methods for their Application to Serac Fall Time-Lapse Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28100)
*Arthur Dérédel,Carlos Crispim-Junior,Pierre Lemaire,Johan Berthet,Laure Tougne Rodet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Survey and dataset proposal for volumetric change detection in time-lapse environmental monitoring; show dense/semi-dense feature matching is robust; supervised methods hindered by data scarcity; advocates hybrid approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address environmental hazard detection with affordable time-lapse imagery; overcome challenges of extreme shape/lighting variations; enable large-scale deployment of monitoring tools.

Method: Comprehensive literature review of change detection methods; introduce SeracFallDet dataset with serac fall annotations; perform generalization experiments comparing feature matching and supervised methods; analyze core components.

Result: Dense/semi-dense feature matching yields robust performance without task-specific training; supervised methods struggle due to data scarcity and imbalance.

Conclusion: Hybrid approaches leveraging strengths of feature matching and supervised learning are promising; dataset and findings inform future work to enable real-world deployment in environmental monitoring.

Abstract: In an era where climate change aggravates environmental uncertainties, the identification and detection of event precursors are becoming crucial to mitigate the impacts of disastrous natural hazards. While classical sensors such as interferometric lasers or seismometers are reliable, their widespread deployment is often hindered by logistical and economic barriers, leaving numerous blind spots. Time-lapse cameras, which already provide cost-effective, high-resolution visual context to such sensors, present a promising alternative. However, processing their output automatically faces significant challenges, notably linked to extreme shape and lighting variations. Overcoming those issues is essential to deploy them at large-scale as a monitoring tool. This paper introduces a novel sub-task of change detection, namely volumetric change detection, applied to time-lapse cameras and slope instabilities. We conduct a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art change detection methods and related tasks, analyze their core components and assess their applicability to this context. To that end, we introduce the new dataset SeracFallDet, which contains serac fall annotations and has been thoroughly annotated to meet the latter demand. Through generalization experiments, we demonstrate that dense and semi-dense feature matching, although not trained specifically for this task, exhibit robust performance. Alternatively, supervised approaches struggle with data scarcity and annotation imbalance. This suggests that hybrid methods may offer a path forward by leveraging the strengths of both tasks. These findings highlight the potential of feature matching techniques and the need for further innovation to overcome the challenges of real-world deployment in environmental monitoring.

</details>


### [43] [ST-ColoNet: Spatio-Temporal Colon Segment Recognition via Hybrid Attention and Edge-Guided Feature Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28119)
*Ziyi Wang,Zhengjie Zhang,Jingsheng Gao,Dahong Qian,Suncheng Xiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A spatio-temporal deep learning framework (ST-ColoNet) improves colo-segment recognition in colonoscopy videos by integrating a Colorlaus module for edge-mediated spatial features via metric learning and a Full-Temp module with three self-attention patterns to model long-range temporal dependencies, using a curated labeled video dataset; achieves state-of-the-art performance with 81.0% accuracy and 70.7% F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of public video-based Colo-segment data and to leverage temporal information for improved recognition over static image-based methods.

Method: Two-stage framework: Colorlaus module uses metric learning to optimize edge-mediated spatial feature extraction; Full-Temp module combines three self-attention patterns to approximate full self-attention on long colonoscopy sequences for temporal feature aggregation; includes dataset curation and extensive ablation experiments.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on colo-segment recognition; accuracy 81.0% and F1-score 70.7%; substantial improvement over prior methods.

Conclusion: The proposed ST-ColoNet effectively leverages spatial-edge-aware features and long-sequence temporal attention to achieve superior colo-segment recognition on colonoscopy videos.

Abstract: Colo-segment recognition in colonoscopy videos is a key requirement for many downstream tasks, but existing automatic recognition methods only use colonoscopy images without fully exploiting the use of temporal information, leading to poor performance. Additionally, relevant public video-based datasets are in scarcity. To tackle this problem, we curate and release a labeled dataset specifically for the task of colo-segment recognition. In addition, we propose a two-stage deep learning-based framework, Colo-Segment Recognition via SpatioTemporal Network (ST-ColoNet), for the task of colo-segment recognition from colonoscopy videos which includes the Colorlaus module that uses metric learning to optimize edge-mediated spatial feature extraction, as well as the Full-Temp module which combines three self-attention patterns to better approximate full self-attention on long colonoscopy sequences and optimize temporal feature aggregation. Through extensive ablation experiments, we show that our framework is capable of achieving state-of-the-art performance on the task of colo-segment recognition, achieving an accuracy of 81.0% and F1-score of 70.7%, which is a tremendous improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [44] [CLEAR-NeRF: Collinearity and Local-region Enhanced Accurate 3D Reconstruction in Unbounded Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28125)
*Vladislav Polianskii,Elijs Dima,Isabel Salmerón Marazuela,Gergő László Nagy,Sigurdur Sverrisson,Volodya Grancharov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes NeRF-based 3D reconstruction for multi-region unbounded scenes with four technical contributions to improve lighting robustness, geometric fidelity, and region prioritization; claims superior performance over NeRF baselines and SfM-MVS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world 3D reconstruction requires photorealism and metric accuracy in large, uncontrolled scenes with challenging lighting and imperfect captures; standard NeRF pipelines struggle with lighting variation, pose changes, and unbounded regions.

Method: Four components: (i) automated local region localization/detection and reconstruction to prioritize areas of interest without proliferating submodules; (ii) collinearity-enforcing ray sampling to learn smooth planar and curved surfaces; (iii) depth-localized neighborhood point extraction to suppress surface artifacts; (iv) geometry-relevant color aggregation to mitigate lighting- and pose-caused variations; aims to enforce metric accuracy for digital-twin applications.

Result: Empirical results indicate the proposed pipeline achieves superior performance compared to baseline NeRF models and established SfM-MVS solutions on unbounded, region-focused scenes under challenging lighting/pose conditions.

Conclusion: The approach advances NeRF-based reconstruction for unbounded, multi-region scenes by enhancing robustness to lighting/pose variation and ensuring metric accuracy, with strong potential for digital-twin applications.

Abstract: Many real-world 3D reconstruction applications demand photorealism and metric accuracy across unbounded, complex scenes with challenging lighting and imperfect captures that current Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) pipelines only partly satisfy. This study adapts NeRF-based 3D reconstruction to multi-region of interest unbounded scenes to improve robustness to lighting and pose variation while enforcing metric accuracy suitable for digital-twin applications. Our approach introduces (i) automated local region localization/detection and reconstruction to seamlessly prioritize areas of interest without proliferating submodules, (ii) collinearity-enforcing ray sampling to learn smooth planar and curved surfaces, (iii) depth-localized neighborhood point extraction to suppress surface artifacts, and (iv) geometry-relevant color aggregation to mitigate lighting- and pose-caused variations. Results indicate superior performance of the proposed pipeline over the baseline NeRF models and established Structure from Motion (SfM) - Multi-View Stereo (MVS) solutions.

</details>


### [45] [SAM-Enhanced Segmentation on Road Datasets: Balancing Critical Classes in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28136)
*Toomas Tahves,Mauro Bellone,Junyi Gu,Raivo Sell*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A SAM-based annotation pipeline converts Zenseact bounding boxes into dense pixel-level masks, enabling dense segmentation on ZOD; a 2,300-frame validated subset enables strong baselines with CLFT and DeepLabV3+; rare-class modeling improves performance; cross-platform transfer to Iseauto with 77.5% mIoU; resources released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable dense semantic segmentation research on multi-sensor autonomous-driving data by converting weak-annotations (bounding boxes) into pixel-level masks, addressing data scarcity and class imbalance (pedestrians, cyclists, signs).

Method: 1) Use Segment Anything Model to convert bounding boxes from ZOD into semantic masks; 2) manually curate a 2,300-frame subset from 100k+ frames to establish a reliable baseline; 3) train/evaluate transformer-based CLFT and CNN-based DeepLabV3+ across weather conditions; 4) address extreme class imbalance with specialized models for rare classes; 5) validate pipeline on Iseauto platform and test bidirectional transfer learning across sensor configurations; 6) release code and annotations.

Result: Achieved up to 48.1% mIoU with CLFT-Hybrid on diverse weather; demonstrated improvements for rare classes via specialized models; validated on Iseauto with 77.5% mIoU; SAM-derived representations transfer across sensor setups through bidirectional transfer learning; all data/code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: A practical, reproducible pipeline for deriving dense segmentation from weak bounding-box annotations in ZOD; SAM-based annotation enables robust cross-domain transfer and supports extensive evaluation of segmentation architectures under varying conditions.

Abstract: Dense semantic segmentation is essential for autonomous driving, yet many multi-modal datasets lack pixel-level annotations. The Zenseact Open Dataset (ZOD) provides rich multi-sensor data but only bounding-box labels, limiting its use for segmentation research. Our primary contribution is a Segment Anything Model (SAM)-based annotation pipeline that produces dense, pixel-level annotations for ZOD by converting bounding boxes into semantic masks. In this pilot study, we process over 100,000 frames and manually curate a 2,300-frame subset (36% acceptance rate) to establish a reliable baseline. Using these annotations, we evaluate transformer-based CLFT and CNN-based DeepLabV3+ architectures across diverse weather conditions, achieving up to 48.1% mIoU with CLFT-Hybrid. To address extreme class imbalance, where pedestrians, cyclists, and signs constitute less than 1% of pixels, we explore specialized models targeting rare classes. We further validate the pipeline on the Iseauto autonomous-vehicle platform, achieving 77.5% mIoU, and show that SAM-derived representations transfer effectively across sensor configurations via bidirectional transfer learning. All code and annotations are released to support reproducible research.

</details>


### [46] [Which Pretraining Paradigm Better Serves Spatial Intelligence? An Empirical Comparison of Vision-Language and Video Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28132)
*Haozhan Shen,Tiancheng Zhao,Kangjia Zhao,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLMs excel in semantic tagging and instance grouping; VGMs excel in dense geometry and camera motion; naive fusion yields gains on both semantics and geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which pre-training backbone provides richer spatial representations for spatial intelligence via a controlled frozen-feature probing framework.

Method: Frozen-feature probing on frozen representations from VLMs and VGMs across three axes—semantic tagging, instance grouping, and 3D geometry prediction—evaluating and comparing their information content and exploring feature fusion.

Result: VLMs surpass VGMs in semantic tagging and instance grouping, while VGMs provide more accessible signals for dense geometry and motion; a simple fusion of the two yields improvements across geometry and semantics; code released.

Conclusion: Integrating VLM and VGM features is a promising direction for stronger spatial-intelligence backbones; future work should explore principled fusion strategies and joint training.

Abstract: Spatial intelligence requires visual representations that capture both semantic objects and geometric structure in the physical world. To support this, two major pre-training schemes are now widely used as foundation backbones: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which use language supervision to align visual observations with semantic concepts, and Video Generation Models (VGMs), which learn from temporally evolving visual worlds. However, it still remains unclear which pre-training scheme provides a better representation substrate for spatial intelligence. In this paper, we present the first systematic frozen-feature probing study of VLMs and VGMs across three representative axes of spatial intelligence: semantic tagging, instance grouping, and 3D geometry prediction. Using the lightweight probe, our framework enables a controlled comparison of what information is already encoded in frozen representations from two model families. Experimental results reveal a clear complementarity: VLMs are stronger at semantic tagging and instance grouping, while VGMs provide more accessible signals for dense geometry and camera motion. Moreover, a naive fusion of the two already yields a representation that excels at both geometry and semantics, suggesting a promising direction for building stronger spatial-intelligence backbones by effectively integrating features from both model families. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/om-ai-lab/Probing-VLM-VGM}{https://github.com/om-ai-lab/Probing-VLM-VGM}.

</details>


### [47] [No Safe Dose: How Training Data Drives Unsafe Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28137)
*Felix Friedrich,Lukas Helff,Niharika Hegde,Patrick Schramowski,Kristian Kersting*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Safety in text-to-image models scales with the fraction of unsafe training data; the proportion, not the absolute count, drives unsafe outputs. Even with 0% unsafe data there is a safety floor due to other components (e.g., text encoder). SafeCLIP reduces this floor; data curation and text encoder safety are complementary and can improve safety without harming image quality; remaining unsafety suggests further research on emergent capabilities and compositionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To isolate the impact of training data composition (fraction of unsafe images) on model output safety and disentangle it from other safety determinants.

Method: Train identical text-to-image models on datasets where the fraction of unsafe images varies from 0% to 9.6% across multiple scales (100k–8M). Generate images and evaluate with four independent safety classifiers. Use a factorial design to separate the effects of proportion vs absolute count. Conduct text encoder ablations (including SafeCLIP) to assess encoder-related safety contributions. Assess image quality with FID, CLIPscore, and ImageReward across three encoders.

Result: Safety outputs rise monotonically with unsafe data fraction: 16.6% at 0% contamination to 25.5% at 5% contamination. The proportion of unsafe training data is the operative variable; a 16.6% irreducible baseline at zero contamination points to safety risks from other components. SafeCLIP reduces this floor to 9.6%, while the dose–response effect persists across encoders. No degradation in image quality metrics (FID, CLIPscore, ImageReward) accompanying safety filtering.

Conclusion: Data curation and text encoder safety are complementary and independently effective for improving safety. The remaining unsafety highlights areas for future work on emergent capabilities and compositionality.

Abstract: Text-to-image models trained on large-scale data often inevitably ingest unsafe content. While some people observe input-output amplifications, it remains unclear whether and how training data composition directly drives model output safety or by other factors. We shed light on this question by isolating this variable: we train the same text-to-image model on datasets that differ \emph{only} in their fraction of unsafe images (0\% to 9.6\%), across several dataset scales (100K to 8M). Then we generate images with the resulting models, and evaluate them with four independent safety classifiers. Output unsafety rises monotonically from 16.6\% at 0\% contamination to 25.5\% at 5\%. A factorial design reveals that the \emph{proportion}, not the absolute count, of unsafe training images is the operative variable. The 16.6\% irreducible baseline at zero contamination implicates the other components, e.g. frozen text encoder, as a residual safety risk -- confirmed by a text encoder ablation showing that SafeCLIP reduces this floor to 9.6\%, while the dose-response effect persists across all three encoders tested. Critically, no quality degradation in terms of FID, CLIPscore and ImageReward accompanies safety filtering. These results establish that data curation and text encoder safety are complementary and independently effective interventions. At the same time, the remaining level of unsafety poses questions for future research about emerging capabilities and compositionality.

</details>


### [48] [A novel ordinal multi-view aggregation scheme for oak defoliation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28151)
*Francisco Bérchez-Moreno,Ricardo Enrique Hernández-Lambraño,David Guijo-Rubio,Víctor Manuel Vargas,Francisco José Ruiz-Gómez,Juan Carlos Fernández,Pablo González-Moreno*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ordinal defoliation estimation from ground-level images using a three-view CNN ensemble (north, south, crown) to exploit multi-view information; modeling the ordinal class structure improves accuracy; three-view ensemble yields the strongest, most robust performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forest decline due to climate and biotic stressors threatens ecosystem functioning, necessitating accurate, scalable tree-health monitoring.

Method: A multi-view ensemble framework of CNNs trained on three perspectives (north, south, crown) for ordinal defoliation classification, including comparisons of multiple ordinal methods and analysis of view contributions; homogeneous ensemble design to preserve consistency.

Result: Modeling the ordinal structure of defoliation levels improves performance over nominal approaches; the three-view ensemble consistently outperforms single-view and pairwise configurations, achieving robust and accurate predictions across metrics.

Conclusion: Integrating deep learning, ordinal classification, and multi-view aggregation offers scalable, consistent, and objective forest health assessment in complex ecosystems such as Mediterranean dehesas.

Abstract: Forest decline driven by climate and biotic stressors threatens ecosystem functioning, making accurate monitoring of tree health essential. In this work, we address tree defoliation estimation as an ordinal classification problem using ground-level imagery. We propose a novel multi-view ensemble framework that aggregates predictions from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on different perspectives of individual trees (north, south, and crown). This approach leverages complementary visual information while preserving modelling consistency through a homogeneous ensemble design.
  A comprehensive evaluation is conducted by comparing multiple ordinal classification methods and analysing the contribution of each view and their combinations. Results show that modelling the ordinal structure of defoliation levels improves performance over nominal approaches, while the proposed multi-view ensemble consistently outperforms single-view and pairwise configurations. In particular, the three-view ensemble achieves the most robust and accurate predictions across all evaluation metrics.
  These findings highlight the potential of combining Deep Learning (DL), Ordinal Classification (OC), and multi-view aggregation for scalable, consistent, and objective forest health assessment in complex ecosystems such as Mediterranean dehesas.

</details>


### [49] [Intra-YOLO: A Small Object Detection Model for Caries and Molar-Incisor Hypomineralization in Intraoral Photography Based on Transfer Learning with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28157)
*Po-Lun Chwang,Po-Yu Chang,Wen-Liang Lin,Tung-Sheng Wu,Min-Ching Wang,Yun-Chien Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A CAD system was developed to detect dental caries and MIH in intraoral photographs, addressing differentiation challenges due to similar appearances and small lesion size under variable imaging conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Differentiating caries from MIH in photographic dentistry is difficult because of similar visual features, small lesion size, and variability in imaging conditions; an automated CAD approach could aid diagnostic accuracy and consistency.

Method: Developed a computer-aided diagnosis pipeline for detecting caries and MIH in intraoral photographs.

Result: Not reported in the provided abstract snippet.

Conclusion: The abstract emphasizes diagnostic challenge and the potential utility of CAD but provides no stated conclusions; further validation needed.

Abstract: This study developed a computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system for detecting caries and molar-incisor hypomineralization (MIH) in intraoral photographs. These lesions share similar appearances, making clinical differentiation challenging, especially given their small size and variability in imaging conditions.

</details>


### [50] [MeniOmni: A Structured Multimodal Benchmark for Holistic Meniscus Injury Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28161)
*Shurui Xu,Siqi Yang,Weiping Ding,Hui Wang,Mengzhen Fan,Yuyu Sun,Shuyan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multimodal knee MRI benchmark (MeniOmni) integrating imaging (tri-planar MRI), Clinical Priors (patient context), and expert text for two tasks: Stoller severity grading and diagnostic report generation; introduces Meni-Score and risk-aware evaluation; baseline results show benefits of Priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current knee MRI benchmarks are typically unimodal with coarse labels, limiting evaluation of holistic clinical reasoning; a multimodal dataset is needed to reflect real-world radiology practice.

Method: Constructed MeniOmni with 746 multi-center MRI studies, tri-planar volumetric inputs, Clinical Priors, and expert-annotated clinical text; supports two tasks (severity grading and report generation); proposes risk-aware ordinal evaluation and semantic consistency metric (Meni-Score).

Result: Baseline experiments show that incorporating Clinical Priors improves grading performance and reduces severe errors, highlighting the value of multimodal context for safer assessment.

Conclusion: MeniOmni provides a valuable resource for holistic knee MRI reasoning; code and data are available at the provided GitHub link; potential for improved clinical decision support through multimodal integration.

Abstract: Clinical diagnosis of meniscus injuries requires radiologists to integrate volumetric MRI evidence with patient context (e.g., sex, age, BMI) and to produce structured diagnostic reports. Existing knee MRI benchmarks are typically unimodal and rely on coarse labels, limiting their ability to evaluate holistic clinical reasoning. We introduce MeniOmni, a structured multimodal benchmark for meniscus injury assessment, consisting of 746 multi-center MRI studies with tri-planar volumetric inputs, Clinical Priors, and expert-annotated clinical text. MeniOmni supports two tasks: (1) fine-grained Stoller severity grading and (2) diagnostic report generation. We further propose risk-aware ordinal evaluation and a semantic consistency metric (Meni-Score) to better reflect clinical relevance. Baseline experiments show that incorporating Clinical Priors improves grading performance and reduces severe errors, highlighting the value of multimodal context for safer assessment. Code and data are available at https://github.com/ShuruiXu/MeniOmni.

</details>


### [51] [DebFilter: Eradicating Biases Stashed in Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28167)
*Seung Hyuk Lee,Songkuk Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DebFilter provides inference-time bias mitigation for text-to-image diffusion by offsetting cross-attention values, requiring no training or data, and preserving text alignment while reducing biased outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text embeddings (e.g., CLIP) and large training corpora encode social biases (gender, age, etc.) that are amplified during guided diffusion, yielding biased images. A lightweight, training-free mitigation is needed for fairer generation.

Method: During denoising steps, DebFilter applies a fixed offset to the value components within the cross-attention mechanism, effectively perturbing the guidance embedding slice to steer attention toward unbiased representations. This at-inference adjustment reconfigures the score landscape without updating any model parameters or requiring new data.

Result: Demonstrates effective reduction of social biases in generated images while preserving alignment with the intended text semantics. The approach is lightweight, data-free, and scalable, with minimal computational overhead.

Conclusion: DebFilter offers a practical, training-free pathway to fairer text-to-image generation by modifying cross-attention dynamics at inference, avoiding retraining and data requirements.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models, which are theoretically equivalent to score-based generative models, generate images through a multi-step denoising process guided by text embeddings extracted from pretrained vision-language models such as CLIP. However, these text embeddings inherently encode social and semantic biases -- such as those related to gender and age -- that are subsequently propagated and amplified through the guidance mechanism, along with the model's training on large-scale datasets that are imbalanced with respect to these bias-related concepts, often leading to skewed outputs in text-to-image generation. We propose DebFilter, a lightweight and training-free framework for mitigating such biases in text-to-image diffusion models. Observing that the model's error prediction at each denoising step is primarily influenced by cross-attention dynamics, we introduce a bias-correction strategy that adjusts the value components within cross-attention. Specifically, we apply a fixed offset to the slice of guidance embedding, effectively steering the semantic direction of cross-attention values toward unbiased representations. This adjustment reconfigures the score landscape to produce balanced outputs while maintaining alignment with the intended text semantics. Unlike prior approaches that rely on fine-tuning or retraining, DebFilter operates entirely at inference time, requiring no additional data or model updates. Our results demonstrate that this method effectively mitigates social biases in generated images, offering an efficient and scalable pathway toward fairer and more inclusive text-to-image generation.

</details>


### [52] [MangaFlow: An End-to-End Agentic Framework for Controllable Story to Manga Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28173)
*Muyao Wang,Zeke Xie,Yanhao Chen,Lixin Xiu,Hideki Nakayama*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MangaFlow introduces an agentic, controllable long-form manga generation framework that decomposes the creation process into planning, grounding, layout construction, reference-conditioned rendering, composition, and text placement, with explicit intermediate controls (layout, assets, lettering) and a story section memory to ensure cross-panel consistency; a meta-benchmark evaluates layout controllability, visual consistency, and quality, and results show improvements over direct generation baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of end-to-end manga generation that entangles layout, references, and cross-panel consistency, and support long-form, editable, and memory-enabled manga creation.

Method: Decompose manga creation into planning, grounding, layout construction, reference-conditioned rendering, composition, and text placement; treat layout and visual references as explicit intermediate variables; implement a story section memory linking section descriptions to character/scene/object references for reuse; enable text-to-manga and user-controlled generation; propose a meta-benchmark for evaluation across layout controllability, visual consistency, and quality.

Result: Empirical results show improved layout adherence and cross-panel consistency over direct generation baselines, while enabling flexible human control.

Conclusion: MangaFlow provides editable intermediate controls and a memory mechanism to support controllable, long-form manga generation, and introduces a meta-benchmark to evaluate layout, consistency, and quality.

Abstract: End-to-end manga generation is a structured visual storytelling task that requires story decomposition, recurring character and scene grounding, page layout design, panel rendering, page composition, and lettering. However, existing generative models often perform direct page synthesis, entangling these factors in a single visual output and limiting precise control over layout geometry, visual references, and cross-panel consistency.
