Unlike prior works that assume known bias attributes or relies on synthetic mixing, our method operates without manual annotations and can leverage off-the-shelf vision-language and editing models. BiasEdit addresses a fundamental challenge in Web-sourced visual AI, mitigating dataset-induced bias and achieving state-of-the-art debiasing performance even when training data are fully biased.

</details>


### [77] [REVEAL: Reference-Grounded Reasoning for Multimodal Manipulation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28459)
*Jun Zhou,Bingwen Hu,Yaxiong Wang,Zhedong Zheng,Yongzhen Wang,Yuchen Zhang,Ping Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: REVEAL reframes multimodal manipulation detection as reference-grounded verification, using a large authentic evidence library, difference-aware fusion, and a task-decoupled MoE to outperform SOTA and enable training-free domain adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on memorizing isolated artifacts and robustness issues with imperceptible manipulations and domain shift by leveraging human-like comparative reasoning against authentic references.

Method: Propose REVEAL framework. Build a large reference library of 170K authentic image-text pairs (40K+ public figures). Use a difference-aware fusion module to detect fine-grained discrepancies between query and retrieved evidence. Employ a task-decoupled Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to jointly perform instance-level detection and fine-grained grounding, mitigating optimization conflicts. Demonstrate training-free domain adaptation by updating the reference library.

Result: REVEAL achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods on multimodal manipulation detection and enables training-free domain adaptation simply by refreshing the reference library; code is released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: The work presents a practical, robust, and scalable solution for evolving misinformation detection by leveraging comparative verification against authentic evidence and effective cross-modal grounding.

Abstract: Multimodal manipulation detection aims to simultaneously identify forged image--text pairs and localize tampered regions, yet existing methods typically rely on memorizing isolated artifacts and struggle with imperceptible manipulation traces or domain shifts. Inspired by human comparative reasoning, we reformulate this task as a reference-grounded verification problem, where authenticity is assessed by comparing a query against retrieved authentic evidence. We propose REVEAL Reference-Enabled Verification for Evidence Analysis and Localization), a framework explicitly designed for this comparative paradigm. To support this paradigm, we construct a large-scale reference library comprising 170K authentic news image--text pairs featuring over 40K public figures. Technically, REVEAL employs a difference-aware fusion mechanism to capture fine-grained discrepancies between the query and retrieved evidence. Furthermore, we introduce a task-decoupled Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to jointly execute instance-level detection and fine-grained grounding, effectively mitigating optimization conflicts between these heterogeneous objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that REVEAL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, and notably enables \emph{training-free domain adaptation} by simply updating the reference library, offering a robust and practical solution for detecting evolving misinformation. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/REVEAL-Reference-A006.

</details>


### [78] [SA4Depth: Consistent Pose-Depth Scale Alignment for Self-Supervised Monocular Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28477)
*Changxuan Li,Nadine Berner,Nassir Navab,Federico Tombari,Stefano Gasperini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SA4Depth refines pose using reprojected features from depth during training to align scene scales between depth and pose networks, improving self-supervised monocular depth estimation without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In self-supervised monocular depth estimation, the depth and pose networks may produce scene scales that are misaligned, limiting depth accuracy. Aligning their scales can improve depth predictions.

Method: A differentiable training-time refinement that uses current depth estimates to reproject learnable visual features across consecutive frames to refine pose, reducing feature alignment residuals. The refinement integrates into existing self-supervised pipelines and does not affect inference time.

Result: Empirically improves depth predictions on outdoor and indoor datasets (KITTI, Cityscapes, NYUv2), improves scale consistency across sequences, and enhances pose refinement on KITTI Odometry.

Conclusion: Aligning the scales of depth and pose networks via SA4Depth yields substantial depth accuracy gains without increasing inference cost; the approach is differentiable and easily plug-and-play with existing self-supervised depth estimation pipelines; code is available.

Abstract: Self-supervised depth estimation from monocular sequences relies on the joint learning of a depth and a pose network. Despite abundant research done to improve the depth network, efforts on the pose remain limited. In this context, even when depth is estimated up to scale, we highlight the importance of the alignment between the scene scales estimated by the pose and depth nets. Then, we introduce SA4Depth, an approach to improve this alignment and boost the depth predictions while keeping the inference time unchanged. Our proposed method uses the depth estimated during training to reproject learnable visual features across consecutive frames and refine the pose estimates by reducing feature alignment residuals. With our method, the estimated scene scales by the separate depth and pose networks are aligned, and the prediction scale consistency is improved across different sequences. Our differentiable refinement integrates seamlessly into existing self-supervised pipelines and substantially improves their depth estimates. We demonstrate this with extensive experiments both outdoors and indoors on KITTI, Cityscapes, and NYUv2. Additionally, results on KITTI Odometry confirm the effectiveness of our pose refinement. Our code is available at https://github.com/Runningchauncey/SA4Depth .

</details>


### [79] [SSR3D-LLM: Structured Spatial Reasoning via Latent Steps for Fine-Grained Grounding in Unified 3D-LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28490)
*Jiawei Li,Ziyi Liu,Weijie Shi,Long Chen,Jiajie Xu,Xiaofang Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SSR3D-LLM introduces a structured latent spatial reasoning interface for 3D grounding, where the LLM outputs a sequence of latent steps and a geometry-aware scorer refines Mask3D proposals to achieve strong, fine-grained grounding across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-pointer grounding compresses relational information into one choice, making fine-grained queries brittle; unified 3D-LLMs need a structured reasoning mechanism to incorporate context and spatial relations while remaining compatible with language tasks.

Method: Use fixed Mask3D proposals; LLM emits latent spatial reasoning steps and memory tokens from the query; a geometry-aware scorer consumes these steps to iteratively refine candidate rankings with step-length masking; training uses target supervision with auxiliary referential-cue signals; inference relies only on query + Mask3D proposals.

Result: SSR3D-LLM achieves state-of-the-art among unified 3D-LLMs on ReferIt3D, ScanRefer, Multi3DRef; shows substantial gains vs single-pointer QPG baseline on fine-grained grounding; consistent improvements over prior unified 3D-LLMs; preserves standard language-task route.

Conclusion: Structured latent reasoning augmented grounding improves robustness for fine-grained 3D grounding tasks and maintains compatibility with existing 3D-LLM pipelines, achieving strong performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: 3D object grounding localizes referred objects in a 3D scene from natural language. Unified instance-centric 3D-LLMs aim to solve grounding together with dialog, QA, and captioning, yet many rely on a single pointer-style grounding decision that compresses a relational instruction into one selection. This is brittle for fine-grained queries where multiple same-class candidates must be ruled out by context objects and spatial relations. We propose Structured Spatial Reasoning 3D-LLM (SSR3D-LLM), a structured grounding interface for unified 3D-LLMs. Given fixed Mask3D object proposals, the LLM writes a sequence of latent spatial reasoning steps and memory tokens from the query, and a geometry-aware scorer reads these latent steps in order to refine candidate rankings step by step with step-length masking. The latent steps are learned from standard benchmark target supervision with auxiliary referential-cue supervision during training, while inference uses only the input query and Mask3D proposals. Across ReferIt3D, ScanRefer, and Multi3DRef, SSR3D-LLM achieves the strongest results among unified 3D-LLM baselines, with substantial gains over the single-pointer QPG baseline on fine-grained grounding and consistent improvements over prior unified 3D-LLMs, while preserving the default language-task route.

</details>


### [80] [DiscoForcing: A Unified Framework for Real-Time Audio-Driven Character Control with Diffusion Forcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28491)
*Kaiyang Ji,Bingsheng Qian,Binghuan Wu,Kangyi Chen,Ye Shi,Jingya Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiscoForcing enables real-time, causal audio-driven motion generation by fusing a streaming, causal music encoder with a diffusion-forcing model, plus a hybrid temporal scheduler and history-guided sampler to balance responsiveness and long-horizon consistency under non-stationary audio.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the poor performance of traditional music-to-motion systems in streaming, real-time contexts where conditioning history becomes stale and low-latency, deployable control is required.

Method: A streaming diffusion framework: (1) a causal music encoder capturing rhythmic structure and phase; (2) a diffusion-forcing sequence model trained with heterogeneous noise across the temporal horizon; (3) a hybrid temporal schedule; (4) a history-guided streaming sampler to trade responsiveness against long-horizon consistency under non-stationary audio.

Result: In an end-to-end real-time system with online avatar playback and humanoid workflows, DiscoForcing achieves more stable long-horizon rollouts and sharper audio-motion alignment than baselines under matched causality/latency, while maintaining real-time throughput.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that real-time, deployment-faithful audio-driven motion can be improved via causal encoders and diffusion-forcing with history-aware sampling; suitable for interactive avatars and streaming media where audio conditions change abruptly.

Abstract: We study real-time audio-responsive character control as a deployment-faithful problem: strictly causal, bounded-latency streaming that must generate coherent full-body motion at interactive frame rates while the audio condition can change abruptly, including tempo shifts, drops, or user edits. Prior music-to-motion systems are largely optimized for offline generation with global context, and degrade in streaming rollouts where conditioning history becomes stale or unreliable. We introduce DiscoForcing, a streaming audio-driven diffusion framework that combines a causal music encoder that captures rhythmic structure and phase dynamics with a diffusion-forcing sequence model trained under heterogeneous noise levels across the temporal horizon. Building on this, we design a hybrid temporal schedule and a history-guided streaming sampler to explicitly trade off responsiveness against long-horizon consistency under non-stationary audio. Implemented in an end-to-end real-time interactive system with online avatar playback and humanoid deployment workflows, DiscoForcing delivers more stable long-horizon rollouts and sharper audio-motion alignment than prior baselines under matched causality and latency constraints while maintaining real-time throughput.

</details>


### [81] [Janus-LoRA: A Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28495)
*Cheng Chen,Pengpeng Zeng,Yuyu Guo,Lianli Gao,Hengtao Shen,Jingkuan Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Janus-LoRA introduces Gradient Rectification and Decoupled Margin Loss to restore stability-plasticity in LoRA for continual learning by decoupling factor updates and promoting feature-level separation, achieving state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA-based continual learning suffers from interference: the composite LoRA update violates orthogonality to the task-specific subspace, reintroducing forgetting, while enforcing orthogonality harms plasticity. A method is needed to preserve learned knowledge while enabling new learning.

Method: Gradient Rectification provides a closed-form decoupling of LoRA A and B updates and enforces orthogonality against the historical knowledge subspace via online estimation. Decoupled Margin Loss promotes plasticity by pushing new feature representations away from old ones, creating low-interference regions for new learning.

Result: Experiments on challenging benchmarks show improved stability-plasticity balance and establish new state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: The combination of parameter-level orthogonality enforcement and feature-level separation in Janus-LoRA yields a superior stability-plasticity balance and state-of-the-art continual learning performance.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as a promising paradigm for Continual Learning. It independently updates its low-rank factors ($A$ and $B$), creating a composite update to the full weight matrix through their interaction. To prevent catastrophic forgetting, this update should remain orthogonal to the task-specific subspace that contains previously learned knowledge. However, we identify that this composite update systematically violates this orthogonality, reintroducing interference and undermining stability. Furthermore, naively enforcing this orthogonality compromises plasticity, disrupting the delicate stability-plasticity trade-off. To resolve these issues, we propose \textbf{Janus-LoRA}, a framework that restores this balance through two novel components. Specifically, we first introduce Gradient Rectification, a closed-form solution that mathematically decouples LoRA's factor updates, enforcing orthogonality against the historical knowledge subspace identified by an efficient Online Estimation. Next, to enhance plasticity, we introduce a Decoupled Margin Loss that promotes feature-level separation by pushing new feature representations away from old ones, thus creating distinct, low-interference regions for new learning. Comprehensive experiments on challenging benchmarks demonstrate that by harmonizing parameter-level orthogonality with feature-level separation, Janus-LoRA achieves a superior balance and establishes new state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [82] [DriveWAM: Video Generative Priors Enable Scalable World-Action Modeling for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28544)
*Chen Shi,Jinrui Xu,Shaoshuai Shi,Kehua Sheng,Bo Zhang,Li Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveWAM: a scalable world-action model that repurposes a pretrained video diffusion transformer into an autoregressive policy for driving, augmented with scene guidance from a frozen vision-language model and selective memory to bound long-horizon rollouts; shows strong planning performance and data scalability on driving benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage rich temporal priors and dynamics captured by video diffusion models for end-to-end driving, addressing long-horizon planning and incorporating high-level semantic guidance while keeping memory and computation tractable.

Method: Unify video and action streams into a single temporal token sequence and train with a joint flow-matching objective that preserves the pretrained video-generation architecture while adapting it for action generation. Introduce scene-evolving driving guidance via a frozen VLM to provide chunk-specific semantic intent for guidance. Implement selective KV memory to bound long-horizon rollouts by maintaining capped modality-specific video/action memory pools using relevance-redundancy cache selection during inference.

Result: DriveWAM achieves strong planning performance on NAVSIM and PhysicalAI-Autonomous-Vehicles benchmarks. A data-scaling study from 4k to 100k driving clips demonstrates the model’s scalability and effectiveness of world-action modeling for end-to-end autonomous driving.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that pretrained video priors can be effectively repurposed for action generation in driving, with additional mechanisms (scene guidance and bounded memory) enabling robust long-horizon planning and scalable data utilization.

Abstract: Pretrained foundation models have become an important basis for end-to-end autonomous driving. In contrast to vision-language models pretrained primarily on static image-text pairs, video generative models capture temporal dynamics and motion priors that are naturally suited for driving. We present DriveWAM, a driving world-action model that adapts a pretrained video diffusion transformer into an autoregressive video-action policy. DriveWAM organizes video and action streams into a unified temporal token sequence and trains them under a joint flow-matching objective, preserving the pretrained video-generation architecture while adapting its large-scale video priors to action generation. To incorporate high-level scene understanding, we introduce scene-evolving driving guidance, where a frozen VLM produces chunk-specific semantic intent to guide video-action generation. To keep long-horizon rollout bounded, we further introduce selective KV memory, which maintains bounded modality-aware video and action memory pools through relevance-redundancy cache selection at inference time. Experiments on NAVSIM and the PhysicalAI-Autonomous-Vehicles benchmark show that DriveWAM achieves strong planning performance, and a data-scaling study from 4k to 100k driving clips further confirms the scaling potential of world-action modeling for end-to-end autonomous driving.

</details>


### [83] [GEM: Generative Supervision Helps Embodied Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28548)
*Ruowen Zhao,Bangguo Li,Zuyan Liu,Yinan Liang,Junliang Ye,Fangfu Liu,Diankun Wu,Zhengyi Wang,Xumin Yu,Yongming Rao,Han Hu,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Jointly train a depth-generation objective with embodied vision-language pre-training (GEM) to improve both semantic grounding and physical execution, enabled by GEM-4M data and GEM-VLA deployment; achieves state-of-the-art on embodied benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high-level semantic VLM pre-training and low-level spatial/physical knowledge required for embodied action.

Method: Integrate a depth map generation task into the VLM pre-training objective; train the generative depth objective alongside the core model; curate and release GEM-4M with grounding, reasoning, planning data and depth supervision; develop and evaluate the deployed action model GEM-VLA.

Result: Substantial improvements in embodied intelligence; state-of-the-art performance across diverse embodied benchmarks; superior task execution in simulation and real-world settings.

Conclusion: Depth supervision during pre-training enhances both semantic understanding and physical operation capabilities, enabling better grounding, reasoning, and planning in embodied tasks.

Abstract: Embodied Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance and generalization in robotics, particularly within Vision-Language-Action frameworks. However, a significant gap remains between the high-level semantic focus of standard text-guided pre-training paradigms and the low-level spatial and physical knowledge critical for execution in embodied environments. In this paper, we introduce GEM, a Generative-supervised Embodied vision-language Model designed to bridge this divide. We propose integrating a depth map generation task directly into the VLM pre-training phase. By training this generative objective jointly with the main model, we observe substantial improvements in embodied intelligence, significantly enhancing both semantic understanding and physical operation capabilities. To support this paradigm, we curate and release GEM-4M, a comprehensive large-scale dataset featuring a mixture of grounding, reasoning, and planning data paired with high-quality depth supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GEM achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse embodied benchmarks. Furthermore, our deployed action model, GEM-VLA, exhibits vastly superior task execution abilities in both simulation environments and real-world evaluations. Code, models, and datasets are available at https://zhaorw02.github.io/GEM/

</details>


### [84] [Resolution-free neural surrogates for geometric parameterization and mapping with spatially varying fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28551)
*Yanwen Huang,Lok Ming Lui,Gary P. T. Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A resolution-free neural surrogate for geometric parameterization and mapping that predicts mappings from spatially varying fields to query locations, using multi-resolution encoding and physics-informed training; demonstrated on quasi-conformal and density-equalizing mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computationally expensive high-resolution solvers for spatially varying parameter fields across instances and grid dependency motivate a fast, grid-free surrogate.

Method: A neural surrogate conditioned on coordinate-augmented samples of the parameter field p; uses a multi-resolution geometric encoding to map query locations x_i to u(x_i) for arbitrary point sets. Trained without labeled solution data by enforcing geometry-aware constraints derived from variational energies, diffusion-based density equalization, and quasi-conformal theory.

Result: Experimental results on quasi-conformal mapping and density-equalizing mapping problems demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Conclusion: The approach provides a resolution-free, grid-agnostic mapping surrogate that reduces dependence on labeled data and accommodates arbitrary point sets, with applicability to distortion correction, deformable registration, and related geometric mapping tasks.

Abstract: Many imaging problems require computing spatial transformations induced by spatially varying intensity, feature, or density fields. Canonical examples include distortion correction, deformable image registration, atlas-based segmentation, and deformation-driven image analysis. These tasks can be formulated as geometric mapping problems in which the transformation is constrained to preserve local structure, control boundary behavior, or regulate angular distortion. Such formulations typically lead to variational models, diffusion processes, or elliptic partial differential equations. However, repeatedly solving high-resolution systems becomes computationally expensive when the underlying parameter fields vary across instances. In this work, we propose a resolution-free neural surrogate for geometric parameterization and mapping problems. Given a spatially varying parameter field $p:Ω\to\mathbb{R}^m$ and query locations $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^N\subsetΩ$, the model predicts mapped locations $\{u(x_i)\}_{i=1}^N$ on arbitrary structured or unstructured point sets. To avoid dependence on a fixed grid, we use a multi-resolution geometric encoding strategy that conditions the network on coordinate-augmented samples of the parameter field. The model is trained without labeled solution data by enforcing geometry-aware constraints derived from variational energies, diffusion-based density equalization, and quasi-conformal theory. Experimental results on quasi-conformal mapping and density-equalizing mapping problems are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

</details>


### [85] [Deformable Gaussian Occupancy: Decoupling Rigid and Nonrigid Motion with Factorized Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28587)
*Yang Gao,Wuyang Li,Po-Chien Luan,Alexandre Alahi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeGO introduces a deformable Gaussian occupancy framework that separates rigid and nonrigid motion, and adds a factorized 4D distillation from VGGT foundation models to improve temporal coherence under weak supervision. It achieves state-of-the-art results on Occ3D-NuScenes with 13.5% gains on human-centric instances and 10.9% overall.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weakly supervised occupancy prediction for dynamic 3D environments is challenged by nonrigid deformations and poor temporal coherence; existing methods assume rigid motion and rely on simple frame-to-frame offsets.

Method: DeGO combines (1) deformable Gaussian occupancy with decoupled rigid/nonrigid motion; Gaussian primitives evolve via deformation and offset updates; (2) a factorized 4D distillation from a VGGT foundation model to produce foundation-aligned features across cameras and frames, enhancing temporal consistency.

Result: On Occ3D-NuScenes, DeGO achieves state-of-the-art performance under weak supervision, with 13.5% improvements on human-centric instances and 10.9% overall gains. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Deformation-aware occupancy modeling combined with foundation-guided distillation yields improved dynamic scene understanding under weak supervision, particularly for nonrigid, human-centric agents.

Abstract: Understanding dynamic 3D environments is essential for safe autonomous driving, particularly when reasoning about human-centric, nonrigid agents. However, existing weakly supervised occupancy prediction frameworks predominantly assume rigid-body motion and rely on simple frame-to-frame offsets, limiting their ability to capture fine-grained deformations and maintain temporal coherence. To address this issue, we propose DeGO, a deformable Gaussian occupancy framework that unifies decoupled Gaussian deformation with factorized 4D foundation-model distillation. DeGO disentangles rigid and nonrigid motion, enabling each Gaussian primitive to evolve through both deformation and offset-based updates. In parallel, a factorized 4D distillation strategy transfers cross-camera and cross-frame knowledge from the VGGT foundation model, producing foundation-aligned features that enhance temporal consistency. Experiments on the Occ3D-NuScenes benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance under weak supervision, delivering 13.5% gains on human-centric instances and 10.9% overall improvements. These results highlight the effectiveness of deformation-aware and foundation-guided occupancy modeling for dynamic scene understanding. The code is publicly available: https://github.com/vita-epfl/DeGO

</details>


### [86] [Mining Multi-Modality Spatio-Temporal Cues for Video Important Person Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28604)
*Xiao Wang,Minglei Yang,Bin Yang,Wenke Huang,Zheng Wang,Xin Xu,Mang Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Video Important Person (VIP) identification with Temporal-VIP dataset and VIP-Net; addresses Temporal Importance Shift using Social Cue Encoder and Temporal Importance Rectifier; achieves 67.3% accuracy and 0.63 mean rationale similarity, with dataset/code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify key individuals in video scenes by leveraging rich spatio-temporal information; static-image focus misses Temporal Importance Shift (TIS) where importance of individuals evolves over time.

Method: VIP-Net comprises a Social Cue Encoder (SCE) for multi-modal spatio-temporal cues, a Temporal Importance Rectifier (TIR) for hierarchical cue fusion and cross-modal alignment, and VIP Inference to rank individuals; training includes rationale annotations to produce textual rationales.

Result: 67.3% accuracy on VIP identification, outperforming SOTA models at 37.5%-53.9%; mean rationale similarity of 0.63 to ground truth via feature-guided LLM refinement; dataset includes 9,249 video segments across 11 categories; code and dataset released on HuggingFace.

Conclusion: Presents a new task (VIP identification) and a large rationale-annotated dataset to mitigate Temporal Importance Shift. Demonstrates that a multi-modal, temporally-aware framework can outperform static-image baselines and provides explainable rationales; enables future exploration in video understanding and surveillance-oriented applications.

Abstract: Identifying key individuals in video scenes is essential for applications such as automated video editing and intelligent surveillance. Current methods primarily focus on static images and immediate visual cues, overlooking the rich spatio-temporal information in videos. This leads to the phenomenon of Temporal Importance Shift (TIS), wherein individuals deemed significant in early frames may be demoted as the entire temporal context is considered. To address this, we introduce the Video Important Person (VIP) identification task, aimed at automatically identifying the most influential individuals in videos while providing textual rationales. We present Temporal-VIP, a large-scale rationale-annotated dataset consisting of 9,249 video segments across 11 categories with aligned importance rationales. To mitigate TIS, we develop the VIP-Net framework, which includes a Social Cue Encoder (SCE) for extracting multi-modal spatio-temporal cues, a Temporal Importance Rectifier (TIR) for hierarchical cue fusion and cross-modal alignment, and VIP Inference for ranking individuals. Experimental results show that VIP-Net achieves 67.3% accuracy, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art models (37.5%-53.9%) and yielding a mean rationale similarity of 0.63 to ground truth through feature-guided LLM refinement. The dataset and code are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/yml2002/Temporal-VIP.

</details>


### [87] [Internally Referenced Low-Light Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28605)
*Peiyuan He,Hainuo Wang,Hengxing Liu,Mingjia Li,Xiaojie Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised LLIE method that builds internal references from degraded input to separate illumination, color, and texture while denoising, achieving state-of-the-art results with a novel GAFM module.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLIE without paired data is hard due to entangled illumination, texture details, and noise; generating reliable internal references can guide illumination estimation and texture preservation.

Method: Three components: (1) local exposure-simulated scheme to obtain a low-frequency pseudo ground-truth for internal physical reference; (2) dual-domain preservation with spatial and spectral constraints: Illumination-Aligned Perceptual loss for global structure under illumination changes, and Shift-Invariant Spectral Correlation loss for fine local structures and high-frequency noise suppression; (3) Gain-Adaptive Feature Modulation (GAFM) that converts the estimated illumination into an internal spatial gain prior to a blind-spot denoiser for spatially-aware noise reduction.

Result: Extensive experiments report state-of-the-art performance in noise suppression and texture fidelity; code will be released.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that internally referenced supervision can effectively disentangle illumination, texture, and noise in LLIE, enabling robust restoration without external paired data; potential for broad applicability and future extensions.

Abstract: Self-supervised low-light image enhancement (LLIE) is highly appealing as it eliminates the reliance on external paired data. However, the lack of external references causes networks to struggle with decoupling entangled illumination, delicate textures, and amplified noise. To resolve this challenge, we propose an Internally Referenced LLIE framework that extracts reliable physical and structural references from the degraded input image itself. First, we introduce a local exposure-simulated scheme to extract a low-frequency pseudo ground-truth. This serves as an internal physical reference to guide global illumination estimation and correct color casts. Second, we propose a dual-domain preservation strategy with spatial and spectral constraints to construct internal structural references. Specifically, an Illumination-Aligned Perceptual loss preserves global structures under illumination shifts, while a Shift-Invariant Spectral Correlation loss captures fine-grained local structures and suppresses high-frequency noise. Finally, we propose a Gain-Adaptive Feature Modulation (GAFM) mechanism to address highly spatially-variant residual noise. By transforming the self-estimated illumination map into an internal spatial gain prior, GAFM dynamically guides a blind-spot network for spatially-aware denoising. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering superior noise suppression and textural fidelity. Code will be publicly released at https://visonj.github.io/IRLE/.

</details>


### [88] [JECA^2: Judgment-Explanation Consistent Adversarial Attack against Forensic Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28609)
*Jiachen Qian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JECA^2 is a white-box adversarial diagnostic that jointly manipulates visual attribution and textual explanations in forensic VLMs to align judgments with explanations, achieving stronger attack success and consistency than baselines; transfer to closed-source models is limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address robustness gaps in forensic VLMs where explanations may still reveal cues or contradict the attacked judgment under adversarial manipulation; existing attacks target only binary decisions.

Method: Joint visual and textual perturbations: Grad-CAM-guided perturbations to reallocate attribution, plus prompt-embedding optimization under token-proximity constraints to make explanations align with the target judgment; evaluated in white-box setting.

Result: JECA^2 yields higher attack success and judgment-explanation consistency than baselines on forensic VLM benchmarks; some transfer to closed-source models but limited.

Conclusion: Uncovers a consistency failure mode in explanation-based forensic VLMs; future robustness evaluation should go beyond binary detection accuracy.

Abstract: Forensic vision-language models (VLMs) have recently been developed to detect image tampering and provide natural-language explanations. However, their robustness against adversarial manipulation remains underexplored. Existing adversarial attacks typically aim to flip the model's binary judgment, while the accompanying explanation may still reveal forensic cues and contradict the attacked judgment. In this paper, we study judgment-explanation consistent adversarial attacks against forensic VLMs and propose JECA^2, a controlled white-box red-team diagnostic that jointly redirects visual attribution and aligns textual explanations with the target judgment. On the visual side, JECA^2 uses Grad-CAM-guided perturbations to divert attribution from tampered regions toward benign regions. On the textual side, it optimizes prompt embeddings toward authenticity-affirming semantics under a token-proximity constraint. Experiments on forensic VLM benchmarks show that JECA^2 achieves higher attack success and automated judgment-explanation consistency than implemented baselines under white-box threat settings, while transfer to closed-source VLMs remains measurable but limited. Our results highlight a consistency failure mode in explanation-based forensic VLMs and motivate future robustness evaluation beyond binary detection accuracy.

</details>


### [89] [Compositional Text-to-Image Generation Via Region-aware Bimodal Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28615)
*Zhuohan Liu,Wujian Peng,Yitong Chen,Zuxuan Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiDPO introduces BiComp, a high-quality, large-scale preference dataset for compositional text-to-image generation, and jointly optimizes image and text preferences via an extended Diffusion DPO, with region-level guidance to align with compositional prompts, leading to improved compositional fidelity across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current T2I models struggle with complex compositional prompts (attributes, relations, counting). A scalable, preference-based fine-tuning approach can align models more closely with human judgments on compositional accuracy without requiring explicit supervision for every possible concept.

Method: 1) Build BiComp by a carefully designed, quality-controlled pipeline to collect a large-scale preference dataset tailored for compositional prompts. 2) Extend Diffusion DPO to jointly optimize image and text preferences, enabling better alignment with complex prompts. 3) Apply region-level guidance to focus learning on regions tied to compositional concepts, improving fine-grained alignment.

Result: BiDPO substantially improves compositional fidelity and consistently outperforms prior methods on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of preference-based fine-tuning for complex T2I tasks.

Conclusion: BiDPO offers a flexible, scalable alternative to existing techniques for compositional text-to-image generation by leveraging preference-based fine-tuning and region-focused guidance, enhancing the model’s ability to follow complex prompts.

Abstract: Despite the rapid progress of text-to-image (T2I) models, generating images that accurately reflect complex compositional prompts (covering attribute bindings, object relationships, counting) still remains challenging. To address this, we propose BiDPO, a framework to enhance T2I model's capability of compositional text-to-image generation. We begin by introducing an carefully designed pipeline to construct a large-scale preference dataset, BiComp, with strictly quality control. Then, we extend Diffusion DPO to jointly optimize image and text preferences, which is shown to greatly effective in improving the models to follow complex text prompt in generation. To further enhance the models for fine-grained alignment, we employ a region-level guidance method to focus on regions relevant to compositional concepts. Experimental results demonstrate that our BiDPO substantially improves compositional fidelity, consistently outperforming prior methods across multiple benchmarks. Our approach highlights the potential of preference-based fine-tuning for complex text-to-image tasks, offering a flexible and scalable alternative to existing techniques.

</details>


### [90] [A Multiscale Kinetic Framework for Image Segmentation: From Particle Systems to Continuum Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28619)
*Horacio Tettamanti,Giulia Guicciardi,Mattia Zanella*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multiscale kinetic framework for consensus-based image segmentation using a coupled space-feature particle system, deriving a kinetic and a first-order macroscopic model, and applying particle-based optimization for robust segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model image segmentation as a multiscale, particle-based system in which pixels interact in space and feature space to achieve consensus segmentation, with a goal of robustness to noise and reduced computational complexity.

Method: Introduce a coupled interaction scheme for particles evolving in position and feature spaces; derive a kinetic density equation in the space–feature domain incorporating transport, aggregation, and diffusion; scale to obtain a first-order macroscopic model describing the evolution of the fraction of pixels carrying specific feature information; develop a data-oriented, particle-based optimization approach for segmentation.

Result: Numerical experiments show effective segmentation performance and robustness under various noise conditions.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled multiscale approach to consensus-based image segmentation, with a reduced macroscopic model enabling efficient, robust segmentation via particle-based optimization.

Abstract: In this work, we present a multiscale kinetic framework for consensus-based image segmentation. By interpreting an image as a system of interacting particles, each pixel is characterised by its spatial position and an internal feature encoding color information. We introduce a coupled interaction scheme governing the evolution of particles in both position and feature spaces, from which we derive a kinetic formulation for the particle density in the space-feature domain combining transport, aggregation, and diffusion effects. Furthermore, through a suitable scaling, we obtain a first-order macroscopic model describing the evolution of the fraction of pixels carrying information on the fraction of pixels having a certain feature. Based on this reduced-complexity model, we present a data-oriented approach where we make use of particle-based optimisation techniques for the accurate segmentation of images. Numerical tests show the effectiveness of the proposed framework and its robustness under different noise conditions.

</details>


### [91] [EntroAD: Structural Entropy-Guided Prompt Adaptation for Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28630)
*Xinyu Zhao,Qingyun Sun,Jiayi Luo,Jianxin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes EntroAD, a structural-entropy-guided zero-shot anomaly detection framework with dynamic routing and dual-branch prompt adaptation, achieving SOTA on 10 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ZSAD methods often rely on a single adaptation pathway (e.g., a fixed prompt or alignment) which struggles with heterogeneous anomaly patterns across domains. Anomalies range from salient, localized disruptions to subtle, diffuse variations, requiring multiple specialized adaptation strategies.

Method: Estimate patch-level structural entropy from self-attention-induced patch relations to quantify relational uncertainty, and use it as a routing signal to form anomaly-aware routed tokens. A dynamic routing mechanism assigns tokens to specialized adaptation paths. Introduce a confidence-aware dual-branch prompt adaptation module to stabilize visual-text alignment while preserving CLIP's transferable priors.

Result: Extensive experiments on 10 industrial and medical benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance in challenging cross-dataset zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) settings.

Conclusion: EntroAD demonstrates that entropy-guided dynamic routing coupled with dual-branch prompt adaptation robustly handles diverse anomaly structures without target-domain fine-tuning. This approach enhances cross-domain generalization for ZSAD and suggests avenues for further exploration of relational uncertainty in multimodal anomaly detection.

Abstract: Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection (ZSAD) aims to detect anomalies in unseen domains without target-domain adaptation. Recent CLIP-based methods have shown promising performance by leveraging prompt learning and visual-text alignment. However, most existing approaches rely on a single adaptation pathway, which may be insufficient for heterogeneous anomaly patterns across domains. In practice, anomalies exhibit vastly different characteristics, ranging from salient, localized structural disruptions to subtle, diffuse, and irregular variations. To address this challenge, we propose EntroAD, a structural entropy-guided zero-shot anomaly detection framework. Unlike previous methods, EntroAD introduces a dynamic routing mechanism to process different types of anomalies with specialized adaptation strategies. Specifically, we estimate patch-level structural entropy from self-attention-induced patch relations and use it as a proxy for relational uncertainty to guide anomaly-aware token routing. Based on this routing signal, we construct anomaly-aware routed tokens to better capture anomaly cues with different structural characteristics. We further introduce a confidence-aware dual-branch prompt adaptation module to stabilize visual-text alignment while preserving CLIP's transferable prior. Extensive experiments on 10 industrial and medical benchmarks show that EntroAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in challenging cross-dataset ZSAD settings.

</details>


### [92] [OSP-Next: Efficient High-Quality Video Generation with Sparse Sequence Parallelism, HiF8 Quantization, and Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28691)
*Yunyang Ge,Xianyi He,Zezhong Zhang,Bin Lin,Bin Zhu,Xinhua Cheng,Li Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OSP-Next is an efficient text-to-video generator that blends sparse and full attention, enabling strong quality with reduced compute via Skiparse-2D, SSP, 8-bit quantization (HiF8), and Mix-GRPO; achieving up to ~1.7x speedups across GPUs while maintaining VBench ~83.7%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadratic cost of full self-attention in diffusion transformers for video generation limits efficiency; need scalable, hardware-friendly T2V approaches that maintain high quality.

Method: Hybrid full-sparse attention architecture using Skiparse-2D Attention for fixed-pattern token/group sparse attention along spatial dimensions; Sparse Sequence Parallelism (SSP) that partitions subsequences across ranks with a single All-to-All to switch sparse patterns; comparison to Ulysses Sequence Parallelism (SP); HiF8 quantization for stable joint training with 8-bit quantization and sparse fine-tuning; Mix-GRPO post-training to improve sparse model performance.

Result: OSP-Next achieves a VBench total score of 83.73%, surpassing Wan2.1. Under 5-second 720P and 5-second 768P settings, it reaches up to 1.64× single-GPU speedup and 1.52× eight-GPU speedup on NVIDIA H200 GPUs. OSP-Next-HiF8 attains 1.69× and 2.27× speedups on a single Ascend 950PR with only a 0.4% drop in VBench, demonstrating cross-hardware efficiency.

Conclusion: The approach delivers efficient, hardware-portable diffusion video generation, combining sparse attention, segmentation parallelism, and quantization to maintain quality while achieving substantial performance gains across GPUs and specialized accelerators.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers achieve strong video generation quality, but the quadratic cost of full attention limits efficiency. We introduce OSP-Next, an efficient text-to-video generation model that integrates sparse attention, parallelism, quantization, and reinforcement learning. OSP-Next uses a hybrid full-sparse attention architecture, where the sparse component is implemented with Skiparse-2D Attention. This fixed-pattern mechanism applies token-wise and group-wise sparse attention along spatial dimensions, leveraging locality while maintaining native compatibility with FlashAttention kernels. Based on the local equivalence of rearrangement in Skiparse-2D Attention, we further propose Sparse Sequence Parallelism (SSP), which partitions subsequences across ranks and switches sparse patterns through a single All-to-All communication. Compared with Ulysses Sequence Parallelism (SP), SSP provides a native parallel strategy for sparse attention and reduces communication volume by 75%. OSP-Next also incorporates HiF8 quantization to enable stable joint training with 8-bit quantization and sparse fine-tuning, and applies Mix-GRPO post-training to improve the performance of the sparse model. Experiments show that OSP-Next achieves a VBench total score of 83.73%, surpassing the Wan2.1 baseline. Under the 5-second 720P and 5-second 768P settings, OSP-Next achieves up to 1.64$\times$ single-GPU speedup and over 1.52$\times$ eight-GPU speedup on NVIDIA H200 GPUs. In addition, with only a 0.4% drop in VBench total score, OSP-Next-HiF8 achieves 1.69$\times$ and 2.27$\times$ speedups under the two settings on a single Ascend 950PR, demonstrating the efficiency and performance of OSP-Next across hardware platforms.

</details>


### [93] [SeeGroup: Multi-Layer Depth Estimation of Transparent Surfaces via Self-Determined Grouping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28735)
*Hongyu Wen,Jia Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SeeGroup, a permutation-invariant, point-process formulation for per-pixel multi-layer depth that abandons predefined layer groupings, enabling adaptive depth-layer assignment and achieving strong gains on LayeredDepth dataset (61.34% -> 70.09%)


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multilayer depth methods fix a front-to-back grouping of 3D points into layers, which is restrictive since multiple valid layerings can exist for transparent scenes. A flexible, data-driven grouping is needed.

Method: Model treats per-pixel multi-layer depth as a point process with depth layers modeled as unordered events along each camera ray. Learns a permutation-invariant likelihood over observed depth layers, yielding a loss that does not depend on any predefined layer grouping.

Result: Substantial improvement on LayeredDepth benchmark: quadruplet relative depth accuracy increases from 61.34% to 70.09%. Code released.

Conclusion: SeeGroup enables adaptive, order-free layer assignment for multi-layer depth and outperforms prior methods, addressing a key limitation by removing fixed layer groupings and using a principled permutation-invariant likelihood.

Abstract: Transparent objects are common in daily life, and it is important to understand their multilayer depth, including the transparent surface and the objects behind it. Existing methods for multilayer depth typically extend single-layer prediction. They define layers by the front-to-back ordering of 3D points and predict the layers sequentially. However, as layered geometry can admit multiple valid groupings of 3D points into layers, a predefined grouping strategy is inherently restrictive. In this work, we propose SeeGroup, a multi-layer depth estimation method that avoids imposing a predefined grouping and allows the model itself to adaptively assign surfaces to depth maps. We formulate per-pixel multi-layer depth as a point process, treating depth layers as unordered events along each camera ray. This induces a permutation-invariant likelihood over the observed depth layers, yielding a loss that naturally supports arbitrary layer groupings. Experiments demonstrate that our method significantly advances the state of the art of multi-layer depth estimation, improving quadruplet relative depth accuracy on LayeredDepth benchmark from 61.34% to 70.09%. Code is available at https://github.com/princeton-vl/SeeGroup.

</details>


### [94] [Self-Prophetic Decoding to Unlock Visual Search in LVLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28741)
*Zhendong He,Qiyuan Dai,Guanbin Li,Liang Lin,Sibei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SeProD: a training-free, plug-and-play self-prophetic decoding framework that enables coherent multi-step visual-search in LVLMs by self-regulating pre- and post-training models and using probability-based prophetic sampling to accept tokens, improving performance across 12 splits of 4 visual-search benchmarks and VQA without extra computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLM visual search suffers deterioration of intrinsic capabilities after post-training and interference from long multi-step reasoning; a method that preserves single-step capabilities and enables coherent multi-step reasoning is needed.

Method: Two key insights: (1) self-regulation between pre- and post-training LVLMs that leverages the pre-training model's intrinsic single-step capabilities to reduce deterioration and long-context interference; (2) probability-based prophetic sampling that replaces naive prompting, with the pre-trained model proposing tokens and the post-training model probabilistically accepting tokens within its output distribution, enabling coherent multi-step reasoning. Implemented as SeProD, a training-free, plug-and-play decoding framework with parallel prophetic acceptance to maintain efficiency.

Result: SeProD consistently improves multiple visual-search LVLMs across all 12 splits of 4 visual-search benchmarks and across general VQA benchmarks, without added computational overhead due to its parallel prophetic acceptance mechanism.

Conclusion: SeProD provides an effective, training-free approach to maintain and leverage single-step capabilities for robust multi-step visual reasoning in LVLMs, demonstrating broad improvements across benchmarks with no extra cost.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are rapidly evolving toward true multimodal reasoning, with visual search representing a concrete instantiation of the thinking-with-images paradigm. However, LVLM visual search faces two key challenges: incompatibility among intrinsic capabilities after post-training, and interference in long multi-step reasoning contexts. To address these, we identify two novel insights. First, self-regulation between pre- and post-training LVLMs leverages the intrinsic single-step capabilities of the pre-training model to mitigate capability deterioration and long-context interference. Second, probability-based prophetic sampling, replacing naive prompting, provides a probabilistic interface where the pre-training model acts as a prophet and the post-training model selectively accepts prophetic tokens under its output distribution, preserving coherent multi-step reasoning. Building on these insights, we introduce SeProD, a self-prophetic decoding framework that leverages intrinsic single-step capabilities to enable coherent multi-step reasoning in a training-free, plug-and-play manner. Experiments show that SeProD consistently improves multiple visual-search LVLMs across all 12 splits of 4 visual search benchmarks, as well as across general VQA benchmarks, without added computational overhead, thanks to its parallel prophetic acceptance mechanism.

</details>


### [95] [Bias Leaves a Gradient Trail: Label-Free Bias Identification via Gradient Probes on Concept Decompositions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28780)
*Thomas Vitry,Kieran Edgeworth,Stefan Wermter,Jae Hee Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Post-hoc, label-free bias discovery in frozen vision models using NMF on intermediate activations and a bias estimator from gradients; improves worst-group accuracy without retraining by identifying and suppressing spurious concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliance on spurious correlations in vision models and limitations of prior bias mitigation requiring curated datasets, labels, or retraining; need a post-hoc auditing/debiasing approach for deployed or unknown biases.

Method: For each target class, collect patches from inputs predicted as that class. Apply non-negative matrix factorization to intermediate activations to form a bank of concept vectors. Rank concepts using a bias estimator based on their interaction with backpropagated gradients on misclassified examples; bias concepts are activated for correcting false negatives and suppressed for false positives.

Result: On Colored MNIST and Waterbirds, recovers known spurious cues; on CelebA surfaces decision-relevant directions partially overlapping gender attribute; suppressing top-ranked concepts at inference time improves worst-group accuracy by up to 17.9 percentage points on Waterbirds and 10.4 on CelebA without retraining.

Conclusion: Identifies decision-relevant spurious directions that need not coincide with annotated attributes, offering interpretable auditing and actionable debiasing for frozen models.

Abstract: Vision classifiers can exploit spurious correlations, achieving high in-distribution accuracy yet failing under distribution shift. Existing approaches to bias mitigation and analysis often depend on curated datasets, spurious-attribute or group labels, or retraining, which may be infeasible once a model is deployed or the relevant bias is unknown. We present a bias-label-free, post-hoc method for identifying spurious concepts in frozen vision models, relying only on standard class labels from a held-out audit dataset. For each target class, we collect patches from inputs predicted as that class and apply non-negative matrix factorization to intermediate activations to obtain a bank of interpretable concept vectors. Candidate concepts are then ranked with a bias estimator derived from their interaction with backpropagated gradients on misclassified examples: bias concepts tend to get activated when correcting false negatives and suppressed when correcting false positives. On Colored MNIST and Waterbirds the method recovers concepts aligned with the known spurious cue, and on CelebA it surfaces decision-relevant directions that only partially coincide with the annotated gender attribute; suppressing the top-ranked concepts at inference time improves worst-group accuracy by up to 17.9 percentage points on Waterbirds and 10.4 on CelebA without any retraining or parameter updates. Our method identifies decision-relevant spurious directions that need not coincide with annotated ones, providing both an interpretable auditing tool and an actionable debiasing handle for frozen vision models. Code is available at https://github.com/vitryt/label-free-bias-identification.

</details>


### [96] [Ω-QVLA: Robust Quantization for Vision-Language-Action Models via Composite Rotation and Per-step Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28803)
*Xinyu Wang,Mingze Li,Sicheng Lyu,Dongxiu Liu,Kaicheng Yang,Ziyu Zhao,Yufei Cui,Xiao-Wen Chang,Peng Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Omega-QVLA is a training-free PTQ framework that uniformly quantizes both the language backbone and the entire diffusion action head (to 4-bit weights and activations, W4A4) for Vision-Language-Action models, eliminating mixed-precision requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable on-device deployment of VLA models by removing mixed-precision constraints and providing a training-free PTQ solution that covers both backbone and action head.

Method: Composite SVD-Hadamard rotation to equalize per-channel weight energy; per-step DiT activation scaling quantization to absorb dynamic-range drift across denoising steps; uniform W4A4 quantization for all components without requiring training.

Result: On LIBERO benchmarks, achieves 98.0% (Pi0.5) and 87.8% (GR00T N1.5) task success rates, matching or surpassing FP16 references (97.1% and 87.0%), while reducing static memory footprint by 71.3%; real-world manipulation is smooth and accurate where prior methods fail.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of uniform W4A4 PTQ for full VLA models without training, challenging the belief that diffusion action heads require mixed-precision and providing a practical compression pipeline.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models unify perception, reasoning, and control within a single policy, yet their multi-billion-parameter backbones and diffusion-based action heads make on-device deployment prohibitively expensive. Prior quantization efforts offer only partial solutions, compressing the LLM backbone while leaving the DiT action head at full precision, or resorting to mixed-precision schemes, driven by the belief that uniformly quantizing the action head is inherently unstable. We challenge this assumption with Omega-QVLA, the first training-free post-training quantization framework that compresses both the language backbone and the entire diffusion action head of a VLA model to a uniform W4A4 precision, eliminating the need for mixed-precision allocation. Omega-QVLA combines a composite SVD-Hadamard rotation that equalizes per-channel weight energy while diffusing residual activation outliers with per-step DiT activation scaling quantization that absorbs dynamic-range drift across denoising steps. On LIBERO, Omega-QVLA compresses Pi 0.5 and GR00T N1.5 to W4A4 with 98.0% and 87.8% task success rates, matching or exceeding their FP16 references of 97.1% and 87.0%, while reducing the static memory footprint by 71.3%. Real-world manipulation experiments further confirm smooth, accurate manipulation where prior methods fail. Code is available at https://github.com/UCMP13753/Omega-QVLA.

</details>


### [97] [Personal Visual Memory from Explicit and Implicit Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28806)
*Viet Nguyen,Thao Nguyen,Vishal M. Patel,Yuheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a personal visual memory benchmark and VisualMem, a hybrid visual–text memory system that stores and reasons over user-specific visual data without reducing images to captions; achieves strong performance on the benchmark and competitive results on text-based memory tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term, personalized AI memory should exploit visual cues that reveal user identity, ownership, and durable facts not captured well by text alone; existing text-centric benchmarks fail to capture the privacy-relevant signals in images.

Method: Create a benchmark for personal visual memory and develop VisualMem, a hybrid architecture that augments a text-memory backend with a structured visual memory module; it preserves raw visual information and uses conversational context to resolve identity/ownership/facts rather than generating captions.

Result: VisualMem substantially outperforms prior memory systems on the personal visual memory benchmark and remains competitive on standard text-memory benchmarks, suggesting visual memory is a distinct, valuable component for personalized AI.

Conclusion: Incorporating personal visual memory is important for long-term personalization; the VisualMem approach demonstrates the benefits of keeping structured visual memory alongside text memory and using dialogue context to extract durable user facts.

Abstract: Long-term memory is increasingly important for personalized AI agents, yet existing benchmarks and methods remain largely text-centric. Even when images are included, the user-specific information needed for later questions is typically recoverable from text alone, and most memory systems reduce image turns to generic captions. Yet images often carry personal information that text rarely states -- both explicit evidence, such as recurring user-associated entities, and implicit evidence, such as latent user facts inferred from visual or multimodal cues. We introduce a benchmark for personal visual memory that targets both forms of evidence, and propose VisualMem, a hybrid visual--text architecture that augments a text-memory backend with a structured personal visual memory module. Rather than collapsing images into captions, VisualMem uses conversational context to resolve identity, ownership, and durable user facts. Experiments show that VisualMem substantially outperforms prior memory systems on our benchmark while remaining competitive on standard text-memory benchmarks, indicating that personal visual memory is a distinct and important component of long-term memory for personalized AI agents.

</details>


### [98] [AREA: Attribute Extraction and Aggregation for CLIP-Based Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28809)
*Zhen-Hao Xie,Yu-Cheng Shi,Da-Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AREA decomposes CLIP-based class-incremental learning into two stabilized stages—attribute extraction on a hyperspherical space anchored by principal geodesic analysis, and attribute aggregation via lightweight task-specific experts with a variational bottleneck—using optimal transport routing for inference, yielding state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In CLIP-based CIL, incremental updates bias both attribute extraction and aggregation toward new classes, causing catastrophic forgetting. Decoupling these stages and stabilizing them on a geometry-aware embedding space aims to maintain robust representations across tasks.

Method: 1) Stabilize extraction by anchoring class-level visual/textual attributes on a hypersphere using principal geodesic analysis; 2) Stabilize aggregation with lightweight task-specific experts, scoring, and residual refinement, regularized by a variational information bottleneck; 3) Inference via routing over task attribute manifolds with optimal transport.

Result: Empirical results show AREA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on CLIP-based CIL benchmarks.

Conclusion: AREA provides a cohesive framework that stabilizes both attribute extraction and aggregation in CLIP-based CIL, leveraging geometry-aware anchors, modular task-specific experts, and OT routing to achieve robust continual learning.

Abstract: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) is important in building real-world learning systems. In CLIP-based CIL, the model performs classification by comparing similarity between visual and textual embeddings obtained from template prompts, e.g., ``a photo of a [CLASS]''. This seemingly monolithic matching process can be decomposed into two conceptually distinct stages: attribute extraction and attribute aggregation. For example, a model may recognize cat using attributes such as fur texture and whiskers. When learning a new class like car, the model must extract additional attributes like wheels and adjust how they are aggregated in the shared representation space. However, since only data from the current task is available, incremental updates can bias both attribute extraction and aggregation toward new classes, leading to catastrophic forgetting. Therefore, we propose AREA for attribute extraction and aggregation in CLIP-based CIL. To stabilize extraction, we anchor class-level visual and textual attributes on the hyperspherical embedding space via principal geodesic analysis. To stabilize aggregation, we learn lightweight task-specific experts with scoring and residual refinement, regularized by a variational information bottleneck objective. During inference, we perform routing over task attribute manifolds via optimal transport for more concise prediction. Experiments show that AREA consistently outperforms SOTA methods. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/ICML2026-AREA.

</details>


### [99] [HarmoVid: Relightful Video Portrait Harmonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28811)
*Jun Myeong Choi,Jae Shin Yoon,Luchao Qi,Roni Sengupta,Joon-Young Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based video relighting framework that uses a lighting deflickering stage and asymmetric alpha mask conditioning to achieve temporally coherent, natural, and physically meaningful video harmonization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video harmonization lacks scalable paired video data (same motion under different lighting). Frame-by-frame image harmonization introduces temporal flicker; thus a temporally stable, data-efficient approach is needed.

Method: 1) Lighting deflickering model to stabilize global/local lighting flicker in video. 2) Train a video diffusion model on deflickered real and synthetic videos for harmonization (relighting) of foreground to target background. 3) Asymmetric alpha mask conditioning to learn clean boundaries from real videos.

Result: Experiments show strong temporal coherence, naturalness, cleaner boundaries, and physically meaningful lighting behavior, while preserving high relighting expressiveness compared to prior image-based and video-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed deflickered, diffusion-based video harmonization pipeline yields robust, scalable relighting with improved temporal stability and boundary handling, outperforming prior approaches.

Abstract: We present a method for harmonizing the lighting of a foreground video to match a target background scene, adjusting shadows, color tone, and illumination intensity (relightful harmonization). Unlike images, acquiring labeled data for videos, where identical motions are recorded under different lighting conditions, is practically infeasible and non-scalable. While one way to create such paired data is to apply existing image-based harmonization models frame by frame to a video, the resulting outputs often suffer from significant temporal jitters. We overcome this problem by introducing a novel lighting deflickering model that can stabilize the global and local lighting flickering artifacts. Our video diffusion model learns from these upgraded deflickered data with a volume of real and synthetic videos to generate high-quality video harmonization results. We further propose an asymmetric alpha mask conditioning technique to learn the clean boundaries from real videos. Experiments demonstrate that our model achieves strong temporal coherence, naturalness, cleaner boundaries, and physically meaningful lighting behavior, while maintaining strong relighting expressiveness compared to prior image-based and video-based harmonization methods.

</details>


### [100] [Gamma-World: Generative Multi-Agent World Modeling Beyond Two Players](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28816)
*Fangfu Liu,Kai He,Tianchang Shen,Tianshi Cao,Sanja Fidler,Yueqi Duan,Jun Gao,Igor Gilitschenski,Zian Wang,Xuanchi Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A scalable multi-agent world model with simplex rotary encoding and hub-based attention enabling permutation-equivalent, independently controllable agents; real-time diffusion rollout at 24 FPS; generalizes from 2 to 4 players without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for principled multi-agent world models that preserve agent independence, permutation symmetry, and efficient inference while ensuring temporal and cross-perspective consistency in interactive environments.

Method: Introduce Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding (agents as vertices of a regular simplex in rotary angle space) to give distinct phases while ensuring permutation equivalence; adopt Sparse Hub Attention with learnable hub tokens to mediate cross-agent interactions and reduce attention complexity from quadratic to linear in the number of agents; perform real-time rollout by distilling a full-context diffusion teacher into a causal student that generates temporal blocks with KV caching.

Result: Improved video fidelity, action controllability, and inter-agent consistency over slot-based and dense-attention baselines; generalizes from two to four players without additional training; achieves 24 FPS real-time generation.

Conclusion: A principled, scalable multi-agent world model pipeline that preserves agent permutation symmetry and independent controllability while enabling efficient inference and real-time interactive video generation, demonstrated to outperform baselines and generalize to more agents without retraining.

Abstract: World models for interactive video generation have largely focused on single-agent settings, where future observations are generated from a single control signal. However, many generated environments require multi-agent interaction: multiple players, robots, or embodied agents act simultaneously within a shared space. Scaling world models to such settings requires a principled multi-agent design: agents should remain independently controllable, permutation-symmetric, and support efficient inference while maintaining consistency across time and perspectives. In this paper, we present our generative multi-agent world model for interactive simulation. It introduces Simplex Rotary Agent Encoding, a parameter-free extension of 3D RoPE that represents agents as vertices of a regular simplex in rotary angle space. This gives each agent a distinct phase while making all agents permutation-equivalent, enabling scalable agent identity without learned per-slot identities or a fixed agent ordering. To avoid dense all-to-all attention across agents, we further propose Sparse Hub Attention, where learnable hub tokens mediate token interaction across agents, reducing cross-agent attention cost from quadratic to linear in the number of agents. For real-time rollout, we distill a full-context diffusion teacher into a causal student that generates temporal blocks sequentially with KV caching, enabling action-responsive generation at 24 FPS. Experiments in multiplayer virtual environments show that our model improves video fidelity, action controllability, and inter-agent consistency over slot-based and dense-attention baselines, while generalizing from two to four players without additional training.

</details>


### [101] [From Pixels to Words -- Towards Native One-Vision Models at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28820)
*Haiwen Diao,Jiahao Wang,Penghao Wu,Yuhao Dong,Yuwei Niu,Yue Zhu,Zhongang Cai,Weichen Fan,Linjun Dai,Silei Wu,Xuanyu Zheng,Mingxuan Li,Yuanhan Zhang,Bo Li,Hanming Deng,Huchuan Lu,Quan Wang,Lei Yang,Lewei Lu,Dahua Lin,Ziwei Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NEO-ov is a native vision-language foundation model that learns cross-frame pixel-word alignment end-to-end without external encoders or adapters, enabling unified spatiotemporal modeling and competitive performance with modular VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs rely on modular pipelines that fragment pixel-level signals across frames and struggle with multi-image/video understanding and spatial reasoning. A native, end-to-end approach could preserve pixel-wide signals and enable seamless cross-frame interactions.

Method: Train and evaluate a native foundation model (NEO-ov) that eliminates module boundaries, learning cross-frame and pixel-word correspondence end-to-end without external encoders, adapters, or post-hoc fusion, to achieve unified spatiotemporal modeling. The work also provides architectural analyses and detailed training recipes, with code and models released.

Result: NEO-ov substantially narrows the gap to modular baselines while excelling at fine-grained visual perception, demonstrating that native "one-vision" architectures are feasible and competitive at scale.

Conclusion: Native one-vision multimodal models are viable and competitive; the work offers systematic architectural insights and training recipes to foster future native multimodal modeling, with publicly available code and models.

Abstract: Current vision-language models (VLMs) typically stitch together separate image encoders and language decoders via multi-stage alignment, a modular framework that inevitably fragments pixel-level signals across frames and scatters early pixel-word interactions. In parallel, native VLMs, despite impressive performance on single images, remain largely unexplored in multi-image, video understanding, and spatial intelligence. Hence, we introduce NEO-ov, a native foundation model that learns cross-frame and pixel-word correspondence end-to-end, without any external encoders, auxiliary adapters, or post-hoc fusion. By eliminating module boundaries entirely, NEO-ov enables fine-grained and unified spatiotemporal modeling to emerge natively inside the model. Notably, NEO-ov largely narrows the gap to modular counterparts while excelling at fine-grained visual perception, validating that native "one-vision" architectures are not only feasible but competitive at scale. Beyond empirical performance, we unveil systematic architectural analyses and detailed training recipes to facilitate subsequent native multimodal modeling. Our code and models are publicly available at: https://github.com/EvolvingLMMs-Lab/NEO.

</details>


### [102] [Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Guided Training Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27495)
*Nithesh Chandher Karthikeyan,Jonas Unger,Gabriel Eilertsen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Representation-conditioned diffusion models (latent diffusion conditioned on learned representations from DINOv2, DINOv3, and CLIP) outperform class-conditioned generation and can surpass models trained on real data when synthetic data size is scaled; also effective for augmentation and sample filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data availability and annotation bottlenecks hinder large-scale supervised learning; synthetic data generation with representation-conditioned diffusion could expand, complement, or replace real datasets while improving sample quality and coverage.

Method: Train latent diffusion models conditioned on representations from DINOv2, DINOv3, and CLIP; compare to class-conditioned generation; scale synthetic dataset size; evaluate on ImageNet100; apply augmentation and sample filtering via the conditioning space.

Result: Representation-conditioned generation outperforms class-conditioned generation by 10.76 percentage points in top-1 accuracy on ImageNet100; scaling synthetic data enables outperforming a classifier trained on real data by 2.0 percentage points top-1; augmentation methods outperform classical augmentation; conditioning space enables sample filtering to further boost training value.

Conclusion: Representation-conditioned diffusion models are a promising approach to augment, complement, or potentially replace real-world datasets in large-scale visual learning tasks.

Abstract: Data availability remains a critical bottleneck in many deep learning applications. Large-scale datasets are often expensive to collect, curate and annotate, which can limit the scalability and applicability of supervised learning methods. In this work, we evaluate the classification performance of models trained on synthetic image datasets produced by generative deep learning. In particular, we use latent diffusion models conditioned on learned representations from DINOv2, DINOv3, and CLIP. Our results demonstrates that this representation-conditioned formulation significantly outperforms class-conditioned generation by a large margin (+10.76 p.p. top-1 accuracy on ImageNet100), by improving sample quality and mode coverage. Furthermore, by scaling the size of the synthetic dataset, we are able to outperform a classifier trained on the real data (+2.0 p.p top-1 accuracy).
  We also demonstrate how generated images can be used for augmentation purposes, outperforming classical augmentation methods, and how the conditioning space can be used for sample filtering to further improve training value. Collectively, these findings highlight that representation-conditioned diffusion models provide a promising approach for augmenting, complementing, or potentially replacing real-world datasets in large-scale visual learning tasks.

</details>


### [103] [Structure over Pixels: Learning Variable-Length Visual Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27696)
*Piotr Wyrwiński,Kacper Dobek,Krzysztof Krawiec*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STROP introduces a discrete visual tokenizer with a learnable per-image length, yielding structural scene representations and adapting the token sequence length to scene complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
