Motivation: Existing adaptive tokenizers rely on post-hoc search or pre-defined rate sets and optimize for pixel reconstruction, which emphasizes texture over structure. A continuous per-image sequence length aligned to scene structure is needed.

Method: STROP employs a four-phase curriculum supervised by local rate–distortion probes against frozen DINOv3 features; it trains a dedicated length head to predict the active prefix length in a single forward pass; it avoids pixel-level reconstruction gradients, shaping the codebook via higher-level latent representations.

Result: The required token sequence length grows with scene complexity, and signs of compositional structure appear in downstream dense-prediction transfer and in inspection of the learned code vocabulary.

Conclusion: STROP provides a continuous, structure-focused discrete tokenizer that adapts per-image length to scene complexity without pixel-reconstruction training, yielding emergent compositional structure in representations.

Abstract: Discrete visual tokenizers translate images into ordered sequences of codes, providing a natural representation for structural description of scenes. Yet existing adaptive tokenizers either require post-hoc search or select among a discrete set of pre-trained rates, rather than learning a continuous per-image sequence length coupled to the model and scene, and they typically train against pixel reconstruction, emphasizing texture rather than structure. We propose STROP, a discrete visual tokenizer architecture that forms structural scene representations and simultaneously learns how long an image's visual program should be. Using a four-phase curriculum supervised by local rate--distortion probes against frozen DINOv3 features, STROP optimizes a dedicated length head that estimates the active prefix length in a single forward pass. By bypassing pixel-level reconstruction gradients, the codebook is shaped entirely by the quality of higher-level latent representations. Program length grows with scene complexity, and signs of compositional structure emerge both in downstream dense-prediction transfer and in direct inspection of the learned code vocabulary.

</details>


### [104] [Mahalanobis PatchCore: Covariance-Aware and Streaming-Compatible Industrial Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27748)
*Niccolò Ferrari,Oligert Osmani,Evelina Lamma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mahalanobis PatchCore: a streaming, covariance-aware extension of PatchCore for industrial anomaly detection; reduces memory usage and uses Mahalanobis distance via whitened embeddings, with online covariance estimation and streaming memory bank; preserves most offline PatchCore performance with a slight AUROC gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PatchCore relies on Euclidean distance and offline, full memory banks. In industrial settings with memory constraints and potential feature correlations, a covariance-aware, streaming approach can improve retrieval and enable bounded, re-iterable training.

Method: Introduce a regularised covariance model in reduced feature space and whiten embeddings so Euclidean nearest-neighbour search equates to Mahalanobis retrieval. Build a bounded-memory, re-iterable training pipeline with incremental dimensionality reduction (online PCA), online covariance estimation, and streaming aggregation to assemble the memory bank without storing all normal patches simultaneously.

Result: Evaluated on a public 15-category industrial anomaly-detection benchmark and three industrial datasets (blow-fill-seal, amber-glass-ampoule, lyophilised-cake vials). Memory peak reduced from 5.41 GB to 2.78 GB; image-level AUROC improved from 0.981 to 0.986, while preserving most offline PatchCore performance.

Conclusion: Covariance-aware, streaming PatchCore achieves comparable anomaly detection performance with substantially lower memory usage, making it well-suited for automated industrial inspection under practical memory constraints.

Abstract: Industrial visual anomaly detection is usually one-class: normal images are abundant, while defects are rare, heterogeneous, and often unavailable during system design. PatchCore-style retrieval suits this setting because it scores test images from a memory bank of normal patch features, but the standard Euclidean geometry ignores feature correlations and its offline construction materialises the full patch pool before subsampling.
  We introduce Mahalanobis PatchCore, a covariance-aware, streaming-compatible extension of PatchCore. Its artificial intelligence contribution is a retrieval detector that estimates a regularised covariance model in reduced feature space and whitens embeddings, so Euclidean nearest-neighbour search after transformation implements Mahalanobis retrieval. A bounded-memory, re-iterable training pipeline builds the memory bank without storing all normal patches at once, using incremental dimensionality reduction, online covariance estimation, and streaming aggregation.
  The engineering application is automated industrial inspection, where visual anomaly detection must remain accurate under practical memory limits. We evaluate the method on a public 15-category industrial anomaly-detection benchmark and three industrial datasets covering blow-fill-seal strip-ampoule meniscus inspection, amber-glass-ampoule bottom inspection, and lyophilised-cake vial inspection. Mahalanobis PatchCore preserves most offline PatchCore image-level performance on the public benchmark while reducing peak memory from 5.41 to 2.78 GB, and improves the selected industrial mean image area under the receiver operating characteristic curve from 0.981 to 0.986.

</details>


### [105] [Residualized Temporal Sparse Autoencoders for Interpreting Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27813)
*Calvin Yeung,Prathyush Poduval,Ali Zakeri,Zhuowen Zou,Mohsen Imani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces residualized temporal sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to analyze diffusion activation trajectories by modeling each trajectory as an initial activation plus residuals not captured by linear dynamics across denoising time, enabling interpretable, time-varying feature directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond single-timestep or time-conditioned analyses and capture temporally structured, nonlinear dynamics in diffusion activations, yielding interpretable, time-resolved feature representations.

Method: Collect activations across denoising time; fit linear predictors between consecutive timesteps; form residualized trajectory representations comprising the initial activation plus residuals unexplained by these linear dynamics; train a sparse autoencoder on this residualized representation; map decoder directions back into activation space to obtain time-resolved feature trajectories.

Result: Reconstruction and ablation studies, spatiotemporal feature analysis, and qualitative steering experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.5 demonstrate that residualized temporal SAEs provide a practical framework for studying temporally structured diffusion activations.

Conclusion: Residualized temporal SAEs offer a useful and interpretable framework for analyzing and steering temporally structured diffusion activations, capturing beyond-linear dynamics and enabling trajectory-level interpretability.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models generate images through an iterative denoising process, so internal neural layers produce trajectories of activations rather than single static representations. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have recently been used to decompose diffusion activations into interpretable feature directions, but most approaches analyze activations at individual timesteps or condition on time rather than learning directly from full activation trajectories. In this work, we introduce residualized temporal SAEs for diffusion activation trajectories. We collect activations across denoising time, fit linear predictors between neighboring timesteps, and represent each trajectory using an initial activation together with residual components not explained by these linear dynamics. Training an SAE on this residualized representation encourages sparse latents to capture structure beyond what is linearly predictable. The residualized decoder directions can be mapped back into activation space, allowing each latent to be analyzed as a feature trajectory over denoising time. Through reconstruction and ablation studies, spatiotemporal feature analysis, and qualitative steering experiments on Stable Diffusion~1.5, we show that residualized temporal SAEs provide a useful framework for studying temporally structured diffusion activations.

</details>


### [106] [Not All NVFP4 QAT Recipes Are Equal: How Architecture and Scale Shape Model Quality for Anomaly Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27616)
*Zijian Du,Oleg Rybakov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Swin Transformer shows robust FP4 quantization performance across architectures and scales for real-time brain tumor anomaly segmentation; architecture choice dominates quantization robustness, with attention models outperforming CNNs especially under gradient quantization; advanced QAT recipes mitigate FP4-induced issues; five-fold CV confirms robustness; Swin Transformer recommended for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how architecture, model scale, and FP4 QAT recipe interact to affect recall-critical real-time brain tumor anomaly segmentation, to guide efficient low-precision deployment.

Method: Unified evaluation across multiple architectures (including CNNs and Swin Transformer), multiple model scales, and FP4 QAT recipes on a brain tumor segmentation task, with five-fold patient-level cross-validation.

Result: Architecture is the largest determinant of quantization robustness. Attention-based architectures (e.g., Swin Transformer) show strong resilience to QAT recipe changes; CNNs degrade under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales. At low capacity, FP4 can discretize softmax attention, but advanced QAT recipes prevent this collapse. At larger scales, advanced recipes mitigate gradient quantization noise that harms CNNs. Five-fold CV confirms robustness to data partitioning. Swin Transformer remains robust to QAT recipe choice across scales, making it the recommended architecture for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.

Conclusion: For FP4-quantized real-time brain tumor anomaly segmentation, adopt Swin Transformer due to its architecture-level robustness to QAT across scales; architecture choice supersedes QAT recipe considerations; CNNs are more sensitive to quantization, especially at higher scales.

Abstract: Real-time anomaly segmentation demands both high recall and efficient low-precision inference. We study the three-way interaction of model architecture, model scale, and FP4 quantization-aware training (QAT) recipe on a recall-critical brain tumor segmentation task, evaluating multiple architectures, scales, and QAT recipes under a unified protocol. We find that architecture choice has the largest impact on quantization robustness, with attention-based architectures showing remarkable resilience to recipe choice while CNN degrades under gradient-quantizing recipes at larger scales. At low capacity, FP4 can discretize softmax attention, but advanced QAT recipes prevent this collapse. At larger scales, advanced recipes mitigate gradient quantization noise that degrades CNN quality. Five-fold patient-level cross-validation confirms these findings are robust to data partition. Our results show that the Swin Transformer is robust to QAT recipe choice across all scales, making it the recommended architecture for FP4-quantized anomaly segmentation.

</details>


### [107] [Can Segmentation Models Understand the World? Towards Proactive Affordance Reasoning via Visual Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27764)
*Yuchen Guo,Junli Gong,Hongmin Cai,Yiu-ming Cheung,Weifeng Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegWorld grounds high-level intents into segmentation masks via proactive, multi-level visual chain-of-thought (CoT), bridging intent-to-action reasoning with mask prediction and introducing an intent-to-part benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world embodied interaction often uses intent-level language that does not name the region; a system must reason from scene to affordance-bearing parts before acting.

Method: Proactively observe the scene before instructions to describe objects and plausible events; upon instruction, reason from the relevant object to the action to the precise interaction site and object part. Formalize as probabilistic inference with scene context; introduce an intent-to-part benchmark for affordance-based part segmentation.

Result: SegWorld matches instruction-driven baselines on target-referential instructions and substantially improves performance on intent-level instructions, demonstrating the value of proactive visual CoT for grounding intents to parts.

Conclusion: Proactive, multi-level CoT enables intent-based grounding in segmentation; the probabilistic framework and new benchmark support future progress in affordance-aware scene understanding for embodied agents.

Abstract: Recent segmentation models couple large language models (LLMs) with mask decoders to ground complex language expressions into masks, yet their instructions remain target-referential: they describe, constrain, or imply the region to be segmented. However, in real-world embodied interaction, human instructions are often at the intent-level, which includes the desired outcome without naming the region that enables it. To bridge this gap, we introduce SegWorld, where the model reasons about the scene through a multi-level visual chain-of-thought (CoT) before committing to a mask. Before receiving any instructions, it proactively observes the scene, describing visible objects and inferring plausible events they may support. Given an instruction, it continues the chain: from the object relevant to the intent, through the action that satisfies it, to the physical interaction site, the object part that affords the action. We formalize SegWorld as probabilistic inference, in which proactive observation supplies a linguistic scene context that improves mask prediction when instructions are given at the level of intent. We construct an intent-to-part benchmark for evaluating affordance-bearing part segmentation from high-level goals. Experiments show SegWorld matches instruction-driven baselines on target-referential instructions and improves substantially on intent-level ones.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [108] [A Factory-Floor Deployment Case Study of VLA Pipelines for Industrial Packaging Task: Workflow, Failures, and Lessons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27461)
*Brian Zhu,Philipp Schmitt,Philine Meister,Lukas Gensler,Momen Khalil,Emmanuele Poggi,Johannes Hechtl,Carsten Braunroth,Kai Wurm,Gokul Narayanan,Eugen Solowjow,Georg von Wichert,Andre Scholz,Felix Albrecht,Maxmillian Metzner*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Empirical study of deploying a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy (Pi0.5) on an industrial packaging task; 2535 on-site episodes; iterative fine-tuning and data-driven recovery; reveals recurring failure modes and actionable deployment workflow recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess real-world effort required to adapt a pretrained VLA policy to a single factory task and identify deployment bottlenecks for reliable operation in cluttered, transparent-bag manipulation.

Method: Deployment loop: collect data on site, curate, fine-tune the policy, evaluate, and collect targeted 'recovery' data; measure performance; accumulate 10 hours across 2535 episodes; analyze failure modes.

Result: Empirical account of factory-floor deployment, with identified recurring failure modes; practical guidance for improving deployment workflow and data collection strategies.

Conclusion: The study provides concrete recommendations to close the gap between research policies and deployable industrial systems, emphasizing iterative, deployment-driven refinement and data-centric troubleshooting.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have shown promising manipulation capabilities, yet their practical impact is often limited by the reliability demands of real-world deployment. We present a deployment study of an industrial packaging task at Siemens Factory (GWE, Erlangen, Germany), where a robot must pick a transparent accessory bag from a cluttered pile, insert it into the remaining cavity of a cardboard package, and ensure that the bag and its contents remain below the closing plane. Our goal is to understand the practical effort required to adapt a pretrained Pi0.5 policy to a single factory-floor task through iterative fine-tuning and deployment-driven refinement. The pipeline consists of repeated loops of data collection, curation, fine-tuning, evaluation, and targeted recovery data collection. We have accumulated 2535 episodes (10 hours) from the on-site factory settings. In this paper, we contribute an empirical account of a factory-floor VLA deployment, highlighting recurring failure modes and lessons that inform how to improve the deployment workflow.

</details>


### [109] [GE-Sim 2.0: A Roadmap Towards Comprehensive Closed-loop Video World Simulators for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27491)
*Boxiang Qiu,Liliang Chen,Yue Liao,Nan Wang,Lintao Wang,Jiayi Luo,Wenzhi Zhao,Shengcong Chen,Di Chen,Ye Li,Chen Gao,Shuicheng Yan,Si Liu,Maoqing Yao,Guanghui Ren*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GE-Sim 2.0 is a closed-loop video world simulator for robotic manipulation that retrains Genie Envisioner on real-world data, adds a state extractor, a world evaluator, and an acceleration engine; it achieves fast long-horizon rollouts, ranks highly on WorldArena with 2B params, and enables scalable evaluation and closed-loop policy learning with real-world gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for accurate, controllable, and efficiently evaluable simulation to train and assess manipulation policies without heavy manual inspection, enabling scalable closed-loop learning and real-world transfer.

Method: Retains and extends Genie Envisioner by (1) pretraining on thousands of hours of real robot data spanning teleoperation and on-robot deployment to improve action-following fidelity; (2) introducing a state expert that decodes proprioceptive state from video latents to support next-chunk prediction; (3) introducing a world judge that scores generated rollouts against task instructions to provide machine-verifiable rewards; (4) implementing an acceleration framework that delivers 25-frame rollouts in 2.3 seconds on a single H100, with up to 4x frame skipping for long-horizon evaluation.

Result: The model tops the public WorldArena leaderboard at 2B parameters, outperforming dedicated robotic world models and closed-source video generators. Policies trained on its rollouts and rewards show measurable real-world improvements.

Conclusion: GE-Sim 2.0 provides a practical, scalable platform for evaluating and closed-loop learning of manipulation policies, combining fast, accurate video-world simulation with verifiable rewards and real-world transfer capability.

Abstract: We introduce GE-Sim 2.0 (Genie Envisioner World Simulator 2.0), a closed-loop video world simulator for robotic manipulation. Building on the action-conditioned video generation framework of Genie Envisioner, GE-Sim 2.0 is re-trained on thousands of hours of real-world robot data spanning teleoperation, contact-rich interaction, and on-robot policy deployment, substantially improving action-following fidelity and trajectory coverage. On top of this foundation, three new modules close the loop from video simulation to policy learning: a state expert that decodes proprioceptive state from video latents to support next-chunk prediction by downstream VLA policies; a world judge that scores generated rollouts against task instructions, yielding machine-verifiable success signals and rewards in place of manual inspection; and an acceleration framework that delivers a 25-frame rollout in 2.3 seconds on a single H100, with up to 4* frame skipping at inference for long-horizon evaluation. GE-Sim 2.0 tops the public WorldArena leaderboard at only 2B parameters, outperforming both dedicated robotic world models and closed-source general video generators, and policies trained against its rollouts and rewards translate into measurable real-world gains, establishing GE-Sim 2.0 as a practical platform for scalable evaluation and closed-loop learning of manipulation policies.

</details>


### [110] [SCALE-COMM: Shared, Contrastively-Aligned Latent Embeddings for MARL Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27532)
*Mahmoud Abouelyazid,Eman Hammad*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCALE-COMM proposes a self-supervised, decoupled communication framework that learns compact latent messages via contrastive alignment, yielding stable, task-relevant communication and improved MARL performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many MARL communication systems suffer from unstable protocols, ungrounded message semantics, and coupling between learning the communication and the policy; robust, grounded, and scalable communication representations are needed.

Method: Train low-dimensional latent messages that encode planning and traffic information; decouple communication learning from policy optimization; enforce cross-agent and temporal consistency via contrastive objectives (shared, contrastively-aligned embeddings). Evaluate on standard MARL benchmarks and a realistic warehouse task.

Result: SCALE-COMM consistently outperforms existing communication frameworks in representation quality and task performance; yields better stability, sample efficiency, and throughput during policy fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Representation-driven communication is effective for scalable multi-agent coordination; decoupling and contrastive alignment enable stable, informative messages that improve coordination and learning efficiency.

Abstract: Emergent communication enables partially observant Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) to coordinate effectively in decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) settings. However, existing approaches often struggle with unstable communication protocols, ungrounded message semantics, and interference between communication learning and policy optimization, leading to degraded coordination over time. We propose SCALE-COMM (Shared, Contrastively-Aligned Latent Embeddings for COMMunication), a self-supervised framework for learning compact, stable, and policy-relevant communication representations. SCALE-COMM decouples communication learning from policy optimization by training low-dimensional latent messages that capture task-relevant planning and traffic information, while enforcing consistency across agents and time. Across standard MARL benchmarks and a realistic warehouse coordination task, SCALE-COMM consistently outperforms existing communication frameworks in both representation quality and task performance. The learned communication space yields improved stability, sample efficiency, and throughput under policy fine-tuning, demonstrating the effectiveness of representation-driven communication for scalable multi-agent coordination.

</details>


### [111] [Inducing Calmness With Pocket-Sized Robotics: Reducing Movement and Heart Rate in Children through Hand-Held Tactile Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27533)
*Morten Roed Frederiksen,Kasper Støy,Maja Matarić*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A tactile, hand-held vibration-matching game reduces arousal and restlessness in children, promoting calm and focused states; observed reductions in heart rate and movement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore embodied self-regulation via tactile interaction, extending prior heart-rate work to include full-body movement and posture.

Method: Within-subjects design (n=18 typically developing children); two conditions (with vs without tactile interaction) during measurement; heart rate and body movement recorded; intervention is a pocket-sized hand-held rhythmic vibration-matching game aimed at attention and stillness.

Result: Heart rate decreased by 3.56 bpm (p<0.01); overall movement decreased by 38% (p<0.05); movement in attention-related regions reduced by 45%; tactile interaction down-regulated physiological activation.

Conclusion: Brief tactile game engagement can down-regulate arousal and foster calm, improved attention and behavior regulation in children.

Abstract: Periods of heightened arousal or restlessness can interfere with children's ability to focus, self-regulation, and physically calm. Technologies that encourage embodied self-regulation through tactile interaction may provide a simple and accessible means of promoting calmness. This paper investigates how interaction with a pocket-sized tactile device influences physiological and behavioral markers of calmness in typically developing children. Building on prior work examining heart rate modulation, we present new findings on how tactile interaction affects full-body movement and postural stability. We employ a device that engages children through a hand-held rhythmic vibration-matching game, designed to focus attention and encourage stillness. Eighteen children participated in a within-subjects study that involved two conditions: with and without tactile interaction with a hand-held device, while having their heart rate and body movement recorded. Results show that the tactile game interaction reduced physiological arousal (heart rate decreased by 3.56 bpm, p < 0.01) and physical restlessness (overall movement decreased by 38%, p < 0.05), with attention-related body regions showing the greatest change toward stillness (45% reduction in movement). These findings demonstrate that brief tactile game-like engagement with a hand-held device can down-regulate physiological activation, promoting the calm and focused states toward sustained attention and behavior regulation.

</details>


### [112] [Synthetic Emotions vs. Gamification: Exploring Engagement Strategies for Small Social Robots in Different Age Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27539)
*Morten Roed Frederiksen,Kasper Støy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Age-dependent engagement strategies for a tactile robot show emotional feedback favored by children, while points-based rewards boost performance in adults over a day.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether emotion-based engagement or reward-based engagement improves engagement and performance for a tactile robot assisting children with anxiety, and whether effects differ by age/context.

Method: Two studies: (1) 16 children aged 6–8 in a preference assessment; (2) 14 university students aged 20–27 in naturalistic day-long interactions; comparison of emotional feedback vs points; outcomes include stated preferences and task accuracy/performance over time; statistical significance p<0.05.

Result: Children preferred emotional engagement; adults showed higher task accuracy and sustained performance with points-based systems; results diverge across groups, highlighting context-dependent effectiveness.

Conclusion: Design assumptions about engagement modalities should be validated across age groups and real-world contexts; the work contributes to understanding age-related differences in HRI engagement strategies.

Abstract: Many children experience challenges in emotional regulation and social interaction, which can limit their participation in everyday activities and therapeutic programs. For socially assistive robots to be effective in this context, it is essential that children remain consistently and meaningfully engaged. We explore engagement strategies for a tactile robot designed to support children suffering from anxiety disorders through daily interactions. The robot delivers either synthetic emotional feedback or point rewards to encourage user participation. We evaluated these strategies through two studies: a preference assessment with 16 school children aged 6-8 years, and a behavioral study with 14 university students aged 20-27 years in naturalistic environments. The study with school children indicated a preference for emotional engagement over points-based approaches. The follow up study with university students across a full day of interactions revealed contrasting results: points-based systems produced significantly higher task accuracy (p < 0.05) and sustained performance over time. Findings from different user groups suggest that stated preferences and behavioral outcomes can diverge depending on engagement context, highlighting the importance of validating design assumptions through observed interaction. This work contributes insights into age-related differences in engagement strategy effectiveness in human-robot interaction design.

</details>


### [113] [Uni-LaViRA: Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation for Unified Embodied Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27582)
*Hongyu Ding,Sizhuo Zhang,Ziming Xu,Jinwen Guo,Hongxiu Liu,Xingzhi Cheng,Zixuan Chen,Haifei Qi,Duo Wang,Hao Xu,Jieqi Shi,Yifan Zhang,Jing Huo,Jian Cheng,Yang Gao,Jiebo Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Uni-LaViRA proposes a zero-shot, unified navigation agent that translates language and vision into actions via a Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation, enabling cross-task/robot generality without training, aided by TODO List Memory and Second Chance Backtrack; achieves competitive results across several datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge: navigation generalization traditionally relies on scaling robot trajectories and data. The paper argues that structural generality—reducing decisions to a Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation and leveraging pretrained multimodal LLMs—can yield broad generalization. Objective: unify four task families (VLN-CE, ObjectNav, EQA, Aerial-VLN) and four heterogeneous robots in zero-shot.

Method: Frame navigation as a Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation: language outputs semantic-level directional commands; vision outputs pixel-level targets. Outputs reside in the natural output manifolds of pretrained multimodal LLMs, enabling reasoning without robot-specific training. Proposes Uni-LaViRA, a unified agentic architecture. Two agent-loop mechanisms: (1) TODO List Memory (TDM) rewrites a structured sub-goal checklist at every step and reads it into the attention window; (2) Second Chance Backtrack (SCB) reverts to pre-error state and conditions the next plan on the failed sub-trajectory. Zero training required.

Result: Zero-shot performance across tasks: VLN-CE R2R 60.7% SR; VLN-CE RxR 51.3% SR; HM3D-v2 77.7%; HM3D-OVON 60.0%; MP3D-EQA 54.7%; OpenUAV 40.0%. Claims to match or surpass recent training navigation foundation models that require millions of samples and thousands of GPU-hours.

Conclusion: Structural generality via L–V–robot actions translation plus minimalistic agent-loop mechanisms enables broad, zero-shot navigation across tasks and robots, reducing dependence on large training data and offering practical cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: Embodied navigation requires an agent to map language and visual observations to a stream of spatial actions that drive a real robot through environments it has never seen. The dominant approach has been to scale vision-language-action (VLA) foundation models on ever-larger collections of robot trajectories. This paper argues that, for navigation specifically, generality can be obtained structurally, not only through data scale. The underlying decision structure of navigation reduces to a single Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation. The language action emits semantic-level directional command and the vision action emits a pixel-level visual target. Both outputs lie inside the natural output manifold of pretrained multimodal large language models (MLLMs), so the task can be reasoned about by an agent rather than learned from robot data. Therefore, we present Uni-LaViRA, a unified agentic architecture that extends the same insight to four task families (VLN-CE, ObjectNav, EQA, and Aerial-VLN) and to four heterogeneous real robots (Wheeled, Quadruped, Humanoid robot, and a self-built UAV) in a zero-shot manner. Two agent-loop mechanisms make this unification practical. TODO List Memory (TDM) rewrites a structured checklist of pending sub-goals at every step, reciting the unfinished items back into the agent's most recent attention window. Second Chance Backtrack (SCB) rolls the robot back to the pre-error state and conditions the agent's next plan on the failed sub-trajectory, turning single-pass navigation into a self-correcting process. With zero training effort, Uni-LaViRA reaches 60.7% SR on VLN-CE R2R, 51.3% on VLN-CE RxR, 77.7% on HM3D-v2, 60.0% on HM3D-OVON, 54.7% on MP3D-EQA, and 40.0% on OpenUAV, matching or even surpassing recent training navigation foundation models that consume millions of samples and thousands of GPU-hours.

</details>


### [114] [Agentic Language-to-Objective Synthesis for Optofluidic Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27643)
*Ivan Saraev,Elena Erben,Weida Liao,Fan Nan,Gerhard Neumann,Eric Lauga,Moritz Kreysing*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular Speak-to-Objective pipeline uses a conditioned LLM to turn spoken/written commands into differentiable objectives for laser-driven micromanipulation, enabling natural-language programmable microassembly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between human intent and machine-executable goals in microfabrication; current goal specification is manual, opaque, and hard to reuse across modalities; a reusable, differentiable objective interface is needed for closed-loop, actuator-agnostic control.

Method: A perceive->compose->propose->act->report&learn loop where an LLM translates commands into geometry, spacing, and topology objectives. These are optimized with a constraint-aware inverse solver (SLSQP) and executed on an optofluidic platform using laser-induced thermoviscous flows; objectives are modular and actuator-agnostic, and the system learns from user feedback.

Result: Demonstrates natural-language-programmable, light-based microscale assembly of particle patterns. The approach yields robust descriptive objectives that can assemble from partial traces and recover after perturbations, and explicit objectives for precise placement.

Conclusion: Shows a path toward AI-assisted optical manufacturing with natural language, differentiable objectives, and laser actuation, enabling reusable digital workflows for programmable microassembly.

Abstract: Light-based advanced manufacturing increasingly requires programmable, closed-loop tools that translate human design intent into executable operations at small length scales. Yet a key bottleneck persists across robotic and manufacturing modalities: turning user intent into machine-readable objectives that are reliably executable. While micro-robotics offers versatile manipulation via optical actuation of fluids, mathematically tractable goal specification remains manual and hard to reuse. Here, we introduce Speak-to-Objective, a modular agentic pipeline that uses a conditioned Large Language Model (LLM) to translate spoken or written commands into fully differentiable objective functions for assembling microparticles in a constraint-aware inverse solver (SLSQP) and on an experimental optofluidic platform. The approach employs a compact loop - perceive -> compose -> propose -> act -> report & learn - that treats the objective as the interface between intent and actuation, separating what to assemble or pattern from how to actuate, while learning from user feedback. The pipeline composes geometry, spacing, and assignment/topology terms to generate robust descriptive objectives that assemble from partial traces and recover after perturbations, as well as explicit objectives for precise placement, all in an actuator-agnostic fashion. Using laser-induced thermoviscous flows as the physical actuation modality, we demonstrate natural-language-programmable, light-based microscale assembly of particle patterns in a microfluidic environment. Beyond its immediate impact on programmable microassembly, and using laser-induced optofluidic actuation as a reduced-complexity experimental platform, our work points toward self-driving, AI-assisted optical manufacturing platforms in which natural language, differentiable objectives, and laser-based actuation are coupled into a reusable digital workflow.

</details>


### [115] [Simulation-Informed Diffusion for Decentralized Multi-robot Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27697)
*Jinhao Liang,Sven Koenig,Ferdinando Fioretto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decentralized multi-robot motion planning method uses Simulation-Informed Diffusion (SID) based on constraint-aware diffusion models to simulate neighbors and plan safely, enabling minimal communication and scalability to large teams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limitations of existing planners: rely on static local observations and lack anticipation of neighboring robots, which degrades performance as robot count and clutter increase. Decentralization and limited communication further constrain safe coordination.

Method: Propose Simulation-Informed Diffusion (SID) built on constraint-aware diffusion models (CADM). SID first uses CADM to simulate future trajectories of neighboring robots from their current observations, then uses CADM again to plan each robot's own trajectory under safety constraints informed by these simulations. A minimal communication scheme triggers coordination only in highly congested scenarios.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows SID outperforms baselines in planning effectiveness and constraint satisfaction across diverse environments, scaling to scenarios with up to 108 robots and 160 obstacles.

Conclusion: SID provides a principled, scalable approach for decentralized multi-robot planning by integrating forward-simulation of neighbors with constraint-aware planning, reducing unnecessary communication and improving safety and efficiency in crowded environments.

Abstract: Decentralized multi-robot motion planning requires each robot to generate collision-free trajectories from local observations, without global sensing or reliable communication. However, most existing planners, whether classical or learning-based, generate trajectories from a static snapshot of the local observation, which limits their ability to anticipate the future behavior of neighboring robots. This limitation is critical as the number of robots increases and the environment becomes more cluttered. To overcome this challenge, this paper introduces Simulation-Informed Diffusion (SID), a decentralized framework built on constraint-aware diffusion models (CADM). SID first uses CADM to simulate the future trajectories of neighboring robots from their currently observed states, and then uses the same CADM to plan each robot's own trajectory under safety constraints informed by these simulations. Crucially, the accurate simulation of neighbors enables a minimal communication scheme that triggers coordination only when necessary in highly congested scenarios. Experiments across diverse environments show that SID consistently outperforms baseline methods in terms of planning effectiveness and constraint satisfaction, and scales to scenarios with 108 robots and 160 obstacles.

</details>


### [116] [VLM-Based Advanced Rider Assistance System for Motorcycle Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27948)
*Mohamed Elnoor,Francesca Baldini,Ananya Trivedi,Faizan M. Tariq,Jovin D'sa,David Isele,Sangjae Bae,Dinesh Manocha,Yosuke Sakamoto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel ARAS for motorcycles combines Vision-Language Models with segmentation-based detection to produce dense per-pixel hazard maps encoding semantic and physical risk attributes, feeding a motorcycle-specific planner to minimize hazard exposure while reaching a destination. Evaluated in CARLA, it outperforms a baseline in success rate and hazard exposure, with interpretable risk maps and safe trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motorcycles have markedly higher crash risk due to limited protection and sensitivity to surface hazards; existing ARAS lag behind ADAS in contextual reasoning and planning. A context-aware perception-to-planning pipeline tailored to motorcycle dynamics could reduce exposure to hazards and improve safety.

Method: Integrate Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for contextual hazard reasoning with segmentation-based detectors to generate dense risk maps. Each map pixel encodes semantic (e.g., pothole severity, puddle slipperiness) and physical (size, depth) attributes, yielding per-pixel hazard costs. A sampling-based planner, tuned to motorcycle dynamics, uses these costs to select throttle and steering actions that minimize hazard exposure while progressing toward the destination.

Result: In CARLA experiments across varied scenarios, the proposed method achieves higher success rates and lower hazard exposure than the baseline. Qualitative results show interpretable risk maps and safe trajectory recommendations.

Conclusion: The approach closes the perception-planning loop for motorcycles by combining semantic hazard reasoning with risk-aware planning, yielding safer trajectories in simulation and offering a foundation for real-world extension with further validation and efficiency improvements.

Abstract: Motorcycles face disproportionately high crash risks compared to cars due to limited protection and heightened sensitivity to surface hazards, yet Advanced Rider Assistance Systems (ARAS) remain underdeveloped relative to Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). We propose a novel ARAS that enhances motorcycle safety through semantic perception and risk-aware planning. Our approach leverages Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for contextual hazard reasoning and integrates them with segmentation-based detection to construct dense risk maps. These maps encode both semantic characteristics (e.g., pothole severity, puddle slipperiness) and physical attributes (e.g., size, depth), which produce per-pixel hazard costs that capture motorcycle-specific risks. These maps are used by a sampling-based planner tailored to motorcycle dynamics to recommend throttle and steering actions that minimize hazard exposure while advancing toward the destination. We evaluate our system in different scenarios in the CARLA simulator. Compared to the baseline method, our method achieves higher success rates and lower hazard exposure, while qualitative results demonstrate interpretable risk maps and safe trajectory recommendations.

</details>


### [117] [Simultaneous Contact Selection and Planning for Contact-Rich Manipulation with Cascaded Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27972)
*Zhe Zhang,Xingrong Diao,Haoxiang Liang,Han Yang,Bi-Ke Zhu,Dandan Zhang,Jiankun Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: We propose SCSP, a cascaded optimization framework for simultaneous contact selection and planning in robust contact-rich manipulation, enabling online global search of contact locations (CSO) and prior-guided, real-time manipulation trajectories (CPO). Demonstrated with simulations and real-robot experiments, showing diverse behaviors and robustness, with generalization to challenging tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing contact-implicit methods enable online hybrid planning but struggle to autonomously generate diverse contact location sequences due to non-smooth, highly coupled contact dynamics and sparse gradients, limiting task complexity.

Method: SCSP comprises two partners: Contact Selection Optimization (CSO) uses a surrogate contact model and discrete-continuous optimization to resolve nonsmoothness and coupling and search online for optimal contact locations; Contact Planning Optimization (CPO) uses the locations from CSO to generate corresponding manipulation trajectories in real time for redundant manipulators; the framework is cascaded to separate contact-location decision from trajectory planning.

Result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that SCSP yields diverse manipulation behaviors and robust control under inaccurate dynamics and perceptual noise, and generalizes to challenging tasks.

Conclusion: SCSP provides a unified, online-capable framework for active contact selection and planning in contact-rich manipulation, enabling diverse, robust behaviors across tasks with real-time feasibility and demonstrated generalization.

Abstract: We propose an optimization-based framework for robust contact-rich manipulation. Recent contact-implicit methods enable online hybrid planning across contact modes, allowing closed-loop manipulation for a given target state and contact location sequence of the robot and object. However, most existing approaches lack the ability to autonomously reason and generate diverse contact location sequences and manipulation trajectories, i.e., active contact location selection, which limits their applicability to relatively simple tasks. Active contact location selection is challenging due to complementarity in contact dynamics and the sparse gradients, making the design of a unified framework for contact selection and planning difficult. To address these challenges, we introduce Simultaneous Contact Selection and Planning (SCSP), a cascaded optimization framework comprising Contact Selection Optimization (CSO) and Contact Planning Optimization (CPO). CSO leverages a surrogate contact model and discrete-continuous optimization to efficiently resolve the nonsmoothness and coupling in contact selection, enabling online global searching of optimal contact locations. CPO performs prior-guided contact planning by evaluating the reference contact locations produced by CSO and generating corresponding manipulation trajectories in real time for redundant manipulators. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that SCSP produces diverse manipulation behaviors and robust control under inaccurate dynamics and perceptual noise. We further validate the generalization of the framework on challenging manipulation tasks.
  Project website: \href{https://sites.google.com/view/scsp-robot}{https://sites.google.com/view/scsp-robot}.

</details>


### [118] [An Operator-Based Approach to STL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28092)
*Panagiotis Rousseas,Dimos V. Dimarogonas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes an operator-based framework for STL that acts on reachability value functions to handle multi-nested specifications and enable online control synthesis, supported by necessary/sufficient conditions for satisfaction and simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current STL verification/control methods struggle with highly nested formulas; there is a need for scalable, online-friendly handling of complex STL specifications.

Method: Introduce an operator on reachability value functions for STL; develop operator-based nesting rules; derive necessary and sufficient conditions for STL satisfaction; validate via simulations with complex fragments.

Result: The framework achieves expressiveness for multi-nested STL; provides formal necessary and sufficient conditions for satisfaction; demonstrates feasibility for online synthesis in simulations involving complex fragments.

Conclusion: Operator-based nesting provides a viable framework for handling complex STL specifications and enabling online control synthesis, with potential for broader applicability beyond reachability-based approaches.

Abstract: Signal Temporal Logic (STL), has recently seen extensive development, owing to its rich expressivenes for autonomous planning and control. Nevertheless, existing verification and control synthesis methods are limited with respect to the complexity and degree of nesting of the formulae. In this work, we propose a novel approach to STL based on an operator acting on reachability value functions. This constitutes a new theoretical framework for handling complex multi-nested formulae while at the same time providing tools for on-line control synthesis. In contrast to focusing on the design of STL-based reachability (or control barrier) functions, we develop operator-based nesting rules directly. Our method's expressiveness is demonstrated both theoretically, where necessary and sufficient conditions for STL formula satisfaction are extracted, as well as in simulations with complex fragments.

</details>


### [119] [ICAN-Deploy: Identity-Stable Canary Deployment for Safety-Critical Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28097)
*Xue Qin,Simin Luan,John See,Zeyd Boukhers,Cong Yang,Zhijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Identity-stable canary deployment (ICAN-Deploy) preserves the cryptographic identity of deployed agents across the canary window by decoupling capability names (frozen/hashed) from capability versions (mutable runtime state). Certified once at identity creation, agents can evolve capabilities within a version/name envelope. Validated via formal methods and 100 robotic experiments; a strawman that folds versions into manifest fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address drift in mainstream canary controllers that changes the deployed system's identity, breaking safety-critical certification and necessitating re-certification for every canary window.

Method: Introduce ICAN-Deploy middleware with a state machine enforcing an identity hash invariant across the canary window by separating capability names from versions. Implement in runtime governance layer for LLM-driven robots; verify with closed-form proofs, AST lint, and TLA+ model-checking; validate on MuJoCo Franka Panda with N=100 canary cycles.

Result: Zero drift observed; entry latency 95% BCa CI [1.52, 2.01] ms; formal proofs and model checks confirm invariance; strawman that folds versions into manifest fails on same workload.

Conclusion: A certified identity allows arbitrary capability evolution under the same certification envelope, reducing re-certification costs and enabling safer, iterative upgrades in embodied robotic systems.

Abstract: Canary deployment routes a fraction of traffic to a new software version, monitors metrics, and rolls back on regression. Mainstream controllers (Argo Rollouts, Spinnaker, Flagger) change the deployed system's cryptographic identity during the canary window. The drift is harmless for stateless microservices but breaks the claim that "the agent you certified is still the agent you have" for safety-critical embodied agents, forcing re-certification per canary. We present ICAN-Deploy (Identity-stable CANary Deployment), a middleware construction whose state machine holds the identity hash invariant across the canary window by separating capability names (frozen, hashed) from capability versions (mutable runtime state). We implement ICAN-Deploy inside a runtime governance layer for LLM-driven robots and verify invariance by closed-form proof, AST lint, and TLA+ model-checking, then corroborate over N=100 real canary cycles on a Franka Panda arm in MuJoCo (zero drift; entry latency 95% BCa CI [1.52, 2.01] ms). A feature-flagged strawman that folds versions into the manifest falsifies on the same workload. A system certified once at identity-creation time can then ship arbitrary capability evolution under that same certification, within the version-and-name envelope.

</details>


### [120] [STR Robot: Design of an Autonomous Mobile Robot from Simulation to Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28110)
*Vinh Nguyen,Gia-Uy Le,Tien-Dat Nguyen,Tri-Tin Nguyen,Vinh-Hao Nguyen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A simulation-to-real workflow for an autonomous mobile robot focusing on onboard control, self-localization, and autonomous navigation, validated from simulation to hardware with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage simulation tools to accelerate development and validation of autonomous robots while mitigating real-world risks and costs in the deployment cycle.

Method: Use an existing mechanical platform as the base; develop onboard control, pose estimation/localization, and autonomous navigation with onboard sensing and computation; first implement and test the framework in simulation, then deploy on the real robot for experiments; release the source code.

Result: Feasibility of the proposed sim-to-real approach is demonstrated; simulation provides an effective foundation for developing reliable autonomous mobile robot systems; hardware experiments validate the methodology; code will be released online.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of simulation-to-real pipelines for autonomous mobile robotics and provides open-source resources to facilitate replication and further work.

Abstract: With the rapid development of simulation tools, the development and validation of autonomous robotic systems have become more efficient before real-world deployment. This paper presents a simulation-to-real implementation of an autonomous mobile robot based on an existing mechanical platform. Instead of focusing on mechanical design, our work concentrates on the development of the onboard control, self-localization, and autonomous navigation system. The proposed robot is equipped with onboard sensing and computation to estimate its pose and navigate autonomously in the environment. The overall framework is first developed and tested in simulation, and then deployed on the real robot for experimental evaluation. The results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed approach and show that simulation provides an effective foundation for developing reliable autonomous mobile robot systems. The source code will be released at https://ntdathp.github.io/outdoor-robot-web.

</details>


### [121] [Provably Guaranteed Polytopic Uncertainty Quantification for SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28172)
*Guangyang Zeng,Yulong Gao,Yuan Shen,Lingpeng Chen,Haoying Li,Guodong Shi,Junfeng Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Provably guaranteed uncertainty quantification (UQ) for 3D-3D landmark-based SLAM using polytopic uncertainty sets, with forward UQ (mapping), backward UQ (pose tracking), and pose compound modules; employs conformal prediction for data-driven calibration; demonstrates theory + practicality with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical robotics require formal, contained UQ for perception. Existing work often lacks complete SLAM UQ, imposes restrictive modeling assumptions, or focuses only on pose estimation. A complete, certified SLAM UQ pipeline is needed.

Method: Three UQ modules: forward UQ for mapping, backward UQ for pose tracking, and pose compound. Uncertainty is represented as polytopes to enable tractable computation and a unified treatment of pose uncertainty. Guarantees: when input uncertainty bounds are deterministic, outputs provably contain true poses and landmarks. Conformal prediction calibrates measurement uncertainty from data with a prescribed probability. The approach targets 3D-3D landmark-based SLAM; results rely on polytope operations and probabilistic calibration.

Result: The authors report provable guarantees and practical usability via simulations and experiments. Code is open-sourced at the given GitHub URL, supporting reproducibility and adoption.

Conclusion: A novel, certified UQ framework for SLAM that combines polytope-based uncertainty representations with conformal prediction to deliver provable containment of true states in a complete 3D-3D landmark-based SLAM pipeline, with demonstrated theoretical and practical benefits and open-source accessibility.

Abstract: In safety-critical robotics applications, guaranteed and practical uncertainty quantification (UQ) in perception is vital. Many existing works either offer no formal containment guarantee, rely on restrictive modeling assumptions, or focus only on pose estimation rather than a complete SLAM pipeline. This paper presents provably guaranteed UQ algorithms for 3D-3D landmark-based SLAM. The algorithms consist of three basic UQ modules: forward UQ for mapping, backward UQ for pose tracking, and pose compound. Each module produces a certified uncertainty set; when the input uncertainty bounds are deterministic, the output sets inherit deterministic guarantees, i.e., they provably contain the true poses and landmarks. Specifically, we use polytopes to represent uncertainty sets, enabling tractable computations and a unified treatment of pose uncertainty. To enhance algorithms' practical usability, we incorporate conformal prediction to calibrate measurement uncertainty from data with prescribed probability. Simulations and experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms provide both strong theoretical guarantees and practical usability. The code is open-sourced at https://github.com/LIAS-CUHKSZ/Polytopic-SLAM-Uncertainty-Quantification.

</details>


### [122] [Visualizing Latent Phase Structures in Locomotion Policies: A Multi-Environment Study with Temporal Feature Extension](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28186)
*Daisuke Yasui,Toshitaka Matuki,Hiroshi Sato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a clustering-based method to reveal latent locomotion phase structures from DRL trajectories by augmenting state features with actions and next-state/action, and introducing a cluster-counting approach that suppresses self-transitions; applied to Ant-v5, HalfCheetah-v5, Walker2D-v5 with improved phase transition regularity over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To visualize internal motion structures learned by DRL controllers for locomotion and align them with biomechanical phase concepts (stance/swing).

Method: Clustering using augmented feature vectors (state, action, next state, next action); introduces a criterion or algorithm to select the number of clusters while reducing self-transitions; evaluate on three MuJoCo tasks.

Result: Identified clearer and more regular phase transition rules than existing method across Ant-v5, HalfCheetah-v5, and Walker2D-v5.

Conclusion: Augmented clustering with self-transition suppression effectively uncovers latent locomotion phases from DRL trajectories.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has been shown to achieve high performance on locomotion control tasks in MuJoCo benchmarks such as HalfCheetah, Ant, and Walker2D. However, visualizing the motion structures internally obtained by a trained policy function implemented as a deep neural network remains challenging. It is known from biomechanics and related fields that locomotion control is realized through the repetition of motion phases such as the stance phase and swing phase. In this study, we propose a framework for uncovering latent motion phase structures from trajectories generated by locomotion control policies through interaction with the environment. The proposed method extends the clustering features from state observations alone to augmented features including actions, next states, and next actions, and introduces a method for determining the number of clusters that suppresses self-transitions. Applying the proposed method to three environments -- Ant-v5, HalfCheetah-v5, and Walker2D-v5 -- we successfully identified phase structures with clearer and more regular transition rules than those obtained by the existing method.

</details>


### [123] [Natural Functional Gradients for Smooth Trajectory Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28202)
*Kisang Park,Chanwoo Kim,Kyungjae Lee,Sungjoon Choi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A trajectory optimization method in function space using natural functional gradients with Gaussian smoothing, enabling collision-free, smooth motions in cluttered environments; relies on Monte-Carlo estimation from black-box evaluations; improves feasibility and smoothness over baselines; applicable without analytic gradients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of generating feasible, smooth trajectories in constrained, narrow passages where traditional discretized optimization struggles, and gradients are hard to obtain due to collision checking and contact-rich dynamics.

Method: Optimize a Gaussian-smoothed surrogate objective in function space; use natural functional gradient; rely on Monte-Carlo estimator requiring only black-box trajectory evaluations; geometry-aware updates that preserve trajectory structure and decouple regularity from time discretization.

Result: Experiments in constrained robotic manipulation show improved trajectory feasibility and smoother motions compared to planning and trajectory optimization baselines, especially in narrow clearances; project page for further details.

Conclusion: The proposed natural functional gradient framework provides a practical, gradient-free (or gradient-free-friendly) path to smooth, feasible trajectories in challenging environments, with intrinsic function-space updates and independence from time discretization.

Abstract: Generating collision-free and smooth motions remains a central challenge in robotic manipulation, particularly in cluttered environments and narrow passages where feasible regions are highly constrained and fragmented. We propose a trajectory optimization framework that performs geometry-aware updates directly in function space using natural functional gradients. The method optimizes a Gaussian-smoothed surrogate objective that regularizes the optimization landscape through smooth trajectory perturbations while preserving trajectory-level structure. Because the updates are defined intrinsically in function space, trajectory regularity can be controlled independently of a particular time discretization. We derive a practical Monte-Carlo estimator of the natural functional gradient that requires only black-box trajectory evaluations, making the method applicable when analytic gradients are unavailable or unreliable due to collision checking and contact-rich simulation. Experiments on constrained robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that the proposed method improves trajectory feasibility and produces smoother motions than representative planning and trajectory optimization baselines in environments with narrow geometric clearances. Additional results, videos, and implementation details are available at the project page: https://kisangpark.github.io/natural-functional-gradient/

</details>


### [124] [ProgVLA: Progress-Aware Robot Manipulation Skill Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28231)
*Seungsu Kim,Jinyoung Choi,Seungmin Baek,Jean-Michel Renders*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ProgVLA is a compact vision-language-action (VLA) model for robot manipulation that compresses long multi-modal sequences via a two-stage Perceiver resampler into fixed context tokens and uses progress heads trained with offline RL to estimate remaining task horizon, achieving competitive performance with 0.1B parameters and delivering gains on long-horizon tasks; ablations highlight the context resampler and visual fine-tuning, with additional gains from progress-aware training; validated on real-world toy-kitchen setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable robot manipulation under tight compute/memory budgets while handling long-horizon, multi-modal tasks by maintaining an explicit representation of task progress.

Method: A multi-modal encoder with two-stage Perceiver resampling compresses visual, language, and proprioceptive streams into a fixed set of context tokens. An auxiliary set of progress heads is trained with offline RL objectives to learn critics over normalized remaining-horizon targets, enabling internal task progress estimates and progress-aware imitation learning. The approach is evaluated on two multi-task manipulation benchmarks with a 0.1B-parameter model and validated in real-world toy-kitchen environments.

Result: ProgVLA achieves success rates competitive with substantially larger pretrained baselines and surpasses them on long-horizon and harder task tiers. Ablations show the learned context resampler and task-adaptive visual fine-tuning as the largest single contributors, with progress-aware training providing additional gains concentrated on long-horizon and multi-object tasks.

Conclusion: Compact VLA models with explicit progress modeling can approach or exceed larger baselines in long-horizon robot manipulation under compute constraints; key contributions are the context resampling mechanism, task-adaptive visual fine-tuning, and progress-aware training, with demonstrated real-world viability.

Abstract: We present ProgVLA, a compact vision-language-action (VLA) model designed for reliable robot manipulation under tight compute and memory budgets. The model specifically focuses on efficiently processing long multi-modal sequences by maintaining an explicit representation of task progress over extended horizons. To this end, ProgVLA integrates two key components. First, a multi-modal encoder with a two-stage Perceiver resampling scheme compresses variable-length visual, language, and proprioceptive streams into a fixed set of control-ready context tokens, substantially reducing sequence length while preserving cross-modal grounding. Second, an auxiliary set of progress heads is trained with offline reinforcement learning (RL) objectives to jointly learn critics over normalized remaining-horizon targets. This provides the policy with an internal estimate of task progress and enables advantage- and success-weighted flow-matching imitation learning. On two well-established multi-task robot manipulation benchmarks, a 0.1B-parameter ProgVLA model reaches success rates that are competitive with, and on long-horizon and harder task tiers exceed, substantially larger pretrained baselines. Ablations indicate that the learned context resampler and task-adaptive visual fine-tuning are the largest single contributors, while progress-aware training provides a consistent additional gain that is concentrated on long-horizon and multi-object tasks. We further validate the approach in real-world toy-kitchen environments.

</details>


### [125] [POINav: Benchmarking and Enhancing Final-Meters Arrival in Real-World Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28237)
*Ruiyan Gong,Meisheng Zhang,Yuxiang Zhao,Mingchao Sun,Yanfen Shen,Zedong Chu,Zhining Gu,Wei Guo,Xiaolong Cheng,Qiming Li,Kangning Niu,Yanqing Zhu,Xiaolong Wu,Tianlun Li,Mu Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First real-world, closed-loop POI-goal navigation benchmark (POINav-Bench) with 3D Gaussian Splatting reconstructions, plus a Brain-Action framework and POINav-Dataset for POI-grounded navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between simulated VLN benchmarks and real-world POI navigation; address final-meters challenge and realism limitations.

Method: 11 commercial areas reconstructed with 3D Gaussian Splatting (126,398 m^2; 163 POIs); traversability annotations and reference trajectories for high-fidelity evaluation; Brain module for POI-grounded reasoning guiding an Action module to predict continuous waypoints; curated POINav-Dataset with 70k signage-entrance pairs.

Result: Demonstrates viability of the Brain-Action framework for real-world POI navigation and provides a high-fidelity, POI-rich benchmark to refine agents; preliminary experiments support potential usefulness.

Conclusion: POINav-Bench and the Brain-Action framework offer a practical path to bridge sim-to-real gaps in POI-goal navigation and encourage development of POI-aware planning and control in real environments.

Abstract: Real-world navigation is fundamentally driven by Points of Interest (POIs), yet reaching a precise POI remains a critical "final-meters" challenge. Existing Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) benchmarks of POI-goal navigation often suffer from coarse granularity or significant sim-to-real gaps due to generated scene. To bridge this gap, we present POINav-Bench, the first benchmark designed for closed-loop evaluation of real-world POI-goal navigation. It comprises 11 commercial areas reconstructed from real-world captures using 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), covering 126,398 $m^{2}$ in total and spanning 163 distinct POIs. With traversability-aware annotations and reference trajectories, POINav-Bench enables high-fidelity evaluation of navigation agents in realistic, POI-rich real-world environments. Building on this, we propose the POINav Brain-Action Framework where a Brain module performs POI-grounded reasoning to guide an Action module in predicting continuous waypoints for real-world execution. We further curate the POINav-Dataset, containing 70K real-world signage-entrance pairs. Experiments show that our framework provides a viable path toward refining real-world POI-goal navigation.

</details>


### [126] [Natural Locomotion: Principle and Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28254)
*Mirado Mortel,Luc Jaulin,Lionel Lapierre,Simon Rohou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Natural locomotion with environmental exchange: a conserved POE framework defines a Natural Locomotion Manifold (NLM) via internal oscillators and zero-mean propulsion–oscillator power; demonstrated on ideal no-slip systems; guides passive-architecture design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit passive dynamics and environment interactions to achieve efficient locomotion; formalize a universal principle to identify and design natural locomotion cycles within energy-conserving constraints.

Method: Conservative realization for continuous environmental constraints; closed/open construction; isolate an internal oscillator via action-angle or modal decomposition; enforce zero mean POE; reconstruct pose with cycles that recur internally.

Result: Demonstrated on Chaplygin-sleigh/pendulum-driven car and a three-body extension; scalar case yields theorem-backed NLM through POE closure; multi-degree requires modal identity and internal return; identifies design space for passive architectures supporting one or more certified NLM families.

Conclusion: Natural locomotion becomes a design question: select passive architectures that support certified NLM families; framework for designing energy-conserving locomotion via environmental exchanges.

Abstract: Robotic locomotion can become efficient when mechanisms exploit passive dynamics, compliance, and resonance rather than track prescribed trajectories. This paper formulates natural locomotion as an exchange principle for systems whose motion is mediated by environmental constraints or interactions. A motion is natural when an internal oscillator returns periodically, the body pose drifts, and the mean Propulsion--Oscillator Exchange power (POE power) vanishes over one cycle. The selected family is a Natural Locomotion Manifold (NLM). We develop the conservative realization of this principle for continuous ideal environmental constraints: the constraints do no external work, total mechanical energy is conserved, and zero mean POE power is an internal exchange with the environment-mediated propulsive channel, not external energy input.
  The method is a closed/open construction. The propulsive channel is first closed to reveal an effective internal oscillator, organized by scalar action-angle structure in one effective degree of freedom or by nonlinear modal sectors in several degrees of freedom. The channel is then reopened, pose is reconstructed, and accepted cycles must preserve internal recurrence and zero mean POE power.
  We demonstrate the principle on two ideal nonholonomic no-slip systems: a Chaplygin-sleigh / pendulum-driven car and a three-body extension. In the scalar case, POE closure is equivalent to the missing internal return condition, giving a theorem-backed computation of the NLM family. In the multi-degree case, POE closure remains necessary but must be completed by modal identity, internal return, dynamics consistency, same fixed passive architecture, and nonzero displacement. Natural locomotion becomes a design question: which passive architectures support no, one, or several certified NLM families?

</details>


### [127] [IMU Propagation as Preintegration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28279)
*Jianzhu Huai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IMU preintegration and propagation are two realizations of the same computation; treating them as equivalent via a convention-agnostic wrapper enables reuse of propagation code to obtain preintegrated measurements, bias Jacobians, and covariance, and vice versa for state-transition matrices and propagated covariances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify IMU preintegration with standard propagation to simplify software reuse, enable translation across different error-state formulations, and provide practical consistency checks for preintegration implementations.

Method: Propose a convention-agnostic view where preintegrated measurements, bias Jacobians, and covariance are produced by wrapping an existing IMU propagation routine; conversely, show that a preintegration module can recover state-transition matrices and propagated covariances; this allows cross-compatibility and reuse of propagation code across VE and inertial integration frameworks.

Result: Experiments with random IMU sequences show close agreement between an RK4-based propagation implementation and GTSAM's tangent and manifold preintegration modules in recovered Jacobians, covariances, and transition matrices.

Conclusion: The perspective simplifies reuse of existing propagation code, supports translation across error-state definitions, and provides practical consistency checks for preintegration; experimental validation supports equivalence between preintegration and propagation implementations.

Abstract: IMU preintegration is widely used in factor-graph-based visual--inertial, lidar--inertial, and radar--inertial state estimation, yet it is often treated as a specialized implementation separate from conventional IMU propagation. This note shows that IMU preintegration and propagation are equivalent realizations of the same underlying computation. We present a convention-agnostic view in which the preintegrated measurement, bias Jacobians, and covariance can be obtained by wrapping an existing IMU propagation routine, while a preintegration module can conversely recover state-transition matrices and propagated covariances. This perspective simplifies the reuse of existing propagation code, supports translation across different error-state definitions, and provides practical consistency checks for preintegration implementations. Experiments with random IMU sequences demonstrate close agreement between an RK4-based propagation implementation and GTSAM's tangent and manifold preintegration modules in the recovered Jacobians, covariances, and transition matrices.

</details>


### [128] [EventShiftFlow: Towards Hardware-efficient FPGA-based Flow Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28312)
*Arianna Alonso Bizzi,Fernando Cladera,C. J. Taylor*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FPGA-friendly streaming velocity estimator for event cameras: discretizes events into fixed time bins, uses a 1-bit occupancy grid, and evaluates multiple velocity hypotheses in fixed-width integer logic with no dividers/DSP; achieves high directional accuracy (99.5%) and low storage (<2 kB) on a single-axis Artix-7 prototype, at 10-40% occupancy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable low-latency, resource-constrained motion estimation from event cameras suitable for reactive robotics, without frame reconstruction or floating-point arithmetic.

Method: Partition asynchronous events into fixed-duration time bins; form a 1-bit spatial occupancy grid; compare several velocity hypotheses in parallel using only shift registers, counters, comparators, and small LUT-based multiplies; no dividers, no DSP blocks, no frame reconstruction, no iterative optimization; output sparse, quantized velocity per active pixel.

Result: On synthetic data with ground-truth velocities, magnitude and direction are recovered; magnitude degrades when intersecting velocities occur. On a real event sequence, directional accuracy reaches 99.5% across four motion segments; robust across occupancy 10-40%; requires <2 kB storage; demonstrates a single-axis FPGA prototype on Xilinx Artix-7.

Conclusion: The approach trades dense sub-pixel optical flow for a sparse, hardware-friendly, velocity-estimation primitive suitable for low-latency robotics on constrained platforms; provides a quantified study of density effects and parameter sensitivity, and demonstrates feasibility of FPGA-based event-driven velocity estimation with minimal resources.

Abstract: Event-based vision sensors offer asynchronous, high-temporal-resolution measurements that are attractive for low-latency robotic perception, but many event-based motion estimation methods are computationally intensive and difficult to map to FPGA hardware. We present a streaming velocity estimator that discretizes asynchronous events into fixed-duration time bins, constructs a 1-bit spatial occupancy grid, and evaluates multiple velocity hypotheses in parallel using only fixed-width integer logic - shift registers, counters, comparators, and small LUT-mapped multiplies - with no dividers and no DSP blocks. It requires no frame reconstruction, no floating-point arithmetic, and no iterative optimization. The method deliberately trades dense sub-pixel optical flow for a sparse, quantized velocity estimate at each active pixel, suited to low-latency tasks such as reactive obstacle avoidance on size-, weight-, and power-constrained platforms. On noisy synthetic data with known ground-truth velocities, the method recovers both magnitude and direction, with magnitude estimates being most challenged when objects of different velocities intersect. On a real event-camera sequence, directional accuracy reaches 99.5% across all four evaluated motion segments, with performance remaining robust across occupancy densities in the 10-40% range. We characterize the algorithm's density-dependent behavior, present a parameter sensitivity analysis, show that the proposed datapath requires less than 2 kB of storage, and implement a single-axis prototype on a low-cost Xilinx Artix-7.

</details>


### [129] [Identifying Explicit Parsimonious Piece-wise Polynomial Relationships in Industrial time-series: Application to manipulator robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28320)
*Mazen Alamir,Sacha Clavel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a method to derive explicit parsimonious piecewise polynomial models from implicit parsimonious identification, and validates on inverse models of 6- and 4-axis robots to compare generalization with DNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
