Motivation: To obtain interpretable, sparse representations in high-dimensional feature settings, extending implicit parsimonious models to explicit piecewise forms, and to assess generalization to unseen contexts relative to deep neural networks.

Method: Leverages a recently proposed identification algorithm that yields parsimonious implicit relationships to construct explicit piecewise representations using the same set of polynomials. Applies the framework to identify parsimonious explicit representations of the inverse model of a 6-axis manipulator robot, with additional experiments on a 4-axis robot to study generalization under unseen contexts and to compare with state-of-the-art DNN structures.

Result: Demonstrates the feasibility of deriving explicit parsimonious piecewise representations from an implicit framework; the robotic experiments illustrate the approach and are designed to compare generalization capabilities against DNNs in unseen contexts.

Conclusion: The work shows that parsimonious explicit piecewise polynomial models can be built from implicit parsimonious identification, providing an interpretable alternative for high-dimensional inverse problems and potentially better generalization to unseen contexts compared with DNNs in robotics.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of identifying parsimonious explicit piece-wise polynomial relationships that might involve a relatively large number of raw features. The algorithm leverages a recently proposed identification algorithm that yields parsimonious implicit relationships enabling to derive normality characterization in the context of anomaly detection and localization. The algorithm proposed in this paper goes a step further by deriving explicit piece-wise representations that are built using the set of polynomials involved in the implicit representations. The framework is illustrated on the problem of identifying parsimonious explicit representations of the inverse model of a 6-axis manipulator robot. Moreover, further experiments on a 4-axis robot are also shown which are designed to investigate the generalization capability of parsimonious models compared to state-of-the-art DNNs structures, when models face unseen contexts of use.

</details>


### [130] [Chance-Constrained MPPI under State and Dynamic Object Prediction Uncertainty and the Evaluation of Collision Risk Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28330)
*Benjamin Serfling,Konrad Doll,Kati Radkhah-Lens*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DUCCT-MPPI: a risk-aware planner that propagates localization and dynamic obstacle uncertainties with an unscented transform and Monte Carlo integration, paired with a rigorous evaluation framework using proper scoring rules to validate collision-risk estimates in closed-loop MPPI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Upstream localization and perception miscalibration undermines probabilistic safety in chance-constrained MPPI, creating overconfident safety violations and overly conservative behavior.

Method: Develop a dual-uncertainty tube MPPI (DUCCT-MPPI) that (i) uses a one-tube Unscented Transform to propagate localization uncertainty and (ii) aggregates dynamic obstacle prediction uncertainty via Monte Carlo sampling; and (iii) evaluate risk predictions with proper scoring rules; validate via extensive physics-based simulations.

Result: In cluttered environments, DUCCT-MPPI outperforms Monte Carlo MPPI baselines by ~28% navigation success, while achieving shorter travel times and reducing social forces, and avoids deadlocks through robust failure-mitigation leading to safe, conservative maneuvers.

Conclusion: Reliable probabilistic safety in autonomous navigation requires expressive risk models and statistically valid uncertainty estimates across the autonomy stack; the proposed framework demonstrates improved robustness and risk-awareness through integrated uncertainty handling and evaluation.

Abstract: Chance-constrained Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control is increasingly adopted for navigation in dynamic environments to explicitly bound collision risk. However, these probabilistic guarantees implicitly assume that upstream uncertainties from localization and perception are well-calibrated. In practice, estimators are often miscalibrated, inducing characteristic closed-loop failure modes: overconfidence leads to systematic safety violations, while underconfidence triggers overly conservative freezing or probability dilution. To address this critical gap, our primary contribution is a rigorous evaluation methodology applying proper scoring rules to assess the statistical validity of predicted collision risks during closed-loop execution. Concurrently, Dual-Uncertainty Chance-Constrained Tube MPPI (DUCCT-MPPI) is proposed as a real-time, risk-aware planning architecture. DUCCT-MPPI jointly integrates localization uncertainty via a one-tube Unscented Transform (UT) approximation and dynamic obstacle prediction uncertainty via Monte Carlo aggregation. Through extensive physics-based simulations, the framework demonstrates robust failure-mitigation, seamlessly transitioning to safe, conservative maneuvering without succumbing to functional deadlocks in highly cluttered environments. In highly cluttered environments, DUCCT-MPPI achieves superior robustness, outperforming established Monte Carlo MPPI baselines by nearly 28\% in navigation success rate, while simultaneously recording the lowest travel times and minimizing induced social forces. Ultimately, these findings establish that reliable probabilistic safety in autonomous navigation dictates not only expressive risk models but statistically valid uncertainty estimates throughout the entire autonomy stack.

</details>


### [131] [Magnet-Based Soft Robotic Skin Using a 3D-Printed Multi-Lattice Structure and CNN-Based Tactile Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28352)
*Yunseong Bang,Joowon Park,Suan Sim,Youngjun Ryu,Sukho Park,Kyungseo Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A magnet-based soft robotic skin combines a distributed Hall sensor network with a magnet-induced tactile lattice and a CNN-based tactile super-resolution model to map contact location and normal force over large, conformal surfaces with tunable compliance, enabling rapid fabrication and real-time sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve large-area, high-resolution tactile sensing with minimal blind spots, enabling safe, scalable, and conformal robotic skins for future robotics and human-robot interaction.

Method: A multilayer soft lattice embeds permanent magnets and an array of Hall-effect sensors. External forces modify magnetic fields; the lattice distributes these changes across the sensing domain. Lattice parameters are tunable to adjust mechanical compliance and transduction. An implicit modeling workflow and SLS 3D printing enable rapid fabrication of conformal structures. A convolutional neural network, trained on experimental measurements, estimates contact location and normal force in real time.

Result: Experimental validation shows accurate contact localization; the method scales to larger surfaces, indicating potential for whole-body robotic skin and safer human-robot interaction.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a rapid-fabrication, tunable, wide-area tactile sensing approach combining magnet-based transduction with neural decoding to produce real-time contact localization and force estimation on conformal robotic skins.

Abstract: This paper presents a magnet-based robotic skin that integrates a multilayer soft lattice with distributed Hall-effect sensor arrays and a tactile super-resolution model. External contact forces are converted to magnetic field changes by embedded permanent magnets, and the lattice spreads these changes across the sensing domain. This gives each sensor a large, overlapping receptive field and enables a large sensing area with minimal blind spots. Lattice parameters are tunable, enabling joint adjustment of mechanical compliance and transduction characteristics. An implicit modeling workflow and selective laser sintering (SLS) 3D printing support rapid fabrication of conformal, high-complexity structures. A convolutional neural network trained on experimental measurements estimates contact location and normal force in real time. Experiments validate localization accuracy and indicate scalability to larger surfaces, suggesting applicability to whole-body robotic skin and safe human-robot interaction.

</details>


### [132] [Accelerating Robot Path Planning via Connectivity-Preserving Region Proposal Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28362)
*Zhanzheng Ma,Cancan Zhao,Shuai Zhang,Bo Ouyang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CP-RPN proposes a segmentation-guided, connectivity-preserving region proposal network using a DAT-based segmentation with a deconvolutional decoder, plus a composite loss including persistent-homology-based topological continuity, to produce compact, connected search regions for path planning via Voronoi diagrams with an A* fallback; demonstrates substantial search-space compression and low-latency, high-success planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate the inefficiency of sampling-based planners in large search spaces and the fragmentation/topology issues of learning-based approaches by generating globally connected, corridor-like regions that guide planning.

Method: A segmentation model aided by a Deformable Attention Transformer (DAT) captures long-range dependencies to ensure global connectivity. A deconvolutional decoder preserves spatial details. A composite loss—Cross-Entropy, Connectivity-Aware loss, and Topological Continuity loss based on persistent homology—enforces pixel-level accuracy, local coherence, and global connectivity. Planning proceeds on high-connectivity regions using Voronoi diagrams with a local A* fallback for robustness.

Result: Candidate region size reduced by over 60.13% relative to MPT baseline; average planning latency 0.11s; 99.60% success rate; outperforms traditional sampling-based methods in stability.

Conclusion: Connectivity-preserving, segmentation-guided region proposals enable fast, robust planning by compressing the search space into connected corridors and guiding Voronoi-based path planning with a reliable A* fallback.

Abstract: Mobile robot path planning methods are often constrained by vast search spaces, resulting in latency in samplingbased algorithms. Learning-based approaches frequently suffer from local region fragmentation and global topological inconsistency. To tackle the problem, we present the Connectivity- Preserving Region Proposal Network (CP-RPN), a segmentationguided model designed to predict compact and topologically connected candidate regions, significantly compressing the search space. Specifically, we design a segmentation model that leverages a Deformable Attention Transformer (DAT) to capture long-range dependencies for global connectivity, with a Deconvolutional decoder to preserve fine-grained spatial details. To guarantee the connectivity of the predicted mask, we design a composite loss function that combines Cross-Entropy loss for pixelwise supervision, a Connectivity-Aware loss to enhance local coherence, and a Topological Continuity loss based on persistent homology to enforce global connectivity. Building on these highconnectivity corridor-like regions, the Voronoi diagram is used to plan the path, backed by a local A* fallback mechanism to ensure robustness. Experimental results demonstrate that CPRPN reduces the candidate region size by over 60.13% compared to the MPT baseline and achieves deterministic low-latency planning (avg. 0.11s) with a 99.60% success rate, outperforming traditional sampling-based algorithms in stability.

</details>


### [133] [Safety-Critical Adaptive Impedance Control via Nonsmooth Control Barrier Functions under State and Input Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28367)
*Faisal Lawan,Xiaoran Han,Joaquin Carrasco,Barry Lennox,Xiaoxiao Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Online adaptive impedance control with a unified safety filter and a position-velocity non-smooth barrier function (NCBF) to enforce joint-state constraints under uncertainty, using interval-type-2 fuzzy compensation, disturbance observation, and soft torque constraints; validated in simulation on a 7-DOF manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe, compliant interaction for robotic manipulators in human-robot interaction and contact-rich tasks under dynamics uncertainty and actuator limits.

Method: A quadratic-program-based safety filter combined with a composed position-velocity non-smooth control barrier function (NCBF) to enforce joint position and velocity constraints through a single relative-degree-one barrier. Unknown dynamics are compensated online with an interval type-2 fuzzy logic system. Actuator torque limits are enforced via soft constraints with exact penalty recovery of feasible solutions. A disturbance-observer-enhanced safety mechanism improves robustness to modeling errors and external interaction forces. Theoretical guarantees are provided via composite Lyapunov analysis, proving forward invariance of the safe set and uniform ultimate boundedness of the impedance-tracking error.

Result: Simulation on a 7-DOF manipulator under severe parametric uncertainty and external wrenches demonstrates safe constraint satisfaction and robust impedance tracking.

Conclusion: The framework offers a unified, theoretically grounded approach for guaranteeing joint-state safety and robust impedance control in uncertain, contact-rich environments, with successful simulation validation and potential for real-world experimentation and extension.

Abstract: Safe physical interaction is critical for deploying robotic manipulators in human-robot interaction and contact-rich tasks, where uncertainty, external forces, and actuator limitations can compromise both performance and safety. We propose an online adaptive impedance control framework that enforces joint-state safety while achieving compliant interaction under uncertain dynamics. The approach combines a quadratic-program-based safety filter with a novel composed position-velocity non-smooth control barrier function (NCBF), enabling joint position and velocity constraints to be enforced through a unified relative-degree-one barrier. Unknown dynamics are compensated online using an interval type-2 fuzzy logic system, while actuator torque limits are handled through soft constraints with exact penalty recovery of feasible solutions. A disturbance-observer-enhanced safety mechanism improves robustness against modelling errors and external interaction forces. Using composite Lyapunov analysis, we prove forward invariance of the safe set and the uniform ultimately boundedness of the impedance-tracking error. Simulations on a 7-DOF manipulator with severe parametric uncertainty and external interaction wrenches demonstrate safe constraint satisfaction and robust impedance tracking.

</details>


### [134] [Tactile-Proprioceptive Sensor Fusion for Contact Wrench Estimation in Whole-Body Physical Human-Robot Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28412)
*Junha Min,Junghyeon Ma,Jiwung Kwon,Sunggyu Bae,Joohyung Kim,Kyungseo Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A tactile-proprioceptive fusion approach for humanoid/robot arms that combines pneumatic skin cues with motor-current proprioception to estimate multi-axis contact forces and enable kinesthetic teaching, using a temporal convolutional network to suppress friction hysteresis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable natural and safe physical human-robot interaction by resolving friction-induced ambiguities in contact force sensing and providing responsive guidance.

Method: 1) tactile cues from pneumatic skin pads as contact indicators; 2) fuse with motor-current-based proprioception to reconstruct multi-axis contact forces; 3) apply temporal convolutional network to mitigate friction hysteresis during stick-slip and improve onset estimation; 4) validate on a skin-integrated robot arm with stationary contact and kinesthetic teaching.

Result: The fusion achieves improved sensitivity and responsiveness over tactile-only and proprioceptive-only baselines; capable of simultaneous force estimation and kinesthetic teaching.

Conclusion: Tactile-proprioceptive fusion offers a reliable, safe, and intuitive pathway for physical human-robot interaction in contact-rich tasks.

Abstract: Direct physical guidance is a natural means of teaching and interacting with robots, and robotic skins make a key contribution by enabling sensitive contact sensing and localization. This paper presents a tactile-proprioceptive sensor fusion framework for natural physical human-robot interaction. Tactile cues from pneumatic skin pads serve as contact indicators that bypass the ambiguity between frictional residues and applied external forces, enabling highly sensitive contact detection without explicit friction identification. We fuse these cues with motor-current-based proprioception to reconstruct multi-axis contact forces on the robot surface. To maintain accuracy during motion, we employ a temporal convolutional network (TCN) to mitigate friction hysteresis during stick-slip transitions, reducing uncertainty at contact onset and yielding smooth, responsive guidance. We validate the approach on a skin-integrated robot arm: (i) multi-axis forces are reconstructed in stationary contacts, and (ii) simultaneous force estimation and kinesthetic teaching are demonstrated. Results indicate improved sensitivity and responsiveness across diverse contact conditions compared with tactile-only and proprioceptive-only baselines, supporting tactile-proprioceptive fusion as a reliable pathway to safe, intuitive physical human-robot interaction.

</details>


### [135] [Self-Supervised Online Robot-Agnostic Traversability Estimation for Open-World Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28442)
*Julia Hindel,Simon Bultmann,Houman Masnavi,Daniele Cattaneo,Abhinav Valada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Self-supervised online traversability estimation with a robot-agnostic online terrain assessment guiding a visual network, memory-efficient continual learning, and cross-platform transfer, evaluated on a 50k-image dataset and released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable continual, label-free traversability learning in open-world robotics while being robot-agnostic and resource-efficient; address limitations of handcrafted scores and data-clustering approaches by combining online terrain assessment with a visual alignment loss; ensure memory efficiency via diversity-aware replay.

Method: A proprioceptive/inertial-based terrain assessment module producing traversability scores; an alignment loss to supervise a visual traversability network; diversity-aware feature selection for compact replay memory to mitigate forgetting; demonstrated cross-robot knowledge transfer.

Result: Dataset ~50k images from two robots across 11 terrains; navigation benchmarks in three outdoor environments; public release of dataset, code, and trained models.

Conclusion: COTRATE enables online, self-supervised continual learning of traversability representations with robot-agnostic transfer, achieving efficiency via feature selection and enabling real-world deployment; supports knowledge transfer across platforms.

Abstract: Self-supervised online traversability estimation enables robots to continuously learn from unlabeled open-world experiences and adapt their navigation behavior toward safe and efficient trajectories. Existing approaches either rely on handcrafted proprioceptive traversability scores, limiting robot-agnosticism, or cluster prior data, preventing online learning. Moreover, many continual learning methods incur substantial memory and computational costs, hindering onboard deployment. We introduce COTRATE, an online learning framework for continuous traversability estimation from multimodal, unlabeled robot experience. Our method first infers robust traversability scores using a robot-agnostic, learning-based online terrain assessment module operating on proprioceptiveand inertial signals. These scores then supervise a visual traversability network through a novel alignment loss that associates visual embeddings with online terrain assessments.To mitigate forgetting during continual learning with minimal overhead, we propose a diversity-aware feature selection strategythat preserves performance using a compact replay memory. We further show that the learned traversability representation supports knowledge transfer across different robot platforms with different locomotion kinematics. We evaluate COTRATE on a dataset of \approx 50,000 images collected with two robotic platforms across 11 outdoor terrains, and benchmark it on navigation tasks in three representative outdoor environments. We make the dataset, code, and trained models publicly available.

</details>


### [136] [A Digital Twin Framework for Virtual Visuo-Haptic Teleoperation of Complex-Shaped Optical Microrobots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28448)
*Zongcai Tan,Lan Wei,Dandan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A digital twin-based visuo-haptic teleoperation framework for complex-shaped optical microrobots, integrating image-based pose estimation, MSDM force modeling, and ROS-enabled teleoperation, enabling simulator-driven haptic feedback that improves task reliability in simulated cell-delivery tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of effective visuo-haptic teleoperation for complex-shaped optical microrobots in multi-trap manipulation, and provide a simulator to evaluate and optimize haptic strategies.

Method: A digital twin environment combining image-based pose/depth estimation, microrobot motion simulation, and model-based haptic rendering within a ROS-connected bimanual teleoperation setup. Force modeling merges Multi-Sphere Distributed Manipulation (MSDM) with optical-force estimation from the Optical Tweezers Toolbox, enabling simulator-driven visuo-haptic feedback.

Result: The framework reproduces representative microrobot motion trends and delivers haptic force rendering consistent with the fitted optical-force model. In simulated cell-delivery tasks, haptic feedback reduced the standard deviations of contact-force and microrobot-to-trap-center distance by 53.2% and 55.2%, respectively, and increased task success from 30% to 80%.

Conclusion: The framework is effective for evaluating visuo-haptic teleoperation strategies for complex-shaped OT-driven microrobots, offering a valuable tool for designing and assessing teleoperation controllers and haptic cues in realistic simulations.

Abstract: Optical tweezers (OT) provide piconewton-scale manipulation for delicate biomedical tasks, where visuo-haptic feedback can improve operator awareness by conveying interaction-force cues and trap-stability information. However, visuo-haptic teleoperation frameworks for complex-shaped optical microrobots remain underdeveloped, particularly in multi-trap manipulation scenarios. This paper presents a digital twin framework for virtual visuo-haptic teleoperation of complex-shaped OT-driven microrobots. The framework integrates a digital twin environment, image-based pose and depth estimation, microrobot motion simulation, and model-based haptic rendering within a Robot Operating System (ROS)-connected bimanual teleoperation system. For force modeling, we combine a Multi-Sphere Distributed Manipulation (MSDM) model with optical-force estimation from the Optical Tweezers Toolbox, enabling simulator-driven visuo-haptic feedback. The framework reproduces representative microrobot motion trends and provides haptic force rendering that is numerically consistent with the fitted optical-force model. In simulated cell-delivery tasks, haptic feedback reduced the standard deviations of the contact-force metric and the microrobot-to-trap-center distance metric by 53.2% and 55.2%, respectively, and improved task success from 30% to 80%. These results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness for evaluating visuo-haptic teleoperation strategies for complex-shaped optical microrobots.

</details>


### [137] [Learning a Kinodynamic Trajectory Manifold for Impact-Aware Compliant Catching of Fast-Moving Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28462)
*Guorui Pei,Mengshi Zhang,Xi Chen,Jinsong Wu,Jiaming Qi,Peng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RL-based learning of a low-dimensional kinodynamic trajectory manifold for fast catching of free-flying objects; runtime mapping to a reference trajectory with compliant tracking, avoiding online optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fast catching is challenging due to short reaction times, impact uncertainty, and kinodynamic constraints; a real-time, robust controller is needed for reliable capture.

Method: Train in simulation with reinforcement learning to collect successful catching trajectories and learn a low-dimensional kinodynamic trajectory manifold. At run time, map the estimated object initial state directly to a reference catching trajectory (no online nonlinear optimization). Track the trajectory with compliant control near contact to improve impact absorption and capture stability.

Result: The learned manifold enables direct reference trajectory execution at run time without online optimization, while compliant tracking enhances impact absorption and capture stability during contact.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning can yield practical fast-catching controllers by encoding kinodynamics into a compact trajectory manifold; runtime efficiency and robust contact handling arise from direct trajectory mapping and compliant tracking.

Abstract: Fast catching of free-flying objects is difficult because of short reaction time, impact uncertainty, and kinodynamic constraints. We use reinforcement learning in simulation to collect successful catching trajectories and learn a low-dimensional kinodynamic trajectory manifold. At run time, the estimated object initial state is mapped directly to a reference catching trajectory without online nonlinear optimization. The trajectory is tracked with compliant control near contact for improved impact absorption and capture stability.

</details>


### [138] [EIT-Pneumatic Hybrid Robotic Skin for Practical and Accurate Force Map Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28468)
*Junhwi Cho,Sunggyu Bae,Junghyeon Ma,Hyosang Lee,Jung Kim,Kyungseo Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hybrid EIT-pneumatic robotic skin enables accurate, scalable tactile sensing with improved force reconstruction and reduced sensitivity non-uniformity; 3D-printed, spray-coated; uses Tikhonov inverse with per-pad calibration; validated via indentation and humanoid chest-mounted integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electrical impedance tomography (EIT) offers large-area tactile sensing but suffers from sensitivity non-uniformity and ill-posed inverse problems. The work aims to create an affordable, scalable skin that preserves accurate force reconstruction.

Method: Fabrication via 3D printing and spray coating; Tikhonov-regularized inverse reconstruction; per-pad pneumatic calibration; validation through load-cell indentation experiments; chest-mounted integration on a humanoid robot; evaluation under multiple simultaneous contacts on a single pad.

Result: Achieves accurate force reconstruction within pads; reduces sensitivity non-uniformity (CV from 0.31 to 0.14) compared to EIT-only baseline; pneumatic signals are robust across diverse contact scenarios, including multiple simultaneous contacts; demonstrates a practical path toward scalable whole-body tactile sensing.

Conclusion: The hybrid EIT-pneumatic approach addresses EIT limitations, delivering accurate, scalable tactile sensing suitable for real robotic systems; affordable fabrication enables broader deployment and potential chest-body integration.

Abstract: We present a hybrid robotic skin that combines electrical impedance tomography (EIT) with pneumatic tactile sensing to improve force reconstruction capability. The developed robotic skin is fabricated entirely by 3D printing and spray coating, making it affordable and easy to build. A Tikhonov-regularized inverse reconstruction, paired with per-pad pneumatic calibration, enables accurate large-area tactile sensing with a simple measurement scheme. For validation, we conducted load-cell indentation experiments; the results showed consistent force reconstruction across locations within a pad. Compared with an EIT-only baseline, sensitivity non-uniformity was also reduced, with the coefficient of variation decreasing from 0.31 to 0.14, indicating that the proposed approach addresses a longstanding limitation of EIT. We further demonstrated chest-mounted integration on a humanoid robot and found that the pneumatic signals remained reliable across diverse contact scenarios, including multiple simultaneous contacts on the same sensing pad. These results indicate a practical path toward accurate, scalable whole-body tactile sensing in real robotic systems.

</details>


### [139] [Mag-VLA: Vision-Language-Action Model for Bimanual Magnetically Actuated Microrobot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28486)
*Yongchen Wang,Kangyi Lu,Lan Wei,Dandan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Mag-VLA: a vision-language-action framework for bimanual magnetic microrobot manipulation achieving high success rates via phase-aware, transformer-based decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Control of magnetically actuated microrobots is difficult due to indirect actuation, limited sensing, and nonlinear magnetic interactions; bimanual coordination offers capabilities but introduces coupled control challenges.

Method: Adapts Qwen2.5-VL-7B with LoRA for visual-language input; introduces motion-aware phase classifier and phase-conditioned ACT decoder; constructs a teleoperated dataset for three task configurations; conducts ablation studies.

Result: Real-robot experiments yield 90% approach success across tasks; transport success rates 80%, 70%, 50% with increasing difficulty; ablations show ACT decoder outperforms alternatives.

Conclusion: Hierarchical VLA modeling is a promising framework for dexterous magnetic microrobot manipulation, enabling coordinated multi-step control via phase-aware decoding and bimanual coordination.

Abstract: Magnetically actuated microrobots have been used as wireless, non-contact manipulation tools at microscales, making them promising for minimally invasive applications. However, their control remains challenging due to indirect actuation, limited sensing, and nonlinear magnetic interactions. In this work, we propose Mag-VLA, a vision-language-action (VLA) model for dexterous magnetic microrobot manipulation using two robotic arms with mounted magnets for dynamic magnetic-field construction. Bimanual coordination enables capabilities such as microrobot reorientation that are difficult or infeasible with a single arm, but it also introduces coupled control challenges, as the policy must generate coordinated trajectories for both actuators within a shared workspace. Our framework adapts a Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to process visual observations and language instructions for action prediction. To capture task progression, we introduce a motion-aware phase classifier and a phase-conditioned Action Chunking Transformer (ACT) decoder for temporally coherent multi-step control. We further construct a teleoperated magnetic microrobot manipulation dataset covering three task configurations. Ablation studies show that the ACT-based decoder substantially outperforms alternative generative action heads. In real-robot experiments, Mag-VLA achieves a 90% approach success rate across all tasks and transport success rates of 80%, 70%, and 50% as task difficulty increases. These results demonstrate that hierarchical VLA modeling provides a promising framework for magnetic microrobot manipulation.

</details>


### [140] [What Frozen VLAs Already Know About Success: A Probing Study of Value-Like Structure in Foundation Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28527)
*Jiachen Zhang,Junnan Nie,Junyi Lao,Wei Cheng,Chenghao Liu,Jiaxin Jiang,Songfang Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Frozen vision-language-action (VLA) policies trained by imitation encode evaluative signals (reward, progress, success) in their frozen representations, which can be read with linear probes to predict outcomes and improve action selection without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether imitation-trained VLA representations contain evaluative information that can guide behavior and be leveraged at test time without modifying the policy.

Method: Train lightweight linear probes on frozen features from OpenVLA, Pi0.5, DINOv2, and CLIP using mixed successful/failed LIBERO-Goal trajectories to recover Monte-Carlo outcome targets. Evaluate probes under same-task, same-timestep controls to rule out shortcuts. Use probe outputs as a test-time selector over sampled Pi0.5 action prefixes and compare against greedy decoding.

Result: Linear probes consistently predict Monte-Carlo outcomes from frozen features; Pi0.5 probes achieve ~92% pairwise ordering accuracy under controlled conditions; labels shuffled controls perform at chance. Test-time use of the probe increases success (e.g., push-plate: 26.7% → 44.3%), with additional positive case on wine-rack. Gains are not universal and require extra inference compute.

Conclusion: Frozen VLA representations encode success-related information beyond the imitation objective, and this information can be leveraged at test time to improve outcomes, albeit with context-dependent benefits and computational costs.

Abstract: Vision--language--action (VLA) policies are trained to imitate actions; their loss never asks them to estimate reward, progress, or future success. Their frozen representations nevertheless carry such information, and it can be read out and used to guide action choice without retraining the policy. From mixed successful and failed manipulation trajectories on LIBERO-Goal, we recover Monte-Carlo outcome targets using lightweight linear probes on frozen features. The targets are consistently predictable from OpenVLA, Pi0.5, DINOv2, and CLIP features, and substantially less so from baselines built on progress, time-to-go, task identity, or proprioception. To rule out task and temporal shortcuts, we evaluate the probes under same-task, same-timestep matched comparisons: Pi0.5 probes still reach roughly 92% pairwise ordering accuracy, while label-shuffled controls stay at chance. Used as a test-time selector over sampled Pi0.5 action prefixes, the same probe turns this offline finding into behavior: on push-plate, success rises from 26.7% under greedy decoding to 44.3%, with a second positive case on wine-rack. The gains are not universal and require additional inference compute, but the underlying finding is clean: frozen VLAs already encode information about success that their imitation objective never explicitly demands.

</details>


### [141] [SPRINT: Efficient Spectral Priors for Humanoid Athletic Sprints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28549)
*Yantong Wei,Kaihong Huang,Hainan Pan,Jiawei Luo,Jiawei Zhou,Ziyan Mai,Zhiwen Zeng,Yaonan Wang,Huimin Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SPRINT uses frequency-adaptive spectral priors derived from five reference locomotion sequences to generate kinematically feasible joint trajectories for humanoid sprinting, enabling extrapolation beyond the reference speeds and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on Unitree G1 to 6 m/s with natural gait transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of humanoid kinematic reference data and the instability of current sprinting frameworks, aiming for data-efficient, biomimetic, high-speed humanoid locomotion that can transfer from simulation to reality.

Method: Characterize human locomotion periodicity in the frequency domain with a reference library of five discrete motion sequences. Use frequency-adaptive spectral priors to generate joint trajectories across speeds, then guide a pretrained policy for zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on the Unitree G1.

Result: Priors enable extrapolation to speeds beyond the reference distribution; sim-to-real transfer achieved without task-specific fine-tuning, reaching 6 m/s in field experiments with seamless gait transitions and biomimetic naturalness.

Conclusion: Frequency-adaptive spectral priors provide a data-efficient foundation for humanoid athletic sprinting, enabling stable, biomimetic, high-speed locomotion and robust sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: The pursuit of humanoid athletic sprints is hindered by a scarcity of humanoid-viable kinematic reference data and the inability of existing frameworks to maintain stability during sprints. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SPRINT, a novel framework driven by efficient, frequency-adaptive spectral priors. By characterizing the fundamental periodicity of human locomotion in the frequency domain using a reference library of five discrete motion sequences, these priors generate kinematically feasible joint trajectories across a broad velocity spectrum, successfully extrapolating to speeds that exceed the reference distribution. Guided by these pretrained priors, the SPRINT policy achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer in field experiments on the Unitree G1 platform, reaching a peak sprinting velocity of 6 m/s and demonstrating seamless gait transitions while preserving biomimetic naturalness. Ultimately, this work establishes frequency-adaptive spectral priors as a highly data-efficient foundation for humanoid athletic sprints. The project page is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/w/SPRINT-138A/.

</details>


### [142] [SARAD: LLM-Based Safety-Aware Hybrid Reinforcement Learning with Collision Prediction for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28583)
*Kangyu Wu,Peng Cui,Guoxi Chen,Ya Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A safety-aware hybrid framework SARAD combines retrieval-augmented LLM-guided decisions with DRL, using an attention discriminator and a collision predictor, to improve safety and efficiency in autonomous driving, validated in Highway-Env.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DRL's unsafe random exploration and slow convergence, together with LLMs' real-time inference latency, motivate a hybrid approach that leverages LLM knowledge while maintaining DRL control.

Method: SARAD integrates retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) from a dynamic expert knowledge repository to guide DRL decisions, uses an attention discriminator to fuse LLM priors into DRL policy optimization, and includes a collision predictor module trained on historical collision data to enhance safety.

Result: Extensive experiments in the Highway-Env simulator show significant performance improvements, validating the effectiveness of SARAD in autonomous driving scenarios.

Conclusion: SARAD effectively balances safety and efficiency by hybridizing LLMs with DRL through RAG, attention-based integration, and a collision predictor, addressing exploration safety and latency concerns in autonomous driving.

Abstract: Ensuring both safety and efficiency in decision-making for autonomous driving systems remains a fundamental challenge. Traditional Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) suffers from unsafe random exploration and slow convergence, while Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate inherent latency in real-time inference operations. To address these limitations, this paper proposes SARAD, a novel safety-aware hybrid framework that synergizes LLMs and DRL for autonomous driving. SARAD substitutes the random exploration of DRL with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-enhanced, LLM-guided decisions sourced from a dynamic expert knowledge repository. An attention discriminator is proposed to integrate the prior knowledge of LLMs into DRL policy optimization. A collision predictor module, fine-tuned with historical collision data, is further designed to improve vehicle safety. Extensive experiments show that SARAD achieves significant performance improvements in the Highway-Env simulator, validating the effectiveness of the proposed model in autonomous driving.

</details>


### [143] [PrimitiveVLA: Learning Reusable Motion Primitives for Efficient and Generalizable Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28634)
*Yutai Li,Shaohui Peng,Jiaming Guo,Di Huang,Zihao Zhang,Yuxuan Guo,Yunkai Gao,Siming Lan,Ling Li,Xing Hu,Yunji Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PrimitiveVLA introduces a primitive-centric Vision-Language-Action framework that disassembles demonstrations into reusable motion primitives via automated disassembly and assembles them at inference time using a VLM planner and an LLM-driven switch, improving data efficiency and zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  tackles data inefficiency and poor generalization in VLA caused by Direct Instruction-to-Control Mapping that memorizes whole trajectories instead of reusable primitives.

Method: Two-phase approach: (1) Fine-tuning-phase Disassembly with an automated pipeline to decompose demonstrations into primitives and learn a shared Multimodal Canonical Representation (MCR); (2) Inference-phase Assembly using a VLM-based planner and an LLM-generated switch module for robust closed-loop execution; MCR unifies both phases.

Result: Extensive experiments show improved data efficiency and superior zero-shot generalization to unseen and long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: Shifts from memorizing task-specific trajectories to learning invariant motion primitives; the primitive-centric disassemble-and-assemble paradigm with MCR enables more data-efficient and generalizable VLA policies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a promising paradigm for generalist robotic policies, yet their adaptation is hindered by data inefficiency and poor generalization. We argue that these bottlenecks stem from the prevailing Direct Instruction-to-Control Mapping, which forces models to memorize monolithic trajectories rather than reusable motion patterns, i.e., primitives. We propose PrimitiveVLA, a framework that shifts this paradigm toward a Primitive-Centric Disassemble & Assemble paradigm. Supported by a shared Multimodal Canonical Representation (MCR), PrimitiveVLA unifies two phases: (1) Fine-tuning-phase Disassembly, which uses an automated pipeline to disassemble demonstrations into reusable primitives; and (2) Inference-phase Assembly, which employs a VLM-based planner and an LLM-generated switch module for robust closed-loop execution. By disassembling tasks into reusable primitives, PrimitiveVLA enables VLA models to learn invariant motion patterns instead of task-specific trajectories. Extensive experiments show that our framework improves data efficiency and achieves superior zero-shot generalization across unseen and long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [144] [Integrated Exploration-Aware UAV Route Optimization and Path Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28654)
*Jimin Choi,Grant Stagg,Cameron K. Peterson,Max Z. Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An integrated, online replanning framework for UAV hazard monitoring that blends ROIs-based routing with exploration-focused trajectory planning under evolving uncertainty. It uses a risk map belief, pseudo-nodes for coverage, and dynamic reallocation of budget; online updates via grid-based belief maps lead to replanning. In 48 scenarios, online replanning yields KL divergence improvements of 15.9% vs offline and 48.6% vs straight-line traversal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In hazardous exploration, prior hazard information is incomplete, imprecise, and evolves during mission. UAV must balance visiting reported hazards with gathering new information under limited flight endurance.

Method: Solve a vehicle routing problem over reported ROIs, augment route with pseudo-nodes to improve spatial coverage, allocate remaining budget across route segments, optimize dynamically feasible B-spline trajectories for local exploration; online measurements update a grid-based belief map and trigger replanning when justified.

Result: Online replanning improved average KL reduction by 15.9% relative to offline optimized planner and 48.6% relative to straight-line traversal across 48 scenarios.

Conclusion: Integrated exploration-aware routing with dynamic replanning significantly improves information-theoretic gains in hazard monitoring tasks under uncertainty.

Abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used for exploration-driven monitoring in hazardous environments such as disaster zones, contaminated sites, wildfire areas, and damaged infrastructure, where limited flight endurance must be allocated between visiting reported locations and gathering new information. In these settings, prior information regarding hazards is often incomplete, spatially imprecise, and subject to change during execution. For example, initial reports may identify a region where a hazard is likely to exist, but the actual hazard may be displaced, partially observed, or entirely unreported. We present an integrated exploration-aware UAV route optimization and path planning framework for hazard monitoring under uncertain and evolving prior information. The environment is represented as a spatial risk map, where each location has an associated belief of hazardous conditions. Reported hazards are modeled as uncertain regions of interest (ROIs) rather than confirmed target locations, requiring the UAV to inspect reported areas while also using its limited flight endurance to explore informative regions. The proposed method solves a vehicle routing problem over reported ROIs, augments the route with auxiliary pseudo-nodes to improve spatial coverage, allocates the remaining flight distance budget across route segments, and optimizes dynamically feasible B-spline trajectories for local exploration. During execution, UAV measurements update a grid-based belief map, and the remaining trajectory is replanned when new information and the remaining budget justify adaptation. Across 48 scenario configurations, online replanning improves average KL reduction by 15.9% over the offline optimized planner and 48.6% over straight-line traversal.

</details>


### [145] [How VLAs Fail Differently: Black-Box Action Monitoring Reveals Architecture-Specific Failure Signatures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28726)
*Krishnam Gupta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLA architectures exhibit architecture-specific motor-command failures; a universal monitor is ineffective. Direction reversal predicts failures across all architectures; jerk monitoring is only useful for discrete-token VLAs, while velocity-based cues are non-predictive for continuous families. Architecture-matched monitors (via SafeContract) are essential for safe deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how different velocity-based learning architectures fail at the motor-command level, and whether a single monitoring strategy suffices across architectures or if monitoring must be architecture-tailored.

Method: Evaluate three architectures (VQ-BeT, Diffusion Policy, ACT) on identical evaluation protocols (n=450 episodes across PushT and ALOHA 14-DOF bimanual manipulation). Monitor failures via metrics: direction reversal rate, jerk violations, and velocity violations; compute AUROC as predictor of failure; compare across architectures; employ SafeContract for training-free, black-box action monitoring with conformal calibration; provide code at GitHub repository.

Result: Findings: (1) direction reversal rate is a universal failure predictor across all architectures (AUROC=0.93, 0.79, 0.91; p<0.001). (2) jerk monitoring predictive only for discrete-token architectures (AUROC 0.88, 0.69, 0.41). (3) velocity violations non-predictive (AUROC 0.41-0.69), yet velocity checking is common in deployment code. (4) for continuous-family VLAs, velocity monitoring provides almost no predictive signal (AUROC=0.52 on ACT, 0.41 on Diffusion). Conclusion: architecture-matched monitors are necessary; results demonstrate discrete/continuous VLA distinction in failure signatures; SafeContract enables training-free monitoring with conformal calibration.

Conclusion: The study advocates architecture-aware monitoring for VLAs, as no single monitor suffices. It also highlights SafeContract as a practical tool for deploy-time validation. The findings are task- and protocol-specific, suggesting broader validation across tasks and more nuanced definitions of failure modes.

Abstract: We discover that VLA architectures fail in fundamentally different, predictable ways at the motor-command level. Running VQ-BeT, Diffusion Policy, and ACT on identical evaluation protocols (n=450 episodes across PushT and ALOHA 14-DOF bimanual manipulation), we find: (1) direction reversal rate is a universal failure predictor across all three architectures (AUROC=0.93, 0.79, 0.91; p<0.001); (2) jerk monitoring is predictive only for discrete-token architectures, following a discrete-to-continuous gradient (0.88, 0.69, 0.41); (3) velocity violations alone are non-predictive everywhere (AUROC 0.41-0.69), yet velocity checking is the most common safety mechanism in VLA deployment code; and (4) for continuous-family VLAs, velocity monitoring provides effectively zero predictive signal (AUROC=0.52 on ACT, 0.41 on Diffusion), proving that architecture-matched monitor selection is essential. These results quantify a monitoring consequence of the well-known discrete/continuous VLA distinction: the two families produce qualitatively different failure signatures that require different monitors. No single monitor works universally; architecture-matched selection is required. This finding was enabled by SafeContract, a training-free, black-box action monitoring toolkit with conformal calibration. Code: https://github.com/krishnam94/vla-edge

</details>


### [146] [Imitation Learning for Robot Assistance in Open Surgery: A Multi-Policy Evaluation on Suture Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28736)
*Xucheng Wang,Zhizhou Yang,Xiaoman Zhang,Sung Eun Kim,Romain Hardy,Pranav Rajpurkar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A comprehensive benchmarking of four general-purpose imitation learning policies (ACT, Diffusion Policy, SmolVLA, π0) for surgeon-robot collaborative suturing in open surgery using 160 teleoperated demonstrations across 28 models and 32 configurations; π0 with a vision-language backbone delivers strongest performance, achieving 92% stitch completion in a trial, while depth perception remains the main failure mode.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the feasibility and data efficiency of general-purpose imitation learning for surgeon-robot collaborative assistance in open surgery, focusing on suture following, and to compare diverse architectures under varied data conditions.

Method: Collect 160 teleoperated demonstrations on an open-source robot arm; benchmark four architecture families (ACT, Diffusion Policy, SmolVLA, π0) across 28 trained models evaluated in 32 configurations along dataset size, camera viewpoint, and background variation; assess task success and depth error; deploy π0 in a surgeon-robot suturing trial.

Result: Under ideal conditions, the four policies achieve 50–75% task success with depth error as the dominant failure mode. π0 achieves the strongest results with data efficiency, robustness to background variation, and smoother trajectories. In a suturing trial, π0 yields a 92% stitch completion rate.

Conclusion: Open-surgery collaborative robotic assistance is a feasible target for imitation learning. Depth perception and end-effector design are key priorities for clinical translation; pretrained vision-language backbones offer substantial data efficiency and robustness improvements, especially under background variation.

Abstract: This study presents the first evaluation of general-purpose imitation learning for surgeon-robot collaborative assistance in open surgery, targeting suture following: the grab-pull-release motion an assistant performs at every stitch. We collect 160 teleoperated demonstrations (32,374 frames) on an open-source robot arm, benchmark four architecturally diverse imitation learning policies (ACT, Diffusion Policy, SmolVLA, $π_0$) across 28 trained models evaluated in 32 configurations along three clinically motivated dimensions: dataset size, camera viewpoint, and background variation. Our results demonstrate that under ideal conditions, the four policies achieve $50$-$75\%$ task success, with depth error as the dominant failure mode across all architectures. Among all policies, $π_0$ achieves the strongest results with a pretrained vision-language backbone, demonstrating superior data efficiency, greater robustness to background variation, and smoother trajectories compatible with surgical workflow. When deployed in a surgeon-robot suturing trial, $π_0$ yields a $92\%$ stitch completion rate. These findings establish collaborative robotic assistance in open surgery as a feasible target for imitation learning and highlight depth perception and end-effector design as key priorities for clinical translation.

</details>


### [147] [Beyond Binary: Sim-to-Real Dexterous Manipulation with Physics-Grounded Contact Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28812)
*Jiahe Pan,Stelian Coros,Jitendra Malik,Toru Lin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Physics-grounded Center-of-Pressure (CoP) tactile representation enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for contact-rich manipulation, outperforming coarse and raw-tactile baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data collection for contact-rich manipulation is bottlenecked; the sim-to-real gap limits effective use of rich tactile data, leading to reliance on coarse low-dimensional features.

Method: Introduce CoP as a tactile representation grounded in physical principles. Develop a differentiable dynamics-based sensor calibration to estimate taxel orientations without ground-truth force measurements. Evaluate on two blind, contact-rich tasks (peg-in-hole insertion and ball balancing) using a multi-fingered hand; policies conditioned on CoP.

Result: CoP-enabled policies achieve zero-shot sim-to-real transfer and outperform binary-contact and raw-tacile baselines across both tasks. Analysis suggests CoP captures task-relevant physical properties (e.g., object mass) as an emergent byproduct of control.

Conclusion: A physics-based tactile representation (CoP), together with differentiable calibration, enables robust sim-to-real transfer for tactile-rich manipulation and yields richer, transferable state representations that encode physical properties relevant to the task.

Abstract: A primary bottleneck in contact-rich manipulation is the difficulty of collecting real-world data. Sim-to-real reinforcement learning offers a scalable alternative, but the simulation-reality gap prevents information-dense modalities like touch from being effectively used. Existing sim-to-real methods often mitigate this gap by simplifying tactile data into coarse low-dimensional features -- sacrificing the richness required for complex manipulation. In this work, we introduce Center-of-Pressure (CoP), an effective tactile representation grounded in physical principles that preserves dense contact information while maintaining robustness for sim-to-real transfer. To support this representation, we propose a sensor calibration scheme based on differentiable dynamics, enabling the estimation of taxel orientations without requiring ground-truth force measurements. We evaluate CoP on two blind, challenging contact-rich manipulation tasks: peg-in-hole insertion and ball balancing. Across both tasks, policies conditioned on CoP achieve zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a multi-fingered hand, and outperform both coarse binary-contact and raw-taxel baselines. Analysis of learned policy states further suggests that CoP-conditioned policies encode task-relevant physical properties, such as object mass, as an emergent byproduct of control.

</details>


### [148] [Turning Video Models into Generalist Robot Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27817)
*Sizhe Lester Li,Evan Kim,Xingjian Bai,Tong Zhao,Tao Pang,Max Simchowitz,Vincent Sitzmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decoupled video-to-action framework (VERA) pairs an action-free video world model with embodiment-specific inverse dynamics models, enabling cross-embodiment zero-shot control with data-efficient training and reusable video planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome tight coupling between video planning and action prediction, enabling embodiment-agnostic planning, modular interchangeability of video models, and easy data collection via self-play while achieving cross-embodiment generalization.

Method: We propose Video-to-Embodied Robot Action Model (VERA): an action-free video planner paired with an embodiment-specific IDM derived from the robot Jacobian to map video latent dynamics to actions, forming a closed-loop policy. The video planner is kept embodiment-agnostic; different IDMs can be plugged in for different embodiments.

Result: VERA achieves strong performance in both simulation and real hardware, including zero-shot Panda arm manipulation and 16-DoF Allegro-hand dexterous cube re-orientation. The same video planner can be reused across embodiments by pairing with different IDMs; the IDM design is data-efficient and scalable to high-dimensional action spaces.

Conclusion: Decoupled video planning plus faithful video-to-action translation is a viable route to zero-shot, cross-embodiment, and generalizable robot control, enabling cross-embodiment reuse of video planners with embodiment-specific IDMs.

Abstract: Video generative models have emerged as a promising robotics backbone, capable of generating videos that depict the completion of complex tasks across embodiments and environments. Recent work proposes robot foundation models that jointly predict future observations and actions by finetuning video models with action-labeled data. In this paper, we test the limits of an alternative approach: leave the video planner as-is while training an embodiment-specific inverse dynamics model (IDM). This decoupling offers several natural benefits: the video planner remains embodiment-agnostic, different video models can be interchanged easily without re-training the IDM, and the IDM can be independently trained with readily available self-play data. We present a closed-loop, video-to-action policy that combines an action-free video world model with a carefully-designed IDM based on the robot embodiment Jacobian. We demonstrate that our IDM design is both data-efficient and scalable to high-dimensional action spaces. Our policy, which we coin the Video-to-Embodied Robot Action Model (VERA), achieves strong performance across simulated and real-world benchmarks, including zero-shot Panda arm manipulation and 16-DoF Allegro-hand dexterous cube re-orientation. The same video planner can be used across multiple embodiments by pairing it with different embodiment-specific IDMs. Our results show that decoupled video planning plus faithful video-to-action translation is a viable alternative route towards zero-shot, cross-embodiment, and generalizable robot control. More results are available on our project website: https://vera.csail.mit.edu.

</details>


### [149] [Trinity: Unifying Class-Agnostic Terrain and Semantic Segmentation for Unstructured Outdoor Environments by Leveraging Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27644)
*Marcus G Müller,Wout Boerdijk,Maximilian Durner,Riccardo Giubilato,Abel Gawel,Wolfgang Stürzl,Roland Siegwart,Rudolph Triebel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A transformer-based Trinity model jointly learns class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation to enable robot-agnostic terrain priors; supported by new synthetic (RUGDSynth) and real (EXTerra) datasets; demonstrations show feasibility and transferability; code/datasets to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-based traversability methods depend on robot-specific annotations or predefined semantic mappings, hindering transfer across platforms and requiring costly re-annotation when robot capabilities change. Traditional semantic segmentation targets predefined classes and misses terrain variety; there is a need for robot-agnostic, appearance-driven terrain priors.

Method: A transformer-based architecture named Trinity that jointly performs class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation within a single network. The approach leverages appearance-based terrain cues to learn robot-agnostic priors. To enable large-scale training, the OAISYS simulator is extended and augmented with RUGDSynth, a synthetic dataset with class-agnostic terrain samples. Real-world data is provided by the EXTerra Dataset with annotations for both class-specific and class-agnostic terrain labels.

Result: Experiments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed task and the effectiveness of the joint segmentation approach in complex outdoor environments; the learned robot-agnostic terrain priors can be combined with robot-specific experience for downstream tasks such as traversability estimation, visual odometry, and mission planning.

Conclusion: The Trinity framework offers a unified way to decouple terrain appearance from semantic class labels, enabling transferability across robots and tasks. The introduced synthetic and real datasets validate the approach, and code/datasets will be released to support further research.

Abstract: Terrain understanding is fundamental for mobile robots operating in unstructured outdoor environments. Existing vision-based traversability estimation methods rely on robot-specific annotations or semantic class mappings, limiting transferability across platforms and requiring costly re-annotation when robot capabilities change, while standard semantic segmentation methods only focus on specific predefined classes, which do not capture the variety of terrains. In this work, we propose a transformer-based architecture that jointly performs class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation within a unified network, called Trinity. Terrain regions are segmented based solely on visual appearance, without predefined semantic labels or robot-dependent traversability scores. This formulation enables the learning of robot-agnostic visual terrain priors that can be combined with robot-specific experience for downstream tasks such as traversability estimation, visual odometry, and mission planning. To enable large-scale training with diverse terrain appearances, we extend the OAISYS simulator and introduce RUGDSynth, a synthetic dataset inspired by RUGD with class-agnostic terrain samples. Furthermore, we present the EXTerra Dataset, providing real-world images annotated with both class-specific and class-agnostic terrain labels. Experiments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed task and the effectiveness of our joint segmentation approach in complex outdoor environments. Code and datasets will be released with this publication (after review).

</details>


### [150] [Frequency-Guided Action Diffusion via Sub-Frequency Manifold Traversal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27919)
*Junlin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A frequency-guided diffusion policy method (FGO) reduces high-frequency artifacts from human demonstrations by moving samples through staged sub-frequency bands, improving action smoothness and temporal consistency while preserving task-relevant details, validated on 15 robotic manipulation tasks across 5 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Natural human demonstrations contain high-frequency noise (jerks, pauses, jitter) that degrades imitation learning, and diffusion-based policies can amplify these artifacts during denoising. A spectral control mechanism is needed to suppress noise without sacrificing fine-grained task details.

Method: Frequency Guidance Operator (FGO): a frequency-based conditioning that steers diffusion policy generation by progressively guiding noisy samples through intermediate sub-frequency manifolds with expanding spectral bands, effectively smoothing actions during generation.

Result: Empirically, FGO yields superior action smoothness and temporal consistency while preserving necessary task-level details across 15 robotic manipulation tasks from 5 benchmarks.

Conclusion: Spectral manipulation via FGO can effectively mitigate high-frequency artifacts in diffusion-based imitation learning, enhancing robustness and smoothness without compromising task performance across diverse manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Learning visuomotor policies via behavior cloning typically involves mimicking expert demonstrations collected by human operators. However, natural human demonstrations inherently contain high-frequency noise, such as intermittent jerks, pauses, and action jitter. Training policies to directly imitate these raw trajectories inevitably causes the model to inherit these suboptimal behaviors. This pathology is particularly pronounced in diffusion-based policies, where iterative denoising steps can inadvertently amplify high-frequency artifacts at the expense of meaningful fine-grained details. To address these limitations, we present a novel frequency-based algorithm that enables implicit spectral maneuvering and smooth action generation. Our method, Frequency Guidance Operator (FGO), steers the generation process of diffusion polices by progressively driving the noisy samples through intermediate sub-frequency manifolds with expanding spectral bands. Validated on 15 robotic manipulation tasks from 5 benchmarks, FGO achieves superior performance in enhancing action smoothness and temporal consistency while preserving the details necessary for successful task execution. Project website: https://henrywjl.github.io/frequency-guidance-operator/

</details>


### [151] [HumanoidMimicGen: Data Generation for Loco-Manipulation via Whole-Body Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27724)
*Kevin Lin,Ajay Mandlekar,Caelan Reed Garrett,Nikita Chernyadev,Yu Fang,Runyu Ding,Yuqi Xie,Justin Tran,Linxi Fan,Yuke Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable data-generation framework, HumanoidMimicGen, synthesizes large, stable, collision-free loco-manipulation data for humanoids by adapting few source demonstrations and interleaving whole-body skills with locomotion planning, enabling improved imitation-learning policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning for humanoid robots is hampered by the need for many demonstrations and the high-dimensional, composite action space of arms, legs, and torso, making teleoperation time-consuming and data-collection-intensive.

Method: HumanoidMimicGen adapts contact-rich whole-body skills from a small set of source demonstrations to new states and object poses, interleaving single- and dual-arm skills with whole-body locomotion/manipulation planning to generate diverse, stable, collision-free data across scenes and layouts; evaluation on a new simulated loco-manipulation benchmark with nine tasks.

Result: The method enables automatic generation of large synthetic datasets for imitation learning; joint-training (co-training) of whole-body visuomotor policies with data from HumanoidMimicGen yields up to 20% improvement over policies trained solely on real-world data.

Conclusion: Synthetic, whole-body data-generation with HumanoidMimicGen is effective for humanoid loco-manipulation imitation learning, enabling scalable data generation, systematic analysis of data-generation decisions, and improved policy performance.

Abstract: Imitation learning is a promising approach for training humanoid robots to both walk and manipulate, but it requires a large number of demonstrations, which are time-intensive and difficult to collect via teleoperation. Existing data-generation algorithms can automatically synthesize demonstrations for manipulators, but they are ineffective on humanoids because their high-dimensional composite action spaces involve arms, legs, and torsos. We present HumanoidMimicGen, a method for generating humanoid legged loco-manipulation data. Our method adapts contact-rich whole-body skills from a handful of source demonstrations to new states, generalizing across changes in object pose. By interleaving these single- and dual-arm skills with whole-body locomotion and manipulation planning, the method generates stable, collision-free data across diverse scenes and layouts. To evaluate our approach, we introduce a new simulated loco-manipulation benchmark containing nine diverse tasks that test humanoid loco-manipulation capabilities. There, we demonstrate that HumanoidMimicGen automatically generates large datasets for imitation learning and enables a systematic study of how data generation and policy learning decisions impact model performance. We show that whole-body visuomotor policies co-trained with data generated by HumanoidMimicGen outperform those trained only on real-world data by 20%.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [152] [Identifying and Understanding Human Values in Text: A Tailorable LLM-based Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27373)
*Eduardo de la Cruz Fernández,Marcelo Karanik,Sascha Ossowski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A modular LLM-based pipeline to detect and quantify human values in text, using value specifications derived from theories, evaluated on ValueEval with good performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Align autonomous decision-making with human values by moving beyond pure utility maximisation; develop scalable, theory-driven value-detection that reduces prompt engineering.

Method: Three-module architecture: (1) generate structured value specifications from foundational theory texts; (2) label texts using these specifications; (3) assign graded support/resistance based on rhetorical/semantic evidence; instantiated with multiple LLMs; theory-agnostic, scalable and reproducible.

Result: Evaluated on the ValueEval dataset; experiments show good detection performance and demonstrate the pipeline's generality and applicability across theories.

Conclusion: The architecture separates conceptualisation from detection, enabling scalable, reproducible detection of human values across theories and providing a flexible framework for value-aligned analysis in autonomous systems.

Abstract: As intelligent systems become more autonomous, the scientific community focuses on creating decision-making mechanisms that include ethical and moral considerations, unlike traditional utility-maximisation models. To achieve this, a key aspect is assessing how well these decisions align with human values. To this end, a promising line of research is centred on developing approaches based on Large Language Models (LLMs) to identify human values from text, whether explicit or implicit, enabling their recognition throughout. This paper introduces a LLM-based architecture to detect and quantify the intensity of human values in text, avoiding the limitations of previous approaches tied to specific value theory or complex prompt engineering. The architecture comprises three coordinated modules: one that generates structured value specifications from the foundational texts of any theoretical framework; one that labels texts using these specifications; and one that assigns graded support or resistance based on rhetorical and semantic evidence. This modular approach separates the tasks of conceptualising from detecting human values, creating a scalable and reproducible process driven by value specifications adaptable to various theories. The architecture was instantiated with multiple LLMs and evaluated using the ValueEval dataset. The experiments demonstrate good detection performance, confirming the generality of the pipeline.

</details>


### [153] [Soro: A Lightweight Foundation Model and Chatbot for Tajik](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27379)
*Stanislav Liashkov,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde,Azizjon Azimi,Khushbakht Shaymardonov,Shuhratjon Khalitbekov,Bonu Boboeva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tajik-specialized LLM Soro built from Gemma 3 via Tajik-only continual pretraining and instruction tuning, with new Tajik benchmarks, quantization for edge deployment; shows strong Tajik performance and preserved English ability; open benchmarks and education deployment in Tajikistan.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address low-resource Tajik language in real-world deployments under tight compute/connectivity; create robust evaluation benchmarks for Tajik.

Method: Continual pretraining on 1.9B-token Tajik corpus; supervised instruction tuning on 40k Tajik teacher-style examples; create/open-source Tajik benchmarks; apply FP8/INT4 quantization; edge deployment pilot.

Result: Soro outperforms same-size Gemma 3 baselines on Tajik benchmarks; maintains strong English performance; quantization preserves Tajik gains; supports education-sector pilot.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of resource-constrained, high-utility Tajik LLMs; open benchmarks enable broader evaluation; potential for scale-out in Tajikistan.

Abstract: We present Soro, a family of Tajik-specialized conversational large language models (LLMs) designed for real-world deployment under tight compute and connectivity constraints in Tajikistan. Starting from open-weight Gemma 3 checkpoints, we perform Tajik-only continual pretraining on a curated 1.9-billion-token corpus spanning filtered web text, PDF documents, and curriculum-aligned educational materials, followed by supervised instruction tuning on 40K Tajik teacher-style examples. To enable rigorous evaluation despite the limited coverage of Tajik in standard benchmarks, we introduce a suite of Tajik benchmarks covering general knowledge, linguistic competence, and school- and university entrance-exam domains, and we open-source them on Hugging Face. Across these Tajik benchmarks, Soro substantially outperforms same-size Gemma 3 baselines while retaining strong English performance on standard datasets. We further show that FP8 and INT4 quantization of Soro preserves most Tajik-language gains while reducing memory requirements for edge deployment, supporting an ongoing education-sector pilot and planned scale-out across schools in Tajikistan.

</details>


### [154] [On the Origin of Synthetic Information by Means of Steganographic Inheritance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27551)
*Ching-Chun Chang,Isao Echizen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a steganography-based, heredity-like framework to trace the lineage of synthetic information, combining theory and empirical validation across various projectors and stegosystems to enable phylogenetic inference in AI-generated offspring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the provenance and trust challenges of increasingly capable AI-generated content by establishing a traceable lineage mechanism akin to heredity, with broad implications for truth, accountability, and governance.

Method: Introduce a projector- and stegosystem-based scheme where, at reproduction, a trait from the parent is steganographically embedded in the offspring. A decoder extracts the trait to infer parentage against a reference pool. Provide theoretical analysis of phylogenetic accuracy as a function of projector and stegosystem properties, and perform empirical evaluations across multiple projectors and stegosystems under various processing operations and semantic modifications.

Result: Theoretical results characterize how phylogenetic accuracy depends on system properties; empirical results across multiple projectors/stegosystems demonstrate viability of accurate parentage inference despite common processing and semantic changes.

Conclusion: A cyber ecosystem with hidden yet traceable lineage traits for synthetic information is plausible, enabling continued evolution with provenance-aware governance. Further work is needed to refine metrics, address robustness, and consider ethical/privacy implications.

Abstract: The origin of species has been the mystery of mysteries in natural science. By analogy, the origin of synthetic information, we suggest, is the mystery of mysteries in information science. The question carries a moral weight that a technical account can neither fully resolve nor responsibly ignore, as its impact on truth, trust, and human intellect extends deep into the broader economy and society. The very power of artificial intelligence makes the evolutionary lineage of synthetic information grow ever harder to trace, for a sufficiently capable model may generate offspring that bear little resemblance, at either the structural or signal level, to the parent source from which they were derived. As in genetics, two individuals may share the same phenotype mirroring each other in outward appearance, yet differ fundamentally in their genotype. We propose, by means of steganography, a mechanism analogous to heredity. At the moment an offspring is reproduced, a projector derives a trait from the parent, and a steganographic encoder invisibly hides it within the offspring. This trait persists throughout the offspring's life cycle in a cyber ecosystem. When parentage is queried, a steganographic decoder extracts the trait from the offspring and compares it against the traits of candidate parents in a reference pool, thereby nominating the most likely one. A theoretical analysis characterises phylogenetic accuracy as a function of projector and stegosystem properties, whilst empirical evaluations across multiple projectors and stegosystems demonstrate the viability of the proposed methodology under a broad spectrum of processing operations and semantic modifications. We envision a cyber ecosystem in which synthetic information, endowed with hidden yet traceable lineage traits, branches from a simple beginning into endless forms that have been, and are being, evolved.

</details>


### [155] [RULER: Representation-Level Verification of Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27569)
*Georgina Cosma,Axel Finke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Representation-level verification reveals residual memorization in unlearning methods that pass output-level checks; M2 detects where forget-set records occupy similar representations to a retrained model, while M4 detects residuals without retraining; across domains, several methods fail to fully erase signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unlearning verification focuses on output-level metrics (membership, accuracy, forget-set accuracy) but can miss retained information in intermediate representations, risking leakage of forgotten data.
