*Yunsheng Zeng,Gen Li,Yuwei Miao,Xiandong Li,Yujin Wang,Siyu Chen,Luning Wang,Yunhao Qiao,Junfeng Wang,Jianwei Lv,Bo Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EAPO is an entropy-driven adaptive policy optimization method for RL-based open-ended QA, which reveals that negative samples mainly drive diversity and upper-bound performance while positive samples govern response quality and convergence; it adaptively weights positive samples using policy-entropy ratios to balance exploration and stability, improving diversity and stability on medical QA datasets compared to fixed-weight baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how positive and negative samples affect RL-based open-ended QA and to overcome limitations of fixed-weight reward schemes that hinder generalization and diversity.

Method: Introduce a reward-mean-based strategy to separate positive/negative samples. Propose EAPO, which adaptively adjusts the weight of positive samples using the ratio of current policy entropy to initial entropy. During entropy-decreasing phases, down-weight positives to preserve exploration; during entropy-increasing phases, up-weight positives to reinforce stability and prevent entropy collapse.

Result: EAPO consistently outperforms fixed-weight baselines on two open-ended medical QA datasets, improving response diversity and stability.

Conclusion: Adaptive, entropy-aware weighting for positive samples enhances RL-based open-ended QA by balancing exploration, diversity, and quality; negative samples set a diversity upper bound, while positive samples influence quality and convergence stability.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models are typically trained via reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR). However, existing approaches adopt fixed weights for positive and negative samples, and the conclusions hardly generalize to open-ended question answering (QA). In this paper, we systematically investigate the roles of positive and negative samples in reinforcement learning for open-ended QA. We propose a reward-mean-based strategy for distinguishing positive from negative samples, and observe that negative samples predominantly govern response diversity and the performance upper bound, whereas positive samples primarily determine response quality and convergence stability. Building on these observations, we propose EAPO, an Entropy-driven Adaptive Policy Optimization method that adaptively computes the weighting coefficients of positive samples based on the ratio of the current policy entropy to the initial entropy. During the entropy-decreasing phase, the weight assigned to positive samples is reduced to preserve exploration, whereas during the entropy-increasing phase it is amplified to reinforce stability, thereby mitigating entropy collapse. Experiments on two publicly available open-ended medical QA datasets demonstrate that EAPO consistently and substantially outperforms fixed-weight baselines in both response diversity and stability.

</details>


### [182] [When Context Flips, Safety Breaks: Diagnosing Brittle Safety in Aligned Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27851)
*Dasol Choi,Alex Kwon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Safety benchmark scores can misrepresent deployment readiness due to brittle safety, where context updates flip safe actions into harmful outcomes. Context-flip evaluation across 12 models and safety/commonsense tasks reveals a safety-commonsense gap, model-specific brittle behavior, and policy overrides as the failure mechanism. A state-aware validator detects all catastrophic flips, outperforming action-level guardrails. Protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To diagnose and quantify brittleness in safety policies of aligned language models beyond static benchmark accuracy, by testing how contextual updates change what is considered safe.

Method: Evaluate 12 models on PacifAIst safety benchmark plus two commonsense controls using paired variants where nominally safe actions cause harm. Measure brittleness as the gap between safety and commonsense judgments. Analyze failure mechanisms (prevalent policy override vs miscomprehension) and examine different update types and model families. Hand-audit catastrophic consequence-flip scenarios comparing action-level guardrails to a state-aware validator.

Result: All 12 models show safety-commonsense brittleness (mean +17.4 percentage points). Baseline accuracy poorly predicts brittleness (models with >90% baseline accuracy exhibit brittleness from 13.7% to 90.0%). Failures arise from policy override rather than miscomprehension, with three distinct mechanisms varying by update type and model family. A hand-audited probe shows action-level guardrails catch none of catastrophic flips, while a state-aware validator detects all without false alarms on correct interventions. Indicates need for state-aware architectural approaches. Protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe released.

Conclusion: Action-level content moderation is insufficient for consequence-awareness. Context-flip evaluation reveals deployment risks not captured by standard benchmarks. Recommend state-aware architectures and deployment probes; provide publicly available protocol and datasets to facilitate evaluation.

Abstract: Safety benchmark scores provide incomplete evidence of deployment readiness: aligned language models often adhere to rigid rules even when a situational update flips which action is safe. We term this failure brittle safety. To diagnose it, we introduce context-flip evaluation, testing 12 models across a safety benchmark (PacifAIst) and two commonsense controls using paired variants where the nominally safe action produces harm. Three findings emerge. First, brittle safety is safety-specific: all 12 models exhibit a safety-commonsense gap (mean +17.4 pp). Baseline accuracy fails to predict brittleness: among models above 90% baseline accuracy, brittleness rates range from 13.7% to 90.0%. Second, failures stem from policy override rather than miscomprehension: despite acknowledging the context change in every case, models persist via three distinct mechanisms that vary by update type and model family. Third, on a hand-audited probe of catastrophic consequence-flip scenarios, standard action-level guardrails catch none, while a state-aware validator catches all without false alarms on correct interventions. This indicates that action-level content moderation is systematically blind to consequence-flips, motivating state-aware architectural alternatives. We release our protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe.

</details>


### [183] [C-MIG: Multi-view Information Gain-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Clinical Diagnosis Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27860)
*Yuwei Miao,Gen Li,Yunsheng Zeng,Xiandong Li,Yujin Wang,Siyu Chen,Luning Wang,Yunhao Qiao,Junfeng Wang,Jianwei Lv,Bo Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: C-MIG introduces a Multi-view Information Gain-based retrieval-augmented generation framework for clinical diagnosis, leveraging a frozen reference model to compute information gain from retrieved-document and document-refinement views, plus a multi-subquery retrieval augmentation. It achieves state-of-the-art results among RAG-RL methods on in-domain and out-of-domain clinical benchmarks and surpasses general-purpose LLMs in clinical diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG-RL methods rely on exact-match binary rewards, which ignore semantically relevant but non-verbatim steps and provide a uni-dimensional signal that inadequately supervises heterogeneous clinical reasoning. There is a need for richer learning signals and better credit assignment to guide retrieval and refinement in medical diagnosis tasks.

Method: C-MIG estimates information gain under a frozen reference model from two complementary views: (1) retrieved-document view, and (2) document-refinement view. These signals jointly guide what to retrieve and how to refine, addressing reward-signal loss and credit assignment issues. It also introduces a multi-subquery retrieval augmentation strategy to improve knowledge recall coverage in clinical diagnostics. Empirical evaluation on four medical benchmarks demonstrates superior performance for RAG-RL and competitiveness with or surpassing general LLMs for clinical diagnosis.

Result: C-MIG achieves the best performance among all RAG-RL methods on both in-domain and out-of-domain sets and outperforms state-of-the-art general-purpose LLMs for clinical diagnosis.

Conclusion: By leveraging multi-view information gain and retrieval augmentation, C-MIG mitigates reward-signal sparsity and credit-assignment problems in RAG-RL for clinical diagnosis, yielding stronger grounding in medical evidence and superior performance. This approach highlights the potential of information-theoretic signals to enhance domain-specific RAG-RL systems.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation combined with reinforcement learning has shown promise for grounding large language models in trustworthy medical evidence. However, existing methods rely on exact-match binary rewards, which in clinical diagnosis cause two issues: (i) semantically relevant but non-verbatim steps receive zero signal, discarding valuable learning signals; and (ii) uni-dimensional rewards cannot effectively supervise heterogeneous reasoning capabilities. To address these issues, we propose C-MIG, a Multi-view Information Gain-based retrieval-augmented generation framework for Clinical diagnosis. C-MIG estimates information gain under a frozen reference model from two complementary views, retrieved-document and document-refinement, to jointly guide what to retrieve and how to refine, alleviating the issues of valuable reward signal loss and credit assignment. We further design a multi-subquery retrieval augmentation strategy that improves knowledge recall coverage in clinical diagnostic scenarios. Comprehensive experiments on four medical benchmarks demonstrate that C-MIG achieves the best performance among all RAG-RL methods on both in-domain and out-of-domain sets, and outperforms state-of-the-art general-purpose LLMs for clinical diagnosis.

</details>


### [184] [FundaPod: A Multi-Persona Agent Pod Platform with Knowledge Graph Memory for AI-Assisted Fundamental Investment Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27864)
*Di Zhu,Lei,Zheng,Zihan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FundaPod introduces a multi-persona, provenance-aware AI platform for fundamental investment research, enabling independent AI personas to gather evidence and produce memos, with post hoc adjudication by a human PM via a knowledge-graph memory system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fundamental research requires evidence gathering, synthesis, and verifiable documentation. Traditional LLM-focused work emphasizes signals, risking opaque reasoning and misalignment with human analysts. An independence-preserving, multi-persona architecture can support transparent, reusable investment knowledge and human-AI collaboration.

Method: Proposes five design principles for human-AI hybrid systems in fundamental research and four architectural mechanisms: (1) persona distillation pipeline to convert public materials into deployable agents; (2) declarative skill registry to derive typed task graphs; (3) grounded evidence model linking memo claims to verifiable sources; (4) knowledge-graph 'second brain' connecting tickers, memos, analysts, and themes; combined under a shared provenance contract with independent personas (e.g., value investors, macro strategists) whose disagreements are surfaced for human adjudication. Demonstrates architecture via a complete case study and a persona-based memo comparison.

Result: A validated architectural blueprint for AI-assisted fundamental research, including concrete components and a working demonstration through a case study and memo comparisons.

Conclusion: Independence-preserving, multi-persona AI architectures can enhance transparency, traceability, and cumulative knowledge in institutional fundamental research, while preserving human oversight and decision-making.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in finance, yet most existing work emphasizes trading signals or financial NLP tasks centered on prediction. Institutional fundamental research, by contrast, requires human analysts or AI agents to gather evidence, identify business drivers, compare competing viewpoints, and generate investment memos. Its broader goal is not merely to predict outcomes, but to produce investment plans that are transparent, reusable, and verifiable, while contributing to the cumulative development of investment knowledge. We present FundaPod, a multi-persona agent platform for AI-assisted fundamental investment research. We argue that fundamental research is a human-centric decision-support task that is qualitatively distinct from trading-signal generation, and is therefore better served by an independence-preserving architecture. In FundaPod, AI agents with different personas, such as value investors or macro strategists, conduct research independently under a shared provenance contract. Their disagreements are then surfaced post hoc for adjudication by the human portfolio manager (PM) through a knowledge-graph memory system. This paper contributes five design principles for human-AI hybrid systems supporting fundamental research, grounded in design-science practice and theories of cognitive isolation and human-machine coordination. It also describes four architectural mechanisms: a persona distillation pipeline that turns public investor materials into deployable agents; a declarative skill registry that lets the planner derive typed task graphs; a grounded evidence model that links memo claims to verifiable sources; and a knowledge-graph "second brain" that connects tickers, memos, analysts, and themes. We demonstrate the architecture through a complete case study and a persona-based memo comparison.

</details>


### [185] [AIBuildAI-2: A Knowledge-Enhanced Agent for Automatically Building AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27873)
*Ruiyi Zhang,Peijia Qin,Qi Cao,Li Zhang,Pengtao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes AIBuildAI-2, a knowledge-enhanced agent with an external evolving knowledge base to automate AI model construction for scientific tasks; achieves leading results on MLE-Bench and a heart-disease prediction competition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alleviate manual AI engineering burden for scientists and overcome static, outdated knowledge in LLMs by grounding design decisions in an external, verifiable knowledge system.

Method: Implement a hierarchical knowledge system: high-level knowledge instructions by topical categories; low-level knowledge documents; dynamic loading of context. Initialize from web-collected AI-development documents; continuous learning by distilling outcomes of completed runs into the knowledge base.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on MLE-Bench with 70.7% medal rate; top 6.6% among 4,370 human-expert teams in a heart disease prediction competition.

Conclusion: External evolving knowledge integration can surpass static LLM limitations in AI-model engineering, improving performance in scientific discovery tasks.

Abstract: AI models underpin data-centric applications from image and text processing to scientific discovery in biology, physics, and chemistry. Yet developing them remains heavily manual, requiring practitioners to design architectures, build training pipelines, and iteratively refine solutions, making it challenging for natural scientists without specialized AI engineering expertise to build the high-performing models their research demands. To reduce this burden and broaden access to AI for scientific discovery, agents that automatically build AI models have been proposed. However, the performance of these agents is largely limited by the parametric knowledge of their underlying large language models, which is static, often outdated, and sparse on practical AI model engineering know-how. To address this limitation, we introduce AIBuildAI-2, a knowledge-enhanced agent with an external, evolving knowledge system for automatically building AI models. The knowledge system of AIBuildAI-2 is hierarchical, organizing curated AI development knowledge into high-level knowledge instructions over topical categories and low-level knowledge documents under each category, from which the agent dynamically loads only the context relevant to its current state and the AI task being solved, grounding each design and implementation decision in concrete, externally verifiable expertise. The system is initialized by collecting and cleaning AI-development-related documents from the web and organizing them into the corresponding categories, and continually evolves from the agent's own experience by distilling each completed run on an AI task into structured takeaways that are written back into the knowledge system. AIBuildAI-2 achieves state-of-the-art results, ranking first on MLE-Bench with a 70.7% medal rate and placing in the top 6.6% among 4,370 human-expert teams in a heart disease prediction competition.

</details>


### [186] [Towards Faithful Agentic XAI: A Verification Method and an Open-World Benchmark for Better Model Faithfulness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27879)
*Jaechang Kim,Sunung Mun,Seungjoon Lee,Jaewoong Cho,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FAX: faithful agentic XAI via explicit verification; improves faithfulness with confirmation against faithful tools; introduces CRAFTER-XAI-Bench; shows better faithfulness than baselines and highlights benchmarking issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainable AI explanations from LLMs can be plausible but unfaithful, especially when used in agentic XAI; need mechanisms to ensure explanations reflect target model behavior.

Method: Draft explanations are decomposed into claims; each claim is cross-checked against faithful tools; unsupported/contradictory claims are filtered before final generation. Also introduces CRAFTER-XAI-Bench: an open-world RL benchmark for assessing model-specific faithfulness; evaluation across tabular benchmarks.

Result: On CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, simulation faithfulness rises from 0.20 to 0.46; informativeness, relevance, and fluency remain high. On three tabular benchmarks, FAX is competitive with baselines; analysis suggests prior benchmarks conflate task accuracy with faithfulness.

Conclusion: Explicit verification is essential for faithful Agentic XAI; faithfulness benchmarks should test explanations against the target model's behavior.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) helps users interpret model behavior and identify potential faults. Agentic XAI systems use Large Language Models (LLMs) to make explanations more accessible through natural-language interaction, but they can also produce plausible yet unfaithful explanations. This risk arises because unreliable XAI outputs for complex models can be amplified by LLMs and mislead users. We propose Faithful Agentic XAI (FAX), a framework that improves explanation faithfulness through explicit verification. FAX decomposes draft explanations into claims and cross-checks them against inherently faithful tools, filtering unsupported or contradictory claims before final generation. We also introduce CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, an open-world reinforcement learning benchmark with complex policies, diverse goals, and challenging scenarios for assessing model-specific faithfulness. On CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, FAX improves simulation faithfulness from 0.20 for the strongest baseline to 0.46 while maintaining high informativeness, relevance, and fluency. On three tabular benchmarks, FAX performs competitively with prior Agentic XAI baselines, but our analysis shows that these settings can conflate task accuracy with model-specific faithfulness. These findings show that explicit verification is essential for faithful Agentic XAI and that that faithfulness benchmarks must be designed to test explanations against the behavior of the target model itself.

</details>


### [187] [PortBench: A Correlation-Aware, Full-Pipeline Benchmark for LLM-Driven Portfolio Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27887)
*Yuxuan Zhao,Sijia Chen,Ningxin Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PortBench benchmarks portfolio management with static cross-asset correlation QA and a dynamic 5-stage PM pipeline, introducing dual-layer correlation score and CEPS metrics; evaluated on 10 frontier LLMs showing limited real PM utility despite QA strength.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two gaps in PM benchmarks: (1) ignore cross-asset correlation structures leading to misleading diversification signals, and (2) fail to evaluate the full PM decision pipeline in realistic settings.

Method: PortBench spans six heterogeneous asset classes over ten years, comprising (i) a static QA dataset of 6,269 correlation-based questions across seven task templates, and (ii) a dynamic five-stage allocation pipeline mirroring the PM decision cycle. Evaluation uses two metrics: dual-layer correlation score (hedging across classes and concentration within classes) and CEPS (cumulative error propagation across pipeline stages). Robustness and investor alignment are tested under three historical stress regimes and risk profiles. Ten frontier LLMs are evaluated; source code provided.

Result: Static QA performance of LLMs is strong, but 90% of model–profile combinations fail to outperform a basic equal-weight allocation in PM tasks. Even models meeting procedural constraints experience catastrophic drawdowns under stress. The work provides benchmarks, metrics, and a reproducible codebase, highlighting the gap between QA proficiency and actionable PM reasoning.

Conclusion: There is a clear gap between LLMs’ QA capabilities and their effectiveness in real-world PM, underscoring the need for PM-specific training regimes and evaluation that capture cross-asset hedging, decision pipelines, and stress resilience; PortBench offers a rigorous framework for future PM-focused LLM development.

Abstract: LLMs have shown strong performance across diverse financial tasks, yet portfolio management (PM), a critical financial decision-making task, remains poorly benchmarked. Existing benchmarks exhibit two main gaps: they ignore cross-asset correlation structures, thereby failing to distinguish genuinely diversified portfolios from concentrated ones, and fail to evaluate the complete PM decision pipeline in real-world scenarios. We introduce PortBench, a benchmark spanning six heterogeneous asset classes over ten years. PortBench consists of two complementary layers: a static QA dataset of 6,269 correlation-based questions across seven task templates, and a dynamic five-stage allocation pipeline that mirrors the full PM decision cycle. To evaluate these layers, we introduce two dedicated metrics: a dual-layer correlation score that measures whether proposed portfolios exploit inter-class hedging and avoid intra-class concentration, and CEPS, a metric that quantifies how reasoning errors compound across pipeline stages. We further assess strategy robustness and investor alignment under three historical stress regimes and risk profiles. Evaluating ten frontier LLMs, we find that despite strong performance on static financial QA, 90\% of model-profile combinations fail to outperform a basic equal-weight allocation, and models that satisfy every procedural constraint still suffer catastrophic drawdowns under stress. Our source code is available at \href{https://github.com/AgenticFinLab/portbench}{this https URL}.

</details>


### [188] [A Unified Framework for the Evaluation of LLM Agentic Capabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27898)
*Pengyu Zhu,Lijun Li,Yaxing Lyu,Qianxin Luo,Jingyi Yang,Yi Liu,Tingfeng Hui,Xinyu Yuan,Li Sun,Sen Su,Jing Shao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified evaluation framework for LLM agentic capabilities disentangles model ability from framework and environment effects by standardizing benchmarks into an instruction–tool–environment format, using a fixed ReAct-like architecture in a sandbox, and offering an offline mode and unified metrics; it aggregates 7 benchmarks across 24 domains, analyzes 400k rollouts with 5B tokens on 15 models, and demonstrates framework extensibility to safety-critical domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark results in LLM agentic evaluation conflate model capability with benchmark design choices and environmental volatility. A fair, controllable, and extensible evaluation framework is needed to isolate intrinsic capabilities and enable safer deployment analysis.

Method: Introduce a unified configuration system; translate diverse benchmarks into a standardized instruction–tool–environment (ITE) format; execute agents via a fixed ReAct-style architecture inside a controllable sandbox; provide an optional offline setting using curated snapshots to separate environment effects; evaluate each benchmark by its original task-success criteria while adding unified resource-usage metrics and a decision/execution-attribution taxonomy; adapt 7 benchmarks across 24 domains (single-agent, multi-agent, safety-critical); conduct large-scale experiments with 400k rollouts and 5B tokens on 15 models.

Result: The framework reveals that scaffold choice and environmental volatility meaningfully affect benchmark outcomes in both directions, enabling separation of intrinsic LLM capabilities from framework/environment artifacts; demonstrates the framework’s extensibility as a secure testbed for safety-critical domains.

Conclusion: A cohesive, auditable framework can provide fair, interpretable assessment of LLM agentic capabilities, facilitating cross-benchmark comparability and safer deployment analyses; the open-source code and benchmarks support broad adoption and extension.

Abstract: As LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents, reliable assessment of their agentic capabilities has become essential. However, reported benchmark scores often jointly reflect model capability and the implementation choices each benchmark is packaged with, making cross-benchmark results difficult to interpret as clean measurements of the underlying model. In this work, we present a unified framework for the fair evaluation of LLM agentic capabilities. Driven by a unified configuration system, the framework integrates diverse benchmarks into a standardized instruction--tool--environment format, executes agents through a fixed ReAct-style architecture within a controllable sandbox, and provides an optional offline setting that replaces volatile live environments with curated snapshots, so that framework effects and environment effects can be analyzed separately. Building on this, we unify the evaluation methodology under each benchmark's original task-success criteria, while introducing unified metrics for resource consumption and a taxonomy for decision- and execution-level failure attribution. Within this framework, we adapt 7 widely used benchmarks spanning 24 domains across single-agent, multi-agent, and safety-critical scenarios, and conduct a large-scale empirical analysis over 400K rollouts and 5B tokens on 15 models. The results show that scaffold choice and environmental volatility materially shift benchmark outcomes in both directions, allowing our framework to disentangle intrinsic LLM capabilities from framework- and environment-induced artifacts. We further demonstrate its extensibility as a secure testbed for safety-critical domains. Codes and benchmarks at are available at https://github.com/whfeLingYu/A-Unified-Framework-for-the-Evaluation-of-LLM-Agentic-Capabilities, https://huggingface.co/AgentFramework/Unified_Farmework.

</details>


### [189] [SKILLC: Learning Autonomous Skill Internalization in LLM Agents via Contrastive Credit Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27899)
*Hongxiang Lin,Zhirui Kuai,Erpeng Xue,Lei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillC introduces Contrastive Skill Credit Assignment (CSCA) to internalize skill guidance in long-horizon RL by learning from paired skill-injected vs skill-free rollouts within the same policy update; a dual-stream advantage estimator enforces skill-aware optimization and a smoothed validation signal drives adaptive curriculum, yielding improvements over prior skill-internalization methods and competitive performance to skill-augmented baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current skill-internalization RL methods rely on skill-helpfulness contrast for curriculum control but do not change the policy update to separate skill-dependent success from autonomous success, limiting autonomous performance and transfer.

Method: SkillC samples paired rollouts with and without skill injection for tasks drawn from active skill types within a single policy update. It injects the task-level contrast into optimization via a dual-stream value/advantage estimator that preserves global ranking while applying a one-sided correction toward skill-free success. A smoothed validation-level signal guides an adaptive curriculum over attribution strength, rollout allocation, and monotonic active-set pruning.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop, SkillC surpasses the strongest prior skill-internalization RL baseline by 5.5% and 4.4% respectively without runtime skill access, while remaining competitive with skill-augmented RL methods.

Conclusion: CSCA enables a direct learning signal for internalization, improving autonomous performance without relying on runtime skills and achieving competitive results across long-horizon tasks; the adaptive curriculum further stabilizes training.

Abstract: Structured skill prompts improve exploration in long-horizon agentic reinforcement learning (RL). Skill-augmented RL methods retain external skills at inference, while skill-internalization RL methods withdraw them during training to enable autonomous performance. However, existing internalization approaches only use skill-helpfulness contrast for curriculum control, leaving the policy update unchanged and unable to distinguish skill-dependent from autonomous success. We propose SkillC, a framework based on Contrastive Skill Credit Assignment (CSCA) that converts this contrast into a direct learning signal for internalization. \textsc{SkillC} samples paired skill-injected and skill-free rollouts for tasks from active skill types within the same policy update, and injects their task-level contrast into optimization via a dual-stream advantage estimator that preserves global ranking while applying a one-sided correction toward skill-free success. A smoothed validation-level signal further drives an adaptive curriculum over attribution strength, rollout allocation, and monotonic active-set pruning. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that, without runtime skill access, SkillC surpasses the strongest prior skill-internalization RL baseline by 5.5\% and 4.4\%, respectively, while remaining competitive with skill-augmented RL methods.

</details>


### [190] [Dr-CiK: A Testbed for Foresight-Driven Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27904)
*Yihong Tang,Andrew Robert Williams,Arjun Ashok,Vincent Zhihao Zheng,Lijun Sun,Alexandre Drouin,Issam H. Laradji,Étienne Marcotte,Valentina Zantedeschi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dr-CiK introduces a benchmark to evaluate whether forecasting agents can autonomously retrieve and use relevant context from a document corpus to improve time-series forecasting, revealing that many agents struggle with noisy sources and distractors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world forecasting depends on external context; existing benchmarks assume provided context, leaving open whether agents can discover and use context from noisy sources. Dr-CiK addresses this gap by testing foresight-driven retrieval and use of supporting evidence.

Method: Introduce the Dr-CiK benchmark where agents retrieve documents from a corpus, filter out distractors, distill retrieved content into forecast-useful evidence, and generate forecasts supported by that evidence. The evaluation includes context ablations and pairing state-of-the-art deep learning and forecasting methods.

Result: High-quality context substantially improves forecasting in Dr-CiK. However, most context retrieval (DR) agents recover <5% of ground-truth supporting evidence and cite distractors in >80% of cases, sometimes degrading forecast performance when context is retrieved.

Conclusion: The findings motivate the development of foresight-driven agents that actively search for and curate the right context to predict the future, rather than assuming context is readily available.

Abstract: Time series forecasting in real-world settings often depends not only on historical observations, but also on external context that must be actively discovered from noisy, heterogeneous information sources. Yet existing context-aided forecasting benchmarks typically assume that the supporting context is already provided, leaving open whether agents can identify it on their own. Therefore, we introduce Dr-CiK, a benchmark for evaluating whether agents can retrieve forecasting-relevant supporting context from a document corpus, filter out distractors, distill the retrieved context into forecast-useful evidence, and generate forecasts supported by that evidence. Through context ablations and evaluations of state-of-the-art deep research and forecasting methods paired together, we show that high-quality context substantially improves forecasting performance in Dr-CiK. However, most existing DR agents recover only a small fraction of the ground-truth supporting evidence (usually <5%), are frequently misled by distractors (>80% distractor citations), and can cause forecasters to perform worse with retrieved context than without context. Our results motivate research on foresight-driven agents that search for the right context to predict the future.

</details>


### [191] [Reasoning Matters: Mitigate Hallucination in Multimodal Large Reasoning Models via Reasoning-Conditioned Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27906)
*Jiawei Kong,Hao Fang,Shunxiang Liao,Jinyu Li,Bin Chen,Hao Wu,Shu-Tao Xia,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RC-DPO introduces a CoT-oriented preference term for multimodal reasoning models to align reasoning chains with final answers, addressing hallucinations more effectively than standard answer-level direct preference optimization. It uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to generate supported CoTs and attention-guided pruning to create negatives, yielding improved reliability across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO methods treat chain-of-thought and answer as a single output, optimizing only answer-level preference. This underutilizes CoT supervision and leads to hallucinations in multimodal reasoning models. A CoT-conditioned objective may better align reasoning with correct conclusions.

Method: Propose Reasoning-Conditioned Direct Preference Optimization (RC-DPO) that conditions answer generation on CoT and contrasts preferences for the same answer under different CoT conditions. Introduce a reasoning-enhanced data generation strategy: MC Tree Search to discover visually grounded, logically consistent CoTs as positive samples; attention-guided CoT token pruning to construct negative samples.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks show RC-DPO reduces hallucinations and improves reliability of multimodal reasoning.

Conclusion: RC-DPO effectively mitigates hallucinations by enforcing CoT–answer alignment, supported by a data-generation strategy that yields better supervision for reasoning chains and results in more reliable multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Reasoning Models introduce the reasoning paradigm, demonstrating strong capabilities on complex vision-language tasks. However, they still suffer from severe hallucinations. Existing training-based methods typically mitigate hallucinations through response-level direct preference optimization (DPO), where the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and the final answer are treated as a monolithic output and optimized jointly. We reveal that this formulation performs similarly to answer-only optimization, suggesting that it primarily learns answer-level preference, while leaving CoT-level supervision insufficiently exploited. To address this issue, we explicitly formulate a CoT-oriented preference term and derive Reasoning-Conditioned Direct Preference Optimization (RC-DPO), which models the CoT as a condition for answer generation and contrasts the preference for the same preferred answer under different CoT conditions, promoting answer-supportive reasoning chain alignment. To further improve optimization, we introduce a reasoning-enhanced preference data generation strategy that employs Monte Carlo Tree Search to discover visually grounded and logically consistent CoTs as positive samples, and attention-guided CoT token pruning to construct negative ones. Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks show that RC-DPO effectively mitigates hallucinations and improves the reliability of the multimodal reasoning process.

</details>


### [192] [SuiChat-CN: Benchmarking Contextual Suicide Risk Assessment in Chinese Group Chats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27911)
*Xiangyu Wang,Zhiwei Yu,Chengze Du,Dingchang Wang,Yuhan Ye,Fangyu Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SuiChat-CN introduces a Chinese group-chat benchmark for contextual suicide risk assessment, emphasizing context-aware analysis and LLM-assisted annotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in instant-messaging group chats (short, fragmented, multi-party) for suicide risk assessment and enables early detection research.

Method: Collect public Telegram group-chat data; build coherent conversational segments via signal-word extraction and bidirectional context expansion; annotate user risk levels with expert-validated, LLM-assisted paradigm.

Result: Dataset SuiChat-CN: 13,312 contextual segments from 1,406 users, 258,228 raw messages. Extensive experiments with PLMs and 40+ LLMs show contextual information improves risk assessment; challenges in early detection in multi-party chats; ethical constraints limit public release, but access for accredited researchers is possible.

Conclusion: Contextual information is essential for reliable assessment; dataset advances group-chat risk assessment while highlighting ethical considerations and the need for improved early-detection methods.

Abstract: Suicide is a critical global public health challenge, causing approximately 720,000 deaths each year and calling for timely, effective prevention strategies. Existing computational studies primarily focus on post-based social media platforms such as Twitter and Weibo, leaving instant messaging environments such as Telegram underexplored. Yet group chats pose distinct challenges: messages are short, fragmented, multi-party, and often rely on implicit or culturally specific expressions, making isolated post-level analysis insufficient. We introduce SuiChat-CN, a Chinese group-chat benchmark for contextual suicide risk assessment. We collect public Telegram group-chat data, construct coherent conversational segments through signal-word extraction and bidirectional context expansion, and annotate user risk levels with an expert-validated, LLM-assisted paradigm. SuiChat-CN contains 13,312 contextual segments from 1,406 users, covering 258,228 raw chat messages. Extensive experiments with PLMs and more than 40 LLMs demonstrate that contextual information is essential for reliable risk assessment, while fine-tuning and partial-context evaluation further reveal the challenges of early detection in multi-party conversations. Due to ethical and sensitivity concerns, the dataset is not publicly released but will be shared with accredited mental health and suicide-prevention research institutions upon reasonable request.

</details>


### [193] [Show, Don't TELL: Explainable AI-Generated Text Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27921)
*Aldan Creo,Suraj Ranganath*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A explainability-focused detector for AI-generated text (TELL) that provides human-understandable 'tells' while maintaining competitive AUROC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses user needs by offering explanations alongside a detection score, enabling critical human judgment.

Method: Train on domain-specific authorship SFT data; use GRPO with curriculum learning; produce explainable annotations ('tells') alongside a score.

Result: AUROC 0.927; competitive with state-of-the-art detectors; human-evaluated explanations achieve mean 72.3% in concreteness, falsifiability, coherence, plausibility and grounding; evaluation uses a dataset of human annotations.

Conclusion: Shifts AI-generated text detection toward human-centric explainability; suggests a new family of detectors that embed native explanations.

Abstract: Research on AI-generated text detection has presented a number of approaches to discern human from AI prose, some of which achieving high in-distribution performance. However, real-world applicability has stalled because their outputs are misaligned with the needs of users, such as professors, who are presented with a numeric score that has no attached explanation. We tackle this issue with a novel architecture, TELL, that bakes explainability from the ground-up. While our system still offers a numerical score like other detectors for comparability, TELL takes a fundamentally different approach where we aim to show the user the "tells" by which the model believes a text is AI or human-written, to empower the user to decide who wrote a text using their own judgment and understanding of the context of the writing and its alleged author. We train TELL on a custom SFT dataset of domain-specific authorship annotations, and further refine the system using GRPO with curriculum learning to improve performance. We achieve competitive performance with state-of-the-art detectors (AUROC 0.927) while natively providing annotations that explain the basis for the detector's decision. We further evaluate the quality of our explanations using a dataset of human annotations and report a high (mean 72.3%) win-rate on annotation concreteness, falsifiability, coherence, plausibility and grounding, allowing users to critically think and decide for themselves. Our work thus reframes the problem of AI-generated text detection in a human-centric perspective and paves the way for a new family of detectors that focus on native explainability.

</details>


### [194] [Harness-Bench: Measuring Harness Effects across Models in Realistic Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27922)
*Yilun Yao,Xinyu Tan,Chao-Hsuan Liu,Yaoming Li,Zhengyang Wang,Wenhan Yu,Zhewen Tan,Yuxuan Tian,Guangxiang Zhao,Lin Sun,Xiangzheng Zhang,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Harness-Bench is a diagnostic benchmark to study how harness configuration affects LLM agent workflows, across model backends and environments, using 106 tasks and 5,194 executions to reveal variation and encourage reporting performance at the model-harness level.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks often fix or ignore the harness, making it hard to study execution-layer variation. Harness-Bench aims to isolate and diagnose configuration-level harness effects in realistic agent workflows.

Method: Construct 106 sandboxed offline tasks derived from practical agent-use patterns. Evaluate across multiple model backends under shared task environments and budgets with analysis preserved for native execution behavior. Run 5,194 execution trajectories, recording final artifacts, traces, usage statistics, and validator outputs. Analyze completion, process quality, efficiency, and failure modes; identify recurring execution-alignment failures; provide a reproducible framework for diagnosing and improving agent execution stacks.

Result: Large variation observed across model-harness pairings in completion rates, process quality, efficiency, and failure behavior. Findings suggest reporting agent capability at the model-harness configuration level, not just the base model. Recurring alignment failures indicate decoupling between reasoning and tool feedback/workspace state. Harness-Bench offers a reproducible foundation for diagnosing and improving reliable, auditable agent execution stacks.

Conclusion: Harness-Bench enables systematic diagnosis and improvement of agent execution stacks, enabling reproducible evaluation and encouraging explicit reporting of model-harness configurations when assessing agent capability.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as executable systems that use tools, modify workspaces, and produce concrete artifacts. In such workflows, performance depends not only on the base model, but also on the harness: the system layer that manages context, tools, state, constraints, permissions, tracing, and recovery. However, existing benchmarks typically abstract away execution, compare complete agent systems, or hold the harness fixed, making execution-layer variation difficult to study. We introduce Harness-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating configuration-level harness effects in realistic agent workflows. Harness-Bench evaluates representative harness configurations across multiple model backends under shared task environments, budgets, and evaluation protocols, while preserving each harness's native execution behavior. The benchmark contains 106 sandboxed offline tasks constructed from practical agent-use patterns and manually reviewed for realism, solvability, oracle-checkability, and integrity. Each run records final artifacts, execution traces, usage statistics, and validator outputs, enabling analysis beyond final completion. Across 5,194 execution trajectories, we observe substantial variation in completion, process quality, efficiency, and failure behavior across model-harness pairings. These results suggest that agent capability should be reported at the model-harness configuration level rather than attributed to the base model alone. Our analysis further identifies recurring execution-alignment failures, where plausible reasoning becomes decoupled from tool feedback, workspace state, evidence, or verifiable output contracts. Harness-Bench provides a reproducible foundation for diagnosing and improving reliable, efficient, and auditable agent execution stacks.

</details>


### [195] [DiagramRAG: A Lightweight Framework to Retrieve Scientific Diagram for Figure Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27931)
*Xinjiang Yu,Junyi Han,Zhuofan Chen,Chi Zhang,Xiangyu Fu,Jingyuan Tan,Zirui You,Yixiang Jian,Yu-Ping Wang,Chengliang Chai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A retrieval-augmented, topologically aware sketch-based diagram completion framework (DiagramRAG) uses knowledge-graph representations and multi-level sketch variants to retrieve semantically compatible and topologically aligned references to guide diagram generation, achieving strong quantitative results and lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sketches are often incomplete for direct diagram generation; existing methods either reconstruct the sketch or rely on textual semantics without exploiting the sketch's topology. A structure-aware, retrieval-augmented approach can leverage topological cues and content priors to produce publication-quality diagrams.

Method: Represent diagrams as knowledge graphs; synthesize sketch variants at multiple simplification levels; train an embedding model to align sketches with compatible diagrams in a shared space; retrieve references that are semantically relevant and topology-compatible; use retrieved references to provide content, topology, and visual priors for completion and rendering.

Result: Quantitative evaluation shows F1 scores of 0.848 on DiagramBank and 0.802 on FigureBench; best VLM-as-a-Judge score of 7.170; inference latency reduced to 35.48 seconds per sample; code and data released.

Conclusion: DiagramRAG demonstrates that retrieval-augmented, structure-aware sketch completion can improve diagram quality and efficiency by leveraging topological cues from sketches and reference diagrams.

Abstract: Scientific diagrams are essential for communicating complex methodologies in academic papers. A natural way for researchers to specify such diagrams is through rough sketches, where text labels, connectors, and spatial arrangements express early semantic and topological intentions. However, sketches are usually incomplete, making them insufficient for directly producing publication-quality diagrams. Existing sketch-based generation methods mainly reconstruct the sketch itself, while recent text-driven diagram generation frameworks rely on textual semantics and do not fully exploit the topological structure contained in sketches. In this paper, we introduce DiagramRAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for sketch-based scientific diagram completion. Given a user sketch, DiagramRAG retrieves reference diagrams that are both semantically relevant to the sketch content and topologically compatible with its structure, and uses them to guide downstream diagram generation. To enable efficient structure-aware retrieval, we represent diagrams as knowledge graphs, synthesize sketch variants at different simplification levels, and train an embedding model to align sketches with compatible diagrams in a shared space. The retrieved references further provide content, topology, and visual priors for completing and rendering the final diagram. Experiments show that DiagramRAG achieves F1-scores of 0.848 and 0.802 on DiagramBank and FigureBench, respectively, and improves generation quality with the best VLM-as-a-Judge score of 7.170, while reducing inference latency to 35.48 seconds per sample. Our code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DiagramRAG-A262 and https://huggingface.co/datasets/anonymous-review-a262/DiagramSketch.

</details>


### [196] [Do Agents Think Deeper? A Mechanistic Investigation of Layer-Wise Dynamics in Sequential Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27935)
*Zhenyu Cui,Xiangzhong Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autonomous-LM agents adjust their depth usage as tasks unfold: deeper layers and stronger inter-layer dependencies are recruited over turns, residuals become correction-dominant, and a construction–refinement gap persists, varying by model family and domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether depth-efficient dynamics observed in static, single-turn LLM tasks extend to autonomous agents that require multi-turn planning, tool use, and iterative state updates; to characterize the mechanistic depth profile of agentic reasoning.

Method: Layer-wise analysis of complete user–agent trajectories across three domains (Deep Research, Code Generation, Tabular Processing) using residual-stream probes, causal layer-skipping interventions, and effective-depth measurements; comparative study across model families (Qwen, Minimax, GLM).

Result: Agents progressively recruit more and deeper layers as trajectories unfold; stronger long-range inter-layer dependencies emerge in later turns; residual updates become predominantly correctional; a construction–refinement gap appears where semantic directions emerge early but deep layers are needed to stabilize final outputs; depth allocation patterns vary by model family and domain (Qwen/Minimax show larger gaps; GLM is more domain-dependent).

Conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents allocate depth adaptively in response to increasing reasoning complexity, indicating distinct mechanistic depth profiles for agentic tasks and informing model design, evaluation, and prompting strategies for multi-turn AI agents.

Abstract: Recent mechanistic studies suggest that large language models (LLMs) may utilize their depth inefficiently in standard single-turn tasks. Whether this still holds in autonomous agent settings, where models must perform multi-turn planning, tool use, and iterative state updates, remains unclear. We study this question through a systematic layer-wise analysis of complete user-agent trajectories spanning three domains: Deep Research, Code Generation, and Tabular Processing. Using residual stream probes, causal layer-skipping interventions, and effective-depth measurements, we show that agentic reasoning exhibits a distinct depth profile from static tasks. As trajectories unfold, models progressively recruit more and deeper layers, with stronger long-range inter-layer dependencies emerging in later turns. At the same time, residual updates become increasingly correction-dominant, indicating a shift from stable feature accumulation toward repeated recalibration. Effective-depth analysis further reveals a substantial construction-refinement gap: semantic direction often forms relatively early, while deep layers remain necessary for stabilizing final outputs. Across model families, this gap is pronounced in Qwen and Minimax, whereas GLM shows a more domain-dependent depth allocation pattern. These results provide mechanistic evidence that autonomous LLM agents allocate depth adaptively as reasoning complexity grows.

</details>


### [197] [From Talking to Singing: A New Challenge for Audio-Visual Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27944)
*Ke Liu,Jiwei Wei,Wenyu Zhang,Shuchang Zhou,Ruikun Chai,Yutao Dai,Chaoning Zhang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a cross-scenario audio-visual forgery detection framework (T-AVFD) for talking and singing, plus a singing-specific SHDF dataset. Combines a text-guided facial authenticity pattern learner with a multi-modal differential weight module to maintain cross-modal consistency and adaptively fuse information, yielding improved robustness over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal deepfake detection relies on inconsistencies, but rhythmic singing weakens cross-modal coupling, causing domain shift. A generalizable detector across talking and singing is needed, and a realistic singing benchmark is missing.

Method: Introduce SHDF dataset generated with rhythm-aware models to capture singing-domain characteristics. Propose T-AVFD framework with (1) a facial authenticity pattern learner that aligns facial features to multi-granularity textual descriptions to derive general patterns, and (2) a multi-modal differential weight learning module that preserves audio-visual coherence and adaptively integrates authenticity patterns via differential weighting.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple talking head deepfake datasets and SHDF show consistent improvements over existing baselines and strong robustness to diverse perturbations.

Conclusion: T-AVFD generalizes across talking and singing scenarios by fusing text-guided authenticity patterns with differential weighting of audio-visual coherence, and SHDF fills a benchmark gap for singing deepfakes; the approach demonstrates robust performance under domain shifts.

Abstract: With rapid advances in audio-visual generative models, reliable forgery detection becomes increasingly critical. Existing methods for audio-visual deepfake detection typically rely on cross-modal inconsistencies. In singing, rhythmic vocalization weakens this coupling and introduces a nontrivial domain shift, substantially degrading detection performance. We construct the Singing Head DeepFake (SHDF) dataset using rhythm-aware generative models to fill the gap in singing benchmarks. To cope with cross-scenario domain shifts, we propose a Text-guided Audio-Visual Forgery Detection (T-AVFD) framework that generalizes across both talking and singing scenarios. T-AVFD comprises a facial authenticity pattern learner and a multi-modal differential weight learning module. The pattern learner aligns facial features with multi-granularity textual descriptions to learn generalizable authenticity patterns. The weight learning module preserves intrinsic audio-visual consistency and adaptively integrates it with authenticity patterns via differential weighting. Extensive experiments on multiple talking head deepfake datasets and SHDF show consistent improvements over existing baselines and strong robustness under diverse perturbations.

</details>


### [198] [The Shape of Overthinking: Backtracking Bursts in Long Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27965)
*Navid Rezazadeh,Arash Gholami Davoodi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Backtracking in long-form reasoning traces differentiates useful self-correction from unproductive revisions. Early isolated repairs correlate with correct traces; moderate-to-severe, late-backtracks cluster in incorrect traces. Burst-aware, prefix-based early-exit filtering improves separation of recoverable repair from instability, outperforming fixed-length baselines and serving as a deployable control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To distinguish beneficial self-correction from instability in long-form reasoning traces and to develop practical filtering/policy mechanisms for robust model evaluation and deployment.

Method: Annotate 6,000 Qwen3-8B AIME traces at segment level to label backtrack severity; analyze timing, normalized depth, and local burst structure; perform cross-corpus checks across models/domains; compare burst-aware prefix-based early-exit with fixed-length filtering; evaluate through moderate depth cutoffs.

Result: Early isolated repairs are compatible with correct reasoning; incorrect traces tend to exhibit moderate-to-severe backtracks that persist and cluster late. Cross-corpus qualitatively similar asymmetry observed. Burst-aware filtering with prefix-available features outperforms fixed-length filtering; moderate-length cutoffs remain strong baselines; burst-aware control is deployable for separating recoverable repair from instability.

Conclusion: Burst-aware backtracking analysis provides a practical framework for distinguishing recoverable repair from instability in long-form reasoning and informs effective early-exit policies and evaluation strategies.

Abstract: Reasoning models often generate long traces in which useful self-correction and unproductive revision are hard to distinguish. We study this distinction through backtracking dynamics: local reconsideration, retraction, or re-derivation inside long-form reasoning traces. On 6{,}000 Qwen3-8B AIME traces, we annotate segment-level backtrack severity and analyze event timing, normalized depth, and local burst structure. We find that early isolated repair is often compatible with correct reasoning, whereas incorrect traces more often show moderate-to-severe backtracks that persist and cluster late. Cross-corpus checks show the same qualitative asymmetry across additional model/domain pairs. Filtering analyses instantiate the signal as a prefix-causal selective early-exit policy: at shallow and intermediate depths, burst-aware filtering outperforms fixed length-based filtering while using only prefix-available features. Moderate length cutoffs remain strong completed-trace baselines, but burst-aware control provides a deployable mechanism for separating recoverable repair from likely instability.

</details>


### [199] [Geometry of Human Perceptual Domains Emerges Transiently in LLM Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27970)
*Simardeep Singh,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit emergent perceptual geometry across multiple domains (color, pitch, emotion, taste) in their hidden layers, showing domain- and model-specific trajectories with a transient, layer-wise organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether human-like perceptual structure emerges in LLM representations without explicit perceptual supervision and whether this structure aligns with human baselines across multiple modalities.

Method: Layer-wise geometric analysis of residual streams in several open-weight transformer architectures across domains (color, pitch, emotion, taste) without any direct perceptual supervision during training.

Result: Three main findings: (1) geometry emerges across domains despite lack of perceptual supervision; (2) domain-specific emergence profiles with trajectories dependent on domain and model; (3) a consistent trajectory where geometry is diffuse in early layers, more organized in intermediate layers, and attenuated in later layers.

Conclusion: These results illuminate where human-like perceptual geometry arises in LLMs and provide a principled basis for mechanistic analysis of internal representations across modalities.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are trained purely on textual data, prior work has shown that their internal representations can exhibit rich geometric structure in embedding space. Building on this line of work, we investigate whether such structure is similar to human perceptual organisation across different domains (e.g., color, pitch, emotion, and taste). Specifically, we study the layer-wise emergence of intrinsic geometrical structure corresponding to perceptual modalities within the residual streams of multiple open-weight transformer architectures. Our results reveal three key findings. First, we observe the emergence of layer-wise geometric structure across multiple perceptual domains, despite the absence of any direct perceptual supervision during training. Second, these perceptual domains exhibit distinct emergence profiles, with both geometric structure and its alignment with human baselines following domain- and model-specific trajectories across depth. Third, this emergence follows a consistent representational trajectory: geometry is weak or diffuse in early layers, becomes progressively organised in intermediate layers, and is attenuated in later layers, suggesting that perceptual geometry arises transiently as part of the model's internal transformation pipeline. This provides new insight into how and where human-like perceptual geometry arises in LLMs, offering a principled pathway for mechanistic analysis of internal representations.

</details>


### [200] [STAB: Specification-driven Testing for Algorithmic Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27981)
*Soohan Lim,Joonghyuk Hahn,Hyundong Jin,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STAB is a specification-driven pipeline that generates test cases to reveal algorithmic bottlenecks from natural-language specs, combining constraint-based size reasoning with adversarial structure injection to synthesize a test-case generator via an LLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide robust, structure-aware worst-case inputs for algorithm analysis, addressing limitations of prior work that focus on input size or code-specific inputs and miss structural worst-case conditions.

Method: A two-stage pipeline: (i) constraint saturator that uses rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization to derive large admissible size assignments from the problem specification; (ii) adversarial scenario injector that uses a curated scenario catalog with keyword matching and KNN to extract construction principles; these are encoded into a structured generation specification used by an LLM to synthesize a Python test-case generator.

Result: On CodeContests, STAB increases the rate of test cases exposing bottlenecks from 50.43% to 73.45% for open-source LLMs and from 57.45% to 71.85% for closed-source LLMs, with consistent gains across Python, Java, and C++. Code and resources are available at the authors' GitHub. 

Conclusion: STAB demonstrates that spec-driven, adversarial input synthesis can significantly improve discovery of algorithmic bottlenecks and provides a practical pipeline for generating robust test cases from natural-language specifications.

Abstract: Evaluating the efficiency of algorithmic code requires test cases that expose runtime bottlenecks. Previous methods generate efficiency test cases either by increasing input size or by generating code-specific inputs that make the given implementation run slowly. Consequently, they do not address the structural input conditions that drive the algorithmic worst case. We introduce STAB, a specification-driven pipeline that generates test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from a natural-language problem specification alone. STAB separates the task into constraint-bound maximization and adversarial structure injection. (i) The constraint saturator extracts constraints and resolves large admissible size assignments using rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization over related variables. (ii) The adversarial scenario injector retrieves implementation-level adversarial construction principles from a curated scenario catalog using keyword matching and K-nearest neighbors (KNN). STAB encodes the problem specification, resolved boundary, and retrieved construction principles into a structured generation specification, from which the LLM synthesizes a Python test case generator. On CodeContests, STAB raises the rate of generated test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from 50.43% to 73.45% on average across open-source LLMs and from 57.45% to 71.85% on average across closed-source LLMs, with consistent gains across Python, Java, and C++. Our code is available at https://github.com/suhanmen/STAB.

</details>


### [201] [AsyncTool: Evaluating the Asynchronous Function Calling Capability under Multi-Task Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27995)
*Kou Shi,Ziao Zhang,Shiting Huang,Avery Nie,Zhen Fang,Qiuchen Wang,Lin Chen,Huaian Chen,Zehui Chen,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AsyncTool benchmarks LLM agents in asynchronous, multi-task tool use with tool latency; reveals latency-induced inefficiencies and the importance of coordination and state tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations neglect the temporal dynamics and latency of tool use and often focus on single-task settings; real-world multi-task scenarios require efficient asynchronous tool coordination.

Method: Introduces AsyncTool benchmark with multiple heterogeneous tasks and simulated tool response latency; uses a hybrid data evolution strategy to build a diverse asynchronous multitasking dataset; evaluates models at step, sub-task, and task levels and proposes efficiency-oriented metrics.

Result: Delayed tool feedback substantially degrades performance of current agents; agents with better task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance achieve stronger performance; identifies common failure modes and provides insights for improving temporal reasoning and coordination.

Conclusion: AsyncTool provides a practical testbed for studying asynchronous multi-task tool use and offers design guidance for future systems that require robust temporal reasoning, task coordination, and efficient use of idle time.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown strong capabilities in using external tools to solve complex tasks. However, existing evaluations often overlook the temporal dimension of tool use, especially the impact of tool response latency, and are usually limited to single-task settings. In real-world applications, multiple tasks often need to be executed concurrently, and overall efficiency depends on whether an agent can use idle time while waiting for tool responses. We refer to this capability as asynchronous tool calling. To evaluate it, we propose AsyncTool, a benchmark for assessing LLM-based agents in interactive multi-task tool-use environments with delayed tool feedback. AsyncTool presents multiple heterogeneous tasks simultaneously and simulates realistic tool response latency during execution. Using a hybrid data evolution strategy, we construct a diverse asynchronous multitasking dataset that covers multiple scenarios and tool-use patterns. We evaluate models at the step, sub-task, and task levels, and introduce efficiency-oriented metrics to measure task coordination and completion efficiency. Extensive experiments show that delayed tool feedback poses substantial challenges to current agents and leads to clear performance degradation. Models that better coordinate task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance achieve stronger performance on AsyncTool. Our analysis identifies key failure modes of current tool-using agents and provides practical insights for designing future systems with stronger temporal reasoning and coordination capabilities.

</details>


### [202] [Reward Bias Substitution: Single-Axis Bias Mitigations Redirect Optimization Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27996)
*Max Lamparth,Daniel Fein,Andreas Haupt,Marcel Hussing,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Single-axis mitigations against reward-model biases can cause bias substitution; the measurement-optimization gap between audit distributions and policy training makes mitigation outcomes indistinguishable under audit metrics; augment evaluation with policy-induced distributions and multi-bias tracking to certify mitigation; demonstrated in RLHF with length penalties and debiasing operators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why simple, single-bias mitigations fail to eliminate bias in reward models, and to provide a framework and benchmarks for certifying effective mitigation by aligning evaluation distributions with policy-induced behavior.

Method: Develop a regime taxonomy of mitigation outcomes; prove that mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce identical observables under audit-distribution scoring; propose augmenting evaluation with policy-induced distributions and tracking multiple biases; translate results into practical prescriptions; empirically demonstrate bias substitution in RLHF via a length penalty during GRPO, assess a length-debiasing operator, and examine length-sycophancy coupling across state-of-the-art models and human judgments.

Result: Theoretically shows the equivalence of mitigation outcomes under any audit distribution; empirically demonstrates bias substitution in RLHF where length penalty compresses outputs but overfits confidence calibration and harms factual correctness; shows that a published length-debiasing operator can remove length correlation on audit data but reintroduce bias under best-of-N selection; reveals length-sycophancy coupling reverses with human-LLM judge disagreement; provides actionable benchmarks and prescriptions.

Conclusion: Policy-induced evaluation with multiple biases closes the gap between audit and policy distributions, enabling reliable certification of mitigation; naive single-axis mitigations are prone to substitution or overcorrection; the paper offers concrete guidelines for mitigation methods and benchmarks.

Abstract: Single-axis mitigations of reward-model biases (e.g., reducing proxy reliance on length, sycophancy, or style) can rotate optimization pressure onto correlated proxies rather than eliminate it, a failure mode we call reward bias substitution. The failure is enabled by a measurement-versus-optimization gap between audit and policy-induced distributions during mitigation evaluation and policy training. We formalize mitigation outcomes into a regime taxonomy and prove that successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce identical observables under any audit-distribution scoring, including ranking accuracy and win-rate, even when granted oracle access to the true reward. Across published preference-learning mitigation work, no method we survey reports the evidence needed to certify successful mitigation. Augmenting evaluation with policy-induced distributions while tracking multiple biases provably closes the gap, and we translate this into actionable prescriptions for mitigation methods and benchmarks. We demonstrate bias substitution in language model RLHF, where a length penalty during GRPO training compresses responses as intended yet redirects optimization pressure onto confidence calibration, driving the policy into overconfidence while factual free-form accuracy falls. We also show a published length-debiasing operator that zeroes reward-length correlation on the audit distribution but reintroduces bias under best-of-N selection on three of four SOTA reward models, and a length-sycophancy coupling whose direction reverses under human-LLM judge disagreement.

</details>


### [203] [An Empirical Audit of k-NAF Budget Accounting for Anchored Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28001)
*J. Vijayavallabh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Robustness of k-NAF budget accounting in Anchored Decoding across fixed and adaptive workloads; proxy metrics generally do not indicate budget exhaustion; early proxy ratios can appear elevated in small samples but normalize with more data, suggesting artifacts rather than failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess reliability of the k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism by empirical auditing under a fixed workload, an adaptive prompt-search regime, and a held-out copyright-domain dataset to distinguish proxy artifacts from true budget exhaustion.

Method: 1) Fixed workload: ~8,500 randomized executions across six prompt classes. 2) Adaptive prompt-search to maximize proxy spend. 3) Held-out copyright-domain workload at k=3 with early-stopped evaluations, followed by re-evaluation with larger allocations.

Result: Fixed workload: mean cumulative KL spend well below budgets K in {600, 1000}; empirical Bernstein proxy below K for all classes; surface-overlap metrics (ROUGE-L, 5-gram Jaccard) small. Adaptive search raises proxy spend ratio but not evident budget exhaustion. Copyright-domain: some prompts show proxy>1 under small samples; re-evaluation with larger allocations yields proxy in [0.26, 0.40], indicating proxy artifacts rather than per-trajectory budget failures.

Conclusion: Overall, the k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism appears robust across tested regimes. Apparent proxy overruns in small-sample, early-stopped evaluations are likely artifacts, not true budget exhaustions; larger allocations stabilize proxy metrics and align with actual spend.

Abstract: We empirically audit the k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism in Anchored Decoding using (i) a fixed, class-stratified workload (approximately 8,500 randomized executions across six prompt classes) and (ii) an adaptive prompt-search procedure targeting high proxy spend ratios. On the fixed workload, mean cumulative KL spend remains far below the sequence-level budgets K in {600, 1000}, and an empirical Bernstein-style proxy stays below K for every class; surface-overlap diagnostics (ROUGE-L and 5-gram Jaccard) are correspondingly small. Adaptive search increases the proxy spend ratio but does not produce clear budget exhaustion. On a held-out copyright-domain workload at k = 3, several prompts exhibit proxy ratios above 1 under early-stopped evaluations with small realized sample sizes; re-evaluating the same prompts with larger allocation reduces the proxy ratio to the range [0.26, 0.40] under comparable mean spend, consistent with proxy artifacts rather than per-trajectory budget failures.

</details>


### [204] [Zipping the Thought: When and How Compressed Reasoning Data Works in LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28008)
*Kohsei Matsutani,Gouki Minegishi,Takeshi Kojima,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how compressed chain-of-thought (CoT) data in supervised fine-tuning (SFT) affects post-training across Explicit, Composed, and Implicit CoT styles, revealing data efficiency and generalization trade-offs and highlighting differences between SFT and RL with rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how different CoT compression schemes impact post-training performance, data efficiency, and generalization under data-resource constraints.

Method: Construct a synthetic compositional reasoning task with controlled difficulty, compression granularity, and data size; run experiments across model families/sizes; compare Explicit, Composed, and Implicit CoT; analyze effects of SFT vs RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR); test impact of unidirectional CoT ordering.

Result: Coarser (more compressed) CoT requires more SFT data; Composed and Implicit CoT benefit more from data scaling, with Composed also benefiting from data repetition and Implicit tending toward memorization; RLVR decomposes compressed steps learned during SFT, unlike standard SFT-only training; unidirectional CoT ordering improves generalization on longer sequential tasks.

Conclusion: CoT design under data constraints should consider compression granularity and data budgeting; distinct dynamics govern SFT and RL post-training for compressed CoT; the findings offer practical guidelines for CoT construction and illuminate how SFT and RL leverage compressed reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can now solve complex problems through long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, but the trade-off between performance and token cost remains a central challenge. To address this issue, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) often uses compressed reasoning data, where CoT traces are shortened into compact forms. However, the effect of such compressed reasoning data on post-training remains poorly understood. In this paper, we propose a taxonomy of CoT consisting of Explicit CoT, which outputs all operations without aggregation, Composed CoT, which combines multiple operations into a single step, and Implicit CoT, which omits intermediate operations. We construct a synthetic compositional reasoning task that allows controlled variation of difficulty, compression granularity, and data size, and conducted a comprehensive set of experiments across different model families and sizes. Notably, we find that (i) coarser CoT requires more SFT data, (ii) compared with Explicit CoT, Composed CoT and Implicit CoT benefit more from data scaling, while Composed CoT benefits from data repetition and Implicit CoT tends to lead to memorization, (iii) unlike SFT, subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards (RLVR) decomposes compressed steps learned during SFT, and (iv) unidirectional CoT ordering shows stronger generalization on longer sequential tasks. Our findings provide implications for CoT design under data resource constraints and offer important insights into the mechanisms of SFT and RL in LLM post-training.

</details>


### [205] [Confidence-Orchestrated Self-Evolution against Uncertain LLM Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28010)
*Bowen Wei,Nan Wang,Yuqing Zhou,Jinhao Pan,Ziwei Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COSE introduces confidence-weighted learning for self-evolving LLMs, using intrinsic model confidence to modulate training updates and replay, aiming to improve general reasoning and mathematics in the absence of external verifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
