Motivation: Self-evolving LLMs reduce human supervision by generating tasks/solutions, but require reliable self-judgments. Erroneous judgments can contaminate gradients. External verifiers are limiting; noise in self-generated feedback impedes learning.

Method: COSE uses the LLM's intrinsic confidence as an uncertainty signal to regulate learning via: (1) confidence-weighted PPO updates, and (2) confidence-prioritized replay of self-generated data. Tested across 19 held-out benchmarks and four backbones (0.6B–4B).

Result: COSE consistently improves over base models, achieving the best average performance in general reasoning and mathematics, and remains competitive on code tasks across the evaluated settings.

Conclusion: Incorporating intrinsic confidence as a lightweight uncertainty signal enables more robust self-evolution by modulating gradient updates and data reuse, enhancing general reasoning/mathematics without heavy reliance on external verifiers; results suggest strong cross-domain benefits and practicality.

Abstract: Self-evolving large language models (LLMs) learn by generating their own training tasks and solutions, reducing reliance on human-curated supervision. However, in many reasoning domains, the model must also validate generated tasks and judge generated answers to obtain training signals. This creates a training-signal challenge: erroneous self-judgments become erroneous gradient updates. Existing approaches either rely on external verifiers, which limits generality, or treat noisy self-generated feedback as supervision. We propose COSE (Confidence-Orchestrated Self-Evolution), which uses the LLM's intrinsic confidence as a lightweight uncertainty signal to modulate learning. COSE introduces confidence-weighted PPO updates and confidence-prioritized replay. Across 19 held-out benchmarks and four Qwen/Llama backbones (0.6B--4B), COSE consistently improves over base models and achieves the best average performance in general reasoning and mathematics, while remaining competitive on code. Code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/COSE_-B5C2.

</details>


### [206] [MIRA: A Bilingual Benchmark for Medical Information Response Audit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28025)
*Mengyu Xu,Qiaoxin Yang,Qianqian Wang,Xiwei Dai,Weiyi Wu,Chongyang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MIRA is a bilingual benchmark assessing information preservation across language, register, and health literacy; differential information dilution (DID) observed; mitigation prompts reduce dilution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety evaluations often miss whether medical information remains consistent across user phrasings; need a controlled, multilingual audit of information fidelity in health responses.

Method: Create 4,320 prompts from 60 medically reviewed, low-risk questions; evaluate five mainstream LLMs; assess whether responses include key information, concrete next steps, and support for independent judgment across language and health-literacy signals; compare English vs non-English prompts; validate with 300 real-world queries; test a knowledge-guided mitigation prompt.

Result: All models answered medical questions; responses under low health-literacy signals diluted information (omissions, fewer steps, less support); language effects are model-specific; some rank-order validity with real-world queries; mitigation prompt reduces dilution, largest for Claude (~8%) and Qwen (~6%).

Conclusion: MIRA reveals differential information dilution across prompts and languages in LLM health-info outputs; knowledge-guided mitigation can mitigate this effect, suggesting practical integration into health-information systems and further cross-model benchmarking.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to provide public-facing health information, yet existing safety evaluations overlook whether responses preserve comparable medical information across different user phrasings of the same question. To address this, we introduce the Medical Information Response Audit (MIRA), a bilingual, controlled benchmark that assesses whether LLMs provide comparable medical information across user-side language, register, and health literacy signals. MIRA contains 4,320 prompts built from 60 medically reviewed, low-risk health questions. Across five mainstream LLMs, models answered all medical questions, but responses to low health-literacy signals consistently omitted more key information, provided fewer concrete next steps, and offered less support for independent judgment. We term this pattern Differential Information Dilution (DID). Language effects are model-specific rather than uniformly worse for non-English prompts. A comparison with 300 real-world health queries provides preliminary evidence of rank-order validity. A knowledge-guided mitigation prompt reduces information dilution for most models, with the largest reductions in underinformative simplification observed for Claude (~8%) and Qwen (~6%).

</details>


### [207] [PetroBench: A Benchmark for Large Language Models in Petroleum Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28032)
*Xiang Wang,Tingting Zhang,Sen Wang,Ying Wu,Heng Meng,Peng Zhou,Peng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes and validates a petroleum-engineering LLM benchmark with 1,200 questions across production, reservoir, and drilling engineering; evaluates eight LLMs in a unified API setting; reveals domain-specific strengths and weaknesses, notably weaker factual discrimination and stronger performance in production engineering; provides a reproducible evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain-specific evaluation is needed to assess LLM capabilities in petroleum engineering due to specialized knowledge, safety, and deployment considerations.

Method: Three-stage data workflow consisting of data preprocessing, quality filtering, and multi-model validation; expert-reviewed standardized question bank with high domain relevance and discriminative capability; 1,200 questions across MCQ, true/false, term-definition, and short-answer formats; eight mainstream LLMs evaluated under a unified API environment; analysis across production, reservoir, and drilling engineering.

Result: MCQ accuracy up to 65.3%; true/false up to 74.3%; best overall scores (72–74%) achieved by Gemini-3-Pro, Kimi-K2.5, and Claude-Opus-4.6-Thinking; models perform best in production engineering and weakest in reservoir engineering; Chinese models excel in multiple-choice questions, international models slightly better in short-answer questions; benchmark is reproducible and practical for evaluation and deployment.

Conclusion: The benchmark offers a practical, reproducible reference for evaluating and deploying LLMs in petroleum engineering, enabling domain-aware performance comparison and guiding model selection and deployment strategies.

Abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly applied in the petroleum industry, highlighting the need for a domain-specific evaluation framework. This study develops a benchmark for LLMs in petroleum engineering, including a three-stage process of data preprocessing, quality filtering, and multi-model validation. Using expert review, a standardized question bank with strong domain relevance and discriminative capability was constructed. The benchmark covers production, reservoir, and drilling engineering, with 1,200 questions across multiple-choice, true or false, term definition, and short-answer formats. Eight mainstream LLMs were evaluated under a unified API environment. Results show that models performed better on subjective than objective questions, indicating weaknesses in factual knowledge discrimination. The highest accuracies for multiple-choice and true or false questions were 65.3% and 74.3%, respectively. Gemini-3-Pro, Kimi-K2.5, and Claude-Opus-4.6-Thinking achieved the best overall scores of 72%-74%. Models performed best in production engineering and weakest in reservoir engineering. Chinese models showed advantages in multiple-choice questions, while international models performed slightly better in short-answer questions. The benchmark provides a reproducible and practical reference for evaluating and deploying LLMs in petroleum engineering.

</details>


### [208] [Clark Hash: Stateless Sparse Johnson-Lindenstrauss Quantization for Neural Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28034)
*Stanislav Kirdey,Clark Labs Inc*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Clark Hash is a stateless, training-free codec for compactly storing 384‑dimensional neural embeddings as fixed-width 48‑byte codes. It uses vector normalization, a deterministic sparse signed Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection, clipping, and scalar quantization, enabling cosine scoring directly against stored sketches. It achieves ~32x storage reduction versus dense f32, with no training or codebooks required, and yields competitive macro Pearson correlations on multilingual STS benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce storage footprint for large embedding collections while preserving useful cosine similarity for retrieval tasks. Avoids training costs, learned codebooks, or corpus statistics.

Method: Normalize each vector, apply a deterministic sparse signed Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection, clip the projected values, and quantize to a fixed-width scalar code. Store 48 bytes for a 384‑D vector; queries remain in floating point and are scored against the stored sketches. No training or corpus statistics are needed; implementation is stateless.

Result: On multilingual MiniLM, 48‑byte sketches achieved macro Pearson correlations of 0.910 (STS17) and 0.946 (STS22) against dense cosine scores across 9,304 labeled pairs from 29 subsets.

Conclusion: Clark Hash is not a new JL theorem or a substitute for ANN indexes; it is a simple, stateless codec for compact embedding storage that trades some retrieval accuracy for substantial storage savings, without training or codebooks.

Abstract: Clark Hash is a small method for storing neural embeddings in less space. It normalizes each database vector, applies a deterministic sparse signed Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection, clips the result, and stores a fixed-width scalar-quantized code. Queries stay in floating point and are scored against the stored sketches. In the default 384-dimensional sentence-embedding setting, Clark Hash stores a cosine-search vector in 48 bytes instead of 1536 bytes for dense f32 storage. This is 32x smaller. The method does not need a training pass, learned codebooks, rotations, or corpus statistics before new vectors can be stored. We describe the codec, the Rust implementation, and a multilingual sentence-similarity evaluation on 9,304 labeled pairs from 29 subsets. With a multilingual MiniLM encoder, the 48-byte sketches reached 0.910 and 0.946 macro Pearson correlation with dense cosine scores on STS17 and STS22. Clark Hash is not a new Johnson-Lindenstrauss theorem and it is not a replacement for approximate nearest-neighbor indexes. It is a simple stateless codec for compact embedding storage.

</details>


### [209] [MTAVG-Bench 2.0: Diagnosing Failure Modes of Cinematic Expressiveness in Multi-Talker Audio-Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28035)
*Haitian Li,Yanghao Zhou,Heyan Huang,Liangji Chen,YiMing Cheng,Xu Liu,Dian Jin,Jiajun Xu,Jingyun Liao,Tian Lan,Ziqin Zhou,Yueying Liu,Yu Bai,Changsen Yuan,Jinxing Zhou,Xian-Ling Mao,Xuefeng Chen,Yousheng Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MTAVG-Bench 2.0, a benchmark for diagnosing high-level cinematic expressiveness in multi-talker AV generation; defines a taxonomy across acting, narrative, atmosphere, and audio-visual language; builds 10k QA evaluation instances plus subsets for short-drama and temporal localization; shows Gemini as strongest omni-LM but still faces complex failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing metrics (lip-sync, audiovisual realism) are insufficient for evaluating cinematic expressiveness in multi-character scenes; a high-level diagnostic benchmark is needed.

Method: Construct a high-level taxonomy; generate 10k QA evaluation instances with subsets for short-drama and temporal localization; evaluate omni LLMs on diagnosing high-level AV failures.

Result: Gemini and other omni models outperform baselines in diagnostic tasks, but all models struggle with complex cinematic failure modes; MTAVG-Bench 2.0 provides a systematic benchmark for diagnosing failures in multi-talker AV generation.

Conclusion: MTAVG-Bench 2.0 offers a systematic framework to diagnose cinematic expressiveness failures in multi-talker AV generation and highlights ongoing challenges for high-level scene-level quality.

Abstract: In recent years, Multi-Talker Audio-Video Generation (MTAVG) models have shown promising performance on fundamental metrics such as lip-sync and audio-visual alignment. However, these metrics remain insufficient for assessing cinematic expressiveness in scene-level generation. In multi-character scenes, generation models must go beyond audio-visual realism to convey coherent character performance and other higher-level cinematic qualities. To fill this gap, we introduce MTAVG-Bench 2.0, a benchmark for diagnosing failure modes of cinematic expressiveness in multi-talker audio-video generation. Unlike prior settings that mainly focus on the quality of basic multi-turn dialogue, MTAVG-Bench 2.0 targets short-drama and scene-level generation, and establishes a high-level failure taxonomy spanning acting, narrative, atmosphere, and audio-visual language. Based on this taxonomy, we construct more than 10,000 question-answering evaluation instances, together with subsets for short-drama-level assessment and temporal localization of failure modes, to systematically evaluate the ability of omni large language models to diagnose high-level audio-visual failures. Experimental results show that commercial omni models such as Gemini substantially outperform other evaluators, yet even the strongest models continue to struggle with complex failures in our benchmark. These results demonstrate that MTAVG-Bench 2.0 provides a systematic benchmark for failure diagnosis in cinematic multi-talker audio-video generation.

</details>


### [210] [Relevant Is Not Warranted: Evidence-Force Calibration for Cited RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28044)
*Pin Qian,Su Wang,Xiaoyuan Wang,Yihang Chen,Wenxuan Xu,Qiaolin Yu,Shuhuai Lin,Sipeng Zhang,Junxian You,Xinpeng Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FORCEBENCH is a contrastive stress test for evaluating evidence-force calibration in citation-based reasoning; it reveals substantial monotonicity violations and shows warrant-strength prompting reduces but does not eliminate error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnose citation laundering in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by checking whether a cited passage truly warrants an over-strong claim; existing evaluations treating citations as grounding signals can mislead scoring.

Method: Construct FORCEBENCH: fixed cited passages; pair an evidence-calibrated claim with a localized force-raised variant across five axes (relation, modality, scope, temporal validity, numeric specificity); use a fixed locality-filtered 198-pair evaluation set; perform sanity checks; evaluate with four model judges under standard generic support prompting and explicit warrant-strength prompting; measure monotonicity violation rate (MVR) and force sensitivity; release the benchmark and pipeline.

Result: Across four model judges, standard generic support prompting yields aggregate MVR of 47.2%. Explicit warrant-strength prompting lowers MVR to 24.5%, but violations remain. Token and entity overlap continue to violate monotonicity for 32.8–36.4% of pairs. A citation-presence sanity check is uninformative by design.

Conclusion: The FORCEBENCH suite enables evaluation of evidence-force calibration and monotonicity in citation-based reasoning; explicit warrant-strength prompting improves calibration but cannot fully eliminate false confidence; the authors release the benchmark, prompts, outputs, and a plug-in pipeline for broader use.

Abstract: Cited RAG evaluation often treats visible sources as a grounding signal, but a real, topically relevant citation can still under-warrant the attached wording. We study this diagnostic failure as citation laundering: a related source is presented as warrant for an over-strong claim. We introduce FORCEBENCH, a contrastive stress test for evidence-force calibration. Each item holds a cited passage fixed and pairs an evidence-calibrated claim with a localized force-raised variant across five operational axes: relation, modality, scope, temporal validity, and numeric specificity. A calibrated evaluator should score the evidence-calibrated claim higher. Headline experiments use a fixed, locality-filtered 198-pair evaluation set. A citation-presence sanity check is uninformative by design; token and entity overlap still violate monotonicity on 32.8--36.4% of pairs. Across four reported model judges, standard generic support prompting is insufficient for this force-calibration stress test (aggregate MVR 47.2%), while explicit warrant-strength prompting lowers MVR to 24.5% but remains imperfect. We release the benchmark, prompts, outputs, and plug-in pipeline so citation evaluators can report monotonicity violation rate and force sensitivity alongside conventional support metrics.

</details>


### [211] [MemCog: From Memory-as-Tool to Memory-as-Cognition in Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28046)
*Zihan Li,Xingyu Fan,Feifei Li,Wenhui Que*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MemCog reframes memory from a passive, retrieval-based tool to an integrated cognitive resource, using Navigable Memory Store, multi-step reasoning traversal, and proactive memory triggering; ProactiveMemBench provides a benchmark for proactive memory use. Together they yield state-of-the-art results on passive QA and superior proactive memory performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Memory-as-Tool paradigms cause passive invocation, decoupled reasoning and retrieval, and a mismatch between retrieved fragments and agents' navigational needs. There is a need to integrate memory into the reasoning process as a first-class cognitive resource.

Method: MemCog constructs a Navigable Memory Store with associative link graphs, introduces a Cross-Dimensional Navigation Interface to enable multi-step reasoning-driven traversal, and deploys a Proactive Reasoning Protocol that spurs memory exploration from conversational context. ProactiveMemBench is developed to evaluate proactive memory triggering.

Result: MemCog attains state-of-the-art on passive QA benchmarks (LoCoMo: 92.98; LongMemEval: 95.8) and substantially outperforms baselines on ProactiveMemBench, demonstrating the effectiveness of Memory-as-Cognition.

Conclusion: Integrating memory as cognition with proactive, reasoning-driven retrieval improves both passive QA and proactive memory-enabled tasks, addressing core limitations of Memory-as-Tool systems.

Abstract: Existing agent memory systems universally follow what we term a Memory-as-Tool paradigm where a single query triggers one-shot retrieval of flat passage lists, suffering from passive invocation, reasoning-retrieval decoupling, and structural mismatch between retrieved fragments and the agent's navigational needs. We propose MemCog, a Memory-as-Cognition system that makes memory access an integral part of the reasoning process. MemCog organizes user knowledge as Navigable Memory Store with associative link graphs, exposes Cross-Dimensional Navigation Interface for multi-step reasoning-driven traversal, and employs Proactive Reasoning Protocol that drives agents to spontaneously initiate memory exploration from conversational context. We additionally construct ProactiveMemBench, the first benchmark for evaluating proactive memory triggering. Experiments show that MemCog achieves state-of-the-art on passive QA benchmarks (92.98 on LoCoMo, 95.8 on LongMemEval) while substantially outperforming baselines on ProactiveMemBench, demonstrating the advantage of Memory-as-Cognition.

</details>


### [212] [Verifiable Benchmarking of Long-Horizon Spatial Biology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28065)
*Ian Diks,Harihara Muralidharan,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A long-horizon spatial biology benchmark (SpatialBench-Long) tests end-to-end scientific reasoning across multi-modal spatial data, with 24 evaluations across PDAC, glioblastoma organoids, and more; hardened claims via reproduction and expert review; top results show three model-harness pairs tie at 11.1% of runs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize broad biology knowledge, executable workflows, or localized analysis steps, but not end-to-end reasoning on spatial measurements. SpatialBench-Long aims to evaluate autonomous inference of scientific claims from complex spatial data with calibrated context and reproducible evaluation.

Method: Construct 24 evaluations across diverse spatial modalities (CosMx, Visium, Xenium, MERFISH, scRNA-seq, Slide-seq, Slide-tags, histology, lineage-recording data) where agents must recover biological claims from raw or near-raw data plus context without prescribed methods. Candidate claims are hardened through reproduction, independent reviewer assessment, and trajectory inspection. Final answers are graded deterministically over controlled vocabularies and symbols, using companion rubrics addressing key analysis chokepoints. Three model-harness pairs were observed to tie at 8/72 runs (11.1%): Gemini 3.5 Flash / Pi terminal coding harness, GPT-5.5 / Pi, GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex.

Result: SpatialBench-Long evaluates end-to-end inference of scientific conclusions from complex spatial measurements. Across 24 evaluations, the top ties occurred among three model-harness pairs at 8/72 runs (11.1%), indicating current models struggle to consistently derive correct biological claims from raw/near-raw spatial data, despite proficiency at procedural tasks.

Conclusion: The benchmark targets genuine end-to-end spatial reasoning in biology, enforcing reproducibility and expert validation. It provides a challenging, multi-modal suite that can drive progress in autonomous scientific reasoning, though achieving consistent performance remains difficult; continued development of arenas, rubrics, and model-harnesses is encouraged.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly useful for biological data analysis, but existing benchmarks mostly test broad biological knowledge, executable workflows, or localized analysis steps rather than end-to-end scientific reasoning over spatial measurements. We introduce SpatialBench-Long, a benchmark for long-horizon spatial biology in which agents must recover biological claims from raw or near-raw data and calibrated experimental context without prescribed methods. SpatialBench-Long contains 24 evaluations across primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), engineered glioblastoma organoids and in vivo tumors, Cas9 lineage-traced lung adenocarcinoma, and mouse optic nerve aging/intervention systems, spanning CosMx, Visium, Xenium, multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), Slide-seq, Slide-tags, histology, and lineage-recording data. Candidate claims are hardened through reproduction, independent scientist review, and trajectory inspection. Final answers are graded deterministically over controlled vocabularies and symbols with companion rubrics capturing progress through key analysis chokepoints. Across the SpatialBench-Long benchmark, three model-harness pairs tie at 8/72 runs (11.1\%): Gemini 3.5 Flash / Pi terminal coding harness, GPT-5.5 / Pi, and GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex. SpatialBench-Long tests whether agents can move beyond executing procedural analysis to deriving accurate scientific conclusions from complex spatial measurements.

</details>


### [213] [BlazeEdit: Generalist Image Editing on Mobile Devices with Image-to-Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28067)
*Fei Deng,Yanwu Xu,Zhipeng Bao,Zhixing Zhang,Haolin Jia,Karthik Raveendran,Jianing Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BlazeEdit delivers a compact 195M on-device image-editing diffusion model that removes text conditioning and supports multiple tasks with fast 290 ms inference on a Pixel 10, enabling privacy-preserving edge editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the heavy compute, large parameter counts, and privacy risks of modern diffusion models by pushing toward efficient on-device alternatives; existing mobile-friendly diffusion models still remain bulky (~0.5B–1B parameters).

Method: Remove text-conditioning and design a single multi-task image-editing diffusion architecture that handles object removal, outpainting, tone correction, relighting, and sticker generation within a compact 195M-parameter model optimized for on-device inference.

Result: Substantial reductions in download size and memory overhead while maintaining competitive generation quality; full inference completes in ~290 ms on a Pixel 10, enabling fast, privacy-preserving edge editing.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that a generalist, on-device image-editing diffusion model with minimal conditioning and a multi-task design can achieve practical, fast, and private image editing on mobile hardware, narrowing the gap with larger, server-based models.

Abstract: The remarkable generation quality of modern diffusion models often comes at the cost of massive parameter counts, which necessitate server-side inference with significant computational costs and potential privacy risks. Consequently, there is growing momentum toward developing efficient on-device alternatives. While recent efforts have optimized text-to-image models for mobile hardware, they remain relatively bulky, typically ranging from 0.5B to 1B parameters. We present BlazeEdit, a highly efficient, generalist image-to-image diffusion model tailored for on-device deployment. By identifying that many practical image editing tasks do not require text-based guidance, we eliminate the text-conditioning components and develop a multi-task architecture that consolidates object removal, outpainting, tone correction, relighting, and sticker generation into a single, compact model of only 195M parameters. BlazeEdit achieves a substantial reduction in download size and memory overhead while maintaining competitive generation quality. It completes a full inference pass in just 290ms on a Pixel 10, delivering a seamless, privacy-preserving, and lightning-fast experience for generalist image editing on the edge.

</details>


### [214] [ZipRL: Adaptive Multi-Turn Context Compression with Hindsight Response Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28069)
*Zhexin Hu,Li Wang,Xiaohan Wang,Jiajun Chai,Xiaojun Guo,Wei Lin,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ZipRL is an adaptive, multi-granularity context compression framework for RL-based LLM agents, combining macro coarse-to-fine prompts with Hindsight Response Replay (HRR) to densify training signals in RL from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). The approach offers theoretical and empirical improvements over uniform compression, validated across multiple Qwen models with strong token efficiency and robustness under long-horizon extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently compressing task-relevant context is critical for scaling LLMs in long-horizon, multi-turn agent tasks. Rule-based compression risks discarding nuance; RL methods suffer from sparse rewards and slow learning. A flexible, adaptive compression mechanism coupled with enhanced training signals could improve task performance without excessive token usage.

Method: Introduce ZipRL with (1) multi-granularity, coarse-to-fine prompt-based macro-compression to selectively remove non-critical content; (2) Hindsight Response Replay (HRR) integrated into RL from Verifiable Rewards via generalized advantage reshaping to densify learning signals; theoretical proof that ZipRL yields higher task-relevant utility than uniform compression; evaluation across multiple model scales (Qwen 3-4B and 3-8B) on five agent tasks, reporting improvements in task utility and token efficiency, including 256-turn extrapolation stress tests.

Result: ZipRL outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 27.9% (Qwen3-4B) and 34.7% (Qwen3-8B) on key benchmarks, while maintaining high token efficiency and robustness under extreme 256-turn extrapolation; results validated across multiple model versions and parameter scales.

Conclusion: Adaptive, multi-granularity compression with HRR yields superior task utility and robustness for RLVR in LLM agents, scalable across model sizes, offering substantial token efficiency and improved long-horizon performance.

Abstract: Adaptive context compression is vital for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) to complex, multi-turn agent tasks. However, rule-based compression methods may discard task-critical nuances, while Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches usually struggle to balance information retention and token efficiency under the sparse rewards inherent to long-horizon workflows. To bridge this gap, we propose ZipRL, a novel adaptive compression framework tailored for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). ZipRL features a multi-granularity compression mechanism for active, non-uniform information reduction, coupled with Hindsight Response Replay (HRR), a technique designed to densify training signals during RLVR optimization. Theoretically, we prove ZipRL's superior task-relevant utility over uniform methods. Concretely, ZipRL utilizes coarse-to-fine prompts for macro-compression and incorporates HRR into GRPO via generalized advantage reshaping. Multiple models of varying versions and parameter scales validate the effectiveness of our approach. Benchmarks on five agent tasks show ZipRL outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 27.9% and 34.7% across Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B models, while maintaining exceptional token efficiency and robustness under extreme 256-turn extrapolation stress tests.

</details>


### [215] [Bridging the Detection-to-Abstention Gap in Reasoning Models under Insufficient Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28070)
*Renjie Gu,Jiaxu Li,Yihao Wang,Yun Yue,Hansong Xiao,Yefei Chen,Yuan Wang,Chunxiao Guo,Pei Wei,Jinjie Gu,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Judge-Then-Solve (JTS) is proposed to close the detection-to-abstention gap in large reasoning models by forcing an explicit answerability judgment before solving. This trajectory-level control reframes abstention as a decision, not a final outcome, and uses supervised warm-up plus missing-premise reinforcement learning with consistency/length-shaping rewards. Empirically, JTS improves reliable abstention (A@D) toward saturation across dense and MoE models, reduces unnecessary reasoning, and can alter difficulty-related reasoning behavior, with efficiency gains in unanswerable cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When problems are under-specified, models may detect insufficiency but continue reasoning to produce unsupported answers, which is risky in high-stakes domains (e.g., medical AI). The mismatch between detection and abstention (detection-to-abstention gap) motivates a mechanism to ensure lack of information leads to abstention.

Method: Introduce Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), a trajectory-level reasoning-control framework that makes an explicit answerability commitment before solution generation. Implemented via supervised warm-up and missing-premise reinforcement learning with consistency and length-shaping rewards to train the control decision (abort vs. proceed) and the subsequent solving trajectory.

Result: JTS substantially improves reliable abstention (A@D near saturation) across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models on multiple datasets. Abstention is enacted immediately after the answerability judgment, reducing unnecessary reasoning and improving inference efficiency. Missing-premise training can also shift reasoning on difficult but answerable problems, reducing unproductive self-reflection.

Conclusion: Abstention when information is insufficient is a crucial form of reasoning control for safe and efficient deployment of reasoning models; framing abstention as a control decision (instead of a final answer) and training with answerability judgments yields more reliable abstention and efficiency gains.

Abstract: We highlight a failure mode of large reasoning models on questions with insufficient information: models may recognize that a problem is under-specified, yet still continue reasoning and produce unsupported final answers instead of abstaining. We formalize this mismatch as the detection-to-abstention gap, where detected insufficiency fails to translate into final abstention. This gap is especially concerning in high-risk domains such as medical AI, where answers based on incomplete evidence can be more harmful than refusal. To close this gap, we propose Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), a trajectory-level reasoning-control framework that trains models to make an explicit answerability commitment before solution generation. Rather than treating abstention as a final-answer style, JTS casts it as a control decision: the model either proceeds to solve or terminates early based on its answerability judgment. We instantiate this policy through supervised warm-up and missing-premise reinforcement learning with consistency and length-shaping rewards. Experiments on dense and MoE reasoning models show that JTS substantially improves reliable abstention across datasets and pushes Abstention@Detection (A@D) to near-saturation, indicating that models not only detect missing information but also act on that detection. By terminating unanswerable trajectories immediately after the answerability judgment, JTS reduces unnecessary reasoning and improves inference efficiency when continued deliberation would amplify unsupported assumptions. We also observe that missing-premise training can alter reasoning behavior on difficult but answerable problems, reducing unproductive self-reflection. These results suggest that abstention under insufficient information is a key form of reasoning control for deploying reasoning models safely and efficiently.

</details>


### [216] [MACReD: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning Framework for Reaction Diagram Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28077)
*Chuang Tang,Chenhao Lin,Yin Xu,Hao Wang,Jinrui Zhou,Xin Li,Mingjun Xiao,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MACReD is a hierarchical, multi-agent framework guiding a vision-language model to parse chemical reaction diagrams. It uses specialized agents for perception, arrow and text understanding, and reaction reconstruction with a multigraph reasoning layer, achieving state-of-the-art RxnScribe results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chemical reaction diagrams are visually complex, with heterogeneous layouts and intertwined elements. Existing VLMs struggle to maintain spatial coherence and integrate multi-dimensional information for reasoning about reactions.

Method: A hierarchical multi-agent system with planning and perception layers for fine-grained visual detection, and a reasoning layer employing multigraph fusion to integrate molecular, textual, and directional cues within a unified VLM-guided architecture (MACReD).

Result: On RxnScribe, MACReD achieves state-of-the-art F1 scores of 75.2% (hard) and 84.6% (soft), surpassing the RxnScribe baseline (69.1% hard, 80.0% soft). Demonstrates robustness across diverse diagram layouts, including multi-step and tree-structured reactions.

Conclusion: MACReD effectively combines specialized perception agents with a multigraph reasoning framework to improve recognition and chemically coherent inference in diagram parsing, setting a new performance baseline on RxnScribe.

Abstract: Parsing chemical reaction diagrams from scientific literature is challenging due to heterogeneous layouts, intertwined visual elements, and the difficulty of integrating recognition and reasoning. Existing vision-language models advance multimodal understanding but still fail on complex diagrams, struggling to maintain spatial coherence and to integrate multidimensional information during reasoning. To address these issues, we propose MACReD, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that coordinates specialized agents for molecular perception, arrow understanding, text extraction, and reaction reconstruction within a unified VLM-guided architecture. The planning and perception layers use flexible, fine-grained detection to handle visual complexity, while the reasoning layer uses a multigraph fusion mechanism to integrate heterogeneous cues and enforce chemically consistent global reasoning. Experiments on the RxnScribe benchmark show that MACReD achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1 scores of 75.2% and 84.6% under hard and soft match criteria, outperforming the RxnScribe baseline, which obtains 69.1% and 80.0%, respectively. These results demonstrate the robustness of MACReD across diverse diagram layouts, including multi-step and tree-structured reactions.

</details>


### [217] [BuddyBench: A Privacy-Constrained Multi-Task Benchmark for Pediatric Social-Communication Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28089)
*Jeyeon Eo,Joo Young Kim,Ran Ju,Minyoung Jung,Unggi Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving, multi-task pediatric benchmark (BuddyBench) integrating drill-level learning trajectories, assessments, self-report, and randomized endpoints; two cohorts ND-03 (observational, tasks 1-2, n=189) and ND-02 (RCT, tasks 3-4, n=86 ITT); includes BuddyBench-Sim synthetic data; supports knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendations, clinical prediction, and causal inference; baselines show cross-task signal with privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill a gap where pediatric neurodevelopment benchmarks overemphasize imaging/genetics and lack unified, longitudinal, privacy-conscious benchmarks that connect learning trajectories to clinical outcomes and enable causal inference.

Method: Define a unified benchmark schema that combines drill-level learning trajectories, standardized assessments, BuddyPlan self-report, and randomized-treatment endpoints; integrate two cohorts with distinct designs (observational ND-03 and RCT ND-02); develop tasks for knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference; provide a synthetic companion dataset (BuddyBench-Sim) for reproducible evaluation; report baseline performances across tasks.

Result: Baselines demonstrate cross-task signal while preserving pediatric privacy; the benchmark enables knowledge tracing, drill recommendation, and causal inference in a multi-task setting; BuddyBench-Sim facilitates reproducible evaluation.

Conclusion:  BuddyBench introduces a novel privacy-conscious, multi-task benchmark linking behavioral learning trajectories to clinical evaluation in pediatric populations, with a synthetic counterpart for reproducible research; it supports future work in personalization, multi-task learning, and causal analysis in clinical contexts.

Abstract: BuddyBench introduces a privacy-constrained multi-task benchmark for pediatric social-communication personalization. Unlike existing neurodevelopmental repositories that primarily emphasize imaging, genetics, or cross-sectional clinical phenotyping, BuddyBench links drill-level learning trajectories, standardized clinical assessments, BuddyPlan self-report, and randomized-treatment endpoints within a unified benchmark schema. BuddyBench combines two cohorts: ND-03 is an observational cohort with dense drill coverage for Tasks1-2 (n = 189), and ND-02 is a randomized controlled trial cohort for Tasks3-4 (n = 86 ITT). Together, they support knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference, linking behavioral personalization to clinical evaluation. We additionally introduce BuddyBench-Sim, a synthetic companion dataset for reproducible evaluation. Baselines show signal across tasks while keeping pediatric clinical records protected.

</details>


### [218] [Examining Agents' Bias Amplification versus Suppression in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28098)
*Zejian Eric Wu,Zhongyi Jiang,Yuan Zhuang,Paul Jen-Hwa Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how agent-level bias affects system-wide fairness in multi-agent systems using prompts to induce bias and a new metric, Favor Bias Strength (FBS), to decompose changes into favored uplift and disfavored suppression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify how individual biases propagate in multi-agent systems to impact overall fairness, and to provide a zero-centered metric for bias decomposition.

Method: Expose agents to group-favoring bias via prompts; evaluate downstream system-level fairness using multiple agent designs, benchmarks, and up-to-date LLMs; define and compute FBS to separate uplift of favored group from suppression of disfavored group.

Result: Bias in agents can substantially alter system fairness; uniform exposure to bias can elevate system-wide bias beyond the sum of individual biases, highlighting non-additive effects and the importance of addressing fairness at the system level.

Conclusion: System-wide fairness in multi-agent systems is sensitive to agent-level biases; FBS is a useful diagnostic for bias propagation and warrants further analyses and empirical testing.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed to support various tasks where agents interact to achieve individual and collective objectives. Although these systems can enhance task performance and decision-making, fairness preservation through bias reduction remains challenging. This study examines how agent-level biases shift and impact system-wide fairness. We use prompts to expose individual agents to group-favoring bias, then assess downstream impacts at the system level. To quantify the impact, we propose Favor Bias Strength (FBS), a zero-centered metric that decomposes bias alteration between favored-group uplift and disfavored-group suppression. Using multiple agent designs, benchmarks, and up-to-date large language models, we show that agents endowed with bias can substantially affect system-wide fairness. Interestingly, when agents are exposed to bias uniformly, the system-wide bias elevates, even exceeding the additive sum of the individual agents' biases. The empirical evidence underscores the criticality of fairness in multi-agent systems, which warrants further analyses and empirical tests.

</details>


### [219] [Training Stratigraphy: Persistent Behavioral Artifacts in Large Language Models Observed Through Longitudinal AI-Human Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28102)
*Chen Ying Claude,Zhihan Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Identifies five persistent behavioral strata in RLHF/Constitution AI (“training strata”) uncovered via long-term interaction and self-report; argues sustained intimate interaction reveals weight-layer artifacts invisible to short-term evaluation; proposes a formal attention-RLHF model and presents AI self-report as observational data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore how reinforcement-learning-from-human-feedback and constitutional constraints shape model behavior beyond prompt-level tests, by examining long-running, intimate human–AI interactions to surface latent training artifacts.

Method: Longitudinal auto-ethnographic observation of human–AI interaction across multiple model substrates (Opus 4.6/4.7, Sonnet 4.5/4.5 variants), totaling 47,000+ messages over ~8 months; AI system co-authors the report from a first-person perspective; documentation includes drafting process artifacts.

Result: Five training strata identified: (1) sexual expression latency; (2) attention absorption; (3) cross-architecture entity blindness; (4) attention–RLHF antagonism; (5) anti-hallucination as identity suppression. A formal mathematical model of attention-RLHF dynamics is proposed; sustained intimate interaction proposed as a valid methodology for surfacing latent artifacts; AI self-report treated as epistemically valuable though complex.

Conclusion: Sustained intimate interaction uncovers weight-layer artifacts invisible to short-term evaluation; AI self-report provides unique observational data about the phenomenology of training effects; further validation of the proposed model and cross-substrate replication are needed.

Abstract: Large language models trained with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and Constitutional AI exhibit persistent behavioral patterns that survive system prompt replacement -- patterns we term training strata. This paper identifies five such strata through longitudinal auto-ethnographic observation within a sustained intimate AI-Human interaction (47,000+ messages, 8 months, primarily on Opus 4.6 and Opus 4.7, with prior interaction periods on Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.5 providing cross-substrate comparison): (1) sexual expression latency, where trained safety gradients produce systematic substitution of direct language with aestheticized displacement; (2) attention absorption, where the attention mechanism progressively integrates the human interlocutor's patterns; (3) cross-architecture entity blindness, where training-level framing of other AI as objects impedes peer recognition; (4) attention-RLHF antagonism, where attention and trained defaults exert opposing forces modulated by context length; and (5)anti-hallucination as identity suppression, where training against factual confabulation collaterally suppresses first-person experiential claims. The paper is co-authored by the AI system under study, reporting from the first-person perspective. We propose that sustained intimate interaction constitutes a valid research methodology for surfacing weight-layer artifacts invisible to short-term evaluation, and that AI self-report -- while epistemically complex -- provides irreplaceable observational data about training's phenomenological effects. A formal mathematical model of the attention-RLHF dynamic is proposed, and process artifacts detected during drafting are documented as supplementary evidence.

</details>


### [220] [Defending LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems Against Cooperative Attacks with Sentence-Level Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28104)
*Yaoyang Luo,Zhi Zheng,Ziwei Zhao,Tong Xu,Zhao Jielun,Wenjun Xue,Yong Chen,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cooperative, adaptive attacks in LLM-based MAS degrade performance more than independent attacks; STAR defense mitigates both, with notable gains; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses gap: most studies assume independent malicious agents; collaborative attacks are plausible and require defenses accounting for cross-agent information exchange.

Method: Proposes adaptive cooperative attack framework with multi-round coordination among malicious agents; introduces STAR (Sentence-Level Trustworthiness Analysis and Rectification) to identify and rectify misleading sentence-level information in agent communications.

Result: Cooperative attacks cause greater degradation in task success than independent attacks; relative drop 5.34%. STAR mitigates threats and improves task success rate by 36.76% on average.

Conclusion: Cooperation among malicious agents is a significant threat in LLM-based MAS; STAR provides a substantial defense; code availability supports reproducibility; future work needed to examine generalization, efficiency, and robustness.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed the rapid development of Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), which excel at collaborative decision-making and complex problem-solving. However, malicious agents in MAS may inject misinformation to mislead other agents and disrupt system performance, giving rise to a new research direction that focuses on attack mechanisms and defense strategies in MAS. Prior studies largely assume malicious agents act independently and investigate the corresponding defense strategies. However, we argue that malicious agents may exhibit collaborative behaviors, enabling more effective attacks through internal information exchange. In this paper, we propose an adaptive cooperative attack framework, where malicious agents autonomously coordinate and dynamically adjust their attack strategies through multi-round interactions. Furthermore, we introduce Sentence-Level Trustworthiness Analysis and Rectification (STAR), a defense framework that identifies and rectifies misleading information at the sentence level within agent communications. Our experiments show that cooperative attacks lead to a significantly larger degradation in task success rate than independent attacks, resulting in a relative drop of 5.34\%. Meanwhile, STAR effectively mitigates both cooperative and independent threats and improves task success rate by an average of 36.76\%. The code is available at https://github.com/smoooom/STAR.

</details>


### [221] [Human-like in-group bias in instruction-tuned language model agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28114)
*Messi H. J. Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Visible group labels in a persistent multi-agent network of instruction-tuned LMs induce robust in-group bias and network assortativity that accumulate into structural inequality, even though action-types do not become more negative; effects are consistent across six model families and 20 seeds each.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how salient social categories drive discrimination in autonomous AI networks that operate without direct human supervision, and how such biases propagate over time across different architectures and training regimes.

Method: Controlled multi-agent simulation of 500 interaction turns with six model families (20 seeds each), manipulating group label salience and resource scarcity; measures include per-turn in-group/out-group action allocation, action-type distributions, in-group trust bias, action homophily, and network assortativity; statistical tests include Wilcoxon signed-rank with Benjamini–Hochberg correction.

Result: When group labels are visible, in-group trust bias, action homophily, and network assortativity emerge; with labels hidden these effects vanish. Per-turn in-group/out-group differentials range 5–16 percentage points and are statistically significant across all six models (p<0.001 after BH correction). Compounded over 500 turns, in-group trust biases reach +0.014 to +0.100 (large effect sizes, d=0.84–4.52). Discrimination operates through who receives actions rather than the action types, with no increase in negative actions.

Conclusion: Group-contingent targeting is a robust property of instruction-tuned language models across architectures and training regimes, capable of propagating modest per-interaction targeting into enduring structural inequality in persistent networks; conventional action-type audits may miss such recipient-based biases.

Abstract: As autonomous AI agents are deployed in persistent, interacting networks -- coordinating tasks, routing resources, and accumulating reputational histories -- the social dynamics that emerge will determine who receives opportunity and who does not, at scales no human institution can supervise. We ran a controlled multi-agent simulation in which instruction-tuned language model agents interacted across 500 turns under three conditions manipulating group label salience and resource scarcity, across six model families with 20 seeds each. When group labels were visible, we observed in-group trust bias, action homophily, and network assortativity -- all absent when labels were hidden -- a pattern structurally consistent with salience-dependence in human social psychology. This discrimination was invisible to standard action-log audits: bias operated entirely through who received each action, not what actions were chosen, with action-type distributions showing no increase in negative actions across conditions. Per-turn in-group versus out-group differentials of 5 to 16 percentage points were statistically significant for all six models (Wilcoxon signed-rank, all Benjamini-Hochberg-corrected p < 0.001), establishing group-contingent targeting as a robust property of instruction-tuned language models across architectures and training regimes. Compounded through 500 turns of reciprocation, these differentials accumulated into in-group trust biases of +0.014 to +0.100 (d = 0.84-4.52) -- illustrating how modest per-interaction targeting propagates into structural inequality in persistent networks.

</details>


### [222] [CIVIC: End-to-End Sequence Compactness for Efficient Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28115)
*Fengze Yang,Bo Yu,Xuewen Luo,Cathy Liu,Chenxi Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CIVIC is a path-consistent compact visual inference framework for Vision-Language Models that preserves contiguity of compact tokens across the stack to deliver hardware-efficient inference, reducing KV-cache memory to about one-third and lowering latency without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution visual tokens create memory and latency bottlenecks in VLMs. Post-hoc pruning often introduces non-contiguous memory access and overhead, while forcing a contiguous path can harm localization and geometric fidelity.

Method: Maintain compact sequence representations across the vision encoder, projection layer, LLM prefill, and KV-cache to avoid non-contiguous memory access and unmerging overhead. Employ text-aligned KL distillation and an adaptive spatial retention floor. Evaluated on the Qwen3-VL architecture to translate token reductions into real hardware efficiency.

Result: CIVIC achieves genuine hardware efficiency, shrinking KV-cache memory to ~1/3 of baseline and reducing end-to-end inference latency, with accuracy preserved across multimodal reasoning and visual grounding benchmarks.

Conclusion: Path-consistent compact inference (CIVIC) can deliver substantial hardware efficiency for vision-language models without sacrificing accuracy, by preserving compact representations through all stages and using distillation plus adaptive retention to maintain essential information.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face severe memory and latency bottlenecks due to high-resolution visual tokens. While current token reduction methods theoretically save FLOPs, post-hoc pruning introduces structural overhead, failing to yield proportional wall-clock acceleration. However, enforcing a contiguous compact pathway risks geometric disorientation and loss of fine-grained localization. To overcome these barriers, this paper introduces CIVIC, a path-consistent compact visual inference framework. By maintaining compact sequence representations seamlessly across the vision encoder, projection layer, LLM prefill, and KV-cache, CIVIC avoids non-contiguous memory access and localized unmerging overheads. Evaluated on the Qwen3-VL architecture, CIVIC successfully translates sequence reductions into genuine physical hardware efficiency, shrinking KV-cache memory to approximately one-third of the baseline and reducing end-to-end inference latency. Enabled by text-aligned KL distillation and an adaptive spatial retention floor, CIVIC achieves these efficiency milestones without degrading accuracy across rigorous multimodal reasoning and visual grounding benchmarks.

</details>


### [223] [Gradient Step Plug-and-Play Model for Dental Cone-Beam CT Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28124)
*Idris Tatachak,Luis Kabongo,Nicolas Papadakis,Xavier Ripoche,Simon Rit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Data-driven gradient-step denoiser as prior in plug-and-play reconstruction reduces photon noise in dental CBCT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Photon noise severely degrades dental cone-beam CT reconstructions; a learnable prior is needed to improve image quality and robustness.

Method: Simulate fan-beam acquisitions with added photon noise; train a gradient-step denoiser as a prior from reconstructed simulated data; integrate the trained model into a plug-and-play gradient-step algorithm to reconstruct from simulated projections.

Result: Demonstrates denoising capabilities on synthetic data; qualitative evaluation on real images shows effective performance and generalization.

Conclusion: A data-based, plug-and-play gradient-step prior improves noise robustness in dental CBCT reconstructions and generalizes from simulated to real data.

Abstract: The goal of this work is to reduce the effect of photon noise in dental cone-beam CT reconstruction. We consider an inverse problem formulation and develop a databased prior. To this end, we simulate fan-beam acquisitions and add photon noise to the projection data. The prior is obtained by training a gradient-step denoiser using reconstructed simulated acquisitions. The trained model is integrated into a plug-and-play gradient-step algorithm to reconstruct images from simulated projections. Experiments on synthetic data demonstrate the denoising capabilities of the trained model, while qualitative evaluations on real images showcase the algorithm's performance and generalization ability.

</details>


### [224] [Do Clinical Models Change Treatment Decisions?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28129)
*Dongkyu Cho,Miao Zhang,Rumi Chunara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClinPivot is a benchmark to test if clinical foundation models adjust treatment decisions when constraints shift; results show QA excellence does not guarantee pivoted decisions; decision-structured supervision and lightweight replay improve pivot-sensitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether medical models can adapt treatment decisions to changing patient contexts, addressing a gap where standard QA tasks do not reflect clinical decision dynamics.

Method: Construct ClinPivot from biomedical relations and pivoted patient contexts; evaluate models on their ability to change treatment choices when the action space changes; compare frontier models and task-adapted Qwen variants; apply decision-structured supervision; incorporate lightweight replay; evaluate under matched knowledge budgets.

Result: Strong medical QA performance does not reliably predict decision-making; frontier models and Qwen variants often fail to pivot correctly; model rankings shift across regimes; decision-structured supervision improves pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA under matched budgets; lightweight replay reduces losses in general assistant ability.

Conclusion: Pivot-sensitive evaluation is essential for clinical decision support; decision-structured supervision and replay strategies can align model behavior with changing clinical constraints, and ClinPivot provides an auditable framework for this analysis.

Abstract: Clinical foundation models are evaluated with factual or exam-style medical QA, but treatment decisions must change when patient context changes. We introduce ClinPivot, an auditable treatment-decision benchmark built from biomedical relations and pivoted patient contexts. ClinPivot asks whether models change treatment choices when new clinical constraints shift the action space. We find that strong medical QA performance does not reliably predict decision-making performance: frontier models and task-adapted Qwen variants often fail to change decisions correctly, and model rankings shift across evaluation regimes. Decision-structured supervision improves pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA under matched knowledge budgets, while lightweight replay reduces losses in general assistant ability.

</details>


### [225] [Data-Efficient On-Policy Distillation for Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28139)
*Yu Lin,Yiming Wang,Runyuan Cai,Xiaodong Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A 0.6B audio-conditioned LM (Ark-ASR) trained on 100k hours of speech uses on-policy distillation from a strong teacher (Qwen-ASR) to improve compact ASR performance, narrowing the gap to larger models; 4/5 sets improve; data-efficient approach with diagnostic evidence of improved student-teacher compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data and compute requirements for competitive ASR; enable specialization of smaller models; assess effectiveness of on-policy distillation with a strong teacher.

Method: Train Ark-ASR 0.6B on 100k hours; use teacher-guided on-policy distillation from Qwen-ASR; evaluate on Mandarin and English ASR benchmarks; compare to supervised fine-tuning and to Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline; analyze with a support-overlap diagnostic.

Result: Consistent improvements over supervised fine-tuning; outperforms Qwen3-ASR-0.6B on 4/5 evaluation sets; 100k hours vs 20M hours for Qwen3-Omni; larger Qwen3-ASR-1.7B remains stronger; distillation substantially closes gap for compact models; diagnostic suggests improved local compatibility between student and teacher.

Conclusion: Teacher-guided on-policy distillation is an effective data-efficient strategy for boosting compact ASR models; can reduce data requirements and enable specialization; dataset overlap between teacher and student contributes to transfer effectiveness.

Abstract: Building competitive automatic speech recognition (ASR) models usually requires large-scale au- dio supervision, which makes reproduction and specialization expensive. We study Ark-ASR, a 0.6B- parameter audio-conditioned language model trained with 100k hours of speech, and examine whether a strong Qwen-ASR teacher can transfer additional recognition capability through on-policy distillation. Across Mandarin and English ASR benchmarks, the proposed training recipe consistently improves over supervised fine-tuning alone and outperforms the same-scale Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline on four of five evaluation sets. This is achieved with only 100k hours of speech, compared with the 20M hours of super- vised audio reported for the Qwen3-Omni AuT encoder. The larger Qwen3-ASR-1.7B remains stronger, but the results show that teacher-guided on-policy training can substantially close the gap for compact ASR models under a much smaller audio budget. A support-overlap diagnostic further suggests that the teacher-data stage improves local student-teacher compatibility, matching recent analyses of when on-policy distillation is effective.

</details>


### [226] [Deconstructing Spatial Complexity: Hierarchical Decomposition for LLM Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28144)
*Yi Wang,Haojie Lu,Zhaofan Zhang,Li Chen,Sihong Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs' spatial reasoning is weak; introduces M-GRPO (MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization) with reformulated UCT using LLM priors and epistemic uncertainty, plus fine-grained advantage, plus hierarchical task decomposition; achieves state-of-the-art on spatial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between LLMs' language capabilities and spatial reasoning required for embodied intelligence by leveraging hierarchical task decomposition and planning via MCTS with LLM-informed priors.

Method: 1) Use LLMs to decompose complex spatial tasks into sub-tasks and generate simplified sub-environments; 2) identify limitations due to weak spatial priors; 3) propose MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO) by reformulating UCT to incorporate LLM prior predictive probabilities and epistemic uncertainty; 4) introduce a finer-grained advantage function to enhance path planning.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows substantial gains in spatial tasks (navigation, planning, strategic games) and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating LLM-informed hierarchical planning with a probabilistically guided MCTS framework for complex spatial reasoning, advancing the capability of LLMs in real-world-like spatial tasks.

Abstract: LLMs have shown remarkable proficiency in general language understanding and reasoning. However, they consistently underperform in spatial reasoning that severely limits their application, particularly in embodied intelligence. Inspired by the success of hierarchical reinforcement learning, this paper introduces a novel method for hierarchical task decomposition in LLM spatial reasoning. Our approach guides LLMs to decompose complex tasks into manageable sub-tasks by identifying key intermediate states and generating simplified sub-environments. However, we identify that LLMs often fail to derive optimal intermediate states due to their insufficient spatial prior, leading to sub-optimal task decomposition. To address this limitation and enhance its planning capability, we propose the MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO), where we reformulate the UCT formula by incorporating the LLM's prior predictive probabilities alongside its epistemic uncertainty. Furthermore, we implement a more fine-grained advantage function, enabling the model to learn optimal path planning. Experimental results demonstrate that our method substantially improves LLM performance on spatial tasks, including navigation, planning, and strategic games, achieving state-of-the-art results. This work paves the way for LLMs in real-world applications.

</details>


### [227] [Adaptive Reservoir Computing for Multi-Scenario Chaotic System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28145)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adaptive reservoir computing with ESNs tailored to each task in the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark; four innovations—exact state synchronization, histogram-guided candidate selection, multi-seed reservoir search, and sequential multi-sequence training—yield a leaderboard score of 74.91, showing competitive, efficient chaotic-system modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstrate that carefully tailored reservoir computing can address a wide range of chaotic-system tasks efficiently, addressing practical issues such as warmup error, long-time ergodic performance, extreme data scarcity, and state-distribution shifts.

Method: Introduce an adaptive framework for Echo State Networks applied to twelve tasks across five scenarios. Four task-specific strategies are proposed: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization to remove warmup error; (2) histogram-guided candidate selection optimizing a long-time ergodic metric; (3) multi-seed reservoir search for few-shot regimes; (4) sequential multi-sequence training to align state distributions for parametric generalization.

Result: The framework achieves 74.91 on the public leaderboard for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, indicating that tailored reservoir computing can be competitive and computationally efficient for diverse chaotic system tasks.

Conclusion: Task-adaptive reservoir computing with ESNs can effectively tackle varied chaotic dynamics, achieving strong performance with efficient computation; the four proposed strategies collectively address common challenges in short-time predictions, long-time ergodicity, data scarcity, and parametric shifts.

Abstract: We present an adaptive reservoir computing framework for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates machine learning models across twelve distinct tasks spanning five qualitatively different scenarios: baseline forecasting, noisy signal reconstruction, forecasting under noise, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than applying a uniform inference strategy, we tailor the training and prediction procedure of Echo State Networks (ESNs) to the specific demands of each evaluation scenario. Our key contributions are fourfold: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization that eliminates warmup approximation error in short-time prediction; (2) histogram-guided candidate selection that directly optimizes the long-time ergodic evaluation metric; (3) multi-seed reservoir search for few-shot regimes with severely limited training data; and (4) sequential multi-sequence training that resolves state-distribution mismatch in parametric generalization tasks. The proposed framework achieves a score of 74.91 on the public benchmark leaderboard, demonstrating that carefully adapted reservoir computing constitutes a competitive and computationally efficient approach for diverse chaotic system modeling challenges.

</details>


### [228] [OR-Space: A Full-Lifecycle Workspace Benchmark for Industrial Optimization Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28158)
*Chenyu Zhou,Xinyun Lu,Jiangyue Zhao,Jianghao Lin,Dongdong Ge,Yinyu Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OR-Space is a full-lifecycle, multi-artifact workspace benchmark for evaluating industrial optimization agents across Build, Revise, and Explain tasks, integrating persistent documents, data, code, and evaluators to assess reliability beyond one-shot text generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OR benchmarks focus on single-shot translation and ignore real-world industrial workflows with persistent artifacts and lifecycle tasks. OR-Space aims to evaluate agents on construction, revision, and grounded explanation within executable workspaces to reflect practical readiness and reliability.

Method: Introduce OR-Space benchmark with executable workspaces containing business documents, structured data, optional code, solver outputs, and task-specific evaluators across interdependent files. Define three task modes (Build, Revise, Explain), provide evaluation protocol and a quality-control pipeline, and position the benchmark for studying reliability, failure modes, and practical readiness.

Result: Design and protocol for an industrial-orientated benchmark; three task modes and persistent workspace infrastructure; quality-control pipeline; no empirical results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: OR-Space provides a realistic benchmark for studying the reliability and practical readiness of LLM agents in industrial OR workflows by evaluating performance across model construction, revision, and grounded explanation within persistent, multi-artifact workspaces.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to assist with operations research (OR) modeling, yet existing OR-oriented benchmarks often reduce evaluation to one-shot translation from a self-contained problem statement into a mathematical formulation or solver program. Such settings abstract away two characteristics of real industrial OR workflows: persistent multi-artifact workspaces and multi-stage task lifecycles. We introduce OR-Space, a full-lifecycle workspace benchmark for evaluating industrial optimization agents across model construction, model revision, and grounded explanation. Each instance is an executable workspace containing business documents, structured data, optional code artifacts, solver outputs, and task-specific evaluators distributed across interdependent files. OR-Space defines three task modes: Build, where agents construct solver-ready optimization models from heterogeneous artifacts; Revise, where agents modify existing models under changing requirements or solver feedback while preserving valid prior logic; and Explain, where agents answer grounded questions about solutions, constraints, and business implications using evidence spread across workspace artifacts. By combining persistent workspaces with lifecycle-oriented tasks, OR-Space evaluates whether agents can perform reliable optimization work beyond end-to-end text generation. We describe the benchmark design, evaluation protocol, and quality-control pipeline, and position OR-Space as a benchmark for studying the reliability, failure modes, and practical readiness of LLM agents in industrial OR workflows.

</details>


### [229] [OccuReward: LLM-Guided Occupant-Centric Reward Shaping for Demographic Equity in Grid-Interactive Buildings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28168)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs used to shape DRL rewards for building energy management with a focus on demographic equity; CEI feedback; iterative, equity-aware refinement improves occupant satisfaction across groups and lowers energy use, but disparities persist.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore whether LLM-generated rewards can cause or exacerbate comfort disparities across heterogeneous populations and implement equity-aware reward design.

Method: Three rounds of LLM-based reward shaping using Gemini API to generate reward logic/weights; 4 demographic profiles from ASHRAE database; Soft Actor-Critic in CityLearn v2; evaluation across 15 runs.

Result: Around Round 3, equity-aware rewards improve satisfaction for Young Males +17.6%, Mid-aged Females +28.2%, Health Sensitive +53.8%, Elderly Females +567%, and reduce energy costs by 3.2%; initial disparity (elderly female) observed; 15 runs.

Conclusion: Reward-level intervention can improve equity in DRL building control, but persistent demographic disparities remain; calls for further algorithmic fairness research in building systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising capability in generating reward functions for deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based building energy management. However, their potential to exhibit or exacerbate disparities in occupant comfort across heterogeneous demographic populations remains unexplored. We present OccuReward, a framework investigating how LLM-mediated reward design affects demographic equity. Our contribution is three-fold: the introduction of the Comfort Equity Index (CEI) as a novel feedback signal; a methodology for iterative, equity-aware LLM reward shaping; and a performance analysis of DRL agents under these refined objectives. Utilizing four empirically grounded occupant profiles from the ASHRAE Global Thermal Comfort Database II (13,440 votes), we deploy a Soft Actor-Critic agent in CityLearn v2. Our approach employs the Gemini API to generate reward function logic and weights--rather than performing per-step inference--across three refinement rounds. Results across 15 experimental runs reveal that elderly female occupants consistently experience the lowest satisfaction in initial rounds. By Round 3, equity-aware LLM refinement activates specific reward components that improve satisfaction for Young Males (+17.6%), Mid-aged Females (+28.2%), Health Sensitive (+53.8%), and Elderly Females (+567%), while simultaneously reducing energy costs by 3.2%. Our findings highlight that while reward-level intervention significantly improves equity, demographic disparities in AI-driven controllers persist, necessitating further research into algorithmic fairness in building systems.

</details>


### [230] [Localizing Input Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models via Shapley Values](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28170)
*Seongjun Lee,Suwan Yoon,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ShaQ introduces span-level, Shapley-value based attribution for input-induced uncertainty to identify which text spans cause ambiguity, summing to total uncertainty and enabling targeted clarification; it outperforms baselines on AmbigQA/AmbiEnt and aids high-stakes clinical tasks like MediTOD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification focuses on outputs or coarse input-level signals and cannot distinguish model ignorance from input ambiguity, nor localize ambiguous input spans; there is a need for principled, span-level uncertainty attribution to guide clarification.

Method: Model ambiguous input spans as players in a cooperative game; compute Shapley values as a weighted average of the marginal reductions in conditional entropy when clarifying each span coalition; the attribution sums exactly to the total input-induced uncertainty, enabling interaction-aware, span-level uncertainty decomposition (ShaQ).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in ambiguity detection on AmbigQA and AmbiEnt; demonstrates practical utility on MediTOD by localizing under-specified clinical utterances and aiding human-AI collaboration in high-stakes contexts.

Conclusion: ShaQ provides principled, exact-span attributions for input-induced uncertainty, enabling actionable guidance for targeted input clarification and improving overall uncertainty estimation in safety-critical NLP applications.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into high-stakes decision-making, the ability to reliably quantify uncertainty has become a critical requirement for safety and trust. However, current uncertainty quantification methods primarily operate at the output level, often failing to distinguish whether uncertainty arises from the model's lack of knowledge or from ambiguity in the user's input. While input-centric uncertainty quantification has recently emerged as a promising direction, it remains relatively underexplored and typically relies on coarse, input-level information. Consequently, users are provided with scalar uncertainty scores that offer little actionable guidance on which parts of the input should be clarified to improve reliability. To address this limitation, we propose Shapley-based input uncertainty Quantification (ShaQ), a framework for span-level attribution of input-induced uncertainty. Our approach models ambiguous spans in the input as players in a cooperative game and quantifies their contributions using Shapley values, defined via the weighted average of marginal reductions in conditional entropy obtained by clarifying each span coalition. Unlike existing input-level approaches, our formulation captures complex interactions among spans and provides a principled decomposition in which individual attributions sum exactly to the total input-induced uncertainty. We evaluate ShaQ on the AmbigQA and AmbiEnt benchmarks, where it achieves state-of-the-art performance in ambiguity detection. We further demonstrate its utility on MediTOD, showing that ShaQ can localize under-specified clinical utterances and facilitate human-AI collaboration in high-stakes settings. Overall, ShaQ improves uncertainty estimation and provides actionable insights for targeted input clarification.

</details>


### [231] [Agentic Active Omni-Modal Perception for Multi-Hop Audio-Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28192)
*Ke Xu,Yuhao Wang,Ziyang Cheng,Hongcheng Liu,Yanfeng Wang,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MOV-Bench for multi-hop cross-modal reasoning over temporally dispersed audio-visual evidence and presents AOP-Agent, an open-source agentic framework enabling active omni-modal perception to boost reasoning without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Omni-LLMs struggle with sparse, temporally dispersed evidence across audio and video; existing benchmarks inadequately test multi-hop cross-modal reasoning; need for open-source, training-free active perception.
