Method: MOV-Bench consists of 519 questions requiring multi-hop reasoning over temporally dispersed audio-visual evidence. AOP-Agent combines a hierarchical omni-modal memory with an observe-reflect-replan loop to enable active perception for open-source Omni-LLMs; no extra training or proprietary models; evaluations on MOV-Bench and OmniVideoBench.

Result: Current Omni-LLMs underperform on multi-hop cross-modal reasoning; AOP-Agent yields consistent performance gains, especially for long videos and reasoning-intensive questions; demonstrated on MOV-Bench and OmniVideoBench.

Conclusion: Active perception with hierarchical memory and observe-reflect-replan improves open-source Omni-LLMs’ multi-hop cross-modal reasoning; MOV-Bench provides a challenging testbed for advancing such capabilities.

Abstract: Multi-hop audio-visual reasoning remains challenging for Omni-LLMs, as relevant evidence is often sparse, temporally dispersed, and distributed across both audio and visual streams. Existing benchmarks provide limited investigation of this setting, typically involving only a limited number of modalities, relevant temporal segments, or reasoning steps. In this work, we introduce MOV-Bench, a benchmark containing 519 carefully curated questions that require multi-hop reasoning over temporally dispersed audio-visual evidence. Evaluations on MOV-Bench reveal that current Omni-LLMs still struggle with multi-hop cross-modal reasoning. To address this challenge, we further propose AOP-Agent, an efficient agentic framework built on open-source Omni-LLMs for active omni-modal perception. By combining a hierarchical omni-modal memory with a collaborative observe-reflect-replan loop, AOP-Agent enables open-source Omni-LLMs to perform active perception without additional training or proprietary models. Experiments on MOV-Bench and OmniVideoBench demonstrate that AOP-Agent consistently improves reasoning performance, with particularly notable gains on long videos and reasoning-intensive questions.

</details>


### [232] [Plant, Persist, Trigger: Sleeper Attack on Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28201)
*Yongxiang Li,Moxin Li,Zhixin Ma,Fengbin Zhu,Dongrui Liu,Wenjie Wang,Fuli Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sleeper Attack: adversarial content can persist in an LLM agent across interactions, remaining dormant until activated by a benign query. A large benchmark (1,896 instances) demonstrates vulnerability across seven LLMs to multiple state targets, underscoring inadequacy of single-interaction defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work largely studies single-interaction attacks. This paper identifies a persistent, cross-session threat where adversarial content can reside in agent state (session context, memory, or reusable skills) and be triggered later, highlighting a new, harder-to-detect safety risk for LLM agents.

Method: Construct a benchmark with 1,896 instances across six real-world harmful outcomes, three attack strategies, and three agent state targets (session context, memory, reusable skills). Evaluate seven strong LLMs (open-source and closed-source). Provide code and data for replication.

Result: State-of-the-art LLM agents remain vulnerable to Sleeper Attacks; even when single-interaction attacks show low success, dormant adversarial content can be activated in later interactions, across multiple state targets. Demonstrated consistency across seven models.

Conclusion: Cross-session safety requires defenses beyond per-request filtering, including memory/state sanitization, isolation mechanisms, and robust cross-interaction safeguards. The work provides a benchmark and empirical evidence to guide future defense research and tooling.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents remain vulnerable to safety threats from the external environment, where attackers inject adversarial content into external observations such as tool-returned data, webpages, or MCP context, causing harmful agentic behaviors such as unsafe actions or incorrect outputs. Existing studies typically focus on single-interaction attacks, where the agent observes adversarial content and immediately exhibits harmful behavior within one user request. However, we show that adversarial content can also persist across interactions served by the same agent, making such threats harder to detect and mitigate. Specifically, adversarial content may persist in the agent state, remain dormant across interactions, and later be activated by a benign user query. We formalize this type of safety threat as Sleeper Attack. To evaluate it, we construct a benchmark with 1,896 instances covering six real-world harmful outcomes, three attack strategies, and three agent state targets: session context, memory, and reusable skills. Experiments on seven strong open-source and closed-source LLMs show that state-of-the-art LLM agents remain vulnerable to Sleeper Attack, even when they achieve low attack success rates under a single-interaction baseline. Our code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/skdvnfu23ihr9wdscnksf1asdffsaef.

</details>


### [233] [The Illusion of Opting in AI-Mediated Consequential Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28210)
*Eugene Yu Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI ethics should address the 'illusion of opting' by safeguarding meta-capacity; evaluate AI by its protection and cultivation of agentive capacity; propose three norms: existential honesty, ecological rationality, counterfactual reparation, with emphasis on disadvantaged groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems risk reducing meaningful choice by narrowing options and shaping behavior; there's a need to reframe evaluation from end-optimization to protection of the socially scaffolded capacity to form, contest, and own ends.

Method: Philosophical/normative analysis based on Ullmann-Margalit's opting; conceptual framework; normative imperatives; discuss implications for design and policy; no empirical data.

Result: Introduces the 'illusion of opting' concept; reframes AI evaluation around meta-capacity; identifies three imperatives; clarifies focus on disadvantaged populations; outlines potential design and policy implications.

Conclusion: To ethically govern AI-mediated decisions, focus on preserving and cultivating meta-capacity; adopt existential honesty, ecological rationality, and counterfactual reparation to acknowledge limits, respect diverse ecologies, and repair foreclosed alternatives.

Abstract: Drawing on Ullmann-Margalit's concept of opting (transformative, irrevocable, and shadowed by foreclosed alternatives), we show that current AI systems raise a profound ethical problem that existing AI ethics has not fully captured: the illusion of opting, in which persons and groups encounter the deceptive appearance of meaningful consequential choice while the agency needed to become genuinely capable of choosing is weakened. Against approaches that treat AI primarily as an optimizer of already given ends, we argue that AI systems should be evaluated by whether they protect and cultivate meta-capacity against the illusion of opting: the socially and institutionally scaffolded agentive capacity through which means and ends can be formed, contested, revised, and owned. This reframing is especially urgent for disadvantaged populations, who are least able to absorb the costs of the illusion of opting when AI-mediated pathways misdirect behavior and action. We propose three normative imperatives for AI-mediated consequential decisions: existential honesty, which acknowledges the limits of prediction; ecological rationality, which situates guidance within heterogeneous lived ecologies; and counterfactual reparation, which acknowledges and repairs foreclosed alternatives when AI-mediated decision-making pathways fail.

</details>


### [234] [Learning When to Optimize: Verified Optimization Skills from Expert GPU-Kernel Lineages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28213)
*Shuoming Zhang,Qiuchu Yu,Yangyu Zhang,Ruiyuan Xu,Xiyu Shi,Guangli Li,Xiaobing Feng,Huimin Cui,Jiacheng Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KLineage creates a curriculum of optimization skills by deriving backward, validation-gated lineage from expert GPU kernels, enabling LLMs to apply sound optimizations on new code, improving kernel quality and optimization efficiency versus memory-based baselines, with a held-out sanity check.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-driven GPU kernel optimization struggles to know when optimizations are valid; without explicit validation conditions and failure modes, LLMs risk incorrect or inefficient transformations. The work aims to capture expert knowledge about when to apply optimizations and under what constraints, making LLM-assisted optimization more reliable and portable.

Method: From expert kernels, KLineage traverses implementations backward through validation-gated simplifications, reversing accepted steps to create reusable optimization skills. Each skill encodes: optimization intent, code locality where it applies, validity conditions, observed effects, and failure conditions to Avoid. A downstream LLM then materializes these skills on new code under the same compile/correctness/profile gates. Evaluation uses five expert workloads across two NVIDIA architectures and a separate 22-instance held-out check to test against memorization.

Result: Lineage-derived skills outperform recent memory-based LLM-kernel baselines in both final kernel quality and optimization efficiency under a fixed budget. The held-out sanity check provides a safeguard against source-case memorization.

Conclusion: KLineage demonstrates that constructing an explicit, validated skill curriculum from expert lineage enables more reliable and efficient LLM-assisted kernel optimization, with improved generalization and reduced reliance on memorized patterns.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly used to generate GPU kernels, but they often know what optimizations to try without knowing when those optimizations are sound. We introduce KLineage, which learns this missing "when" knowledge from expert kernels: instead of relying on forward rollouts, KLineage walks expert implementations backward through validation-gated simplifications and reverses each accepted step into a reusable optimization skill. Each skill records not only the optimization intent, but also where it applies in code, what conditions made it valid, what effect it had, and what failures its assumptions avoid. A downstream LLM materializes these skills on new code surfaces under the same compile/correctness/profile gate. On five expert workloads across two NVIDIA architectures, these lineage-derived skills serve as an effective optimization curriculum, exceeding recent memory-based LLM-kernel baselines in both final kernel quality and optimization efficiency under the same fixed budget. We additionally use a separate 22-instance held-out check as a sanity test against source-case memorization.

</details>


### [235] [Explaining is Harder Than Predicting Alone: Evaluating Concept-based Explanations of MLLMs as ICL Visual Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28215)
*Carmen Quiles-Ramírez,Leticia L. Rodríguez,Nicolás Martorell,Natalia Díaz-Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Concept-based explainability in frozen multimodal LLMs under few-shot in-context learning is harder than classification; forcing formal, axiomatic explanations reduces accuracy, though correct predictions align with discriminative visual features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically study concept-based explainability of frozen MLLMs under few-shot ICL across increasing formal rigour, and assess whether explicit reasoning aids accuracy using an independent LLM-as-judge pipeline.

Method: Evaluate four state-of-the-art MLLMs on five increasingly formal conditions from baseline classification to Description Logics axiom generation, employing an independent LLM to judge explanations and measure predictive accuracy.

Result: Explanations degrade accuracy monotonically (93.8% to 90.1%) while explaining is harder than predicting; however, when models articulate class-discriminative visual features, explanation quality correlates with correct predictions.

Conclusion: MLLMs excel at visual classification but lack guidance via instruction-tuning for formal, machine-verifiable explainability; explicit reasoning is not universally beneficial and improved explainability requires targeted instruction or alignment.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to classify images from a few labelled examples. Yet, how these models use the provided context remains opaque. While Chain-of-Thought prompting is widely used, recent work argues that it may not reflect true internal computation.
  In this paper, we systematically evaluate the concept-based explainability of frozen MLLMs under few-shot ICL using five conditions of increasing formal rigour, ranging from baseline classification to Description Logics (DL) axiom generation.
  Evaluating four state-of-the-art MLLMs via an independent LLM-as-a-judge pipeline, we demonstrate that explaining is genuinely harder than predicting alone. Surprisingly, forcing models to generate formally structured, concept-based explanations degrades predictive accuracy monotonically (from 93.8% to 90.1%), contradicting the assumption that explicit reasoning universally aids performance.
  However, when models successfully articulate class-discriminative visual features, explanation quality strongly correlates with correct predictions. Our findings suggest that while MLLMs excel at visual classification, they lack the specific instruction-tuning required for formal, machine-verifiable explainability.

</details>


### [236] [When Does Memory Help Multi-Trajectory Inference for Tool-Use LLM Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28224)
*Xinzhe Li,Yaguang Tao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unified evaluation shows memory-method effects depend on the inference strategy in multi-trajectory tool-use LLM agents; four memory methods (trajectory-level reflection, raw observation injection, atomic fact extraction, within-expansion injection) under three inference strategies reveal that inference strategy is a confound; reflection matters primarily with MCTS; within-expansion injection boosts diversity for beam search; atomic fact extraction is accuracy-neutral but shortens trajectories by 19–26% on tasks with reusable structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the ambiguity in prior work where cross-trajectory memory gains could be due to the memory abstraction or the inference method. Provide a unified two-axis memory decomposition and a cross-strategy evaluation across diverse tool-use benchmarks to disentangle effects.

Method: Propose two axes for memory transfer: scope (within-expansion vs across trajectories) and content abstraction. Evaluate four memory methods (trajectory-level reflection, raw observation injection, atomic fact extraction, within-expansion injection) under three inference strategies (best-of-N, beam search, MCTS) on four tool-use benchmarks spanning SQL, knowledge-graph, and CLI, in a verifier-free deployment setting.

Result: The experiment reveals that the inference method is a confound: identical memory methods yield statistically different outcomes under different inference strategies on the same data. Reflection becomes significant only under MCTS; within-expansion injection improves diversity only for beam search; atomic fact extraction is accuracy-neutral but reduces trajectory length by 19–26% on tasks with reusable environmental structure.

Conclusion: Memory design interacts with the chosen inference strategy; a unified evaluation framework is essential to separate benefits due to memory representation from those due to reasoning strategy. Practically, atomic fact extraction reduces cost without sacrificing accuracy, while other memory methods provide conditional gains depending on the inference method.

Abstract: Multi-trajectory inference for tool-use LLM agents - generating multiple reasoning attempts and selecting among them - benefits from transferring knowledge across attempts so that later ones avoid the pitfalls of earlier ones. Existing cross-trajectory memory methods (trajectory-level reflection, atomic fact extraction, raw observation injection) are each evaluated under a single inference strategy on a single task, making it unclear whether reported gains reflect properties of the memory abstraction or of the inference method. We propose a unified framework that decomposes memory along two axes -- the scope of transfer (within an expansion vs. across trajectories) and the abstraction of the transferred content -- and evaluate four methods under three inference strategies (best-of-N, beam search, MCTS) on four tool-use benchmarks spanning SQL, knowledge-graph, and CLI environments, in a verifier-free setting that matches the deployment regime of practical agents. The experiment matrix identifies the inference method as a confound: the same memory method produces statistically distinct results under different inference strategies on the same examples. Reflection reaches significance only under MCTS (not under best-of-N); within-expansion injection (conditioning each candidate on prior siblings' outcomes) helps only diversity-starved beam search; and atomic fact extraction is accuracy-neutral but shortens trajectories by 19-26% on tasks with reusable environmental structure.

</details>


### [237] [PIRS: Physics-Informed Reward Shaping for SAC-Based Building Energy Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28232)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces PIRS: Physics-Informed Reward Shaping for SAC in building energy DRL, grounding comfort with ISO 7730 PMV within a weighted multi-objective reward. Shows competitive KPIs versus rule-based and manual baselines and clear gains over non-physics rewards, especially for load ramping and peak demand, though not claiming dominance under limited compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward design for deep RL in buildings is currently ad hoc and lacks explicit grounding in thermal–comfort physics. Anchoring comfort in PMV provides interpretability and standards-consistent guidance for balancing comfort with energy efficiency.

Method: Replace ad-hoc comfort proxies with a PMV-based comfort term inside a weighted multi-objective reward for Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Evaluate in CityLearn v2.1.2 with a central SAC agent trained for 50k steps over five seeds, comparing to a rule-based controller (RBC), a manually engineered reward (E2), an energy-only reward (E3), and a naive temperature-deviation comfort reward (E4).

Result: PIRS delivers district KPIs (cost, carbon, electricity) on par with the manual baseline while substantially outperforming non-physics-grounded designs, particularly on load ramping (1.78x) and daily peak demand compared to RBC (vs ~2.4x RBC for some baselines). All DRL policies exceed RBC at the given training budget, and the authors frame PIRS as an interpretable, standards-aligned foundation for reward design rather than a claim of dominance under limited compute.

Conclusion: Physics-grounded, ISO 7730 PMV-based reward shaping yields interpretable, standards-aligned improvements over ad-hoc designs and non-physics rewards in DRL-based building control; useful as a robust baseline for reward design, with performance approaching manual baselines under finite compute.

Abstract: Occupant comfort and grid-aware energy efficiency are competing objectives whose joint optimization depends critically on how reward functions are specified in deep reinforcement learning (DRL) controllers for buildings. Yet reward design remains largely ad hoc: comfort terms are either hand-tuned heuristics or simple temperature-deviation proxies without explicit grounding in thermal-comfort physics. We present PIRS (Physics-Informed Reward Shaping), which replaces these ad-hoc comfort proxies with the ISO 7730 Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) formulation inside a weighted multi-objective reward for Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). By anchoring the comfort signal in the ISO 7730 PMV formulation, PIRS improves reward interpretability and provides a standards-grounded comfort proxy without changing any other component of the learning pipeline. We evaluate PIRS in CityLearn v2.1.2 (challenge 2022 phase 1) with a central SAC agent trained for 50k steps over five random seeds, and compare against a rule-based controller (RBC), a manually engineered reward (E2), an energy-only reward (E3), and a naive temperature-deviation comfort reward (E4). District-level key performance indicators (KPIs), reported as ratios versus RBC, show that PIRS attains cost, carbon, and electricity metrics on par with the manual baseline while substantially outperforming non-physics-grounded designs -- particularly on load ramping (1.78x vs. ~2.4x RBC) and daily peak demand. All DRL policies remain above RBC at this training budget; we interpret this gap honestly and position PIRS as an interpretable, standards-aligned foundation for reward design rather than a claim of dominance over classical control at limited compute.

</details>


### [238] [AI, Take the Wheel: What Drives Delegation and Trust in Human-Computer Cooperative Question Answering?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28255)
*Maharshi Gor,Yoo Yeon Sung,Yu Hou,Eve Fleisig,Irene Ying,Tianyi Zhou,Jordan Boyd-Graber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two decoupled reliance decisions (delegation and adoption) shape human-AI collaboration. In a QA game with 23 experts and 16 AI agents, 387 delegation and 1440 adoption decisions were analyzed. Humans often under- or over-rely on AI, even when AI is correct or misleading. Confidence signals are poor when humans and AI disagree, and confirmation bias worsens under-reliance when AI aligns with an incorrect human answer. Calibrated confidence, evidence-grounded explanations, and trust refinement mechanisms are proposed to close the gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve human-AI collaboration by dissecting when humans delegate AI autonomy and when they adopt AI suggestions, addressing the gap that prior work treated these reliance decisions in isolation and often used unrealistic settings.

Method: Empirical study of collaborative human–AI teams in a question-answering game. 24 matches with 23 expert humans and 16 AI agents, yielding 387 delegation decisions and 1440 adoption decisions. Analyses measure decision quality, reliance errors (missed opportunities, over-reliance), model confidence, and confirmation bias.

Result: Collaborations outperform individuals, but humans exhibit suboptimal patterns: 3.9% missed opportunities when AI was correct, 1.7% over-reliance when AI misled. Confidence reports are near chance when AI and human disagree; confirmation bias leads to 64.5% under-reliance when AI agrees with an initial incorrect human answer.

Conclusion: Calibrated confidence, evidence-grounded explanations, and mechanisms to refine trust are proposed to improve human–AI collaboration.

Abstract: AI systems are fallible, and humans can make mistakes in deciding whether to trust AI over their own judgment. Thus, improving human-AI collaboration requires understanding when, why, and how humans decide to rely on AI. We study two distinct reliance decisions: the delegation choice -- deciding when to let AI act autonomously without knowing its output, and the adoption choice -- evaluating AI suggestions and deciding how to use them. Both of these decoupled reliance patterns shape collaboration, but prior work rarely studies them together in realistic settings with the same users. We address this gap by studying collaborative human--AI teams competing in a question-answering game in which humans can choose when and how to work with AI agents to win. Our 24 matches pair 23 expert humans with 16 AI agents, capturing 387 delegation and 1440 adoption decisions. While human--AI collaboration performs better than either AI or humans alone, humans make suboptimal collaboration decisions, both under-relying on correct AI suggestions (3.9% of opportunities missed) and over-relying when AI misleads them (1.7%). Both parties contribute wrong answers: reported model confidence is near chance when humans and AI disagree, while confirmation bias drives higher under-reliance (64.5%) when an AI suggestion agrees with humans' initial incorrect answer. To close this gap, we recommend calibrated confidence, evidence-grounded explanations, and mechanisms that help users refine trust.

</details>


### [239] [Entropy Distribution as a Fingerprint for Hallucinations in Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28264)
*Mattia J. Villani,Pranav Deshpande,Akshay Seshadri,Romina Yalovetzky,Niraj Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Single-pass, black-box detection of LLM hallucinations using Calibrated Entropy Score (CES), which leverages the distributional shape of token-level entropy with finite-sample calibration, achieving strong detection performance and scale-compatible guarantees across models and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations undermine trust and deployment in high-stakes settings. Existing detectors require multiple forward passes or access to model internals. A lightweight, calibrated, single-pass method that generalizes across models and tasks is highly desirable.

Method: Model the full distribution of token-level entropy during generation, combine mean entropy with a maximum entropy signal, and calibrate against a reference CDF. Introduce the Calibrated Entropy Score (CES) that uses black-box access to token logits in a single forward pass. Provide finite-sample calibration guarantees via a random-length Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz inequality and prove exponential convergence of hallucination detection probability with generation length.

Result: CES achieves the highest detection performance among single-pass black-box methods across eight QA benchmarks and ten generator models. It provides formal error guarantees unavailable to prior heuristics and is statistically indistinguishable from multi-sample methods, effectively closing the gap between lightweight and expensive detectors.

Conclusion: CES enables real-time, large-scale deployment of hallucination detection with strong theoretical guarantees, showing that a single-pass, calibrated entropy-based approach can rival multi-sample methods in effectiveness.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate factually incorrect outputs, commonly termed hallucinations, that undermine trust and limit deployment in high-stakes settings. Existing hallucination detection methods typically require multiple forward passes, or access to model internals. In this work, we provide theoretical background and empirical evidence that the distribution of token-level entropies, beyond the mean captured by perplexity or length-normalised entropy, serves as a fingerprint of hallucination, with distributional shape and tail behaviour carrying independent signal. We formalize hallucination detection as a statistical hypothesis test and propose the Calibrated Entropy Score (CES), a lightweight algorithm requiring only a single forward pass and black-box access to token logits. CES combines the mean signal with the maximum signal of the generated entropy through a calibrated reference CDF, producing scores that are directly comparable across models and tasks. We establish finite-sample calibration guarantees via a novel random-length Dvoretzky--Kiefer--Wolfowitz inequality, and also prove that CES detects hallucinations with probability converging to one exponentially fast in the generation length. Across eight QA benchmarks and ten generator models spanning open-source and API access models, CES achieves the highest detection performance among all single-pass black-box methods while providing formal error guarantees that existing heuristics lack. Remarkably, CES is statistically indistinguishable from multi-sample methods that require far greater computational cost, closing the gap between lightweight and expensive detection and making it suitable for real-time, large-scale deployment.

</details>


### [240] [Global Policy-Space Response Oracles for Two-Player Zero-Sum Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28273)
*Junyu Zhang,Feihong Yang,Jian Wang,Chao Wang,Xudong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Global PSRO introduces a two-phase exploration–selection framework that directly minimizes Population Exploitability (PE) to guide DRL-based expansion of the strategy space in Policy-Space Response Oracles (PSRO). It yields more sample-efficient convergence to Nash equilibria in large two-player zero-sum games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PSRO variants expand the restricted strategy set via best responses to meta-strategies computed from restricted-game payoffs, which can produce inefficient expansions and slow global improvement. There is a need for a practical method to select expansions that better approximate the full game under limited computational budgets.

Method: Two-phase exploration–selection framework instantiated as Global PSRO. It uses deep reinforcement learning to efficiently generate candidate responses and estimates Population Exploitability (PE) through parameter-sharing conditional neural networks to assess expansion quality. The goal is to minimize PE during expansion, producing a more representative strategy population without excessive computation.

Result: Global PSRO achieves lower exploitability and faster convergence toward Nash equilibria compared to prior PSRO methods, requiring significantly fewer policy iterations across multiple two-player zero-sum games.

Conclusion: Directly optimizing for population quality via PE during expansion yields a more efficient and scalable PSRO variant that better approximates Nash equilibria with fewer resources; the approach is practical for large zero-sum games but may rely on neural approximations and PE estimation fidelity.

Abstract: The Policy-Space Response Oracles (PSRO) framework scales equilibrium computation to large zero-sum games by iteratively expanding a restricted strategy set using deep reinforcement learning (DRL). A central challenge is to construct, under limited computational budgets, a small strategy population whose induced game well approximates the full game. Existing PSRO variants typically expand the population using best responses to meta-strategies computed from restricted-game payoffs, which can lead to inefficient expansions that provide limited global improvement. We propose to guide population expansion by directly evaluating the post-expansion population quality. Specifically, we adopt Population Exploitability (PE) to measure how well a restricted strategy set represents the full game, and introduce a two-phase exploration--selection framework that explicitly minimizes PE during expansion. We instantiate this framework as Global PSRO, a practical DRL-based algorithm that efficiently generates candidate responses and estimates PE via parameter-sharing conditional neural networks. Experiments across multiple two-player zero-sum games show that Global PSRO achieves lower exploitability and approximates Nash equilibria with significantly fewer policy iterations than prior PSRO methods.

</details>


### [241] [Do LLMs Build World Models From Text? A Multilingual Diagnostic of Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28277)
*Zhikai Pan,Chih-Ting Liao,Chunrui Liu,Xi Xiao,Yitong Qiao,Chunlei Meng,Zhangquan Chen,Xin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MentalMap benchmarks multilingual spatial reasoning in LLMs and reveals a universal L3 reasoning cliff: LLMs struggle with viewpoint reasoning once simple atomic accuracy falls above ~40%, across languages and prompting strategies; human results align, suggesting text-only working memory as a bottleneck; advocates multimodal/scratchpad approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs construct internal spatial world models from pure text across languages and whether such capabilities transfer cross-linguistically, by introducing a comprehensive multilingual diagnostic benchmark.

Method: Construct MentalMap with a six-level capability hierarchy (L0-L5) and four diagnostic axes (frame of reference, reading-direction bias, reasoning-effort allocation, hallucination). Build from 100 ProcTHOR household scenes, cover eight typologically diverse languages plus a structured-text control, include 39 task families across 1,950 evaluation cells; evaluate thirteen LLMs across scales and model families; include human evaluation under identical pure-text protocol.

Result: Identifies a universal L3 reasoning cliff: once baseline atomic accuracy exceeds ~40%, models retain less than half of L0 performance on viewpoint reasoning. Cliff persists across languages, scales, and prompting strategies; structured-output failures and reasoning patterns vary by model. Human evaluation under the same protocol reproduces the failure pattern, suggesting the bottleneck is text-only working memory rather than model architecture. Multimodal and scratchpad-augmented reasoning emerge as future directions.

Conclusion: Reframe pure-text spatial reasoning as a multi-axis world-modeling problem and motivate the integration of multimodal inputs and scratchpad-based reasoning to overcome the observed limitations.

Abstract: Whether large language models (LLMs) construct internal spatial world models from pure-text descriptions remains contested, and whether such capabilities transfer across languages has not been systematically studied. We introduce MentalMap, a multilingual diagnostic benchmark with a six-level capability hierarchy (L0-L5) spanning atomic spatial facts to generative world-graph construction, together with four diagnostic axes probing frame of reference, reading-direction bias, reasoning-effort allocation, and hallucination. MentalMap is built from 100 ProcTHOR household scenes, covers eight typologically diverse languages plus a structured-text control, and contains 39 task families across 1,950 evaluation cells. Evaluating thirteen LLMs across scales and model families, we identify a universal L3 reasoning cliff: no model retains even half of its L0 performance on viewpoint reasoning once baseline atomic accuracy exceeds 40%. The cliff persists across languages, scales, and prompting strategies, while structured-output failures and reasoning patterns vary substantially across models. Human evaluation under the identical pure-text protocol reproduces the same failure pattern, suggesting that the bottleneck arises from text-only working memory constraints rather than being specific to current LLM architectures. Our findings reframe pure-text spatial reasoning as a multi-axis world-modeling problem and motivate multimodal and scratchpad-augmented reasoning as future directions.

</details>


### [242] [ResearchLoop: An Evidence-Gated Control Plane for AI-Assisted Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28282)
*Yihan Xia,Taotao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ResearchLoop, an evidence-gated, repository-backed control plane for AI-assisted computational research that enforces auditable state across questions, contracts, evidence, claims, and bindings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Publication claims in AI-assisted research risk being hard to audit due to conflating ideation, implementation, evaluation, and writing; durable state and auditability are needed.

Method: Presents a complete protocol: state model, transition rules, claim-admission algorithm, and insight-compounding mechanism; implements as a repository-backed runtime; organizes research artifacts into questions, task contracts, evidence objects, claim ledgers, closeouts, and paper bindings; provides nine-version experimental record with self-hosting, task-suite ablations, math olympiad evaluation, SciCode boundary experiment; all artifacts stored in the repo.

Result: Provides a complete protocol specification, state model, transition rules, and algorithms, plus an extensive experimental record; preserves all artifacts in the project repository.

Conclusion: Offers a rigorous governance framework for AI-assisted research to enhance auditability and reproducibility; demonstrated feasibility through diverse studies and a self-hosting case with full artifacts.

Abstract: AI-assisted research compresses ideation, implementation, evaluation, and manuscript writing into a single interactive loop. This compression is useful, but it also creates a publication risk: paper claims can become easier to state than to audit. We present ResearchLoop, an evidence-gated control plane for AI-assisted computational research. ResearchLoop treats research questions, task contracts, evidence objects, claim ledgers, closeouts, and paper bindings as durable project state, realized here as a repository-backed runtime. This technical report provides the complete protocol specification, state model, transition rules, claim-admission algorithm, and insight-compounding mechanism. It also reports the full experimental record spanning nine versions (V0--V9), including a self-hosting case study, a controlled task-suite study with component ablations, a mathematical olympiad evaluation, and a supplementary SciCode boundary experiment evaluated with the official generated-code harness. All artifacts, manifests, and verification reports are preserved in the project repository.

</details>


### [243] [Where Rollouts Begin: Low-Load, High-Leverage First-Token Diversification for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28295)
*Soeun Kim,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: REFT diversifies RLVR rollouts by randomizing the first token after the reasoning marker, expanding rollout coverage without perturbing correctness signals, yielding higher Pass@k across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rollout diversity is a central bottleneck in RLVR. Existing diversification techniques (temperature, prefix, rollout selection) alter exploration but may not effectively widen policy-covered regions. The first-token after the reasoning marker shows a high-variance yet correctness-decoupled distribution, making it an ideal diversification lever.

Method: Introduce REFT (Rollout Exploration with First-Token Diversification): during rollout generation, sample the first token uniformly from the policy’s top-N candidates and distribute rollouts evenly across these choices, while leaving all other components of the RLVR pipeline unchanged.

Result: REFT improves aggregate Pass@1, Pass@8, and Pass@64 compared to DAPO and GRPO baselines, across four base models (0.5B–7B) and three difficulty regimes.

Conclusion: A simple, plug-in diversification technique—first-token diversification—can substantially enhance RLVR performance by widening rollout coverage without affecting the underlying correctness signal.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains reasoning models without labeled trajectories, relying on grouped rollouts to expose the policy to alternative reasoning paths and a verifier to score them. Rollout diversity has accordingly emerged as a central bottleneck in RLVR, with most existing methods broadening exploration through temperature, prefix, or rollout-selection adjustments. We identify a structurally distinguished but overlooked position for broadening this diversity: the first token after the reasoning marker. The policy's first-token distribution exhibits a sharply peaked yet correctness-decoupled phenomenon, and this first token position can broaden the regions a rollout group covers without altering the correctness signal. We introduce REFT (Rollout Exploration with First-Token Diversification), a light addition to the RLVR pipeline that samples first tokens uniformly from the policy's own top-$N$ candidates and allocates rollouts evenly, leaving every other component unchanged. Trained on the resulting diversified rollouts, REFT improves aggregate Pass@1, Pass@8, and Pass@64 over DAPO and GRPO baselines across four base models (0.5B-7B) and three difficulty regimes.

</details>


### [244] [REED: Post-Training Representation Editing for Cross-Domain Linguistic Steganalysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28298)
*Ruohan Lei,Jianxin Gao,Wanli Peng,Huimin Pei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Post-training representation editing for cross-domain linguistic steganalysis achieves strong cross-domain performance without retraining, using domain-offset and source-cover directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain unseen-domain variability degrades detection; need effective domain adaptation/generalization without updating model after training.

Method: Train on source domain; freeze feature extractor and classifier; deterministically edit intermediate representations; domain-offset vector from marginal source/target for adaptation; source-domain cover-to-stego direction for generalization; no architectural changes or parameter updates after source-domain training.

Result: Experimental results show competitive or superior cross-domain detection performance, especially F1-score, compared to advanced methods.

Conclusion: Post-training representation editing is effective for cross-domain linguistic steganalysis and avoids additional training or architecture changes.

Abstract: In real-world scenarios of linguistic steganalysis, tested texts usually come from unseen domains with different vocabularies, topics, writing styles, and steganographic generation patterns, which can significantly degrade the detection performance. Although existing cross-domain steganalysis methods can effectively alleviate this problem through distribution alignment, domain-invariant feature learning, etc., the detection performance is not satisfactory. In this paper, we propose a post-training representation editing method for cross-domain linguistic steganalysis. Specifically, the detector is first trained on source-domain data, and then the feature extractor and classifier are kept frozen, and the intermediate representations are deterministically edited before classification. For domain adaptation, we construct a domain-offset vector from marginal source and target representations. For domain generalization, we derive a source-domain cover-to-stego direction to guide sample-specific editing. Experimental results show that compared with the advanced methods, the proposed method can achieve high cross-domain detection performance, especially in terms of F1-score, while requiring no architecture modification or parameter updates after source-domain training.

</details>


### [245] [From Fact Overwriting to Knowledge Evolution: Causal Editing via On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28303)
*Shuaike Li,Kai Zhang,Xianquan Wang,Jiachen Liu,Shengpeng Mo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Causal editing (CODE) replaces static fact overwriting in LLMs with causal, on-policy distillation, reducing self-refutation and boosting multi-hop accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static Fact Overwriting fragments pre-trained logic, causing epistemic dissonance as legacy priors resist updates; grounding updates in explicit causal narratives can align priors and enable coherent knowledge evolution.

Method: CODE combines causal bootstrapping with asymmetric on-policy distillation to imprint causal transition logic into the model's parametric memory.

Result: Self-refutation drops from 95.6% under a zero-distortion proxy to 1.8% with CODE; multi-hop accuracy up to 83.5%; grounding updates reduces conflict rate to 6.6%, validated on LLaMA-3.1 and Qwen-2.5.

Conclusion: Advocates a paradigm shift toward causal editing, providing CODE as a practical mechanism to embed causal knowledge evolution in LLMs.

Abstract: While Knowledge Editing (KE) enables efficient updates, its dominant Static Fact Overwriting paradigm treats LLMs as discrete databases, forcibly injecting isolated facts. Fracturing pre-trained logical topologies, this triggers Epistemic Dissonance -- a pathology where un-evolved legacy priors force the model to explicitly negate the injected update. Idealized interventions reveal that this is an inherent structural flaw rather than mere algorithmic noise, with a zero-distortion proxy yielding a catastrophic 95.6% self-refutation rate. Given the causally driven nature of real-world knowledge, grounding updates in explicit causal narratives effectively collapses this conflict rate to just 6.6%, underscoring the imperative for a paradigm shift toward Causal Editing. To internalize this evolution, we propose CODE (Causal On-policy Distillation for Editing). By coupling causal bootstrapping with asymmetric on-policy distillation, CODE engraves causal transition logic directly into parametric memory. Experiments on LLaMA-3.1 and Qwen-2.5 show CODE drastically suppresses self-refutation to 1.8% while securing robust multi-hop accuracy (up to 83.5%), seamlessly transforming discrete fact injection into coherent knowledge evolution. Code is available at https://github.com/CrashBugger/CODE.

</details>


### [246] [An Enhanced Large Neighborhood Search Approach for the Capacitated Facility Location Problem with Incompatible Customers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28337)
*Ida Gjergji,Lucas Kletzander,Nysret Musliu,Andrea Schaerf*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) for a capacitated facility location problem with customer incompatibilities; hybrid destroy operators; exact repair; outperforms state-of-the-art metaheuristics with new best solutions on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a practical CFLP variant where certain pairs of customers cannot be served by the same facility, capturing hazards, pollution, or competition; such incompatibilities complicate routing/assignment and demand effective, high-quality heuristics.

Method: Within an LNS framework, three destroy operators are proposed and combined in a hybrid manner; an exact solver is used in the repair phase; multiple algorithmic components are evaluated to design the LNS.

Result: The method achieves new best solutions for all available benchmark instances, outperforming existing state-of-the-art metaheuristics.

Conclusion: The proposed LNS with multiple destroy operators and exact repair is effective for CFLP with incompatibilities, delivering state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks; future work could explore scalability, component sensitivity, and extensions to related problems.

Abstract: A new variant of the classic capacitated facility location problem, which considers incompatibilities between customers, has recently been introduced in the literature. This problem captures the situation where given pairs of customers cannot be served by the same facility. Such a feature is crucial for many practical cases of location problems, such as the presence of hazardous or polluting materials and contention between competing costumers. In this paper, we propose a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) method to solve this problem. Within the framework of LNS, we introduce three different destroy operators, which are combined in a hybrid manner, and we use an exact solver in the repair phase. Different algorithmic components are investigated for the design of LNS. The experimental analysis shows that our new method outperforms existing state-of-the-art metaheuristics, providing new best solutions for all available benchmark instances.

</details>


### [247] [SafeMed-R1: Clinician-Audited Safety and Ethics Alignment for Medical Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28338)
*Chao Ding,Mouxiao Bian,Tianbin Li,Minjia Yuan,Yidong Jiang,Yankai Jiang,Jinru Ding,Jiayuan Chen,Zhuangzhi Gao,Pengcheng Chen,Zhao He,Rongzhao Zhang,Meiling Liu,Luyi Jiang,Jie Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeMed-R1 uses a clinician-audited Clinical Trust Signals pipeline to anchor reasoning to rubric scores and edit histories, coupled with safety/ethics supervision and red-team testing, achieving 79.6% macro accuracy on clinical benchmarks. It shows lower adversarial risk and improved medication safety in expert comparison, suggesting governance benefits without inference-time retrieval grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address governance challenges for clinical LLMs: require auditable reasoning, safety/ethics alignment, and resilience to adversarial misuse to enable routine clinical use.

Method: Train an LLM with a Clinical Trust Signals (CTS) pipeline that links reasoning steps to clinician rubric scores and edit histories. Apply safety and ethics supervision and red-team stress testing. Evaluate macro-averaged accuracy on clinical benchmarks. Conduct adversarial safety testing to assess risk. In a paired expert study (30 medication safety vignettes), compare SafeMed-R1 to PGY1/PGY2 residents on medical correctness and medication safety, guideline consistency, and clinical usefulness.

Result: Macro-averaged accuracy: 79.6% across benchmarks. Under adversarial testing, lowest aggregated risk and 3–5% reduction in unsafe outputs relative to baseline. In expert study, matches PGY1/PGY2 on medical correctness and scores higher for medication safety, guideline consistency, and clinical usefulness.

Conclusion: Clinician-audited supervision provenance with domain-tailored safety/ethics alignment strengthens governance-relevant evidence without relying on inference-time retrieval or citation grounding.

Abstract: Large language models(LLMs) increasingly match expert performance on licensing examinations, yet routine clinical use remains limited because governance requires auditable reasoning, safety and ethics alignment, and resilience to adversarial misuse. Here we present SafeMed-R1, trained with a traceable Clinical Trust Signals(CTS) pipeline that links each reasoning instance to clinician rubric scores and edit histories, and aligned through safety and ethics supervision and red team stress testing. SafeMed-R1 attains a macro-averaged accuracy of 79.6% across clinical benchmarks. Under adversarial safety testing, it shows the lowest aggregated risk and reduces unsafe outputs by about 3 to 5% relative to its baseline. In a paired expert study of 30 medication safety vignettes, SafeMed-R1 matches PGY1 and PGY2 residents on medical correctness and scores higher for medication safety, guideline consistency, and clinical usefulness. Collectively, these results suggest that clinician-audited supervision provenance, together with domain-tailored safety and ethics alignment, can strengthen governance-relevant evidence without relying on inference-time retrieval or citation grounding.

</details>


### [248] [Picid: A Modular Evaluation Infrastructure for Reproducible PHM Across Tasks and Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28345)
*Lev Telyatnikov,Raffael Theiler,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PicID provides a modular, reproducible PHM evaluation pipeline enabling standardized cross-task comparisons across diagnostics and prognostics, demonstrated on 13 models and 12 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PHM evaluation suffers from nonstandardized, opaque protocols; results are hard to reproduce due to implicit data splits, preprocessing, label alignment, temporal windowing, and metrics.

Method: Introduce PicID as a modular evaluation infrastructure that formalizes the PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit, executable protocol with deterministic, leakage-safe dataset construction, unified interfaces for fault detection (classification), diagnostics, and prognostics (regression); standardizes data contracts and evaluation boundaries; extensible to new datasets and model classes without violating protocol invariants.

Result: Empirical evaluation of thirteen models on twelve datasets spanning batteries, bearings, turbofan engines, hydraulics, filtration systems, and buildings, demonstrating reusable foundation for standardized, fair, and reproducible PHM evaluation.

Conclusion: PicID enables standardized, fair, and reproducible evaluation in PHM and facilitates cross-task comparisons across diagnostic and prognostic tasks, with extensibility to new datasets and model classes.

Abstract: Progress in Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) is hindered by the lack of standardized and reusable evaluation practices across tasks, datasets, and application domains. Reported results are often difficult to reproduce and compare, as key protocol choices, such as data splits, preprocessing, label alignment, temporal windowing, and metrics, are often implicit or implemented ad hoc. We introduce \picid, a modular evaluation infrastructure that formalizes the PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit, executable, and reproducible protocol. Through well-defined abstractions, \picid enforces deterministic, leakage-safe dataset construction while remaining flexible across diverse PHM settings. The framework supports fault detection, diagnostics, and prognostics through a unified interface and can be extended to new datasets and model classes without violating protocol invariants. By standardizing data contracts and evaluation boundaries, \picid also enables fair cross-task comparisons across diagnostics (classification) and prognostics (regression), allowing identical model families to be evaluated consistently across heterogeneous settings. We demonstrate \picid through an empirical evaluation of thirteen models on twelve datasets spanning batteries, bearings, turbofan engines, hydraulics, filtration systems, and buildings. This work establishes a reusable foundation for standardized, fair and reproducible evaluation in PHM.

</details>


### [249] [FedMPT: Federated Multi-label Prompt Tuning of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28347)
*Xucong Wang,Pengkun Wang,Zhe Zhao,Liheng Yu,Shuang Wang,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel federated multi-label recognition method (FedMPT) that uses causal front-door adjustment, an LLM-driven condition discovery pipeline, and region-level alignment via optimal transport to reduce spurious label activations in VLM-based FL, achieving competitive or superior results on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In realistic federated settings, private, heterogeneous data can cause VLM-based MLR models to overfit co-occurring labels and trigger irrelevant categories. A causal framing with front-door adjustment and intermediate variables can mitigate these spurious associations and improve generalization across clients.

Method: FedMPT adopts a causal model with front-door adjustment, decoupling MLR into intermediate variables that amplify oracle label co-occurrence. An LLM-driven pipeline discovers the underlying conditions governing label dependencies. Optimal transport aligns condition-enriched prompts with image patches to reveal multiple region-level semantics. A gating mechanism combines predictions from different conditions to form final outputs.

Result: Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show competitive results and in some settings outperform state-of-the-art methods under varied settings.

Conclusion: FedMPT is the first method specifically designed for federated MLR, demonstrating that leveraging generalizable conditions via causal reasoning and LLM-informed prompts can mitigate erroneous label activations in federated VLM-based MLR.

Abstract: Multi-Label Recognition (MLR) based on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) aims to leverage their pre-trained knowledge to better adapt complex recognition scenarios, thereby enhancing model robustness. However, for realistic decentralized applications requiring federated learning, adapting VLMs to each client that possesses private and heterogeneous data can cause the model to overfit spurious label correlations, consequently triggering irrelevant categories when encountering new samples. To tackle this problem, we reconsider the federated learning for MLR with a causal model, in which we adopt a front-door adjustment and decouple the MLR modeling process by intermediate variables that magnify the oracle label co-occurrence. Guided by our analysis, we propose our FedMPT, the first method specifically designed for federated MLR. The core idea of FedMPT is to leverage generalizable conditions to steer federated MLR to mitigate erroneous label activations. To achieve this, FedMPT introduces an Large Language Model (LLM)-driven pipeline to decipher the underlying conditions that govern label dependencies. Furthermore, we introduce an optimal transport between the condition-enriched prompts and the image patches to uncover multiple region-level semantics. Finally, we generate synergistic predictions from different conditions with a crafted gating mechanism. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our proposed approach achieves competitive results and outperforms SOTA methods under varied settings.

</details>


### [250] [Plan Before Search: Search Agents Need Plan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28354)
*Zhipeng Qian,Zihan Liang,Yufei Ma,Ben Chen,Huangyu Dai,Jiayi Ji,Chenyi Lei,Wenwu Ou,Xiaoshuai Sun,Qibin Hou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Plan offers a structured retrieval-anchored agent for multi-hop QA, highlighting model-dependent RL failure modes and proposing seed-model self-bootstrapping to enable Plan without external distillation; shows robust, model-agnostic gains across 3B–14B models on multi-hop benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two neglected factors in LLM training for retrieval-augmented reasoning: (1) the dependency structure among sub-skills, and (2) the limits of distillation as a sole route to capability. Explore whether a seed model can activate Plan across diverse models.

Method: Introduce Plan: a structured agent that decomposes a question into ordered sub-questions before retrieval to anchor each search step. Evaluate across 3B–14B parameter families to observe RL failure modes under identical reward. Propose a self-bootstrapping paradigm where a small seed model generates filtered trajectories that activate Plan in target models, removing the need for distillation from a stronger external model. Assess performance on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

Result: Identical reward signals induce qualitatively different RL failure modes across model sizes; feasibility conditions (initial entropy, training stability, prerequisite sub-skills) govern success. The seed-model self-bootstrapping approach activates Plan across all tested models and consistently outperforms competitive baselines on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Reward design alone is insufficient; sub-skill dependencies and model feasibility critically influence training. Distillation is not the only path to capability—seed-model bootstrapping can robustly deploy Plan across diverse models, yielding strong multi-hop QA performance.

Abstract: Training large language models as retrieval-augmented reasoning agents typically combines reinforcement learning with an SFT cold start distilled from a stronger model. However, this paradigm overlooks two fundamental factors: the dependency structure among sub-skills, and the possibility that distillation is not the only route to capability acquisition. We study this through Plan, a structured agentic behavior for multi-hop retrieval that decomposes a question into ordered sub-questions before any retrieval is performed, so that each search step can be anchored to a pre-designed sub-question instead of drifting under the influence of partially relevant documents retrieved earlier. However, across three model families spanning 3B to 14B parameters, we find that an identical reward signal induces qualitatively different RL failure modes. This phenomenon indicates that successful training hinges not only on reward design but also on model-specific feasibility conditions: sufficient initial entropy, training stability, and prerequisite sub-skills. Motivated by this, we propose a self-bootstrapping paradigm in which a small-scale seed model generates filtered trajectories that activate Plan in any target model, eliminating the need for distillation from an external stronger model. Our pipeline activates Plan across every tested model and consistently outperforms competitive baselines on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

</details>


### [251] [From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28359)
*Taojie Zhu,Wentao Zhao,Rui Sun,Beidi Luan,Jiacheng Lu,Sinuo Wang,Jing Li,Daxin Jiang,Yonghong He,Zuo Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces KTD-Fin, a leakage-controlled, attribution-aware end-to-end benchmark for LLM trading agents. Uses data-side masking to anonymize identifiers and calendar info, plus a Barra-style attribution to decompose returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha. Evaluates ten frontier LLMs on the CSI300 (2024–2026). Finds masking shifts agent reasoning toward factor-based approaches and that returns are largely explained by passive market/style exposure rather than persistent stock-selection alpha; offers a reproducible template for robust evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end LLM trading benchmarks are vulnerable to leakage from historical data and to conflating market returns with genuine stock-picking skill. There is a need for evaluation protocols that separate historical memory from decision-making and attribute performance to its sources, ensuring that reported alpha reflects transferable investment skill.

Method: Data-side masking protocol to anonymize tickers, dates, prices, and calendar cues in prompts and tools; end-to-end trading setup evaluated on the CSI300 for 2024–2026; Barra-style performance attribution decomposing portfolio returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components; evaluation conducted across ten frontier LLM agents.

Result: Masking substantially alters agent rationales, steering them toward anonymized factor-based reasoning; attribution analysis shows cumulative returns under leakage-controlled evaluation are largely driven by passive market and style exposure, with limited evidence of persistent stock-selection alpha.

Conclusion: To progress toward meaningful LLM-based finance benchmarks, evaluations should assess not only profitability but also the source of returns, emphasizing transferable investment skill. KTD-Fin provides a reproducible framework for leakage-controlled and attribution-aware assessment of LLM trading agents.

Abstract: Evaluating whether large language model (LLM) agents can profit in capital markets is increasingly framed as end-to-end trading: place an agent in a historical market, let it trade, and measure portfolio returns. This setup is vulnerable to two evaluation failures. First, long backtests often overlap with the knowledge cutoffs of frontier LLMs, allowing memorized tickers, dates, prices, and market narratives to substitute for investment reasoning. Second, raw returns are a noisy proxy for stock-selection ability, since positive performance may come from market beta, style exposure, or favorable regimes rather than genuine alpha.
  We introduce KTD-Fin (Knowing-To-Doing Financial Benchmark), an end-to-end stock-market trading benchmark that addresses both issues. KTD-Fin uses a data-side masking protocol to anonymize key identifiers and calendar information consistently across prompts and tools, separating historical market memory from investment decision-making. It also incorporates a Barra-style performance attribution framework that decomposes portfolio returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components.
  Across ten frontier LLM agents evaluated on the Chinese CSI300 over a 2024--2026 window, masking substantially changes agent rationales, pushing them towards anonymized factor-based reasoning. Attribution analysis further shows that LLM agents' cumulative returns under leakage-controlled evaluation are largely explained by passive market and style exposure, with limited evidence of persistent stock-selection alpha. These findings suggest that financial LLM benchmarks should evaluate not only whether an agent makes money, but also whether the source of returns reflects transferable investment skill. We release KTD-Fin as a reproducible template for leakage-controlled and attribution-aware evaluation of LLM trading agents.

</details>


### [252] [Prompt Codebooks: Discrete Compositional Optimization for Language Model Instruction Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28360)
*Jyotirmoy Nath,Neeraj Kumar,Brejesh Lall*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PCO enables compositional prompt optimization by learning a discrete codebook of natural-language instincts and routing inputs to subsets of entries, achieving per-instance prompt assembly with improved accuracy and shorter prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome brittleness and non-reusability of monolithic, instance-blind prompts in traditional APO by enabling reusable sub-behaviors and per-input adaptation.

Method: Introduce a discrete codebook of instincts; an LLM-based encoder routes each input to a small subset of codebook entries; a generator composes the selected instincts into a prompt for a fixed target model; a critic provides structured, per-variable textual gradients; train encoder, generator, and codebook with a language-valued min-max objective.

Result: On six benchmarks with Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B, PCO improves over zero-shot by up to 30.36 points; beats GEPA by 3.34 on HotpotQA and 1.11 overall; reduces deployed prompt length by up to 14.1x vs MIPROv2 and 3.0x vs GEPA using only 16 instincts.

Conclusion: PCO enables per-instance, compositional prompt construction through a discrete codebook, achieving strong empirical gains with shorter prompts and demonstrating reusable, instantiatable instincts suitable for scalable APO.

Abstract: Automatic prompt optimization (APO) has driven significant gains in LLM-based agentic workflows. However, existing methods treat each task's prompt as a monolithic, instance-blind string optimized through global edits, producing brittle updates and preventing the reuse of learned sub-behaviors. We propose Prompt Codebooks (PCO), a novel compositional prompt optimization framework that recasts APO as discrete learning over a finite vocabulary of natural-language instincts - atomic, reusable instruction units. PCO organizes prompt-construction knowledge in a discrete codebook and routes each input to a small subset of entries via an LLM-based encoder; a generator composes them into a prompt for the frozen target model; a critic emits a structured verdict that decomposes by attribution into per-variable textual gradients, jointly training the encoder, generator, and codebook under a language-valued min-max objective. The resulting routing is per-instance: different inputs in the same task receive different instinct compositions, a regime structurally inexpressible under instance-blind methods. Across six benchmarks on Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B, PCO improves over zero-shot by up to +30.36 points, surpasses the strongest prior baseline (GEPA) by +3.34 on HotpotQA and +1.11 in aggregate, and reduces deployed prompt length by up to 14.1x versus MIPROv2 and 3.0x versus GEPA using only K=16 instincts.

</details>


### [253] [Risk-Controlled Lean-as-Judge for Natural-Language Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28365)
*Pauline Bourigault,Xiaotong Ji,Matthieu Zimmer,Rasul Tutunov,Haitham Bou Ammar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract analyzes how Lean-derived signals for natural-language math answers depend on formalization coverage and fidelity, showing strong coverage dependence and sparse, often unfaithful signals. It introduces COVCAL to bound selective risk in accepting answers, comparing autoformalizers (7B) to a prover-specialized formalizer, with results showing limited feasibility for 7B and improved coverage for the specialized formalizer, yielding about 48% acceptance at high accuracy; self-consistency remains 91% accurate. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify the reliability of Lean-based natural-language proofs as a signal for correctness, given partial formalization, and to develop a risk-controlled selector that decides when to accept or abstain. 

Method: Define COVCAL, a selective-signal framework using Lean-trace diagnostics to compute finite-sample selective-risk bounds under two regimes: a Bonferroni bound (conservative) and a tighter dev-then-cal rule. Empirically evaluate on MATH-500 across bootstrap partitions, comparing an autoformalizer (7B) versus a prover-specialized formalizer in terms of coverage, faithfulness, and acceptance under error bounds. 

Result: 7B autoformalizer yields sparse coverage and, under Bonferroni, abstains on all bootstrap partitions; a prover-specialized formalizer yields 79% coverage and makes 17 of 20 partitions feasible, accepting ~48% of problems at 0.98 accuracy; self-consistency alone already achieves 91% accuracy. 

Conclusion: Partial formal signals can be trusted under risk control, but feasibility depends on the formalizer used. The study precisely characterizes when and with which formalizer the partial signal is actionable, highlighting the gap between self-consistency and formal-proof faithfulness and the role of coverage in risk-bounded decision making.

Abstract: Lean is increasingly used to judge natural-language mathematical answers, but its signal is partial: many answers never formalize, and a failed proof may reflect an ill-typed statement or a missing library fact, not a wrong answer. On MATH-500 we show this signal is (i) sharply coverage-dependent, that is the proof-winning answer is correct 96% of the time at high proved coverage but 20% at low, and (ii) sparse and often unfaithful: a 7B autoformalizer proves a class for only 28% of problems, and a manual audit finds only approximately 43% of those proofs faithful. We propose COVCAL, a selector over Lean-trace diagnostics that certifies a finite-sample selective-risk bound on accepted answers or abstains, under two regimes (a conservative Bonferroni bound and a tighter dev-then-cal rule). Feasibility depends on autoformalization coverage: with the 7B formalizer the signal is too sparse and Bonferroni abstains on all 20 bootstrap partitions, whereas a prover-specialized formalizer reaches 79% coverage and flips it to feasible on 17 of 20, accepting approximately 48% of problems at 0.98 accepted accuracy. Since self-consistency alone is already 91% accurate, our contribution is a precise account of when, and with which formalizer, a partial formal signal can be trusted under risk control.

</details>


### [254] [CyberJurors: A Multi-Agent Simulation Task for E-Commerce Disputes Verdict](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28369)
*Yanhui Sun,Wu Liu,Haifeng Ming,Xinru Wang,Hantao Yao,Yongdong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multimodal, crowdsourced-evidence verdict task with VerdictBench benchmark and CyberJurors multi-agent framework; demonstrates structured reasoning and improved alignment with real jury voting; dataset and code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gaps where e-commerce dispute adjudication requires grounding across multimodal clues and platform-specific conventions; existing LLM-based methods fail to simulate crowdsourced juror reasoning and voting; need benchmarks and multi-agent reasoning to reflect real-world processes.

Method: Introduce EDV task; build VerdictBench with 6,000 real-world cases; propose CyberJurors consisting of Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought (four-stage reasoning) and Jury Consensus Verdict (multi-round discussion/voting with precedents) to reduce cognitive biases.

Result: Empirical results show CyberJurors outperform SOTA LLMs/MLLMs and court simulators on VerdictBench and align with real jury voting patterns; datasets/code provided.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of structured, multi-agent, chain-of-thought reasoning for e-commerce dispute verdicts and provides a valuable benchmark and framework for future research.

Abstract: E-commerce platforms have begun recruiting crowdsourced jurors to adjudicate massive volumes of transaction disputes. Unlike formal legal judgment, E-commerce dispute verdicts require grounding pivotal clues from redundant, multi-round, multimodal evidence and making decisions under flexible platform-specific conventions. These characteristics render existing methods insufficient for this scenario. To bridge this gap, we introduce a pioneering task, E-commerce Dispute Verdicts (EDV), and present VerdictBench, a multimodal benchmark comprising 6,000 real-world cases designed to reflect crowdsourced jury decisions. Building upon this, we propose CyberJurors, a multi-agent framework to clarify the dispute logic and regulate the verdict process. At the individual level, Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought decomposes the EDV task into four structured reasoning stages, enabling fine-grained clue perception and clarifying causal logic between pivotal clues and the dispute focus. At the collective level, Jury Consensus Verdict simulates multi-round discussion and voting among jurors, while incorporating verdict precedents to mitigate cognitive biases toward either disputant. Experiments on VerdictBench show that CyberJurors outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs, MLLMs, and court simulators, while achieving stronger alignment with real-world jury voting patterns. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/YanhuiS/CyberJurors and https://huggingface.co/datasets/piggi/VerdictBench.

</details>


### [255] [From paper to benchmark: agentic, framework-based reproduction of under-specified methods in machine health intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28371)
*Raffael Theiler,Ludovico Comito,David Leko,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Lev Telyatnikov,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic, framework-based PHM paper reproduction translates papers into executable, benchmark-ready implementations using a slot-binding interface, enabling assumption-aware, standardized benchmarking across papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducing PHM methods is hampered by restricted industrial data, poor reporting, and implicit design choices; existing paper-to-code tools produce incomparable artifacts.

Method: Introduce a slot-binding, framework-based reproduction pipeline that maps equations and protocols into structured components (task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing, targets, models, evaluators) with explicit unresolved assumptions; validate via standardized task contracts and evaluation hooks; compare 16 PHM papers using framework-enhanced and baseline agents.

Result: Coupling agentic generation with a shared PHM framework yields executable, assumption-aware, and more comparable benchmark implementations across papers.

Conclusion: The approach transforms paper reproduction from isolated code synthesis into standardized, assumption-aware, executable benchmarks enabling consistent cross-paper comparison.

Abstract: Industrial Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) provides a representative case study for a broader challenge in applied machine learning: translating published papers into executable, benchmark-ready implementations. Reproducing under-specified methods in PHM is particularly difficult due to restricted access to industrial datasets, incomplete reporting of preprocessing and evaluation protocols, and implicit design choices (e.g., windowing, target construction, data splits) that critically affect performance. Existing paper-to-code systems generate implementations for individual papers, but these artifacts are often not directly comparable due to inconsistencies in assumptions and evaluation settings. We introduce \emph{agentic, framework-based PHM paper reproduction}, where an agent translates a paper into a shared PHM benchmark framework via a \emph{slot-binding interface}. This interface maps equations and protocol descriptions into structured components (task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing, targets, models, and evaluators), while explicitly recording unresolved assumptions. The resulting implementations are validated against standardized task contracts and evaluation hooks, enabling consistent and comparable benchmarking. We evaluate this approach on 16 PHM papers, comparing framework-enhanced, skill-based and prompt-based agentic reproduction against a recent framework-free paper-reproduction agent. We assess reproduction success, model-based code evaluation, framework binding of paper assumptions, and cross-paper benchmark comparability under standardized protocols. Our results show that coupling agentic generation with a shared framework transforms paper reproduction from isolated code synthesis into executable, assumption-aware, and systematically comparable benchmark implementations.

</details>


### [256] [Mechanistically Interpreting the Role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28388)
*Yue Cheng,Jiajun Zhang,Xiaohui Gao,Weiwei Xing,Zheng Wang,Zhanxing Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sample difficulty non-monotonically affects RLVR performance; easy/medium problems yield best improvements; hard problems provide weak signals and can degrade pre-existing capabilities; difficulty-adaptive strategies and T-SAE-guided signals improve reward density and credit assignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how sample difficulty shapes RLVR optimization and representation dynamics and to identify strategies to leverage hard samples effectively.

Method: Perform difficulty-wise and one-sample analysis on RLVR; examine internal representations with Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE); experiment with backward-reasoning reformulation and T-SAE-guided training signals; propose adaptive strategies.

Result: Easy/medium problems yield strongest, most stable reasoning improvements; hard problems yield weak learning signals and can cause degenerate behaviors; T-SAE reveals feature dynamics (easy reinforces direct-answer and basic computations; hard engages reasoning features but only useful with successful trajectories; medium provides balanced signal). Difficulty-adaptive strategies improve reward density and credit assignment.

Conclusion: Sample difficulty is a key factor governing RLVR optimization and representation evolution; propose difficulty-adaptive strategies to utilize hard samples effectively and enhance RLVR performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) is empirically shown to notably enhance the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs), particularly in mathematics and programming. However, the mechanistic role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR remains poorly understood. In this paper, we investigate RLVR through the lens of difficulty-wise and one-sample analysis.
