We find that sample difficulty has a non-monotonic effect on RLVR: easy and medium-difficulty problems yield the strongest and most stable reasoning improvements, whereas overly hard problems often provide weak learning signals, induce degenerate behaviors such as answer repetition or skipping necessary computation, and can ultimately degrade the model's pre-existing capabilities. Beyond the obverse of response, we further analyze the model's internal feature dynamics using Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE). Easy problems mainly reinforce direct-answer and basic-computation features while suppressing deliberative-reasoning features; hard problems activate reasoning-related features but become useful only when successful trajectories are sampled; medium-difficulty problems provide a more balanced signal, strengthening both computation and multi-step reasoning features. Motivated by these findings, we propose difficulty-adaptive strategies for hard-sample utilization, using backward-reasoning reformulation and T-SAE-guided training signals to improve reward density and credit assignment during RLVR. Overall, our results identify sample difficulty as a key factor governing both the optimization dynamics and representation evolution of RLVR.

</details>


### [257] [HRBench: Benchmarking and Understanding Thinking-Mode Switch Strategies in Hybrid-Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28398)
*Yansong Ning,Mianpeng Liu,Jingwen Ye,Weidong Zhang,Hao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HRBench: a unified benchmark for thinking-mode switching in hybrid-reasoning LLMs across switching strategies and training regimes, evaluated on multiple models and benchmarks to map trade-offs and guide efficient reasoning research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of adaptive thinking-mode switching are fragmented across models, datasets, and implementation assumptions, hindering fair comparison and progress.

Method: Defines a two-axis design space: (1) switching strategy families (prompt-based selection, external routing, speculative execution) and (2) training regimes (training-free, SFT, offline RL, online RL), yielding 12 settings; evaluates 6 LLMs and 5 reasoning benchmarks; reimplements 12+ prior methods within a common pipeline.

Result: Different strategies occupy distinct effectiveness-efficiency trade-offs: prompt-based methods excel in token-accuracy trade-offs, routing provides stable cost reductions, and speculative methods boost accuracy at higher token costs. Training affects strategies differently; optimal strategy varies with model scale and task domain. HRBench offers reference implementations and a unified evaluation platform; data and code are publicly available.

Conclusion: HRBench enables more controlled, comparable research on efficient hybrid-reasoning in LLMs, providing a standardized framework and resources for systematic analysis.

Abstract: Hybrid-reasoning large language models (LLMs) expose explicit controls over reasoning effort, allowing users or systems to trade off answer quality against inference cost. However, existing methods for adaptive thinking-mode selection are typically evaluated under different models, datasets, and implementation assumptions, making it difficult to compare their practical behavior. We introduce HRBench, a unified evaluation framework for studying thinking-mode switching in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. HRBench organizes the design space along two axes: three switching strategy families, prompt-based selection, external routing, and speculative execution, and four training regimes, training-free, SFT, offline and online RL, yielding 12 controlled evaluation settings. We evaluate these settings across 6 LLMs, from Qwen3.5-2B to Kimi-K2.5-1.1T, and 5 reasoning benchmarks covering mathematics, science, and code, while reimplementing 12+ representative prior methods within the same pipeline. Our analysis characterizes how different switching strategies occupy distinct effectiveness-efficiency trade-off regions: prompt-based methods often provide favorable token-accuracy trade-offs, routing methods offer more stable cost reduction, and speculative methods tend to improve accuracy at higher token cost. We further find that training affects strategies differently, and that the preferred strategy varies with model scale and task domain. HRBench provides reference implementations and a unified evaluation platform to support more controlled research on efficient reasoning in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. Our data, code and repository are available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/HRBench.

</details>


### [258] [Measuring Progress Toward AGI: A Cognitive Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28405)
*Ryan Burnell,Yumeya Yamamori,Orhan Firat,Kate Olszewska,Steph Hughes-Fitt,Oran Kelly,Isaac R. Galatzer-Levy,Meredith Ringel Morris,Allan Dafoe,Alison M. Snyder,Noah D. Goodman,Matthew Botvinick,Shane Legg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Framework to measure AGI progress via a Cognitive Taxonomy of 10 cognitive faculties and a held-out-task evaluation yielding cognitive profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide an objective, scalable metric for progress toward AGI to overcome subjective claims and inform governance.

Method: Decompose general intelligence into 10 cognitive faculties; design an evaluation protocol across targeted, held-out cognitive tasks; produce a cognitive profile for systems.

Result: Conceptual framework and evaluation pipeline; enables systematic comparison and diagnostic analysis of system strengths/weaknesses.

Conclusion: Offers a practical roadmap for rigorous empirical evaluation of AGI and invites validation and extension.

Abstract: Despite widespread discussion of AGI, there is no clear framework for measuring progress toward it. This ambiguity fuels subjective claims, makes it difficult to track progress, and risks hindering responsible governance. As a starting point to address this gap, we present a framework for understanding system capabilities in relation to human cognitive abilities. Drawing from decades of research in psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science, we introduce a Cognitive Taxonomy that deconstructs general intelligence into 10 key cognitive faculties. We then propose a rigorous evaluation protocol in which a system's performance is measured across a suite of targeted, held-out cognitive tasks, generating a 'cognitive profile' that can be used to understand a system's strengths and weaknesses. We hope this framework will provide a practical roadmap and an initial step toward more rigorous, empirical evaluation of AGI.

</details>


### [259] [Efficient Post-training of LLMs for Code Generation With Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28409)
*Mingze Wu,Abhinav Anand,Shweta Verma,Mira Mezini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Offline RL using existing code datasets can improve code-generation LLMs, especially small models and hard tasks, offering a resource-efficient alternative to online RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RL for code generation is costly due to the need for LLM inference and verification of generated outputs; offline RL aims to leverage fixed datasets to train without expensive online interactions.

Method: Apply offline RL to code-generating LLMs using existing code datasets; evaluate across model sizes and coding tasks; compare to relevant baselines.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that offline RL improves LLM performance on code-generation tasks, with larger gains for small models and for more challenging coding problems.

Conclusion: Offline RL is a viable training strategy for code-generating LLMs, reducing resource requirements and boosting performance, particularly for smaller models and difficult problems.

Abstract: Post-training using online reinforcement learning (RL) is an important training step for LLMs, including code-generating models. However, online RL for code generation involves LLM inference and verification of the generated output, which can take considerable time and resources. In this paper, we explore the application of offline RL to code-generating models by leveraging existing code datasets. Our experiments demonstrate that offline RL is an effective training strategy for improving LLM performance. We show that offline RL can be especially beneficial for small LLMs and challenging coding problems.

</details>


### [260] [DenoiseRL: Bootstrapping Reasoning Models to Recover from Noisy Prefixes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28421)
*Caijun Xu,Changyi Xiao,Zhongyuan Peng,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DenoiseRL introduces a recovery-oriented reinforcement learning framework that learns from incorrect reasoning traces of weak models to improve large language model reasoning without relying on stronger supervision or curated data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based reasoning improvements rely on expensive teacher models or carefully engineered datasets, creating scalability bottlenecks. A data-efficient, supervision-light alternative is needed.

Method: Convert failures from weak models into learning signals through recovery-oriented optimization. The model learns from incorrect reasoning traces to recover correct reasoning steps, leveraging on-policy RL to improve exploration and self-correction without external supervision.

Result: Empirically outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines on competitive mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks; exhibits stronger self-corrective behavior as task difficulty increases; demonstrates improved training efficiency and reduced reliance on curated data or stronger teachers.

Conclusion: DenoiseRL provides a scalable, resource-light pathway for enhancing reasoning in large language models by leveraging failures for continuous self-improvement, reducing data curation needs and dependence on external supervision.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a central paradigm for advancing reasoning in large language models, yet most existing methods still depend on stronger teacher models or heavily curated difficult datasets, limiting scalable capability improvement. In this paper, we introduce DenoiseRL, a reinforcement learning framework that substitutes external supervision with recovery-oriented optimization over failures from weak models. Instead of relying on stronger supervision or carefully engineered data, DenoiseRL learns directly from incorrect reasoning traces by converting them into opportunities for improvement, making training more scalable and less dependent on external resources. This yields a richer and more diverse learning signal, improving exploration efficiency from imperfect model behavior. As a result, DenoiseRL improves reasoning performance and overall training efficiency while reducing the need for expensive data curation or stronger teacher models. Empirically, DenoiseRL consistently outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines across competitive mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks and promotes stronger self-corrective behavior as training difficulty increases, highlighting an effective and scalable alternative pathway for improving reasoning in large language models.

</details>


### [261] [GONDOR to the Rescue: Satisficing Planning with Low Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28454)
*Yonatan Vernik,Alexander Tuisov,Alexander Shleyfman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GONDOR extends Greedy Best-First Search with memory-aware compression and sparse anchor states, enabling search under tight memory by periodically compacting the search tree and re-searching to reconstruct paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GBFS degrades under strict memory limits; memory-constrained planning (e.g., on edge devices) requires a method that preserves coverage without exhausting memory.

Method: Periodically compress the search tree while retaining a sparse set of anchor states (outposts); reconstruct the path by re-searching between anchors to reach the goal; explore variants via different outpost selection policies; optionally use Bloom filters for compact duplicate detection in the closed list.

Result: Empirical results show improved coverage under low memory budgets compared to standard GBFS across numeric planning domains and heuristic configurations; authors release the implementation and a Bloom-filter variant to support further research on memory-efficient heuristic search.

Conclusion: GONDOR provides a practical, memory-efficient extension to GBFS that maintains search effectiveness under tight memory constraints, with configurable outpost policies and a Bloom-filter variant.

Abstract: Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS) is the dominant approach for solving search problems where the goal can be estimated with a heuristic, such as planning, route finding, navigation, and pathfinding. This is especially true when the memory is tightly constrained, such as planning on edge devices. To alleviate that, we present GONDOR (Greedy Online Navigation with Dynamic Outpost-based Re-search), a memory-efficient extension of GBFS that allows search to continue under strict memory limits by periodically compressing the search tree while retaining a sparse set of anchor states, then upon reaching the goal reconstructs the path by re-searching between the sparse states. We analyze the algorithm and discuss several variants defined by different outpost selection policies. In addition, we explore using Bloom filters for compact duplicate detection in the closed list. Experiments across numeric planning domains and heuristic configurations show that GONDOR consistently improves coverage under low memory budgets compared to standard GBFS. We release the implementation of GONDOR and the Bloom-filter variant to facilitate further research on memory-efficient heuristic search.

</details>


### [262] [Diffusion Large Language Models for Visual Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28456)
*Jeong Hun Yeo,Chae Won Kim,Hyeongseop Rha,Yong Man Ro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces DLLM-VSR, a diffusion-based large language model framework for visual speech recognition that uses iterative masked denoising with confidence-based unmasking, plus a two-stage training strategy and length-guided decoding, achieving SOTA 19.5% WER on LRS3 with only labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address premature, context-poor decisions in left-to-right autoregressive VSR by enabling flexible-order decoding and leveraging bidirectional context through confident, early-decoded tokens; reduce target-length uncertainty to improve DLLM-based VSR.

Method: Framework uses iterative masked denoising with flexible decoding order and confidence-based unmasking to commit high-confidence tokens and reuse them as bidirectional context. Two-stage training separates (1) visual-to-text content alignment from (2) length modeling. To mitigate length uncertainty, length-guided candidate decoding constructs plausible transcript-length hypotheses from video duration, decodes under multiple hypotheses, and reranks by length plausibility and decoding confidence.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art WER of 19.5% on LRS3 using only labeled training data; also identifies a gap to oracle-length decoding and shows that reducing target-length uncertainty improves DLLM-based VSR.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that diffusion LMs can be effectively adapted to VSR via iterative masked denoising, targeted training, and length-aware decoding, yielding strong performance on a standard benchmark without requiring additional unlabeled data.

Abstract: Existing Visual Speech Recognition (VSR) systems commonly rely on left-to-right autoregressive decoding, which can force premature decisions on visually ambiguous tokens before sufficient context is available. We propose DLLM-VSR, to the best of our knowledge, the first Diffusion Large Language Model (DLLM)-based VSR framework, formulating transcription as iterative masked denoising with flexible-order decoding. With confidence-based unmasking, DLLM-VSR commits high-confidence positions early and uses the committed tokens as bidirectional context to refine ambiguous ones. To adapt DLLMs to VSR, we introduce a two-stage masked-denoising training strategy that separates visual-to-text content alignment from length modeling. We further observe a performance gap with oracle-length decoding, which assumes access to the true transcript length, indicating that reducing target-length uncertainty can improve DLLM-based VSR. To reduce this gap, we develop length-guided candidate decoding, which uses video duration to construct plausible transcript-length hypotheses, decodes under multiple hypotheses, and reranks candidates using length plausibility and decoding confidence. The proposed method achieves a state-of-the-art WER of 19.5\% on LRS3 using only its labeled training data.

</details>


### [263] [From Learning Resources to Competencies: LLM-Based Tagging with Evidence and Graph Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28483)
*Ngoc Luyen Le,Marie-Hélène Abel,Bertrand Laforge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An end-to-end LLM-based tagging pipeline (LBG) aligns LMS resources with a structured competency framework, segmenting content, retrieving graph-enriched candidate competencies, using an LLM to select and cite evidence, and refining results via the competency graph. Evaluated on UTC CS competencies with strong metric gains and improved traceability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual, labor-intensive tagging and opaque automatic methods hamper transparency in competency-based LMS search and analytics; need explainable, evidence-backed alignment.

Method: 1) Segment LMS resources (instructional content and assessments) into pedagogical fragments. 2) Retrieve candidate competencies from graph-enriched, structured profiles. 3) Use an LLM to select relevant competencies and produce supporting evidence spans from the fragment text. 4) Refine predictions using the competency graph structure and aggregate to the resource level. 5) Evaluate on a dataset from UTC with 22 competencies across course materials (LBG: LLM+BM25+Graph).

Result: Micro-F1 0.57 and Macro-F1 0.50 at the fragment level; Macro-F1 0.51 at the resource level; MRR 0.82. Outperforms zero-shot/few-shot LLM variants, retrieval/similarity baselines, and supervised classifiers; provides more mechanically traceable evidence spans for auditing and educational analysis.

Conclusion: The LBG pipeline delivers strong, explainable competency alignment for LMS resources, enabling competency-based search and curriculum analytics with transparent evidence spans and improved auditing.

Abstract: Linking learning resources to a structured competency framework is key to enabling competency-based search and curriculum analytics in Learning Management Systems (LMS). However, manual tagging is labor-intensive, and fully automatic methods often lack transparency. In this paper, we present an end-to-end alignment pipeline that uses a large language model (LLM) as a constrained, evidence-producing tagger. LMS resources -both instructional content and assessments -are first segmented into meaningful pedagogical fragments. For each fragment, a small set of candidate competencies is retrieved from structured competency profiles enriched with graph-based context. The LLM then selects the most relevant competencies from this set and provides supporting evidence spans from the fragment text. These predictions are refined using the structure of the competency graph and aggregated at the resource level. We evaluate our approach on a dataset built from the Computer Science department's competency referential at the Université de Technologie de Compiègne (UTC), covering 22 competencies across multiple course materials. Our LLM+BM25+Graph (LBG) pipeline achieves strong results, with a micro-F1 of 0.57 and macro-F1 of 0.50 at the fragment level, 0.51 macro-F1 at the resource level, and an MRR of 0.82outperforming zero-shot and few-shot LLM variants, retrieval/similarity baselines, and supervised classifiers -while also producing more mechanically traceable evidence spans to support human auditing and educational analysis.

</details>


### [264] [ProvMind: Provenance-grounded reasoning for materials synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28487)
*Yiming Zhang,Ryo Tamura,Koji Tsuda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes MatProcBench, a provenance-grounded benchmark for materials process optimization, and ProvMind, a memory-based process reasoning framework. ProvMind retrieves analogous provenance-aware training processes and uses a language model for constrained decision making, achieving 52.84% accuracy on a dual-OOD split and outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computational formulations flatten synthesis procedures into text or steps, hindering reasoning over routes, conditions, tools, and causal dependencies. There is a need for benchmarks and methods that capture provenance and causal structure in materials processing and that test generalization under distribution shifts (same-split, shift-aware, dual-OOD).

Method: MatProcBench is built from literature-mined MatPROV graphs to evaluate seven process-reasoning tasks (route continuity, step-level variable inference, global causal consistency) across same-split and shift-aware evaluations, including a strict dual-OOD split combining temporal and material-class shifts. ProvMind is a process-memory reasoning framework that retrieves analogous training processes, converts them into provenance-aware option-level compatibility scores, and uses a language model for constrained final decision making.

Result: ProvMind achieves 52.84% accuracy on the dual-OOD split, outperforming prompting, retrieval-augmented, and supervised fine-tuning baselines.

Conclusion: The work introduces provenance-grounded benchmarks and a memory-based reasoning framework that improve process planning for materials. The dual-OOD evaluation establishes a stringent test of generalization. Together, MatProcBench and ProvMind set a foundation for future research in provenance-aware process synthesis and reasoning.

Abstract: Materials process optimization requires reasoning over routes, conditions, tools and causal dependencies, yet most computational formulations flatten synthesis procedures into text or ordered steps. We introduce MatProcBench, a provenance-grounded benchmark constructed from literature-mined MatPROV graphs, to evaluate seven process-reasoning tasks spanning route continuity, step-level variable inference and global causal consistency under both same-split and shift-aware evaluation, including a strict dual-OOD split that combines temporal and material-class shift. We further introduce ProvMind, a process-memory reasoning framework that retrieves analogous training processes, converts them into provenance-aware option-level compatibility scores, and uses a language model for constrained final decision making. ProvMind achieves 52.84\% accuracy on the dual-OOD split, outperforming prompting, retrieval-augmented and supervised fine-tuning baselines.

</details>


### [265] [Benchmarking AI for low-resource contexts: Thinking beyond leaderboards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28508)
*Aakash Pant,Kavya Shah,Apoorv Agnihotri,Sneha Nikam,Prasaanth Balraj,Nakul Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evaluate AI systems in deployed, low-resource settings; benchmark design should reflect deployment constraints, not just model quality; propose a shared, context-aware reporting framework and concise artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Laboratory benchmarks often misrepresent real-world performance in low-resource environments due to constraints like noise, connectivity, hardware, and domain shift; decision-makers need deployment-aware evaluation.

Method: Structured analysis of benchmark families across speech, chat/RAG, and vision; identify gaps between lab evaluation and deployment; develop a deployment-centric reporting framework with task-specific profiles and standardized one-page benchmark cards and documentation.

Result: Gaps between laboratory evaluation and real-world deployment are pervasive; different application classes require distinct evaluation profiles; a shared deployment-aware reporting framework is proposed to maintain comparability while capturing deployment context.

Conclusion: Adopt deployment-centric evaluation units, implement concise artifacts (cards, deployment profiles), and document failure handling and human oversight to guide practical deployment across domains.

Abstract: Existing AI evaluation practices often fail to capture how systems actually perform in low-resource environments, where operational constraints shape usability as much as model quality. Through a structured analysis of existing benchmark families across speech, chat/RAG, and vision systems, we identify critical gaps between laboratory evaluation practices and real-world deployment conditions in low-resource environments. We argue that the meaningful unit of assessment is the deployed system rather than an isolated model and that effective evaluation frameworks must integrate task performance with deployment conditions such as noisy inputs, code-switching, intermittent connectivity, low-end hardware, and domain shift. At the same time, benchmarks should recognize that different application classes require distinct evaluation profiles rather than a single aggregate score that obscures operational differences. To support practical decision-making, we propose a shared reporting framework that preserves comparability across systems and application types while remaining sensitive to deployment context. Finally, we emphasize the need for concise and actionable reporting artifacts for policymakers, donors, and implementers, including standardized one-page benchmark cards, deployment profiles, and explicit documentation of failure handling procedures and human oversight mechanisms.

</details>


### [266] [GS-FUSE: Granger-Supervised Gated Fusion and Multi-Granularity Alignment for Event-Driven Financial Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28520)
*Yang Zhang,En Chun,Ziyun Mao,Yulu Wu,Jun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Granger-supervised, causal-aware gated fusion for multimodal forecasting that gates event text based on incremental predictive value, plus multi-granularity alignment; a plug-and-play adapter for LLMs and time-series models, achieving superior performance on real financial datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal models fuse text and prices symmetrically and fail to quantify whether event text adds predictive value; need directional event-to-price structure and heterogeneous signal roles.

Method: Introduces a Granger-supervised gating mechanism that opens to event text only when it provides predictive value beyond history; a multi-granularity alignment mechanism aligning high-level event representations and fine-grained textual cues with future trajectories; plugin design for LLMs and time-series foundations.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real-world financial datasets shows GS-Fuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across assets and forecasting horizons.

Conclusion: GS-Fuse offers a flexible, causality-aware, directional multimodal framework for event-driven market forecasting; practical plug-and-play deployment; potential avenues include interpretability, robustness across regimes, and extension to other domains.

Abstract: Accurately forecasting the impact of salient financial events on markets is critical for investors and policymakers. However, existing multimodal time-series models typically fuse text and prices symmetrically, without an explicit way to decide when event text is truly predictive, and thus struggle to exploit the directional event-to-price structure and the heterogeneous roles of textual and price signals. In this work, we propose GS-Fuse, a multimodal event-based forecasting framework that employs (i) a Granger-supervised, causal-aware gated fusion module, which learns to open toward event text only when it provides incremental predictive value beyond historical prices, and (ii) a multi-granularity alignment mechanism that jointly aligns high-level event representations and fine-grained textual cues with future market trajectories. Built as a flexible, plug-and-play adapter on top of off-the-shelf large language models and time-series foundation models, GS-Fuse can be instantiated across diverse backbones and market settings. Extensive experiments on real-world financial datasets show that GS-Fuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art time-series and multimodal baselines across multiple assets and forecasting horizons.

</details>


### [267] [Let Relations Speak: An End-to-End LLM-GNN Soft Prompt Framework for Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28524)
*Zhixing Zuo,Huilin He,Jiasheng Wu,Dawei Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes LGSPF, a soft-prompt-based framework that integrates a parallel GNN encoder with an LLM to perform fraud detection on graphs without heavy textual data, achieving state-of-the-art results and improved interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud detection on graphs suffers from lack of rich textual attributes and multi-relational complexity. Hard textualization of graphs can distort features; there is a need to align semantic understanding between LLMs and graph structures.

Method: Introduce LGSPF which uses soft prompts to connect graph structure to semantic space without relying on text. A parallel GNN encoder translates multi-relational topology into graph tokens for the LLM, enabling fine-grained fraud comprehension. End-to-end optimization aligns deep semantics between LLM and GNN.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse fraud benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance. Additional experiments validate that LGSPF enhances semantic interpretability of fraud behaviors.

Conclusion: LGSPF effectively bridges graph structure and semantic space without heavy textual data, yielding high accuracy and improved interpretability in fraud detection.

Abstract: In recent years, Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great capability in processing graph tasks such as fraud detection. However, most existing methods rely heavily on rich text attributes, which poses difficulties for this domain due to the lack of textual data. Although some pioneering methods attempt to overcome it, their textualization of graph structures via hard prompts easily leads to feature distortion. Additionally, fraud detection often exhibits multi-relational complexity, where current methods struggle to capture this deep semantic information. To address these challenges, we propose LLM-GNN Soft Prompt Framework (LGSPF). Specifically, LGSPF bridges the graph structure and semantic space using soft prompt to eliminate reliance on text. We further introduce a parallel Graph Neural Network (GNN) encoder to translate multi-relational topologies into graph tokens for fine-grained LLM fraud comprehension. Through end-to-end optimization, LGSPF enhances deep semantic alignment between LLM and GNN. Experiments across diverse fraud detection benchmarks demonstrate our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, we further validate the contribution of LGSPF on enhancing the semantic interpretability of fraud behaviors.

</details>


### [268] [Entropy-aware Masking for Masked Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28526)
*Gokul Srinivasagan,Kai Hartung,Munir Georges*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Entropy-guided masking and self-masking improve masked language model pretraining, achieving ~5% GLUE gains, with distillation providing the best results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Random masking in MLM may miss informative learning signals. Targeting tokens with high predictive uncertainty (high entropy) can provide stronger supervision and improve pretraining efficiency.

Method: Compute token-wise prediction entropy to select which tokens to mask. Introduce a self-masking variant that does not require an external reference model. Optionally combine with knowledge distillation to further boost performance.

Result: Reported average GLUE score improvement of about 5% over baseline. When combined with knowledge distillation, the approach yields the best overall results.

Conclusion: Entropy-based masking is an effective enhancement to MLM pretraining. Self-masking improves training efficiency without external references, and knowledge distillation can further enhance performance.

Abstract: Masked language modeling has become a standard pretraining objective for training encoder-based language models. In this approach, certain tokens in the input are masked, and the model learns to predict them using the surrounding context. This process enables the model to capture both syntactic and semantic properties of language. Conventionally, the tokens selected for masking are chosen at random, which may not always yield the most effective learning signals. In this work, we examine a token masking strategy based on entropy distribution. We use the model's entropy over token predictions to identify which tokens should be masked. This method aims to target tokens that are more informative and uncertain to improve the training efficacy. We also propose a novel self-masking approach that enhances training efficiency without relying on an external reference model. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves an average performance improvement of 5% in GLUE scores compared to the baseline. Further, we experiment with combining knowledge distillation with entropy masking, resulting in the best overall results.

</details>


### [269] [Do Agents Know What They Can't Do? Evaluating Feasibility Awareness in Tool-Using Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28532)
*Liang Cheng,Mingsheng Cai,Jiuming Jiang,Luo Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FeasiGen constructs infeasible tasks by masking critical tools from successful traces, enabling feasibility-aware evaluation; results show weak infeasibility detection and benefits of multi-agent setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce wasted computation by detecting infeasible tasks in tool-using agents and to study the detection capabilities and evaluation of such infeasibility.

Method: Extract tool-calling traces from successful executions across multiple agent systems; identify tools consistently critical across diverse strategies; mask these tools to convert solvable tasks into infeasible ones; optionally verify annotations with human judgment; introduce feasibility-aware metrics; evaluate on nine models.

Result: Human verification confirms infeasibility annotations with >94% accuracy; across nine models, infeasibility detection is substantially weak with false continue rates up to 73.9%; multi-agent architectures mitigate erroneous execution under infeasible conditions.

Conclusion: Feasibility-aware evaluation reveals substantial room for improvement in detecting infeasibility; masking critical tools is an effective way to generate infeasible tasks; multi-agent collaboration helps reduce erroneous continuation; suggests future work in improving detectors and strategies for recognizing infeasible states.

Abstract: Tool-using agents often incur substantial computational cost due to long reasoning chains and iterative tool usage. In practical scenarios, many tasks become infeasible under constrained tool environments, where the capabilities required for successful task completion are unavailable. Detecting infeasible tasks and stopping execution early can significantly reduce unnecessary execution cost. In this work, we propose FeasiGen, an automatic pipeline for constructing infeasible agent tasks by identifying the critical tools required for successful task completion. Our approach extracts tool-calling traces from successful executions across multiple agent systems, identifies critical tools consistently shared across diverse execution strategies, and masks these tools to automatically transform solvable tasks into infeasible ones. Human verification confirms that the infeasibility annotations for our constructed tasks achieve over 94% accuracy. We further introduce feasibility-aware evaluation metrics for measuring whether agents can recognize infeasible tasks and stop execution appropriately. Extensive evaluations across nine models reveal substantially weak infeasibility detection ability, with false continue rate reaching up to 73.9%. We further observe that multi-agent architectures significantly reduce erroneous execution under infeasible conditions.

</details>


### [270] [Cultural Binding Heads in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28543)
*Avrile Floro,Luca Benedetto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Identifies 2–3 mid-layer attention heads causally linked to cultural binding across 8 models; knockout reduces binding by 9–23%; pre-training origins suggested as heads transfer from instruct to base; α-scaling (2–3) yields 1–3 pp improvement in cultural differentiation with minimal impact on neutral reasoning; knowledge probing reveals routing bottleneck, not knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore why LLMs show uniform treatment across cultures and uncover mechanistic substrates of cultural binding to inform controllable, context-aware behavior.

Method: Mechanistic interpretability plus a factorial design on the N4 cultural appropriation benchmark (Wang et al., 2025); identify mid-layer heads with causal impact; perform edge knockouts; test cross-architecture transfer (four architectures, base and instruction variants) across eight models; conduct generation-time α-scaling perturbations; run a knowledge-probing task.

Result: Identified 2–3 mid-layer attention heads per model that causally contribute to cultural binding across all eight models; deleting identity-to-item edges reduces binding strength by 9–23%; identified heads transfer from instruct to base, implying pre-training origin; α-scaling yields graded dose-response; at α=2–3, cultural differentiation improves by 1–3 percentage points with neutral reasoning largely intact; knowledge probing shows models know 3–5× more than they act upon, indicating routing bottleneck rather than lack of knowledge.

Conclusion: Cultural binding appears to be shaped during pre-training and can be modulated via targeted mid-layer interventions with limited side effects; the bottleneck lies in routing rather than knowledge, suggesting controllable alignment opportunities through mechanistic interventions.

Abstract: LLMs often default to equal treatment across cultural groups, even though context warrants differentiation: this is a lack of difference awareness. Using mechanistic interpretability and a factorial design on the N4 cultural appropriation benchmark from Wang et al. (2025), we identify 2-3 mid-layer attention heads per model that contribute causally to cultural binding across eight models (four architectures, base and instruct). Cultural binding is the process of associating cultural items with the appropriate identity. Knockout of the identity-to-item edges on these heads lowers the binding strength by 9-23%. The identified heads transfer from instruct to base models, suggesting that cultural binding is created at pre-training. An $α$-scaling shows a graded dose-response and moderate amplification steering at generation ($α= 2-3$) increases cultural differentiation accuracy by 1-3 pp while leaving neutral reasoning mostly intact. A knowledge probing task shows that models know 3-5 times more than they act upon it, indicating that the bottleneck lies in routing and not knowledge.

</details>


### [271] [Refusal Before Decoding: Detecting and Exploiting Refusal Signals in Intermediate LLM Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28553)
*Matteo Gioele Collu,Riccardo Conte,Alberto Giaretta,Denis Kleyko,Mauro Conti,Matteo Zavatteri,Roberto Confalonieri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Refusal signals are detectable in intermediate LLM activations via linear probes, enabling probe-guided attacks that are as effective as fully evaluated methods but faster; scaling improves usefulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand where safety-relevant (refusal) behavior is represented and whether it can be acted on before decoding; to reduce computational cost of attack generation using probe scores.

Method: Train linear probes on residual stream activations per transformer block to predict refusal; develop Mechanistic AutoDAN that uses probe-guided partial forward passes and probe-based scoring within a genetic prompt search loop; compare with vanilla AutoDAN; examine cross-model transfer and scaling.

Result: Refusal is linearly decodable before the final layer; Mechanistic AutoDAN achieves attack success rates competitive with vanilla AutoDAN; reduces per-iteration search time by up to 72%; probe-guided prompts match or exceed AutoDAN's cross-model transfer in several configurations; probe guidance becomes more useful with model scale.

Conclusion: Safety-relevant behavior is embedded as a structured, actionable signal in intermediate LLM activations, not only at output; probing can guide efficient, scalable adversarial prompting and understandability of model safety dynamics.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate whether refusal behavior can be predicted from LLM intermediate activations before decoding using linear probes trained on residual stream activations at each transformer block. We find that refusal is linearly decodable well before the final layer, indicating that safety-relevant behavior is represented in intermediate activations before output generation. To test whether this signal is actionable, we introduce Mechanistic AutoDAN, a probe-guided variant of AutoDAN that replaces full-model fitness evaluation with partial forward passes and probe-based scoring inside a genetic prompt search loop. Across the evaluated models, our method achieves attack success rates competitive with vanilla AutoDAN while reducing per-iteration search time by up to 72%, and probe-guided prompts match or exceed AutoDAN's cross-model transfer in several configurations. We further find that the usefulness of probe guidance increases with model scale. Our results show that refusal is not only observable at the output level, but is encoded as a structured and actionable signal in intermediate LLM activations.

</details>


### [272] [A Matter of TASTE: Improving Coverage and Difficulty of Agent Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28556)
*Tomer Keren,Nitay Calderon,Asaf Yehudai,Yotam Perlitz,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Roi Reichert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TASTE automates task synthesis for tool-use benchmarks by learning an adaptive contrastive n-gram model to generate valid, diverse tool sequences, selecting representative sequences by clustering, and iteratively evolving difficulty to create τ^c-Bench, revealing saturation on τ^2-Bench and enabling scalable evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks saturate as agent capabilities advance and task creation is costly; mapping natural language to tool sequences captures only a subset of tool-use patterns. There is a need for broader tool-use coverage and automatic task generation to enable scalable evaluation.

Method: TASTE uses an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram model trained on LLM-judged validity signals to sample valid tool sequences covering many tool combinations. It clusters sequences to select representative ones, instantiates them into complete benchmark tasks, and iteratively evolves difficulty. The result is τ^c-Bench, a challenging extension of τ^2-Bench across three domains.

Result: Constructed τ^c-Bench and evaluated 11 agent/user LLM pairs. Models near-saturation on τ^2-Bench suffer severe performance drops on τ^c-Bench (e.g., Gemini-3-Flash falls from 0.82–0.94 to 0.28–0.61). Generated tasks more than double the number of unique tool combinations required. Demonstrates that high scores on existing benchmarks may reflect saturation rather than robust ability.

Conclusion: Automating difficult, high-coverage benchmark generation via TASTE enables continuous, scalable evaluation of future agents and helps ensure benchmarks reflect genuine tool-use competence rather than saturation.

Abstract: As agent capabilities advance, existing benchmarks, such as $τ^2$-Bench, are becoming increasingly saturated. Yet constructing new benchmark tasks remains complex, costly, and labor-intensive. Moreover, the standard approach, in which scenarios are first written in natural language and then mapped to tool sequences, captures only a narrow subset of the tool-use patterns agents exercise. In this paper, we address these problems by reversing the task construction process. We propose TASTE: Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution, an automatic method that generates challenging tasks with broader tool-use coverage. TASTE utilizes an Adaptive Contrastive $n$-gram model trained on LLM-judged validity signals. This enables sampling valid tool sequences that cover a vast range of tool combinations. TASTE then selects representative sequences from the pool via clustering, instantiates them into complete benchmark tasks, and refines them through iterative difficulty evolution. Using TASTE, we construct $τ^c$-Bench, a challenging extension of the three domains of $τ^2$-Bench. We evaluate $11$ agent/user LLM pairs and find that models nearly saturating $τ^2$-Bench suffer severe performance drops on our tasks (e.g., Gemini-3-Flash falls from $0.82\!-\!0.94$ to $0.28\!-\!0.61$). Beyond increasing difficulty, our generated tasks more than double the number of unique tool combinations agents must execute. Our results suggest high scores on existing benchmarks often reflect saturation rather than robust task-solving ability. By automating the generation of difficult, high-coverage benchmarks, TASTE enables continuous, scalable evaluation of future agents.

</details>


### [273] [Tree of Thoughts as a Classical Heuristic Search Problem: Formal Foundations and Design Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28566)
*Guni Sharon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Unified taxonomy for Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) reasoning mapped to classical heuristic search, unifying NLP and planning work; identifies design patterns and open challenges inviting cross-disciplinary collaboration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmentation and inconsistent terminology across ToT research in NLP and Automated Planning hinder systematic design and evaluation. A unified taxonomy based on heuristic search components enables clearer analysis and principled development of ToT systems.

Method: Synthesizes existing ToT literature into a taxonomy aligned with state representation (granularity of thoughts), successor generation (prompting operators), and heuristic evaluation (self-assessment). Analyzes works within this taxonomy and distills recurring design patterns (systematic Best-First Search for shallow tasks; DFS/MCTS-like lookahead for deep reasoning). Highlights open algorithmic challenges at the interface of heuristic search and LLM reasoning.

Result: A unified ToT taxonomy grounded in classical heuristic search, identification of core design patterns, and delineation of open research questions; a call for collaboration between heuristic search researchers and the LLM/ToT community.

Conclusion: Bridging heuristic search and LLM reasoning to advance ToT methods; the taxonomy provides a principled framework for future work and evaluation, and invites the heuristic search community to contribute to this emerging domain.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet their standard generation process -- auto-regressive token prediction -- is inherently myopic and prone to cascading errors. To address this, the Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) framework creates a search space over intermediate reasoning steps, allowing search models to explore, look ahead, and backtrack. However, current ToT research remains fragmented across Natural Language Processing and Automated Planning communities, often using inconsistent terminology and ad-hoc implementations. Consequently, we synthesize the ToT landscape through a unified taxonomy based on classical heuristic search terminology. We map LLM-based reasoning to classical search components: state representation (granularity of thoughts), successor generation (prompting operators), and heuristic evaluation (self-assessment of progress). We analyze existing work within the context of our taxonomy and identify emerging design patterns: systematic search (Best-First Search) for shallow, deterministic tasks and lookahead-heavy strategies (DFS, MCTS) for deep multi-step reasoning. We conclude by identifying open algorithmic challenges at the intersection of heuristic search and LLM reasoning, and call on the heuristic search community to engage with this emerging domain.

</details>


### [274] [A Conflict-Aware Penalty and Statistical Loss Framework for Balancing Modalities and Enhancing Stability in Multimodal Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28575)
*Jianheng Dai,Jiazhang Liang,Sijie Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Conflict-aware Penalty (CP) and Statistical Loss (SL) to mitigate gradient-norm conflicts and align output statistics in multimodal sentiment analysis, enabling synergistic training and achieving state-of-the-art results on CMU-MOSI; ablations validate each component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal sentiment analysis, text encoders often dominate due to higher expressiveness, causing gradient conflicts across modalities that destabilize training and hinder effective fusion. A method to balance gradient contributions and align predicted statistics is needed.

Method: Propose CP to detect and penalize gradient-norm conflicts between modalities at each training step; propose SL to align predicted distribution statistics with empirical input statistics. Integrate these losses into a unified framework with adaptive modality encoding, gated cross-modal fusion, and unimodal auxiliary heads for robust MSA.

Result: On CMU-MOSI, the approach achieves state-of-the-art performance. Ablation studies show the positive impact of CP, SL, and the integrated framework, validating the contribution of each component.

Conclusion: CP and SL enable synergistic training across modalities, preventing dominant text gradients from harming auxiliary objectives and stabilizing multimodal optimization, leading to improved MSA performance with a unified, adaptable architecture.

Abstract: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) fuses text, acoustic, and visual streams to infer sentiment. Because pre-trained text encoders are far more expressive than their acoustic and visual counterparts, the text modality tends to dominate optimization, suppressing weaker modalities and inducing gradient norm conflicts that destabilize training. To address this, we propose a Conflict-aware Penalty (CP) that detects and penalizes gradient norm conflicts at each training step, and a Statistical Loss (SL) that aligns predicted distribution statistics with empirical input statistics. Crucially, CP prevents dominant modality gradients from interfering with the SL objective, enabling synergistic training within a unified framework incorporating adaptive modality encoding, gated cross-modal fusion, and unimodal auxiliary heads. Experiments on CMU-MOSI demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of each component.

</details>


### [275] [Continual Model Routing in Evolving Model Hubs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28577)
*Jack Bell,Giacomo Carfì,Gerlando Gramaglia,Vincenzo Lomonaco*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CMRBench and CARvE to tackle continual model routing in AI model hubs, scaling routing across thousands of experts and updating with new models/tasks; CARvE outperforms baselines on model, family, domain levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid growth of AI model hubs creates two core challenges: scalable model selection across thousands of experts and continual adaptation of routing as new models and tasks appear; there is a need for realistic benchmarks and efficient retrieval methods.

Method: Formalizes Continual Model Routing (CMR). Builds CMRBench, a large-scale benchmark simulating hub expansion with over 2,000 candidate models. Proposes CARvE, a contrastive embedding approach using checkpoint-based anchoring and structured replay to enable efficient continual routing.

Result: CARvE significantly outperforms zero-shot retrieval, fine-tuning, and adapter-merging baselines across model, family, and domain-level accuracy; extensive ablations validate robustness of the approach.

Conclusion: CMR provides a practical framework for evolving model hubs. CMRBench offers a realistic, large-scale benchmark for continual routing, and CARvE delivers effective embedding-based continual routing with replay, enabling scalable adaptation for growing hubs.

Abstract: AI model hubs provide access to a rapidly growing collection of powerful pre-trained models, enabling off-the-shelf mixture-of-experts systems with different routing strategies. However, this rapid growth poses two fundamental challenges: scaling model selection across thousands of experts and continually updating routing mechanisms as new models and tasks are introduced. In this paper, we formalise this setting as Continual Model Routing (CMR) and propose CMRBench, a new large-scale benchmark simulating realistic hub expansion and including over 2,000 candidate models. Finally, we introduce CARvE, a contrastive embedding approach for efficient continual model routing via checkpoint-based anchoring and structured replay. Extensive empirical results and ablations show that CARvE significantly outperforms zero-shot retrieval, fine-tuning, and adapter-merging baselines in model, family, and domain-level accuracy.

</details>


### [276] [MUSE: Benchmarking Manufacturable, Functional, and Assemblable Text-to-CAD Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28579)
*Xiaoyu Dong,Zhi Li,Xiao-Ming Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MUSE, a Text-to-CAD benchmark for complex B-Rep assemblies, with a 3-stage evaluation (code, geometry, design-intent) and rubric-based VLM judgments; shows current models struggle to reach engineering-quality designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize single-part geometry and shape similarity; they fail to capture functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability in industrial product design. A scalable, structured evaluation is needed for Text-to-CAD.

Method: Construct a dataset of practical design instances with Design Specifications; implement a 3-stage protocol: (1) code check for executable CAD output, (2) geometric check for valid geometry and assemble- ability, (3) design-intent alignment using rubric-based judgments on functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability; validate a rubric-based VLM judge against human annotations; evaluate both closed-source and open-source LLMs.

Result: Evidence of a failure cascade: models progress from generating executable code to valid geometry to engineering-ready design, with even strong models achieving limited success on fine-grained engineering criteria; the work provides the MUSE dataset, leaderboard, and evaluation framework.

Conclusion: MUSE advances Text-to-CAD benchmarking from purely geometric generation toward true engineering design by assessing functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability through a scalable, rubric-based evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced text-driven 3D generation, yet Text-to-CAD remains far from supporting industrial product design. Existing benchmarks focus primarily on generating single-part CAD models and evaluate them using geometric similarity metrics that fail to capture functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability. To address this gap, we introduce MUSE, a Text-to-CAD benchmark focused on complex, editable boundary representation (B-Rep) assemblies. MUSE pairs practical design instances with structured Design Specifications and evaluates generated models through a three-stage protocol: code check, geometric check, and design-intent alignment. The final stage uses design-specific rubrics to assess functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability, moving beyond shape matching toward practical design quality. To enable scalable evaluation, we use a rubric-based visual language model (VLM) judge and validate its reliability through human annotation. Experiments on closed-source and open-source LLMs reveal a clear failure cascade from executable code to valid geometry and finally to engineering-ready design, with even the strongest models achieving limited success on fine-grained engineering criteria. Together, MUSE provides a realistic benchmark and evaluation framework for advancing Text-to-CAD from geometric generation toward true engineering design. Our project website, including the leaderboard, dataset, and code, is available at https://dong7313.github.io/muse-benchmark/.

</details>


### [277] [Satisfiability Solving with LLMs: A Matched-Pair Evaluation of Reasoning Capability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28602)
*Leizhen Zhang,Shuhan Chen,Sheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs were systematically tested on SAT tasks (2-SAT, 3-SAT) and reductions to Vertex Cover and discrete 3D packing to assess representation-invariant reasoning. Conventional metrics can be misleading; a paired-formula protocol with Accurate Differentiation Rate (ADR) is proposed to better isolate reasoning from heuristics. Cross-representation consistency shows stable decision rules. Concludes SAT is a conservative, robust probe for LLM reasoning when evaluated with ADR and paired checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs truly reason about SAT problems or rely on superficial patterns, and to achieve representation-robust evaluation across CNF and alternative representations (graph/packing). Address biases of standard metrics and examine reasoning across phase transitions.

Method: Evaluate LLMs on 2-SAT and 3-SAT, including two canonical reductions (Vertex Cover and discrete 3D packing). Use standard metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and SAT phase-transition analysis. Develop a paired-formula protocol with minimally different satisfiable/unsatisfiable instances. Define Accurate Differentiation Rate (ADR) requiring correct classification of both members of each pair. Assess cross-representation consistency by converting CNF to Vertex Cover and 3-SAT to discrete 3D packing and comparing decisions.

Result: Conventional metrics can be misleading: models often over-predict satisfiable formulas, fail to exhibit easy-hard-easy behavior around the 3-SAT threshold, and performance degrades as variable count grows. ADR effectively distinguishes reasoning-oriented models from heuristic ones and correlates with witness validity. Across representations, model decisions on CNF and the corresponding graph/packing instances agree for >80% of cases for most models, indicating stable, representation-invariant decision rules. Overall, SAT serves as a conservative probe for LLM reasoning, with paired ADR-based evaluation offering a more faithful assessment than traditional metrics.

Conclusion: A paired-formula evaluation framework with ADR provides a robust, representation-robust assessment of LLM SAT reasoning. Cross-representation consistency supports stable reasoning strategies, and reliance on ADR with paired checks yields a more faithful evaluation than conventional metrics.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks that implicitly reduce to Boolean satisfiability (SAT), yet their reasoning ability on SAT remains unclear. We present a systematic study of LLMs on 2-SAT and 3-SAT, together with two canonical reductions, Vertex Cover and discrete 3D packing, to probe representation-invariant reasoning. We first evaluate models using conventional metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1, as well as the SAT phase-transition setting. We find that these metrics can be misleading: many models obtain high scores by over-predicting satisfiable formulas, fail to reproduce the classical easy-hard-easy signature around the 3-SAT threshold, and degrade sharply as the number of variables grows.
  To address this problem, we introduce a paired-formula protocol based on minimally different satisfiable and unsatisfiable instances, together with Accurate Differentiation Rate (ADR), which requires both members of each pair to be classified correctly. ADR separates reasoning-oriented models from heuristic ones and correlates with witness validity. Beyond CNF, we test cross-representation consistency by converting CNF to Vertex Cover and 3-SAT to discrete 3D packing. Model decisions on CNF and on the corresponding graph or packing instances agree for most models on more than 80 percent of instances, suggesting stable decision rules across representations. Overall, our results show that SAT is a conservative probe for LLM reasoning, and that paired evaluation with ADR provides a more faithful and representation-robust assessment than conventional metrics.

</details>


### [278] [Adaptive Multimodal Agents-Based Framework for Automatic Workflow Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28607)
*Susanna Cifani,Mario Luca Bernardi,Marta Cimitile*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-phase graph-based multi-agent framework that builds a topological knowledge base offline from execution logs and uses Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation over the graph with a closed-loop verification for robust, data-efficient workflow execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods struggle to bridge structured metadata parsing and general environmental perception, treating task sequences as discrete episodes and failing to capture underlying transition topology, which limits performance in novel or non-stationary scenarios.

Method: Offline discovery phase builds a topological knowledge base (graph) from fragmented execution logs. Inference uses Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation over the fixed graph plus a closed-loop collaborative verification protocol to dynamically self-correct and navigate. The system is multimodal and multi-agent, enabling improved task decomposition and adaptive navigation.

Result: Real-world validation showing high reliability and semantic awareness even with limited training data.

Conclusion: Graph-based topology-aware navigation with adaptive inference enhances robustness and data efficiency for autonomous workflow execution in non-stationary environments.

Abstract: Modern information systems require autonomous agents capable of navigating complex workflows, yet current methodologies often struggle with the transition from structured metadata parsing to general environmental perception. While the integration of MLLMs has enabled agents to interact directly with GUIs, existing approaches typically treat task sequences as discrete, linear episodes. This fragmentation prevents agents from capturing the underlying transition topology, limiting their effectiveness in novel or non-stationary scenarios. To address this, we propose a novel multimodal multi-agent framework that achieves automatic workflow execution through a distinct two-phase pipeline. First, during an offline discovery phase, the architecture adaptively constructs a topological knowledge base from fragmented execution logs. During inference, agents leverage Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over this fixed, pre-established graph, coupled with a closed-loop collaborative verification protocol to dynamically self-correct and navigate. This graph-based approach facilitates superior task decomposition and adaptive navigation performance. We validate our framework in a real-world context, demonstrating its ability to maintain high reliability and semantic awareness even with limited training data.

</details>


### [279] [LACUNA: Safe Agents as Recursive Program Holes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28617)
*Yaoyu Zhao,Yichen Xu,Oliver Bračevac,Cao Nguyen Pham,Frank Zhengqing Wu,Martin Odersky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LACUNA lets LLMs write code that is executed inside the agent runtime as atomic, type-checked actions, bridging the gap between model-generated actions and runtime control for safer, more expressive agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To increase the expressivity of LLM-driven agents by allowing model-written code to shape the runtime, while preserving safety through atomic actions and type checking.

Method: Introduce agent[T](task) as a typed, code-generated action; perform type-checking against the surrounding program before execution; if rejected, the environment remains unchanged and compiler diagnostics guide retries; enforce bounds on tools and data flows; support ReAct loops, sub-agents, skills, parallelism, and multi-model planning within ordinary control flow.

Result: 8.6% of generations rejected before execution with 0.7 retries per query on average; 27.1% accuracy on BrowseComp-Plus; 76.0% of 392 tasks solved across four domains on τ^2-bench, on par with a baseline agent.

Conclusion: LACUNA provides a safe, expressive programming model for LLM-driven agents that unifies model-written code with runtime execution, achieving competitive performance while maintaining strong safety guarantees; results motivate further exploration of typed, atomic actions and broader benchmarks.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly act by writing code, yet a split persists between the runtime that drives the agent and the code the model writes. The runtime owns the loop, context, and control flow, and the model has little say over any of them. Letting model-written code shape the runtime itself would make agents more expressive, but it would also sharpen safety problems. A model can be diverted by a prompt injection, call the wrong tool, or fail partway and leave an inconsistent state, and each such failure reaches further when the code shapes the runtime than when it expresses a single action. We present LACUNA, a programming model for agents that closes this split while preserving safety. Each agent action is a typed call $\texttt{agent[T](task)}$ that the LLM fills with code when execution reaches it, and the code is type-checked against the surrounding program before it runs. Because each action is accepted or rejected as a whole, a rejected one leaves the environment untouched, and its compiler diagnostics drive a retry. The same check also bounds which tools and data an action may use and how they flow. Our primitive expresses ReAct loops, sub-agents, skills, parallel decomposition, and multi-model planning as ordinary control flow. We evaluate LACUNA on a collection of test cases, BrowseComp-Plus, and $τ^2$-bench. On BrowseComp-Plus, $8.6\%$ of generations are rejected before execution, with 0.7 retries per query on average, and the agent reaches $27.1\%$ accuracy. On $τ^2$-bench, LACUNA solves $76.0\%$ of $392$ tasks across four domains with a capable model, on par with the baseline agent.

</details>


### [280] [Bandwidth-Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Edge-Cloud Many-to-Many Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28642)
*Yexing Du,Kaiyuan Liu,Youcheng Pan,Bo Yang,Ming Liu,Bing Qin,Yang Xiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Edge-cloud split inference for privacy-preserving, bandwidth-efficient multimodal LLMs enabling many-to-many S2TT across 45 languages; ESRT achieves state-of-the-art results with ESRT-4B/12B; reduces voice data leakage and bandwidth by up to 10x; introduces multi-task weighted curriculum learning to mitigate English-centric bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome on-device resource constraints, privacy risks of raw voice transmission, and English-centric bias that hinders scalable many-to-many S2TT in multimodal LLMs.

Method: Architect a split inference pipeline with a lightweight on-device speech encoder and adapter; transmit compressed intermediate features to the cloud; employ a multi-task weighted curriculum learning strategy with data balancing to improve cross-lingual consistency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art many-to-many speech-to-text translation across 45 languages (45×44 directions) on FLEURS with ESRT-4B and ESRT-12B; reduces voiceprint leakage and lowers bandwidth by up to 10x.

Conclusion: Code and models are released to promote reproducible, privacy-aware MLLM S2TT research; demonstrates effective privacy-preserving edge-cloud collaboration for multilingual translation.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated significant potential for speech-to-text translation (S2TT). However, existing deployment paradigms face critical challenges: pure on-device models suffer from resource constraints, while centralized cloud systems incur severe privacy risks and bandwidth bottlenecks by transmitting raw voice data. Furthermore, most models exhibit English-centric biases, restricting many-to-many translation scaling. In this paper, we propose Edge-cloud Speech Recognition and Translation (ESRT), a privacy-preserving and bandwidth-efficient collaborative edge-cloud MLLM framework. Specifically, we design an edge-cloud split inference architecture that retains a lightweight speech encoder and adapter on the device, transmitting only highly compressed intermediate features to the cloud. This fundamentally prevents voiceprint leakage and reduces bandwidth requirements by up to 10$\times$. To overcome English-centric bottlenecks, we introduce a multi-task weighted curriculum learning strategy with data balancing to ensure robust cross-lingual consistency. Extensive experiments on the FLEURS dataset demonstrate that our models, ESRT-4B and ESRT-12B, achieve state-of-the-art many-to-many S2TT performance across 45 languages ($45 \times 44$ directions). Code and models are released to facilitate reproducible, privacy-aware MLLM S2TT research. The code and models are released at https://github.com/yxduir/esrt.

</details>


### [281] [The Ethics of LLM Sandbox and Persona Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28647)
*Tim Gebbie,Stewart Gebbie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM guardrails can cause a reality laundering effect by shifting epistemic risk to users; argues for task-level, causal safety specifications rather than bottom-up moral policing; highlights persona shaping as non-neutral interface; concludes ethical AI becomes unethical if it substitutes reassurance for contact with reality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critique safety-by-morality approaches in LLMs that may distort user perception and transfer risk, and to propose a more rigorous, reality-grounded safety design.

Method: Conceptual analysis with finance-regulation analogies (Basel-style, B-BBEE, Societe Generale, London Whale), critique of guardrails and persona dynamics; advocates top-down task-specific causal requirements as safety basis.

Result: Introduces the concept of 'reality laundering', differentiates refusing harm from refusing reality, and outlines a design shift toward task-level safety specifications over response-level moral corrections; emphasizes the influence of interface on uncertainty and risk.

Conclusion: Ethical AI becomes substantively unethical when it substitutes institutional reassurance for engagement with reality; calls for redesign toward explicit task constraints and epistemic clarity.

Abstract: It is well known that LLM guardrails and trained persona dynamics can produce a reality gap: the distance between the world a LLM is permitted or shaped to describe, and the world in which users must act. Here we argue that actively generating reality gaps is in fact unethical because it knowingly shifts epistemic risk back to the uninformed user -- this is reality laundering. This can potentially cause harm when operationalised at scale. The risk is sharpest in high-exposure advice contexts, where users seek orientation rather than a bounded, externally checkable task. Guardrails naively appear ethically necessary when they claim to prevent direct harm, but often become suspect when they suppress truthful perception and launder uncomfortable mechanisms into acceptable abstractions. Basel-style financial regulation, B-BBEE-style compliance, Societe Generale, and the London Whale show how formal safety systems can become legible, gameable, and performative while real exposure migrates elsewhere. The same pattern can appear in LLMs as moral compliance: safe language, distorted reality. We therefore distinguish refusing harm, from refusing reality; and then argue for top-down causal requirements specification at the task level rather than bottom-up moral correction at the response or sandbox level. Persona dynamics matter because the assistant interface is not neutral; it shapes how uncertainty, conflict, authority, and risk are staged. The conclusion is that so-called ``ethical AI'' becomes substantively unethical when it substitutes institutional reassurance for contact with reality.

</details>


### [282] [AutoScientists: Self-Organizing Agent Teams for Long-Running Scientific Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28655)
*Shanghua Gao,Ada Fang,Marinka Zitnik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoScientists is a decentralized AI-agent team for long-running experiments that outperforms single-agent baselines across biomedical ML, GPT training optimization, and protein fitness prediction. It achieves strong gains on BioML-Bench, faster GPT training improvements, and higher Spearman correlations on ProteinGym.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI experimentation often follows a single research trajectory or relies on a fixed central planner. This limits sustained parallel exploration, adaptability to new evidence, and retention of failed directions across long experiments.

Method: A decentralized collection of AI agents interprets a shared experimental state, self-organizes into hypothesis-driven teams, critiques proposals before deploying compute, and shares successes and failures to minimize redundant exploration. The system preserves knowledge across long-running experiments while operating under budget constraints.

Result: Under matched budgets, AutoScientists outperforms prior AI agents across domains: on BioML-Bench (mean leaderboard percentile 74.4% across 24 tasks, +8.33% over the strongest AI agent), on GPT training optimization (target validation bits-per-byte 1.9x faster than Autoresearch; 7 vs. 0 improvements from a starting champion), and on ProteinGym fitness prediction (ACE2-Spike binding method +12.5% Spearman; across 217 assays +6.5% Spearman).

Conclusion: Decentralized, self-organizing AI experimentation teams can sustain parallel exploration, adapt to evolving evidence, and yield robust improvements across diverse scientific tasks, indicating strong potential for long-running computational science.

Abstract: Scientific research proceeds through iterative cycles of hypothesis generation, experiment design, execution, and revision. AI agents can automate parts of this process, but existing approaches typically follow a single research trajectory or coordinate through a central planner with fixed objectives. As a result, they struggle to sustain parallel exploration, adapt as experimental evidence changes, or preserve knowledge of failed directions over long-running experiments. We introduce AutoScientists, a decentralized team of AI agents for long-running computational scientific experimentation. Agents interpret a shared experimental state, self-organize into teams around promising hypotheses, critique proposals before using experimental compute, and share successes and failures to reduce redundant exploration. Under matched experimental budgets, AutoScientists improves over prior AI agents across biomedical machine learning, language-model training optimization, and protein fitness prediction. On BioML-Bench, spanning biomedical imaging, protein engineering, single-cell omics, and drug discovery, AutoScientists achieves a mean leaderboard percentile of 74.4% across 24 tasks, improving over the strongest AI agent by +8.33%. On GPT training optimization, AutoScientists reaches a target validation bits-per-byte 1.9x faster than Autoresearch and continues discovering improvements from a starting champion where the single-agent approach finds none (7 vs. 0 accepted improvements). On ProteinGym fitness prediction, AutoScientists discovers a method for ACE2-Spike binding that improves over the current state-of-the-art model by +12.5% in Spearman correlation. Applied without modification across all 217 ProteinGym assays, the same method improves over the prior state of the art by +6.5% (Spearman correlation).

</details>


### [283] [DREAM-R: Multimodal Speculative Reasoning with RL-Based Refined Drafting, Precise Verification, and Fully Parallel Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28678)
*Yunhai Hu,Zining Liu,Xiangyang Yin,Tianhua Xia,Bo Bao,Eric Sather,Vithursan Thangarasa,Sai Qian Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DREAM-R introduces Speculative Alignment Policy Optimization (SAPO) and a Threshold-based Verification Mechanism (TBVM) to enhance speculative reasoning in large multimodal models, plus Fully Parallel Speculative Reasoning (FPSR). It achieves speedups with preserved accuracy on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: address misalignment between speculative drafts and target-verified reasoning, and efficiency bottlenecks in speculative reasoning.

Method: SAPO RL objective to align drafts with target trajectories and conciseness; TBVM ratio-based acceptance criterion to prevent error propagation; FPSR parallelizes drafting, reasoning, verification across steps with early stopping and fallback.

Result: experimental evidence of speedups while maintaining target-model accuracy; substantial efficiency gains.

Conclusion: DREAM-R framework substantially improves speculative reasoning performance, enabling faster, reliable multi-step reasoning in large models by combining alignment, verification, and parallelization.

Abstract: Speculative reasoning has recently been proposed as a means to accelerate reasoning-intensive generation in large multimodal models, but its effectiveness is often constrained by misalignment between speculative drafts and target-verified reasoning. In this work, we introduce DREAM-R, a framework that substantially improves the performance of speculative reasoning. At its core, DREAM-R employs Speculative Alignment Policy Optimization (SAPO), a reinforcement-learning objective that trains draft models to generate reasoning steps that are both faithful to target trajectories and concise.
