### [309] [Bayesian Deployment Approval for Learned Landing Controllers under Finite Rollout Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27720)
*Fei Jiang,Lei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Bayesian approval framework for learned autonomous landing controllers to quantify deployment readiness under finite rollout evidence, using posterior approval probability and deployment risk and a sequential validation scheme, showing improved uncertainty calibration over empirical metrics (PPO/SAC experiments).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirical RL metrics (cumulative reward, success frequency) from finite rollouts may be overconfident about deployment readiness under uncertainty; a probabilistic, deployment-focused evaluation is needed.

Method: Introduce a probabilistic landing capability formulation based on touchdown safety under uncertain conditions; apply Bayesian posterior inference to quantify deployment capability; define posterior approval probability and posterior deployment risk; implement a sequential validation framework for approve/reject/continue during progressive rollout testing.

Result: Simulation experiments with PPO and SAC indicate that relying on empirical success/reward can yield overconfident deployment interpretations, whereas posterior approval inference yields uncertainty-calibrated deployment readiness assessments.

Conclusion: The framework links RL evaluation with deployment-oriented validation under uncertainty and can generalize to broader autonomous systems.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning and data-driven autonomous controllers are commonly evaluated using cumulative reward and empirical success frequency under finite simulation trajectories. However, such empirical metrics do not necessarily provide sufficient statistical evidence regarding deployment readiness under uncertainty. This work develops a Bayesian approval framework for learned autonomous landing controllers under finite rollout evidence. A probabilistic landing capability formulation is introduced based on touchdown safety satisfaction under uncertain operating conditions, while Bayesian posterior inference is used to quantify uncertainty regarding the true deployment capability of learned policies. Posterior approval probability and posterior deployment risk are further introduced for deployment-oriented evaluation, together with a sequential validation framework supporting approve/reject/continue decisions during progressive rollout testing. Simulation experiments using PPO and SAC controllers demonstrate that empirical success and reward optimization may produce overconfident deployment interpretation under limited validation evidence, whereas posterior approval inference provides a more uncertainty-calibrated assessment of deployment readiness. The proposed framework provides a practical statistical connection between conventional reinforcement-learning evaluation and deployment-oriented validation under uncertainty and may be generalized to broader classes of learned autonomous systems.

</details>


### [310] [NUCLEUS-MoE: Unified Model of Pool Boiling for Liquid Cooling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27722)
*Arthur Feeney,Xianwei Zou,Sheikh Md Shakeel Hassan,Siddhartha Rachabathuni,Aparna Chandramowlishwaran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NUCLEUS is a mixture-of-experts surrogate model for pool boiling that unifies saturated and subcooled boiling across multiple fluids using neighborhood attention, SDF-based interface consistency, and emergent expert routing. It matches or surpasses baselines, shows coherent specialization, and generalizes zero/few-shot to a new fluid, offering a scalable path toward universal boiling surrogates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Boiling involves strong coupling between phase change, turbulence, and transport and is highly sensitive to fluid properties and thermodynamic conditions, making accurate modeling difficult. Existing surrogates are often condition- or fluid-specific, limiting generalization.

Method: A mixture-of-experts model (NUCLEUS) that uses neighborhood attention, signed distance field reinitialization for interface consistency, and routing among experts that self-specialize across distinct boiling dynamics. Trained on high-fidelity pool boiling simulations to jointly model saturated and subcooled boiling across dielectric, refrigerant, and cryogen fluids.

Result: Expert routing exhibits coherent spatial structure and specialization without explicit supervision. NUCLEUS matches or exceeds baselines while maintaining physical consistency across heterogeneous configurations. Demonstrates zero-shot and few-shot generalization to a new fluid (Opteon 2P50) for immersion cooling.

Conclusion: Mixture-of-experts models provide a scalable, unified surrogate framework for boiling dynamics and enable broader generalization across scientific ML applications.

Abstract: Two-phase boiling enables heat transfer rates an order of magnitude higher than single-phase cooling, but it remains difficult to model due to the strong coupling between phase change, turbulence, and transport, as well as extreme sensitivity to fluid properties and thermodynamic conditions. Existing learning-based surrogates are either condition- or fluid-specific, limiting generalization and requiring separate models. We present NUCLEUS, a mixture-of-experts model for pool boiling that replaces collections of specialized surrogates with a single architecture. NUCLEUS combines neighborhood attention, signed distance field reinitialization for interface consistency, and expert routing that exhibits emergent specialization across distinct boiling dynamics.
  Trained on high-fidelity simulations of pool boiling, NUCLEUS jointly models saturated and subcooled boiling across three fluid classes (dielectrics, refrigerants, and cryogens), resolving failure modes of prior models on extreme fluids. We show that expert routing exhibits coherent spatial structure and specialization without explicit supervision. Quantitatively, NUCLEUS matches or exceeds baselines while maintaining physical consistency across heterogeneous boiling configurations. We also show zero-shot and few-shot generalization capabilities on downstream tasks such as a new fluid (Opteon 2P50 developed for immersion cooling). These results demonstrate that mixture-of-experts models are a scalable pathway toward unified surrogate modeling of boiling dynamics and lay the groundwork for broader generalization across scientific ML.

</details>


### [311] [Can Entry-Wise Clipping Give Spectral Control of Stochastic Gradients?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27733)
*Zitao Song,Cedar Site Bai,Zhe Zhang,Brian Bullins,David F. Gleich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method to control spectral behavior of stochastic gradient noise (SGN) in language-model pretraining by combining entry-wise heavy-tailed mitigation with spectral normalization, yielding practical training gains and provable convergence under Cauchy-contaminated noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training instabilities in language models stem from heavy-tailed stochastic gradient noise due to rare data and deep, multi-layer architectures. Vector-norm clipping fails to respect matrix structure, while spectral normalization incurs higher cost. A method that preserves structure at reasonable cost and provides spectral control is desirable.

Method: First-order perturbation analysis reveals localization of noise, enabling a simple entry-wise approach to spectral control. The authors derive a tractable surrogate for the Bayes-optimal entry-wise estimator under a Gaussian prior, and prove convergence O(ε^{-4}) under Cauchy-contaminated noise. They empirically evaluate smooth shrinkage on Adam for NanoGPT pretraining and show additional gains by combining entry-wise clipping with spectral normalization ( Muon ).

Result: The approach yields an O(ε^{-4}) convergence guarantee under Cauchy-contaminated noise. Empirically, smooth shrinkage improves Adam on NanoGPT pretraining, saving about 7% of training tokens; applying entry-wise clipping before spectral normalization provides an additional ~2% token saving on top of Muon.

Conclusion: Balancing structure and cost is feasible: an entry-wise, spectral-control method under a Gaussian prior can achieve practical training gains and provable convergence in heavy-tailed SGD settings, with additive improvements when combined with existing spectral normalization techniques.

Abstract: Training instabilities such as loss spikes are frequently the result of stochastic gradient noise. Because of rare expressions in language training data, and multiple layer composition, the noise impact is heavy-tailed and survives mini-batch averaging. Existing remedies trade off structure against cost: vector-norm clipping ignores the matrix structure of weight updates, while spectral normalization (e.g., Muon (Jordan et al., 2024)) respects it at additional cost. We show that this trade-off can be balanced. Real gradient noise appears to be similar to entry-wise heavy-tailed contamination, and a first-order perturbation analysis reveals a localization property of such noise, under which a simple entry-wise method achieves spectral control. Exploiting this, we derive a tractable surrogate for the Bayes-optimal entry-wise estimator under a Gaussian signal prior. We establish $O(ε^{-4})$ convergence guarantee under Cauchy-contaminated noise. Empirically, we find that smooth shrinkage improves Adam on NanoGPT pretraining, saving ${\sim}7\%$ of training tokens. We further find that applying the entry-wise clipping before spectral normalization yields a ${\sim}2\%$ token saving on top of Muon.

</details>


### [312] [Learn from your own latents and not from tokens: A sample-complexity theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27734)
*Daniel J. Korchinski,Alessandro Favero,Matthieu Wyart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent prediction in data2vec/JEPA achieves constant sample complexity in latent-depth L for a CFG-generated data, unlike token-level SSL which requires exponential samples; hierarchical stacking (H-JEPA) offers limited benefit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify data-efficiency benefits of latent prediction and hierarchical prediction, and compare to traditional token-level SSL, using a tractable generative model.

Method: Theoretically analyze a probabilistic context-free grammar that generates data from a hidden L-level latent tree; derive sample complexity results showing constant samples w.r.t. L for latent prediction (up to log factors); validate with (i) hierarchical clustering, (ii) end-to-end neural predictor-clusterer architecture trained by gradient descent to predict latents at each level, and (iii) a sample-complexity analysis of data2vec demonstrating implicit hierarchical latent prediction.

Result: Latent prediction achieves sample complexity that is constant in L (up to log factors), whereas supervised/token-level SSL requires exponential samples in L; hierarchical latent prediction is effectively captured by data2vec without explicit multi-scale stacking; the proposed NN setup confirms the theory.

Conclusion: Explicit multi-scale stacking (e.g., H-JEPA) provides little to no additional data-efficiency benefit on data generated from hierarchical latent structures; latent-prediction approaches suffice and align with observed performance.

Abstract: Generative models, from diffusion models to large language models, achieve remarkable performance but at a cost in training data orders of magnitude larger than what biological learners require. An alternative paradigm has emerged in which networks are trained to predict their \emph{own} latent representations of related views or masked regions, as in data2vec and JEPA -- an idea related to predictive-coding accounts of the cortex. Despite strong empirical results, the theoretical understanding of these methods remains limited. Central questions include: by how much does latent prediction actually improve data efficiency? Is there a benefit to stacking such methods into multi-scale hierarchies? We answer both using as data a tractable probabilistic context-free grammar that captures the compositional structure of natural language and images. Such a grammar generates strings of visible tokens by recursively applying production rules along a tree of hidden symbols of depth $L$. For such data, supervised or token-level SSL require a number of samples \emph{exponential} in $L$ to recover the latent tree; we prove that latent prediction achieves this with a number of samples \emph{constant} in $L$, up to logarithmic factors. We confirm this bound with (i) a hierarchical clustering algorithm, (ii) an end-to-end neural network whose predictor-clusterer modules predict their own latents at each level via gradient descent, and (iii) the first sample-complexity analysis of data2vec, which we show implicitly performs hierarchical latent prediction. This suggests that explicit stacking such as H-JEPA is largely redundant.

</details>


### [313] [Explicit Critic Guidance for Aligning Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27736)
*Zhengyang Liang,Qihang Zhang,Ceyuan Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a state-aligned latent actor-critic for diffusion post-training, enabling trajectory-level PPO and a reusable critic for inference, extended to multi-reward optimization, with gains across UNet/DiT backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address credit assignment along denoising trajectories and stabilize value-based optimization in online RL for aligning diffusion models with non-differentiable objectives; enable post-training steering.

Method: Propose a state-aligned latent actor-critic where the diffusion model acts as a timestep-conditioned value function and predicts values directly on noisy latent states. Enables trajectory-level PPO training, simple conditioning and value pretraining for stable actor-critic optimization, and reuses the learned critic at inference time. Extend to multi-reward optimization with joint training to mitigate reward hacking. Applicable to UNet- and DiT-based backbones.

Result: The approach consistently outperforms prior group-relative RL and actor-critic baselines on single-reward and multi-reward benchmarks, with test-time steering providing additional gains in generation quality, across both UNet- and DiT-based backbones.

Conclusion: A state-aligned latent actor-critic framework provides stable, high-quality RL-based diffusion alignment, supports reusable critics for inference, and scales to multi-reward objectives while improving generation quality through test-time steering.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning is becoming increasingly important for aligning diffusion models with non-differentiable objectives. However, existing methods still face limitations in assigning fine-grained credit along denoising trajectories and in realizing stable value-based optimization. We propose a state-aligned latent actor-critic framework for diffusion post-training, in which the diffusion model serves as its own timestep-conditioned value function and predicts values directly on noisy latent states. This enables trajectory-level PPO training, supports stable actor-critic optimization with simple conditioning and value pretraining strategies, and naturally allows the learned critic to be reused for inference-time steering. We further extend the framework to multi-reward optimization, where joint training with complementary rewards helps alleviate reward hacking. Across both UNet- and DiT-based backbones, our method consistently outperforms prior group-relative RL and actor-critic baselines on single-reward and multi-reward benchmarks, while test-time steering provides additional gains in generation quality.

</details>


### [314] [Worker Disagreement Reveals Sharp Directions in Local SGD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27739)
*Tolga Dimlioglu,Kristi Topollai,Anna Choromanska*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Local SGD reveals and estimates the dominant Hessian subspace via worker disagreement; a cheap Hessian-free proxy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DNN loss landscapes are highly anisotropic with a few sharp curvature directions; identifying the dominant subspace can aid optimization and understanding, but direct Hessian methods are costly; a cheap estimator is valuable.

Method: The paper analyzes Local SGD and shows the worker-average gap covariance is shaped by stochastic-gradient noise and Hessian curvature, causing disagreements along sharp directions. It posits that worker-average gaps form a subspace that approximates the dominant Hessian eigenspace and validates this as a Hessian-free estimator through experiments.

Result: The worker-average gap subspace captures a substantial fraction of the gradient component in the dominant Hessian eigenspace; experiments across MLPs, CNNs, and Transformers support the estimator's effectiveness.

Conclusion: Worker-average gaps provide a cheap, Hessian-free estimator of the dominant subspace, enabling insights into training dynamics and potential diagnostic or optimization applications.

Abstract: Deep neural network training often exhibits highly anisotropic loss geometry, where a few sharp dominant Hessian directions coexist with a large flatter bulk. Gradients tend to align disproportionately with these dominant directions, although stable progress often requires movement through flatter bulk directions. Estimating the dominant subspace is therefore useful but costly with direct Hessian-based methods. We show that standard Local SGD exposes this geometry through worker disagreement. We theoretically show that the worker-average gap covariance is shaped by stochastic-gradient noise and Hessian curvature, causing workers to disagree along sharp, curvature-sensitive directions. Thus, worker-average gaps provide a cheap Hessian-free estimator of the dominant subspace. Experiments on MLPs, CNNs, and Transformers show that subspaces formed by worker-average gaps capture a substantial fraction of the gradient component lying in the dominant Hessian eigenspace.

</details>


### [315] [High-Fidelity Industrial Crash Dynamics Prediction via Geometry-Aware Operator Learning with Memory-Efficient Low-Rank Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27758)
*Deepak Akhare,Mohammad Amin Nabian,Corey Adams,Sudeep Chavare,Sanjay Choudhry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces GeoTransolver, a geometry-aware operator-learning surrogate for high-fidelity automotive crash dynamics, with one-shot temporal prediction and FLARE-based memory-efficient attention; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and speed on bumper-beam and full-vehicle data, enabling industrial-scale crash analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crashworthiness analysis requires accurate prediction of large-scale nonlinear, transient deformations and energy dissipation, but full FEM is too slow for iterative industrial design; operator learning offers speed but must handle complex geometry, contact nonlinearities, and fast transients.

Method: Proposes GeoTransolver to capture multi-scale geometric context; evaluates temporal strategies (one-shot, time-conditional, autoregressive); introduces FLARE modification to the attention backbone to reduce memory ~2x and improve accuracy for long-range, high-frequency transients; benchmarks on bumper-beam and full-vehicle crash datasets.

Result: GeoTransolver delivers accurate predictions of plastic deformation and occupant-location accelerations; one-shot temporal prediction achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with reduced training overhead and inference latency; FLARE reduces memory usage and enhances performance for high-frequency transients; demonstrates practical viability of geometry-aware operator learning for complex crash dynamics.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware operator learning is a viable, high-fidelity surrogate modeling approach for industrial-scale automotive crash analysis, effectively handling geometry, contact nonlinearities, and transient dynamics while maintaining efficiency.

Abstract: Automotive crashworthiness optimization remains a safety-critical challenge, requiring the management of large-scale nonlinear structural deformations and energy dissipation through iterative, high-fidelity simulations. While traditional finite element solvers are computationally prohibitive, emerging operator learning frameworks provide rapid surrogate predictions; however, applying them to industrial-scale crash analysis, where complex geometry, contact nonlinearities, and rapidly evolving transient deformation coexist, remains an open challenge. In this paper, we demonstrate that the GeoTransolver framework provides a viable solution for accurate, high-fidelity crash dynamics prediction at industrial scale. Benchmarked on complex bumper beam and full-vehicle crash datasets, GeoTransolver captures multi-scale geometric context and accurately resolves plastic deformation patterns as well as acceleration profiles at critical occupant locations. Beyond the architecture itself, we propose and systematically evaluate a suite of temporal prediction recipes, including one-shot, time-conditional, and autoregressive rollout strategies, demonstrating that the one-shot approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly reduced training overhead and inference latency. As a secondary contribution, we introduce a Fast Low-rank Attention Routing Engine (FLARE)-based modification to the GeoTransolver attention backbone that reduces memory overhead by approximately 2x while further improving predictive accuracy for O(N) long-range, high-frequency transients, preserving the geometry-aware cross-attention strengths of the base framework. Our results highlight the practical viability of geometry-aware operator learning for high-fidelity surrogate modeling of complex, safety-critical automotive dynamics.

</details>


### [316] [A Paired Testing Protocol for Batch-Conditioned Refusal Robustness in LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27763)
*Sahil Kadadekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Batch-level serving configuration influences safety signals in language model evaluations; after artifact-backed adjudication, genuine behavioral flips are rare; invariant batching reduces flips; exact-stack, batch-aware validation is recommended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To treat batch condition as a testable treatment variable in LM safety evaluations and synthesize four artifact-backed studies into a paired testing protocol to quantify how safety vs. capability signals shift across batch settings.

Method: Study A: local discovery, scorer-corrected adjudication, true-batching confirmation; Study B: cross-model generalization; Study C: continuous-batch composition; Study D: batch-invariant-kernel ablation. Metrics include safety vs. capability label flips, adjudication precision, and stability indicators; analyses report flip rates, parity tests, effect sizes (η^2), correlations (r), bootstrap CIs.

Result: Local test: safety-label changes 0.51% vs capability-label changes 0.14%. Adjudication of 63 candidate rows yields 17 genuine behavioral flips, implying a corrected full-set rate of 0.16%. The 15-model extension finds no universal safety-over-capability skew: flips ~ parity (0.94x); alignment type has no detectable association (p=0.942, η^2=0.033); output instability is the strongest fragility screen (r=0.909, bootstrap 95% CI [0.65, 0.97]). Kernel ablation shows standard vLLM reproduces 22/55 flips; enabling VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1 reduces to 0/55 flips. Composition test finds no aggregate effect at 4.7pp sensitivity.

Conclusion: Recommendation: exact-stack validation — evaluate refusals at the served batch setting, pair safety prompts with capability controls, and report low-rate directional flips separately from aggregate null effects. The findings suggest batch-aligned evaluation can reveal subtle safety-capability dynamics with minimal net effect but meaningful fragility signals that can be mitigated via batch-invariant configurations.

Abstract: Safety evaluations of language models often treat serving configuration as fixed background infrastructure, but batch condition is an untested treatment variable whenever the same prompt may be evaluated alone, in a synchronized batch, or inside a continuous-batching scheduler. We synthesize four artifact-backed studies into a paired testing protocol: Study A combines local discovery, scorer-corrected adjudication, and true-batching confirmation; Study B tests cross-model generalization; Study C tests continuous-batch composition; and Study D runs a batch-invariant-kernel ablation. The local test finds safety-label changes more often than capability-label changes (0.51% vs. 0.14%), but adjudication of 63 candidate rows leaves only 17 genuine behavioral flips, implying a corrected full-set rate of 0.16%. The 15-model extension finds no detectable universal safety-over-capability skew: flips are near parity (0.94x), alignment type has no detectable association ($p=0.942$, $η^2=0.033$), and output instability is the strongest tested fragility screen ($r=0.909$, bootstrap 95% CI [0.65, 0.97]). In the targeted kernel ablation, standard vLLM reproduces 22/55 label flips on current score-flip candidates, while enabling VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1 reduces the same test to 0/55 flips; the composition test separately finds no aggregate effect at 4.7pp sensitivity. The testing recommendation is exact-stack validation: evaluate refusal at the served batch setting, pair safety prompts with capability controls, and report low-rate directional flips separately from aggregate null effects.

</details>


### [317] [Restoring the Sweet Spot: Pass-Rate Weighted Self-Distillation for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27765)
*Zehao Liu,Yuanpu Cao,Jinghui Chen,Vasant G. Honavar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SC-SDPO introduces scale-consistent weighting for self-distillation policy optimization by weighting each question’s loss with the square root of its predicted success probability times one minus itself, derived from normalized reward learnability. This yields an implicit curriculum and improved performance over SDPO on LLM RL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SDPO lacks a notion of problem difficulty due to KL-based advantage; GRPO’s normalization suggests that learnability suffers from variance scaling. The work seeks to restore difficulty-aware credit assignment in SDPO through normalization-inspired weighting.

Method: Extend the learnability framework to normalized rewards; show that normalization cancels the p(1-p) variance, leaving sqrt(p(1-p)) as the residual; propose SC-SDPO by weighting each question’s SDPO loss by [p_hat(1-p_hat)]^0.5; derive weights as a zero-cost byproduct from batch-adaptive normalization during on-policy rollouts, creating an implicit curriculum; validate on scientific reasoning and tool-use benchmarks.

Result: SC-SDPO consistently improves SDPO, with gains of +3.2/+4.3 (mean@16/maj@16) on Qwen3-8B and +1.8/+3.0 on OLMo-3-7B, while maintaining stable training dynamics.

Conclusion: Scale-consistent credit assignment via normalization-induced weighting yields improved SDPO performance and stable optimization; introduces an implicit curriculum that tracks model competence during on-policy training.

Abstract: Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) provides dense token-level credit assignment for reinforcement learning with large language models by leveraging the model's own feedback-conditioned predictions as a self-teacher. Unlike GRPO, however, whose group-relative advantage naturally concentrates learning on a sweet spot of intermediate-difficulty questions, SDPO's KL-based advantage lacks an implicit notion of difficulty awareness.
  We analyze this gap through the lens of GRPO's advantage normalization. Extending the learnability framework to normalized rewards, we show that normalization absorbs the variance term $p(1-p)$, equalizing leading-order learnability across questions and leaving $\sqrt{p(1-p)}$ as the sole residual scaling factor in the per-question gradient. This analysis yields a simple prescription: weight each question's SDPO loss by $[\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})]^{1/2}$, resulting in SC-SDPO, a scale-consistent variant of SDPO.
  The proposed weights are obtained as a zero-cost byproduct of on-policy rollouts with batch-adaptive normalization, inducing an implicit curriculum that dynamically tracks the model's evolving competence. Experiments on scientific reasoning and tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that SC-SDPO consistently improves over SDPO, yielding gains of +3.2/+4.3 (mean@16/maj@16) on Qwen3-8B and +1.8/+3.0 on OLMo-3-7B, while preserving stable training dynamics throughout optimization.

</details>


### [318] [Fine-Tuning Dynamics of In-Context Factual Recall in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27774)
*Ruomin Huang,Eshaan Nichani,Jason D. Lee,Rong Ge*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IC-recall formalizes in-context learning as retrieval from an internal associative memory: a transformer learns a pairwise attention pattern to infer hidden relations and recall answers, with polylogarithmic sample complexity in the number of stored triplets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To connect in-context learning with explicit factual knowledge recall, revealing how prompts guide retrieval from model parameters and enabling efficient, memory-augmented reasoning.

Method: Introduce IC-recall: given (subject, answer) pairs from a hidden relation and a query subject, infer the hidden relation and retrieve the answer. Model uses a simple MLP associative memory storing (subject, relation, answer) triplets. Analyze supervised fine-tuning dynamics of a one-layer transformer on IC-recall data, proving convergence to a particular pairwise attention pattern. Show polylogarithmic sample complexity in the number of stored triplets. Experiments confirm the predicted attention pattern, even when the MLP layer is pretrained rather than constructed.

Result: The transformer succeeds at IC-recall by converging to a defined pairwise attention pattern. Fine-tuning requires only polylogarithmic samples in the number of stored triplets. Experiments verify the theoretical predictions and show the pairwise attention pattern emerges even if the MLP memory is pretrained.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that in-context learning can leverage stored factual knowledge via a memory-guided, attention-based retrieval mechanism, achieves strong sample efficiency, and provides a theoretical characterization (pairwise attention pattern) of the retrieval process in a simple transformer setup.

Abstract: In-context learning \ -- performing tasks based on examples given in the prompt \ -- is an important capability that has emerged in large language models and has received significant attention in both theory and practice. Existing theoretical work often focuses on settings where the learning uses information purely from the prompt. However, many practical instances of in-context learning require the model to retrieve factual knowledge stored in the model's parameters, with the context serving to identify which knowledge is relevant. In this work, we study how in-context learning leverages factual knowledge recall. We formalize this behavior by introducing the \emph{in-context factual recall (IC-recall)} task, where a transformer is provided a context of (subject, answer) pairs generated from a hidden relation, along with a query subject, and must both infer this hidden relation and retrieve the corresponding answer. Factual knowledge is modeled by the transformer having access to a simple pre-constructed MLP associative memory storing (subject, relation, answer) triplets. We analyze the supervised fine-tuning dynamics of a one-layer transformer on IC-recall data and prove that the model successfully performs IC-recall by converging to a particular pairwise attention pattern. This fine-tuning stage requires a very small number of samples \ -- only polylogarithmic in the number of stored knowledge triplets. Experiments verify our theoretical predictions and show that the pairwise attention pattern emerges even when the MLP layer is pretrained instead of constructed.

</details>


### [319] [Revisiting ML Training under Fully Homomorphic Encryption: Convergence Guarantees, Differential Privacy, and Efficient Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27782)
*Yvonne Zhou,Mingyu Liang,Ivan Brugere,Danial Dervovic,Yue Guo,Antigoni Polychroniadou,Min Wu,Dana Dachman-Soled*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First convergence analysis for machine learning under fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) combined with a tailored differential privacy (DP) training algorithm; achieves efficiency gains over DP-GD with comparable utility, via polynomial-approximate gradients and data-independent hyperparameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable private, secure ML on sensitive data by enabling encrypted computation (FHE) with DP guarantees, addressing computational bottlenecks and privacy concerns inherent in DP training on encrypted data.

Method: Prove convergence of approximate gradient descent using polynomial approximations of activation and loss functions to fit FHE; design DP training that avoids costly per-sample gradient clipping; provide data-independent hyperparameter selection and theoretically grounded strategies for polynomial approximation.

Result: Convergence guarantee for encrypted, DP-inclusive training; improved computational efficiency relative to standard DP-GD while achieving comparable utility; scalable encrypted learning; broad strategies for polynomial approximation.

Conclusion: Advances feasibility of efficient, private, and secure ML on sensitive data by marrying FHE with DP in a theoretically grounded framework, offering practical guidance for hyperparameter choice and polynomial approximation; potential impact on privacy-preserving ML is significant, with remaining challenges related to approximation error and computational resource requirements.

Abstract: We present the first theoretical convergence analysis of machine learning training under fully homomorphic encryption (FHE), combined with a differentially private (DP) training algorithm tailored to encrypted computation. Our approach improves computational efficiency over standard differentially private gradient descent (DP-GD) while achieving comparable utility. In particular, we prove convergence of approximate gradient descent using polynomial approximations of activation and loss functions, which are required for FHE compatibility. To preserve privacy in downstream tasks, we integrate differential privacy without relying on costly per-sample gradient clipping, enabling scalable encrypted learning. We also provide data-independent hyperparameter selection and theoretically grounded strategies for polynomial approximation which can be of independent interest. Together, these contributions advance the feasibility of efficient, private, and secure machine learning on sensitive data.

</details>


### [320] [Locality-Aware Redundancy Pruning for LLM Depth Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27786)
*Vincent-Daniel Yun,Youngrae Kim,Woosang Lim,YoungJin Heo,Minkyu Kim,Sunwoo Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRP is a training-free, one-shot depth pruning method guided by representation locality, using a Representation Locality Score (RLS) to determine inter-layer redundancy and cluster-based pruning; validated across diverse LLMs with improved perplexity and downstream accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit representational redundancy across depth, enabling depth pruning for efficiency. Existing one-shot pruning relies on fixed or local redundancy assumptions and may not capture architecture-dependent locality patterns.

Method: Introduce Locality-Aware Redundancy Pruning (LoRP). Use a small calibration set to compute pairwise inter-layer hidden-state similarities, derive Representation Locality Score (RLS), cluster layers by similarity, and allocate pruning based on residual intra-cluster redundancy. It is training-free and one-shot.

Result: Experiments across diverse LLM families show improvements in perplexity and downstream task accuracy, demonstrating that pruning guided by representation locality can enhance efficiency without retraining.

Conclusion: LoRP provides a framework to quantify and exploit inter-layer redundancy locality in LLMs. By measuring RLS and clustering layers, it enables effective depth pruning without training, adapting to whether redundancy is localized or global across architectures.

Abstract: Large language models are known to contain representational redundancy across network depth, making depth pruning an effective approach for improving inference efficiency. Existing one-shot pruning methods rely on local layer importance or fixed redundancy assumptions across architectures. We propose Locality-Aware Redundancy Pruning (LoRP), a training-free one-shot depth pruning framework guided by representation locality. We show that inter-layer redundancy can be either localized or globally distributed depending on the LLM architecture. To characterize this phenomenon, we introduce Representation Locality Score (RLS), derived from global inter-layer hidden-state similarity. Using a small calibration set, LoRP computes pairwise layer similarity, clusters layers by representational similarity, and allocates pruning according to residual intra-cluster redundancy. Experiments across diverse LLM families show improvements in both perplexity and downstream task accuracy.

</details>


### [321] [Knowing When to Ask: Segment-Level Credit Assignment for LLM Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27788)
*Abhijit Kumar,Zoey Wu,Mohit Suley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CARL trains a competence-aware critic to credit tool-use decisions by breaking rollouts at natural boundaries and evaluating each segment with a binary outcome, achieving strong separation of parameteric vs tool-dependent questions and improving EM on arithmetic, multi-hop QA, and financial-table reasoning, especially at smaller model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans know when to seek help; current prompting and RL approaches fail to teach models the boundary of their own knowledge due to poor credit assignment and lack of fine-grained signals for tool-use decisions.

Method: Decompose each rollout at natural tool-use boundaries (e.g., code fences, context transitions) and train a critic on the model's own rollouts to assign independent credit to each segment using a single binary outcome. This yields segment-level advantages without external judges or step-level annotations and results in a parametric vs tool-dependent knowledge partition.

Result: CARL achieves AUC 0.93 at 7B for distinguishing solvable-from-tool-dependent questions. Across five benchmarks (arithmetic, multi-hop factual QA, and numerical reasoning over financial tables), it improves exact-match (EM) by 6.7 points at 7B and 9.7 points at 3B versus the best RL baseline; Musique shows the largest gains (+8.3 EM at 7B, +9.0 EM at 3B). The model uses 53% fewer tool calls on parametrically answerable questions while maintaining ~10 EM points higher accuracy on those questions.

Conclusion: Gains are largest at smaller scales, indicating that competence-aware tool-use signaling yields disproportionate benefits for models with limited parametric memory by accurately separating solvable from tool-dependent queries and reducing unnecessary tool invocations.

Abstract: Humans know when to reach for help e.g. $347 \times 28$ warrants a calculator while $2+2$ does not. Language models do not. Prompt-based approaches can instruct a model when to invoke tools, but this scaffolding does not teach it to recognize the boundary of its own knowledge. RL approaches that assign a single outcome reward to the whole trajectory fare no better: trajectory-level credit cannot isolate which tool call in a successful episode actually helped, nor penalize unnecessary calls. We propose \textbf{CARL} (\textbf{C}ompetence-\textbf{A}ware \textbf{R}einforcement \textbf{L}earning), which trains a critic on the model's own rollouts to learn where parametric knowledge suffices and where it needs external help. By decomposing each rollout at natural tool-use boundaries (e.g., code fence delimiters and context block transitions), CARL assigns independent credit to each segment from a single binary outcome, without external judges or step-level annotations. As a result, erroneous tool calls, incorrect extractions, and unnecessary calls each receive appropriately signed advantages. The trained critic captures the model's domain competence: it separates parametrically solvable from tool-dependent questions with AUC 0.93 at 7B. On five benchmarks spanning arithmetic, multi-hop factual QA, and numerical reasoning over financial tables, CARL improves exact-match accuracy by 6.7 points at 7B and 9.7 points at 3B over the best RL baseline, with the largest gain (+8.3 EM at 7B, +9.0 EM at 3B) on Musique. The model issues 53\% fewer tool calls on parametrically answerable questions while remaining ${\sim}10$ EM points more accurate on them. Gains are largest at small scale: the 3B improvement is $1.4\times$ the 7B improvement, suggesting that knowing when to ask disproportionately benefits models with smaller parametric memory.

</details>


### [322] [SYNAPSE: Neuro-Symbolic Visual Thought-to-Text Decoding via Topological Semantic Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27790)
*Akshaj Murhekar,Abhijit Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight neuro-symbolic framework, SYNAPSE, stabilizes EEG-to-imagined-text generation at inference by symbolic regularization, yielding semantic stability and competitive performance without fine-tuning and with local privacy-preserving processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frozen LLM-based EEG-to-text systems are vulnerable to biological noise causing hallucinations or unstable semantics; need robust performance without end-to-end fine-tuning and with privacy preservation.

Method: SYNAPSE uses inference-time symbolic regularization by purifying EEG-derived semantic candidates using a commonsense graph structure and latent exemplars; processing remains in the encoder stack to keep raw EEG local.

Result: Across EEG benchmarks and multiple frozen LLM backends, shows gains over unconstrained prompting, robustness under object-label ablation, and performance comparable to fine-tuned systems with far greater resources.

Conclusion: SYNAPSE demonstrates that neuro-symbolic regularization at inference can stabilize neural-to-text decoding while preserving privacy, offering a practical alternative to full fine-tuning.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models have accelerated open-vocabulary EEG-to-imagined-text decoding, where non-invasive neural activity recorded during visual perception is translated into coherent natural language descriptions of viewed stimuli. However, existing systems remain highly vulnerable to biological noise, where corrupted neural projections induce hallucinated or semantically unstable generation in frozen language models. We introduce SYNAPSE (Symbolic Neural Alignment for Precise Semantic Extraction), a lightweight neuro-symbolic framework that stabilizes neural text generation through inference-time symbolic regularization. By purifying EEG-derived semantic candidates using commonsense graph structure and latent exemplars, SYNAPSE improves semantic stability without end-to-end LLM fine-tuning. Experiments across popular EEG decoding benchmarks and multiple frozen LLM backends demonstrate consistent gains over unconstrained prompting baselines, robustness under object-label ablation, and performance commensurate with substantially more resource-intensive fine-tuned systems, while preserving biometric privacy by localizing raw EEG processing entirely within the encoder stack.

</details>


### [323] [Density-aware Sample-specific Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27809)
*Qiyuan Wang,Yao Li,Raymond K. W. Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that optimal triggers for backdoor attacks lie in low-density regions of the clean data distribution, and introduces a bilevel framework using conditional time-score matching to place triggers there, yielding near-perfect pre-defense ASR and strong post-defense immunity, exposing a gap in current defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reexamine backdoor objectives under a Bayes-optimal training model, and derive principled, distribution-aware criteria for sample-specific trigger construction that preserve both attack success and clean accuracy while resisting post-training defenses.

Method: A bilevel optimization framework that estimates density ratios via conditional time-score matching and optimizes a mixture-model objective to steer triggered samples into sparse, low-density regions of the clean distribution; evaluations across MNIST, CIFAR-10, GTSRB, and TinyImageNet.

Result: Achieves >99% attack success rate (ASR) before defenses; under fine-tuning defenses, post-defense ASR is 50–85 percentage points higher than the strongest baselines; under neuron-pruning defenses, shows complete immunity with zero neurons identified for removal across thresholds.

Conclusion: Highlights a fundamental gap in current defense paradigms by showing that defenses focusing on the clean-data support can be bypassed; recommends defenses that operate beyond the support of the clean distribution.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in backdoor attacks, existing methods remain susceptible to post-training defenses that erase the backdoor through fine-tuning or pruning. We revisit the core objectives of backdoor attacks and derive principled criteria characterizing optimal sample-specific trigger construction under a Bayes-optimal model of the victim's training. Our analysis reveals that both attack success and clean-accuracy preservation are simultaneously optimized when triggered samples are steered into low-density regions of the clean data distribution, a distributional condition that controls all moments of the poisoned distribution at once rather than a handful of input-space summary statistics. We introduce a bilevel optimization framework that estimates density ratios via conditional time-score matching and optimizes a mixture-model objective to place triggered samples in these sparse regions. Extensive evaluations on MNIST, CIFAR-10, GTSRB, and TinyImageNet demonstrate that our method achieves above 99\% attack success rate before defense and retains 50--85 percentage points higher post-defense ASR than the strongest baselines under fine-tuning defenses. Against neuron-pruning defenses, the method exhibits complete immunity, with zero neurons identified for removal across all pruning thresholds. These results expose a fundamental gap in current defense paradigms and underscore the need for defenses that operate beyond the support of the clean distribution.

</details>


### [324] [ReSAE: Residualized Sparse Autoencoders for Multi-Layer Transformer Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27819)
*Prathyush Poduval,Calvin Yeung,Neel Desai,Mohsen Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Residualized Sparse Autoencoders (ReSAEs) fit an affine map between selected transformer layers, train later-layer SAEs on residuals, and reconstruct via the affine chain. This reduces decoder redundancy and improves interpretability and intervention outcomes across multi-layer perturbations, especially under teacher-forcing and sparsity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In deep transformers, cross-layer activations are strongly coupled; traditional layerwise SAEs may redundantly represent the same carried-forward information, complicating multi-layer interventions. A method to isolate the unexplained residuals could improve efficiency and interpretability.

Method: Fit an affine (linear) relation between chosen layers. Train each subsequent SAE on the residual (activation minus affine-predicted previous layer). Reconstruct activations by chaining the affine maps to map residuals back to the original activation space. Evaluate with standard SAE interventions on Pythia-1.4B and Gemma-2-9B. Compare to ordinary SAEs in terms of decoder redundancy, sparse probing, targeted perturbations, and cross-entropy under multi-layer replacement.

Result: Residualization reduces decoder redundancy and improves sparse probing and targeted perturbations in most settings. Despite reconstructing less of raw variance, ReSAEs yield higher cross-entropy recovery under multi-layer replacement, especially with teacher-forcing and higher sparsity.

Conclusion: Removing linearly predictable cross-layer structure is a useful default for multi-layer SAE interventions; ReSAEs better capture activation components relevant to downstream computation and can enable more reliable multi-layer interventions.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders are usually trained one layer at a time, even though transformer residual stream activations are strongly coupled across depth. This creates a practical problem for multi-layer interventions: different layerwise dictionaries can spend capacity representing the same carried-forward information, and replacing several layers at once can produce interactions that are not predicted by single-layer behavior. We introduce Residualized Sparse Autoencoders (ReSAEs), which fit an affine map between selected layers and train each later-layer SAE on the unexplained residual rather than on the full activation. Reconstructions are mapped back into the original activation space through the fitted affine chain, so ReSAEs can be evaluated with the same intervention protocols as ordinary SAEs. On Pythia-1.4B and Gemma-2-9B, residualization reduces decoder redundancy and improves sparse probing and targeted perturbation in most tested settings. Despite reconstructing less of the raw activation variance, ReSAEs recover more transformer cross entropy under multi-layer replacement. This gain is clearest under teacher-forcing and at sufficient sparsity online, indicating that ReSAEs preserve the components of the activation most relevant to the model's downstream computation. These results suggest that removing linearly predictable cross-layer structure is a useful default for multi-layer SAE interventions.

</details>


### [325] [Decentralized Parameter-Free Online Learning with Compressed Gossip](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27831)
*Tomas Ortega,Hamid Jafarkhani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A parameter-free decentralized online learning algorithm (DECO-EF) using coin-betting with error feedback and compressed gossip to achieve sublinear network regret under compressed communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle decentralized online convex optimization with communication constraints, achieving parameter-free guarantees without horizon or comparator scale tuning.

Method: DECO-EF merges coin-betting predictions with compressed difference-based gossip. Each agent maintains a clean accumulated state and a compressed tracker, and exchanges only compressed state differences during gossip steps; the approach is parameter-free w.r.t horizon, comparator norm, and learning rate.

Result: Proves expected comparator-adaptive network-regret bounds; claims to be the first known expected sublinear network-regret guarantee for parameter-free decentralized online learning under compressed communication.

Conclusion: Advances parameter-free decentralized online learning theory under communication constraints; suggests potential for practical deployment and future extensions to broader settings and compressions.

Abstract: We study decentralized online convex optimization when agents communicate over a graph and messages may be compressed. Classical decentralized online methods typically require learning-rate choices that depend on the horizon, comparator scale, or other problem parameters, while compressed communication introduces additional disagreement that must be controlled. We propose DECO-EF (DEcentralized COin-betting with Error Feedback), a decentralized parameter-free online learning algorithm that combines coin-betting predictions with compressed difference-based gossip. Each agent maintains a clean accumulated state and a compressed tracker, and communicates only compressed state differences during gossip steps. The method is parameter-free in the online-learning sense: it does not tune to the horizon, the comparator norm, or the learning rate. We prove expected comparator-adaptive network-regret bounds for DECO-EF under compressed communication. To the best of our knowledge, this gives the first expected sublinear network-regret guarantees for parameter-free decentralized online learning under compressed communication.

</details>


### [326] [Reward Transfer from Inverse Reinforcement Learning: A Coupled Minimax Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27834)
*Guang-Yuan Hao,Lars van der Laan,Aurélien Bibaut,Nathan Kallus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transfers rewards learned via inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) across environments by solving coupled Bellman equations for source and target soft-Q functions; shows that a coupled approach mitigates source residual error and provides finite-sample error bounds and regret guarantees, validated in a sepsis simulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reusing demonstrations collected in a controlled environment to drive reinforcement learning in a different, possibly mismatched environment; reduce data collection burden and improve sample efficiency in real-world tasks.

Method: Formulate a joint system of Bellman equations across source and target environments; derive minimax estimators for the target soft-Q function; compare sequential (estimate source reward then control target) vs coupled (jointly solve both) strategies; analyze local behavior, derive finite-sample error bounds for soft-Q and regret guarantees; validate with experiments on a sepsis simulator.

Result: The coupled approach removes the first-order influence of source Bellman residual error, yielding better statistical stability and stronger regret guarantees; the sequential approach suffers from propagating source errors; finite-sample bounds and empirical validation support the theoretical claims.

Conclusion: Coupled IRL-to-RL transfer is more robust to source residuals and offers provable performance guarantees; the framework provides a principled method for cross-environment reward transfer and could extend to broader transfer learning scenarios in RL with IRL.

Abstract: We study the transfer of rewards learned using inverse reinforcement learning from expert demonstrations in one environment to reinforcement learning in a new, different environment. This arises naturally when demonstrations are collected in a controlled environment. We formulate the problem as a joint system of Bellman equations across the source and target environments and develop minimax estimators for the target soft-$q$-function. Whereas a sequential solution approach first estimates the source reward and then plugs it into the target control problem, a coupled approach solves the source and target system of equations jointly. We show that, in contrast to the sequential approach, the coupled approach removes the first-order influence of source Bellman residual error. We characterize the local behavior of each approach, develop finite-sample soft-$q$-function error bounds, and prove regret guarantees for the resulting soft-control policy. An empirical investigation using a sepsis simulator validates the theoretical comparison.

</details>


### [327] [CAREF: Calibration-Aware Regularization for Explanation Faithfulness Without Rationale Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27835)
*Naphat Nithisopa,Teerapong Panboonyuen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAREF is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that unifies entropy-based calibration with token-level sparsity regularization to improve accuracy and explanation faithfulness without rationale supervision; CAREF-AQ achieves state-of-the-art average accuracy and explanation alignment on four NLE benchmarks with 6.43% trainable parameters, outperforming LoRA and AdaLoRA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve predictive accuracy and explanation faithfulness in interpretable LLM fine-tuning while keeping parameter efficiency; address the need for a unified objective that combines calibration and sparsity without requiring rationale supervision.

Method: Joint optimization via a single Calibration-Aware Regularization for Explanation Faithfulness (LSCED) loss that couples entropy-based calibration with token-level sparsity control within the CAREF framework; no rationale supervision; includes a lightweight CAREF-AQ variant; evaluated on Flan-T5 across COS-E, ECQA, ComVE, e-SNLI.

Result: CAREF-AQ achieves the best average accuracy (89.04) and explanation alignment (81.00 nBERT) on the four NLE benchmarks using only 6.43% of trainable parameters, outperforming LoRA and AdaLoRA.

Conclusion: CAREF is the first method to unify entropy and sparsity regularization in a single training objective for interpretable LLM fine-tuning, demonstrating effective parameter-efficient improvements in both predictive performance and explanation faithfulness.

Abstract: We introduce CAREF, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that jointly optimizes predictive accuracy and explanation faithfulness via calibration-aware regularization. At its core, CAREF couples entropy-based calibration with token-level sparsity control through a single unified loss, the Calibration-Aware Regularization for Explanation Faithfulness (LSCED), without requiring rationale supervision. Evaluated on four NLE benchmarks (COS-E, ECQA, ComVE, e-SNLI) with Flan-T5, our lightweight CAREF-AQ variant attains the best average accuracy (89.04) and explanation alignment (81.00 nBERT) using only 6.43% of trainable parameters, outperforming LoRA and AdaLoRA. To our knowledge, CAREF is the first method to unify entropy and sparsity regularization in a single training objective for interpretable LLM fine-tuning.

</details>


### [328] [From Detection to Mechanism: Cross-Attention Graph Neural Networks Enable Drug-Drug Interaction Type Prediction An Ablation Study with Acetylsalicylic Acid Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27861)
*Juergen Dietrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-attentive dual MPNN (CrossAtt) improves mechanism-type DDI prediction over a concatenation baseline, with modest gains in binary detection; a ternary MPNN with an interaction graph underperforms due to training instability; held-out ASA validation shows CrossAtt achieves perfect 10/10, while Ternary fails; two consistent failure cases align with structural limits from a companion toxicity study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissect how different GNN architectures handle drug–drug interaction (DDI) prediction by separating binary interaction detection from mechanism-type classification and to assess the role of inter-molecular communication in enabling mechanism-type inference.

Method: Systematic ablation of three GNN architectures on a public benchmark: 38,337 positive DDI pairs across 86 interaction types; training on 61,339 pairs. Architectures compared under identical conditions: a siamese dual MPNN with concatenation (Concat), a dual MPNN with four-head cross-attention (CrossAtt), and a ternary MPNN incorporating an interaction graph (Ternary). Evaluation of multi-class F1-macro and binary AUC; held-out validation on 10 ASA drug pairs.

Result: CrossAtt improves multi-class F1-macro by +0.186 absolute (+45%) over Concat, while improving binary AUC by only +0.012 (+1.3%). The Ternary architecture underperforms despite equal data, consistent with a training-instability hypothesis. In held-out ASA validation (10/10 correct DDI-type for CrossAtt vs 0/10 for Ternary), there are two consistent failure cases across architectures, linked to structural limits from a companion toxicity study.

Conclusion: Atom-level inter-molecular communication via cross-attention enhances mechanism-type DDI classification; the ternary graph approach is unstable under these conditions; observed failure modes point to structural constraints relevant to toxicity-related limits and suggest that improving cross-modal communication is critical for mechanism-type inference.

Abstract: Predicting whether two drugs interact (binary detection) is a substantially dif- ferent task from predicting the mechanism type of that interaction (multi-class classification). This study presents a systematic ablation study of three Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures for drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction on a publicly available benchmark dataset comprising 38,337 positive pairs across 86 interaction types. Three architectures are compared under identical training conditions (n = 61,339 pairs): a siamese dual Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) with concatenation (Concat), a dual MPNN with four-head cross-attention (CrossAtt), and a ternary MPNN incorporating an interaction graph (Ternary). CrossAtt improves multi-class F1-macro by +0.186 absolute (+45%) over Concat, while improving binary AUC by only +0.012 (+1.3%) - confirming that atom-level inter-molecular communication specifically enables mechanism-type classification. The ternary architecture underperforms despite equivalent training data, with its failure consistent with a training instability hypothesis. Validation on ten acetylsali- cylic acid (ASA) drug pairs, held out prior to training, demonstrates 10/10 correct DDI-type predictions for CrossAtt versus 0/10 for Ternary. Two consistent failure cases are identified across all architectures, linking to structural limits established in a companion toxicity study.

</details>


### [329] [SPAR: Support-Preserving Action Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27877)
*Jiaxin Zhao,Weihang Pan,Xun Liang,Binbin Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPAR (Support-Preserving Action Rectification) reframes offline policy improvement as local residual rectification anchored to a frozen pure behavior cloning policy, augmented by Latent Self-Imitation. Theoretical analysis claims gradient and manifold drift issues are resolved, and empirical results on D4RL show state-of-the-art performance against suboptimal baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline policy optimization must both maximize value and stay close to the data distribution. Traditional in-sample weighted regression is stable but overly conservative, suppressing high-value actions in the tail; gradient-based methods optimize value but risk drifting off the data manifold due to conflicting gradient signals. A method that constrains learning to a residual space around a safe baseline can reconcile these tensions.

Method: Introduce SPAR to perform global learning as local residual rectification tied to a frozen pure behavior cloning policy, enabling fine-grained, residual-space fitting and policy improvement with a contracted search space. Add Latent Self-Imitation using latent-sampling weighted regression to address fitting-improvement gradient conflict in the residual space. Theoretically show this mechanism eliminates manifold-normal drift of standard value gradients.

Result: Theoretical results show the latent-sampling weighted regression removes manifold drift of value gradients. Empirically, SPAR yields significant gains over suboptimal baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance on D4RL benchmarks.

Conclusion: SPAR provides a principled framework for offline policy improvement by confining learning to a residual space around a frozen BC policy and using latent-self imitation to resolve gradient conflicts, leading to stable training and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Offline policy improvement faces an inherent conflict between maximizing value and fitting the data distribution. While in-sample weighted regression is stable, it suffers from over-conservatism that suppresses high-value actions in the distribution tail; conversely, gradient-based approaches often exhibit a fitting-optimization conflict of gradients, which drives the policy off the data manifold. To address this, we propose Support-Preserving Action Rectification (SPAR), which reframes global learning as a local residual rectification anchored to a frozen pure behavior cloning policy. This framework performs fine-grained fitting and local policy improvement in the residual space, thereby contracting the search space. We further introduce Latent Self-Imitation, utilizing a latent-sampling weighted-regression mechanism to address fitting-improvement gradient conflict in the residual space. Theoretically, we prove this mechanism eliminates the manifold-normal drift of standard value gradients, while extensive D4RL experiments show SPAR extracts significant gains from suboptimal baselines to achieve state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [330] [FedEHR-Gen: Federated Synthetic Time-Series EHR Generation via Latent Space Alignment and Distribution-Aware Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27892)
*Jun Bai,Ziyang Song,Yue Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedEHR-Gen: a federated two-stage framework for synthetic time-series EHR generation across distributed hospitals, achieving centralized-like fidelity via latent-space alignment and distribution-aware aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy constraints and data-sharing limits in healthcare; enable cross-hospital synthetic data generation without pooling sensitive EHR data; tackle high dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-hospital heterogeneity that destabilizes federated training.

Method: Stage 1: federated autoencoder with layer-wise matching aggregation to map high-dimensional sparse EHR features to a unified latent space. Stage 2: federated temporal conditional variational autoencoder operating on the aligned latent space with distribution-aware aggregation for stable temporal generation across heterogeneous data.

Result: On eICU and MIMIC-III, FedEHR-Gen achieves generation fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy risk comparable to centralized training and outperforms standard federated baselines.

Conclusion: First federated framework for synthetic time-series EHR across distributed hospitals; effective latent-space alignment and distribution-aware federated VAE enable privacy-preserving, high-quality synthetic data under severe cross-hospital heterogeneity.

Abstract: Synthetic Electronic Health Record (EHR) generation provides a promising avenue for data augmentation and cross-hospital modeling in privacy-constrained healthcare settings. However, most existing EHR generative models are centralized and require pooling data across hospitals, which is often infeasible when real-world data sharing is restricted. While federated EHR generation offers a natural solution, direct federated modeling often collapses or diverges due to the high dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-hospital heterogeneity of EHR data. In this work, we propose FedEHR-Gen, the first federated framework for synthetic time-series EHR generation across distributed hospitals. FedEHR-Gen uses a two-stage learning paradigm. First, we introduce a federated autoencoder that projects high-dimensional and sparse EHR features onto a compact latent space. To ensure semantic consistency across hospitals, we develop a layer-wise matching aggregation mechanism that aligns local encoders into a unified global latent space. Second, operating on this aligned latent space, we train a federated temporal conditional variational autoencoder (TCVAE) with distribution-aware aggregation, enabling stable temporal generative modeling under severe cross-hospital heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on the eICU and MIMIC-III datasets demonstrate that FedEHR-Gen achieves generation fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy risk comparable to centralized training, while consistently outperforming the standard federated baseline.

</details>


### [331] [Where LLM Annotators Fail: Label-Free Learning on Graphs with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27913)
*Safal Thapaliya,Jiatan Huang,Chuxu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes CANE to estimate cluster-conditional LLM label reliability without ground truth and use it to select/correct pseudo-labels for label-free graph learning; shows improvements on benchmarks, especially when noise is cluster-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-generated labels for graph node attributes are noisy. Traditional noise models assume global or class-conditional noise, but real noise varies across feature-space clusters; a cluster-aware approach could improve label-free semi-supervised GNNs.

Method: Cluster-Aware Noise Estimation (CANE): estimate cluster-conditional reliability of LLM labels without ground-truth labels, and use these estimates to decide which pseudo-labels to trust and which to correct, integrating into label-free graph learning with GNN backbones.

Result: CANE improves over the strongest label-free baselines, with the largest gains on datasets exhibiting stronger cluster-conditional noise.

Conclusion: Cluster-aware handling of region-dependent label noise is effective for label-free graph learning; CANE provides robust gains across datasets and GNN architectures.

Abstract: Node classification on graphs often requires labeled nodes, yet obtaining labels at graph scale is expensive. When node attributes contain semantic content, such as paper abstracts, web pages, or product descriptions, large language models (LLMs) can provide low-cost supervision by annotating a small subset of nodes. However, these LLM-generated labels are noisy, and existing label-free graph learning methods usually treat this noise as either global or class-conditional. We find that LLM annotation errors are not only class-dependent but also region-dependent: within the same class, reliability can vary sharply across feature-space clusters. In light of this, we propose Cluster-Aware Noise Estimation (CANE), a label-free learning framework that estimates cluster-conditional LLM reliability without ground truth labels, and uses this estimate to decide which pseudo-labels to trust, and which labels to correct. Across various graph benchmarks and GNN backbones, CANE improves over the strongest label-free baselines, with the largest gains on datasets exhibiting stronger cluster-conditional noise.

</details>


### [332] [Cyclical Entropy Eruption: Entropy Dynamics in Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27954)
*Wendi Li,Shawn Im,Sharon Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Agent RL training exhibits cyclical entropy eruption, a repeatable oscillation in policy entropy during training. The authors analyze it, show it can embed and propagate degeneration such as duplication/hallucination, and propose SEAL to separate correct/incorrect trajectories in representation space. SEAL stabilizes training and improves downstream agent performance across tasks and algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and diagnose instabilities in agent RL training dynamics and to propose a practical remedy.

Method: Identify and characterize cycles of sudden entropy increases during agent RL training; decompose into three phases with theoretical and empirical analyses; demonstrate persistence of degenerate behaviors; introduce SEAL (Separation-Enhanced Agent Learning), an auxiliary loss that separates correct and incorrect trajectories in representation space; evaluate across multiple benchmarks, models, and RL algorithms.

Result: Detailed description of the cyclical entropy eruption dynamics; evidence that degenerations (e.g., duplication, hallucination) can persist across cycles; SEAL reduces instability and improves downstream performance.

Conclusion: Cyclical entropy eruption is a core challenge in agent RL; a separation-based auxiliary loss like SEAL can mitigate these cycles and yield improved stability and performance across tasks.

Abstract: Agentic large language models are increasingly used to solve real-world tasks by reasoning over goals, invoking tools, and interacting with external environments. Reinforcement learning provides a natural framework for improving these behaviors, and recent agent RL methods have achieved strong results across domains. However, the training dynamics of agent RL remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to diagnose instabilities and design more effective training algorithms. In this work, we identify a previously underexplored phenomenon in agent RL, which we term cyclical entropy eruption. Unlike single-turn reasoning RL, where entropy typically collapses and stays low, agent RL training exhibits unique recurring cycles of sharp entropy eruption and gradual subsidence. We decompose this dynamic into three phases and provide theoretical and empirical analyses of each, explaining the mechanisms underlying its cyclical oscillation. We further show that degenerate patterns such as sentence duplication and hallucination, once acquired during eruption, can persist and accumulate across cycles. Motivated by these findings, we propose SEAL (Separation-Enhanced Agent Learning), a lightweight auxiliary loss that separates correct and incorrect trajectories in representation space, directly targeting the root cause of entropy eruption. Experiments across multiple benchmarks, models, and RL algorithms demonstrate that SEAL stabilizes training and yields stronger downstream agent performance.

</details>


### [333] [Continual Learning in Modern Hopfield Networks with an Application to Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27975)
*Ken Takeda,Masafumi Oizumi,Ryo Karakida*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We link modern Hopfield networks (MHNs) to diffusion models to study continual learning in generative models; high-energy (outlier-like) samples are more forgettable after task changes, and energy-guided replay mitigates forgetting, with experiments on MHNs and diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, pixel-space DDPM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand what aspects of a learned generative distribution are most susceptible to forgetting after task switches, and how to prioritize replay samples.

Method: Use Hopfield energy as a forgetting proxy. Prove in tractable MHN settings that high-energy, outlier-like samples incur larger energy increases after a task change. Propose energy-based replay sample selection. Validate empirically on MHNs and diffusion models (Stable Diffusion and pixel-space DDPM) by showing energy tracks reconstruction-based forgetting and replay reduces forgetting in an energy-dependent manner.

Result: High-energy samples experience larger energy increases after task changes; energy-guided replay is particularly effective for these samples, reducing forgetting. The Hopfield energy tracks forgetting in diffusion models, with experimental replay benefiting energy-dependent forgetting mitigation.

Conclusion: An energy-based Hopfield framework provides a principled lens for continual learning in diffusion models, giving actionable guidance for replay prioritization and validating the MHN→diffusion connection across model families.

Abstract: Generative models, including diffusion models, are increasingly used as foundation models and adapted through sequential fine-tuning, making continual learning an essential problem setting. However, continual learning in such generative models remains poorly understood: after a task change, what aspects of the learned distribution are most easily lost, and what replay samples should be prioritized? We address these questions through the modern Hopfield energy. Recent links between modern Hopfield networks (MHNs) and diffusion models allow analyses in MHNs to be transferred to diffusion models. We introduce intrinsic forgetting as an increase in Hopfield energy after the task change. In tractable settings in an MHN, we prove that high-energy, outlier-like samples undergo a larger energy increase than cluster-like samples, implying that samples located in sharp, isolated basins are more forgettable. We further analyze memory replay and show that replay is particularly effective for high-energy samples, enabling an energy-based selection of replay samples. We validate these predictions in experiments on MHNs and two diffusion models under continual-learning settings: Stable Diffusion and a pixel-space DDPM. In these diffusion models, Hopfield energy tracks reconstruction-based forgetting, and replay experiments reveal energy-dependent mitigation of forgetting that is consistent with the MHN analysis.

</details>


### [334] [Law of Neural Interaction: Depth-Width Shape, Interaction Efficiency, and Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27989)
*Wenjie Sun,Jinning Yang,Shuai Zhang,Mengnan Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends the notion of superposition from parameter space to gradient space via the Neural Feature Ansatz, defines neural interaction, and shows that near an efficient interaction interval determined by the depth-width ratio (R_D/W) under fixed budgets, LLMs generalize better and utilize resources more efficiently; this interval remains stable as budget grows, with small dense LLMs near it performing well on MMLU-Pro; provides design guidance for model initialization and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling laws imply growing resource demands for LLMs, but it is unclear whether models make effective use of resources under a fixed budget. Prior work identifies superposition as contributing to loss; this work seeks to generalize the concept to gradients and investigate how resource efficiency and generalization relate to model shape under budget constraints.

Method: Introduce the Neural Feature Ansatz to extend superposition to gradient space, defining neural interaction. Analyze how a fixed budget constrains performance and how the depth-width ratio R_D/W controls an efficient interaction interval. Compare small-scale dense LLMs to observe performance trends, and evaluate on MMLU-Pro. Provide code for Neural Interaction Law.

Result: Good generalization under a fixed budget coincides with efficient neural interactions. Models can be placed in an efficient interaction interval by choosing an appropriate R_D/W. As budget increases, this efficient interval remains relatively stable. Models operating near this interval, among existing small-scale dense LLMs, tend to perform better on MMLU-Pro. The findings link R_D/W to resource utilization efficiency and generalization, offering guidance on model shape initialization and generalization mechanisms.

Conclusion: The depth-width ratio (R_D/W) directly affects how efficiently resources are utilized and how well a model generalizes under budget constraints.
