These insights inform initialization strategies and deepen understanding of generalization mechanisms in LLMs. Code for Neural Interaction Law is provided.

Abstract: The guidance of scaling laws has increased the resource demands of modern large language models (LLMs), yet it remains questionable whether these models utilize resources effectively under a fixed budget. Previous research has proved superposition as a key contributor to loss. By leveraging the Neural Feature Ansatz, we extend superposition from parameter space to gradient space and define it as neural interaction. We find that under a fixed budget, good generalization is usually accompanied by efficient neural interactions, and the model can be placed in an efficient interaction interval by adjusting its depth-width ratio ($R_{D/W}$). In addition, as the budget scales up, the efficient interaction interval of the model remains relatively stable. By comparing existing small scale dense LLMs, we observe that models operating near this interval tend to perform better on the MMLU-Pro benchmark. Our findings reveal that the $R_{D/W}$ influences resource utilization efficiency and thereby affects generalization, providing insights into model shape initialization and the understanding of model generalization mechanisms. Code for Neural Interaction Law is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Neural_Interaction_Law-D788

</details>


### [335] [Teacher-Student Representational Alignment for Reinforcement Learning-Driven Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28372)
*Meraj Mammadov,Pedro Zuidberg Dos Martires,Johannes Andreas Stork*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a contrastive-learning-based shared embedding to align teacher and student IL without exposing private observations, reducing imitation gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inevitably, IL suffers from an irreducible imitation gap when the teacher uses privileged state information unavailable to the student. The work aims to avoid post-IL RL fine-tuning and to construct imitable policies by design.

Method: Train a shared embedding space that hides agent-specific observations. Use self-supervised contrastive learning in parallel with the teacher policy and prevent the encoder gradients from updating to avoid leaking private information. The result is policies that are easier for the student to imitate from observations.

Result: Empirical evaluations on several domains show higher student performance and a substantially reduced imitation gap compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: A shared-embedding IL framework can produce more imitable teacher policies and reduce the need for RL fine-tuning, improving student performance across domains.

Abstract: Imitation learning (IL) from a state-based reinforcement learning (RL) policy is a common approach to overcome the curse of dimensionality in complex and high-dimensional observation spaces prevalent in robotics. This paper addresses the irreducible imitation gap that emerges when teacher and student are learned in isolation, and the teacher policy has the liberty to rely on privileged state information that the student cannot infer from its observations. Instead of improving poor student performance with RL finetuning after IL, which often requires a whole new training setup, we propose a novel algorithm which learns a shared embedding space that hides agent-specific observations and thus trains imitable teacher policies by construction. We train the shared embedding space with self-supervised contrastive learning in parallel to the teacher policy and prevent it from extracting private information by limiting its gradients from updating the encoder networks. We perform evaluations on several example domains and compare to state-of-the-art baselines showing that our algorithm enables higher student performance with substantially reduced imitation gap.

</details>


### [336] [Geometry-Correct Diffusion Posterior Sampling with Denoiser-Pullback Curvature Guidance and Manifold-Aligned Damping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27990)
*Seunghyeok Shin,Minwoo Kim,Dabin Kim,Hongki Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Replaces scalar guidance with per-noise-level damped Gauss–Newton corrections in diffusion posterior sampling, improving stability and speed for various inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-consistency updates in diffusion models rely on hand-tuned guidance weights and struggle with stiff, operator-dependent curvature, limiting stability and efficiency.

Method: Compute a per-noise-level damped Gauss–Newton correction in diffusion-state coordinates. Use a one-sided curvature model that avoids forward denoiser Jacobians, and apply diffusion-calibrated rank-one damping aligned with the denoiser residual. Solve each correction with matrix-free GMRES using automatic differentiation. Use a variance-preserving Langevin transition with a closed-form drift/noise split for sampling.

Result: On FFHQ and ImageNet across inverse problems, achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS and is markedly faster than most baselines; on accelerated MRI reconstruction, attains the best PSNR/SSIM among compared baselines.

Conclusion: The approach yields stabilized, data-consistent diffusion sampling with principled corrections, improving robustness to operator-dependent curvature and enabling faster, competitive performance across diverse inverse problems.

Abstract: Diffusion posterior sampling conditions diffusion priors on measurements, but data-consistency updates are typically scaled by hand-tuned guidance weights and can destabilize sampling under stiff, operator-dependent curvature. We replace scalar guidance with a per-noise-level damped Gauss--Newton correction computed in diffusion-state coordinates. The correction pulls likelihood gradients back through the denoiser, uses a one-sided curvature model that avoids forward denoiser Jacobians, and applies diffusion-calibrated rank-one damping aligned with the denoiser residual. Each correction is solved with matrix-free GMRES using automatic differentiation, and sampling proceeds with a variance-preserving Langevin transition with a closed-form drift/noise split. On FFHQ and ImageNet across inverse problems, it achieves competitive PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS while running markedly faster than most of the compared baselines; on accelerated MRI reconstruction, it achieves the best PSNR/SSIM among the compared baselines.

</details>


### [337] [Learning Compositional Latent Structure with Vector Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28007)
*Niclas Pokel,Benjamin F. Grewe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Vector Network introduces a hierarchical recurrent architecture that uses a library of reusable rank-1 weight atoms per layer, inferred sparsely per input to form input-specific low-rank weights; achieves strong in-distribution performance and notably better out-of-distribution generalization on compositional tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve compositional generalization by enabling selective, reusable components within deep nets, rather than relying on end-to-end fitting of many tasks into a single dense parameter substrate.

Method: At each layer, replace the fixed weight matrix with a library of rank-1 weight atoms. For each input, minimize a layer-local energy to infer a sparse set of active atoms and their coefficients, constrained by bottom-up input reconstruction and top-down feedback. The atom coefficients produce an input-specific low-rank weight matrix. After convergence, slow learning updates apply only to the selected atoms via local residual signals scaled by the coefficients.

Result: The approach matches strong baselines on four compositional benchmarks (1D signals, 2D spatial decoding, N-body dynamics, compositional MNIST) and often yields substantially lower out-of-distribution error when recombining familiar factors, indicating improved compositional generalization.

Conclusion: Compositional generalization arises from the architectural and inference structure of Vector Networks; this makes the ability to recombine factors a structural property rather than a brittle byproduct of fitting into a single shared substrate.

Abstract: Deep networks are powerful function approximators, but they typically store many different computations in shared weight matrices, making it difficult to selectively reuse or adapt parts of them when a familiar structure appears in novel combinations. We introduce the Vector Network (VN), a hierarchical recurrent architecture in which each layer replaces a fixed weight matrix with a library of reusable rank-1 weight atoms. For each input, VN minimizes a layer-local energy to infer a sparse set of active weight atoms and their coefficients, jointly constrained by bottom-up input reconstruction and top-down feedback consistency. These weight atom coefficients then compose an input-specific low-rank weight matrix for that sample. After convergence, slow learning updates only the selected weight atoms through local residual signals scaled by the inferred coefficients. We evaluate VN on four compositional benchmarks spanning 1D signals, 2D spatial decoding, N-body dynamics, and compositional MNIST. VN matches strong baselines in distribution while often achieving out-of-distribution error about an order of magnitude lower when familiar factors must be recombined in novel ways. Vector networks thus make compositional generalization a structural property of the architecture and inference process rather than a brittle byproduct of fitting many behaviors into one shared dense parameter substrate.

</details>


### [338] [AOE: Exhaustive Out-of-Distribution Detection via Recalibrating Outlier Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28021)
*Fengqiang Wan,Qing-Yuan Jiang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOE introduces adaptive soft targets for OOD data via temperature scaling, preserving ID–OOD relations to achieve higher-entropy predictions and a larger OOD–ID margin.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform OOD labeling ignores semantic relations between OOD samples and ID categories, causing over-softening that narrows the separability margin; exploiting relations can improve OOD detection.

Method: Compute temperature-scaled predictions on OOD samples to form adaptive soft targets; employ a learnable temperature to calibrate the distribution; supervise OOD data with these targets to preserve semantic proximity while encouraging high entropy.

Result: Empirical results across diverse benchmarks show that AOE improves OOD detection performance over traditional uniform-label OE approaches.

Conclusion: AOE offers a simple, effective calibration of OOD supervision that preserves inter-class relations for OOD samples and increases entropy to enlarge the decision margin, yielding better OOD detection.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential for deploying machine learning models in open-world and safety-critical scenarios, where test inputs may deviate from the training distribution and overconfident predictions on unknown samples can lead to unreliable decisions. Outlier Exposure (OE) has emerged as a promising OOD detection paradigm by introducing auxiliary outliers during training to enlarge the margin between in-distribution (ID) and OOD samples. Existing OE-based methods typically enlarge this margin by employing uniform labels to maximize the entropy of OOD samples over ID categories. However, we theoretically show that uniform labels inevitably disregard the relations between OOD samples and ID categories, termed the over-softening effect, leading to a suboptimal margin bound. Our theoretical analysis further reveals that explicitly exploiting such relations can instead yield improved OOD detection performance. Motivated by this insight, we propose \underline{A}daptive Confidence \underline{OE} (AOE), a simple yet effective method that leverages temperature scaling to recalibrate outlier labels. Specifically, AOE generates adaptive soft targets from temperature-scaled model predictions for OOD samples, where the learnable temperature smooths the prediction distribution without fully erasing class-wise relational information. By supervising OOD samples with these adaptive soft targets, AOE preserves the semantic proximity between OOD samples and ID categories while encouraging the softened targets to approach a high-entropy distribution, thereby suppressing overconfident OOD predictions and enlarging the separation margin. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of AOE.

</details>


### [339] [RW-TTT: Batched Serving for Request-Owned Test-Time Training State](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28053)
*Jian Yang,Zhizhuo Kou,Yao Tian,Hao Zhang,Han Chen,Sirui Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RW-TTT introduces read-write test-time training serving for LLMs by tagging decode steps with owner/version/READ/WRITE, enabling phase-compatible batching and owner-only commits; on a single GPU with eight fast-weight InPlace-TTT streams, it achieves 274.61 aggregate tok/s (9.31x vs sequential, 3.44x vs per-stream replicas) while preserving long-context behavior on RULER and passing owner/version checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No-batch compatibility problem: standard batched LLM serving assumes static weights; test-time training updates break this, causing incorrect state sharing across requests. RW-TTT aims to preserve correctness with higher throughput.

Method: Formalize as read-write TTT serving. Tag each decode step with owner, version, and READ/WRITE effect; batch only compatibles phases; commit updates only to the owner; evaluate on one GPU with eight fast-weight InPlace-TTT streams; compare to sequential serving and per-stream replicas under the same memory budget; validate on RULER long-context benchmark and owner/version checks.

Result: Achieves 274.61 aggregate tokens per second on one GPU with eight streams; 9.31x faster than sequential serving and 3.44x faster than per-stream replicas under the same memory budget; preserves behavior on RULER and passes owner/version checks.

Conclusion: RW-TTT provides a scalable, correctness-preserving approach to accelerate test-time training for LLMs, enabling higher throughput in batched serving while maintaining per-request state isolation.

Abstract: Test-time training (TTT) adapts an LLM during generation by reading and updating request-owned state, such as fast weights, low-rank deltas, or streaming learner state. This breaks batched LLM serving, which assumes shared static weights: serial execution is correct but slow, while naive batching can corrupt request state. We formulate this problem as read-write TTT serving and present RW-TTT , which tags each decode step with its owner, version, and READ/WRITE effect, batches only compatible phases, and commits updates only to the owner. On one GPU with eight fast-weight InPlace-TTT streams, RW-TTT reaches 274.61 aggregate tok/s, 9.31x over sequential serving and 3.44x over per-stream replicas under the same memory budget. It preserves behavior on RULER, a long-context benchmark, and passes owner/version checks.

</details>


### [340] [On the Learnability of Test-Time Adaptation: A Recovery Complexity Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28057)
*Zhi Zhou,Ming Yang,Shi-Yu Tian,Kun-Yang Yu,Lan-Zhe Guo,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical framework for TTA learnability under non-stationary streams, introducing Recovery Complexity and TTA Learnability, with discrete surrogate and order-wise bounds revealing adaptivity-information trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of principled theory for TTA that aligns with its objectives under evolving distributions and information constraints; bridge theory with non-stationary test streams.

Method: Define (ε,δ)-Recovery Complexity and (ε,ρ)-TTA Learnability; introduce a discrete surrogate for non-stationary streams; derive order-wise matching lower/upper bounds; analyze both gradual and abrupt shifts; establish adaptivity-information trade-off.

Result: Provide unified learnability guarantees for TTA, complementing regret-based analyses; quantify recovery time and long-term reliability under non-stationarity; reveal fundamental limits of TTA.

Conclusion: The paper lays a foundational theory for TTA learnability with principled performance metrics and bounds, enabling systematic assessment of adaptivity under distribution shifts and informing design of TTA methods.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adapt models to maintain reliable performance on non-stationary test streams without requiring labeled data. Despite its empirical success, the learnability of TTA under non-stationary streams remains unexplored. A key challenge is the lack of a principled theoretical framework that simultaneously aligns with the TTA objective and captures both continuously evolving distribution shifts and intrinsic information constraints. To address this gap, we propose the first theoretical framework for studying the learnability of TTA and introduce $(ε,δ)$-Recovery Complexity and $(ε,ρ)$-TTA Learnability. Recovery complexity measures the post-shift time needed to maintain excess risk below a target level with high probability, and is further extended to TTA learnability, which measures the long-term reliability of TTA. Within this framework, we introduce a novel discrete surrogate for non-stationary test streams, enabling a unified and tractable analysis of both gradual and abrupt shifts. We derive order-wise matching lower and upper bounds on recovery complexity, revealing fundamental limits of TTA and an intrinsic adaptivity-information trade-off. These results provide unified learnability guarantees for TTA that complement regret-based analyses.

</details>


### [341] [PINE: Pruning Boosted Tree Ensembles with Conformal In-Distribution Prediction Equivalence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28068)
*Haruki Yajima,Yusuke Matsui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINE is a conformal-calibrated, region-limited pruning method for tree ensembles that improves compression up to 30% while preserving predictions within an in-distribution region.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard pruning methods optimize an accuracy-compression trade-off and may alter predictions, potentially harming decision consistency. Faithful pruning preserves prediction equivalence but often yields low compression. PINE targets a practical balance by providing guarantees within a controllable in-distribution region.

Method: Introduce PINE, a pruning method that preserves prediction equivalence within a defined in-distribution region and controls the region size via a single parameter α using conformal calibration. This yields a tunable, region-based fidelity guarantee while increasing compression. Evaluation on 12 public tabular datasets.

Result: PINE achieves up to a 30% improvement in compression ratio over existing faithful pruning methods while preserving predictions at a level comparable to these methods.

Conclusion: PINE offers a practical, tunable fidelity guarantee for pruning tree ensembles: by constraining fidelity to an in-distribution region via α and conformal calibration, it improves compression without sacrificing predictive reliability within that region, as demonstrated on multiple tabular datasets.

Abstract: Tree ensembles are machine learning models with strong predictive performance and interpretability, and remain widely used for tabular data. Standard pruning methods for tree ensembles typically optimize an accuracy-compression trade-off and may change a subset of predictions, potentially compromising decision consistency. Faithful pruning methods address this issue by preserving prediction equivalence over the entire input space, but this requirement leads to lower compression ratios. We propose PINE, a pruning method that provides strong guarantees within an in-distribution region. PINE preserves prediction equivalence within this region and controls the region size using a single parameter $α$ via conformal calibration. Experiments on 12 public tabular datasets show that PINE improves the compression ratio by up to $30\%$ while preserving predictions at a comparable level to existing faithful pruning methods.

</details>


### [342] [Measure-to-measure Regression with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28075)
*Matthew Vandergrift,Martha White,Yury Polyanskiy,Philippe Rigollet,Lazar Atanackovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces nonlinear measure-to-measure (M2M) regression and two transformer-based approaches (static M2M maps and dynamic M2M velocity fields) to learn maps between probability distributions. Demonstrates scalability and expressivity, with generalization to unseen measures in synthetic tests, interacting particle systems, and a large organoid dataset for predicting treatment response.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predict evolution of populations whose state is a probability distribution under unknown transformations; distributions (not individuals) are the data points, motivated by biology where whole populations evolve collectively.

Method: Formalize nonlinear M2M regression and implement two transformer-based operators: a static M2M map using transformers and a dynamic velocity-field formulation; leverage measure-dependent and mean-field structure of transformers to operate on probability distributions at scale.

Result: Empirically shows generalization to unseen measures on synthetic data, interacting particle systems, and a colorectal cancer organoid dataset for predicting treatment response.

Conclusion: Transformers can serve as expressive, scalable operators on the space of probability measures to perform nonlinear M2M regression, enabling learning of population-level transformations with strong generalization across varied distributions.

Abstract: Many learning problems require predicting how populations evolve under an unknown transformation. A natural representation for such populations is a probability measure, with point clouds as a key example. In this work, we study the measure-to-measure (M2M) regression problem, in which one seeks to learn a map between probability measures from a finite collection of observed input-output pairs. In contrast to classical regression, where individual samples are transformed independently, M2M regression treats entire distributions as the data points. This perspective is vital in certain scientific applications, for example, cellular and molecular biology, where cells are known to evolve not as independent data points but as a collection. However, few existing approaches address the problem of M2M regression with sufficient expressivity and scalability. We present a formalization of nonlinear M2M regression and introduce two easy-to-use, expressive, and scalable approaches to learn such operators: transformers as static M2M maps and transformers as dynamic M2M velocity fields. Our approach leverages the natural measure-dependent and mean-field structure of transformers to learn nonlinear M2M maps on the space of probability distributions. We illustrate the effectiveness of our proposed method to generalize to unseen measures on synthetic experiments, interacting particle systems, and a large-scale patient-derived organoid dataset for predicting treatment response in colorectal cancer.

</details>


### [343] [Long Live The Balance: Information Bottleneck Driven Tree-based Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28109)
*Hao Jiang,Shurui Li,Tianpeng Bu,Bowen Xu,Xin Liu,Qihua Chen,Hongtao Duan,Lulu Hu,Bin Yang,Minying Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces IB-Score to quantify exploration-exploitation trade-off in online RL for LLMs and IB-TPO, a tree-based policy optimization using Information Bottleneck, achieving improved stability and performance with more efficient sampling; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome imbalanced exploration-exploitation, instability, and sub-optimal optimization in online RL for large language models by providing a principled metric and a corresponding optimization framework.

Method: Define IB-Score based on Information Bottleneck theory to balance step-level reasoning diversity and mutual information with the correct answer. Develop IB-TPO with an IB-guided tree sampling strategy that improves online sampling efficiency (≈50% more trajectories at the same token budget) and reuses tree structure for Monte Carlo IB-Score estimation.

Result: IB-TPO outperforms the GRPO baseline by 2.9%–3.6% and surpasses other state-of-the-art online RL methods across standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: The IB-Score provides a principled objective for balancing exploration and exploitation in online RL for LLMs, and the IB-TPO framework delivers improved performance and sampling efficiency, validated by extensive experiments. Code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: Recent advances in online reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising performance in complex reasoning tasks. However, they often exhibit an imbalanced exploration-exploitation trade-off, resulting in unstable optimization and sub-optimal performance. We introduce IB-Score, a novel metric grounded in Information Bottleneck theory that evaluates policy's exploration-exploitation balance by quantifying the trade-off between step-level reasoning diversity and mutual information shared with the correct answer. Analysis based on IB-Score shows that popular online RL approaches (e.g., GRPO) with common regularizers fail to consistently maintain balance during training with suboptimal results. To address this, we propose Information Bottleneck-driven Tree-based Policy Optimization (IB-TPO), a principled framework that formulates IB-Score as a fine-grained optimization objective and utilizes a novel IB-guided tree sampling strategy that not only improves the efficiency of online sampling with 50% more trajectories under the same token budget, but also reuses the tree structure for effective IB-Score Monte Carlo estimation. Extensive experiments across standard benchmarks show that our method significantly outperforms GRPO baseline by 2.9% to 3.6% and also outperforms other state-of-the-art online RL approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/alibaba/EfficientRL.

</details>


### [344] [Chreode: A Cell World Model for One-Step Temporal Dynamics and Perturbation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28111)
*Mufan Qiu,Genhui Zheng,Yinuo Xu,Ruichen Zhang,Ying Ding,Qi Long,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chreode is a one-step, action-conditioned cell-world model with a structured residual transition operator that predicts cell-state transitions in a single pass. It relocates distributional evolution to training time, enabling efficient generation and preserving a Waddington-inspired decomposition of dynamics. Pretrained on a 2.4M-cell mouse atlas with a shared scVI encoder and a DiT-based dynamics backbone, it improves targeted metrics and transfers to perturbation tasks, including GEARS, with competitive zero-shot clonal fate scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of static control-to-treated maps that ignore time and per-dataset multi-step ODE/Schrödinger-bridge methods. There is a need for scalable, interpretable, and transferable models that capture developmental trajectories and perturbation-induced transitions across datasets.

Method: Chreode uses a one-step transition with a structured residual operator to model action-conditioned cell-state changes. Distributional evolution is learned at training time rather than inferred at test time. The model comprises a shared scVI encoder and a DiT-based dynamics backbone, pretrained on a 2.4M-cell mouse embryonic atlas across 7 datasets. It decomposes dynamics into downhill landscape flow, rotational in-tangent dynamics, and stochastic spread. Fine-tuning initialization improves performance over scratch models, PI-SDE, and PRESCIENT. Evaluation uses per-target Sinkhorn distance; transfer learning to GEARS uses pretrained dynamics representations. Zero-shot clonal fate scores are produced and are competitive with dynamic-OT baselines.

Result: On Weinreb hematopoiesis and Veres islet differentiation, Chreode improves per-target Sinkhorn distance over matched scratch models, PI-SDE, and PRESCIENT. It reduces Norman Perturb-seq’s DE20 mean squared error from 0.2121 to 0.1858 (12.4% relative). It also yields transferable dynamics representations that improve GEARS training without changing its procedure and produces zero-shot clonal fate scores competitive with strong baselines.

Conclusion: Pretrained developmental-trajectory dynamics encode differentiation primitives transferable to CRISPR-induced state shifts. The Chreode backbone provides interpretable, transferable dynamics that enable single-pass generation, improved perturbation prediction, and competitive zero-shot clonal fate scoring across datasets and tasks.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell will change its transcriptional state under a developmental signal or a genetic perturbation is the computational core of in-silico biology and the AI Virtual Cell program. Existing approaches either fit static control-to-treated maps that discard time, or solve multi-step ODE / Schrödinger-bridge problems on each dataset independently. We introduce Chreode, a one-step cell world model that predicts action-conditioned cell-state transitions through a structured residual transition operator. It shifts distributional evolution from inference time to training time, enabling single-pass generation while preserving a Waddington-inspired decomposition into downhill landscape flow, rotational in-tangent dynamics, and stochastic spread. The model is pretrained with a shared scVI encoder and a DiT-based dynamics backbone on a 2.4M-cell mouse embryonic atlas spanning 7 datasets. As a fine-tuning initialization, Chreode improves per-target Sinkhorn distance on Weinreb hematopoiesis and Veres islet differentiation over matched scratch models, PI-SDE, and PRESCIENT. As a transferable gene-state embedding for GEARS, the pretrained dynamics representation reduces shared-vocabulary DE20 mean squared error on Norman Perturb-seq from 0.2121 to 0.1858, a 12.4% relative improvement, without changing the GEARS training procedure. We interpret this transfer to perturbation prediction as evidence that pretrained developmental-trajectory dynamics encode differentiation primitives transferable to CRISPR-induced state shifts, since both involve cell-state transitions in a shared latent geometry. The pretrained backbone additionally produces zero-shot clonal fate scores on Weinreb that are competitive with strong dynamic-OT baselines.

</details>


### [345] [Adaptive Coarse-to-Fine Subgoal Refinement for Long-Horizon Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28127)
*Kaiqiang Ke,Shenghong He,Chengdong Xu,Yuheng Luo,Xiangyuan Lan,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new offline goal-conditioned RL method, CFHRL, adaptively refines distant goals into subgoals in a coarse-to-fine hierarchy, with a stopping criterion based on a learned reachability cost, improving long-horizon performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon offline GCRL suffers from weak supervision and bootstrapping errors; fixed hierarchical structures with fixed horizons are misaligned with varying task difficulty. An adaptive, coarse-to-fine approach could better balance progress and computational/learning effort.

Method: CFHRL starts from the final goal and recursively proposes intermediate targets using replay-supported candidates. It trains subgoals and a reachability cost to judge local executability, stopping refinement when the current target is deemed executable. Subgoals need not be exact midpoints or globally optimal; they should enable progress and reduce remaining difficulty, allowing subsequent refinement over shorter horizons.

Result: A stylized theoretical analysis suggests robustness of approximate recursive contraction. Empirically, CFHRL achieves substantial gains on long-horizon tasks in OGBench. Ablations show the refinement and stopping mechanisms are beneficial.

Conclusion: CFHRL provides a practical, adaptive coarse-to-fine hierarchical framework for offline GCRL, mitigating horizon mismatch, reducing bootstrapping error, and improving long-horizon performance; the approach is validated by theoretical and empirical analyses and ablations.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is challenging in long-horizon tasks, where distant state--goal pairs provide weak supervision and value estimates become vulnerable to accumulated bootstrapping errors. Hierarchical methods mitigate this difficulty by introducing intermediate subgoals, but fixed temporal abstractions or fixed hierarchy depths can be mismatched to state--goal pairs with different reachability horizons. We propose Coarse-to-Fine Hierarchical Goal Reinforcement Learning (CFHRL), a fully offline GCRL framework that adaptively refines distant goals before execution. Starting from the final goal, CFHRL recursively proposes intermediate targets, trained from replay-supported candidates, and stops refinement once the current target is estimated to be locally executable by a learned reachability cost. The key idea is that a subgoal need not be an exact midpoint or globally optimal waypoint; it only needs to provide reliable progress and reduce the remaining reaching difficulty, enabling subsequent refinement over shorter horizons. A stylized analysis further supports the robustness of approximate recursive contraction. Experiments on OGBench show substantial gains on several long-horizon tasks, with ablations validating the proposed refinement and stopping mechanisms

</details>


### [346] [Learning to Bid in Repeated Second-Price Auctions with Dynamic Values and Aggregated Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28133)
*Benjamin Heymann,Otmane Sakhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic-value bidding in continuous-time repeated second-price auctions with aggregated feedback; a confidence-bound algorithm achieves near-optimal regret: ~O(log N) for piecewise-linear primitives and ~O(N^{1/3}) for general smooth primitives, without explicit randomization, supported by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate learning to bid when bidder value decays with time since last win, requiring balancing current gains against future value and learning unknown environment parameters under limited feedback.

Method: Plug-in estimators coupled with a differential-equation characterization of the optimal policy; a confidence-bound algorithm is analyzed to derive regret bounds for two primitive classes (piecewise-linear and general smooth).

Result: Regret bounds: ~O(log N) for piecewise-linear primitives; ~O(N^{1/3}) for general smooth primitives; near-optimal performance without explicit randomization; numerical experiments corroborate theory.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that dynamic-value bidding in continuous-time repeated auctions can be effectively learned with provable regret guarantees under aggregated, horizon-end feedback, and clarifies how primitive structure affects regret scale.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning to bid when the bidder's value is dynamic, i.e., when the current value depends on past outcomes. Specifically, we consider a bidder participating in repeated second-price auctions whose value depends on the time elapsed since their last successful bid, with auctions arriving in continuous time and only aggregated feedback revealed at the end of the horizon. Such a bidder must (1) balance the immediate benefit of winning the current auction against its impact on future values and (2) learn unknown environmental parameters. We derive regret bounds for a class of learning methods that combine plug-in estimators with a differential-equation characterization of the optimal policy, and show that a specific confidence bound algorithm learns the optimal policy with a near optimal regret of $\widetilde{O}(\log N)$ for piecewise linear primitives, and $\widetilde{O}(N^{1/3})$ for general, smooth primitives, achieving these regrets without explicit randomization. These theoretical results are supported by numerical experiments.

</details>


### [347] [Self-Consistency via Marginal Sharpening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28142)
*Aleksei Arzhantsev,Otmane Sakhi,Nicolas Chopin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Inference-time sampling should sharpen the marginal over answers, not the full output, to better motivate reasoning. The authors define self-consistency as the inference objective and introduce an autoregressive parallel sampling method to approximate this sharpened answer marginal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing power-sampling methods optimize the joint likelihood of entire generated outputs, which entangles reasoning traces with final answers. A robust rationale requires that many plausible reasoning paths support an answer; thus targeting the answer marginal at inference time better aligns with reasoning quality.

Method: Shift the target from full-output distributions to the sharpened answer marginal and propose a simple autoregressive parallel sampling algorithm that efficiently approximates sampling from that marginal.

Result: The method yields stronger performance than standard power sampling on mathematics and coding benchmarks and is orders of magnitude faster.

Conclusion: Fostering self-consistency by optimizing the answer marginal at inference time is a promising approach for improving reasoning in LMs, achieving both higher accuracy and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Inference-time sampling can elicit strong reasoning abilities from language models without additional training. Existing power-sampling methods do so by sharpening the distribution over full generated outputs, favoring completions that are individually likely under the model. We argue that this is the wrong object to target for reasoning: a completion entangles a reasoning trace with a final answer, whereas what matters is whether an answer is supported by many plausible reasoning paths. We therefore shift the target from the full-output distribution to the sharpened answer marginal, making self-consistency an inference-time objective rather than a post-hoc voting criterion. Surprisingly, this marginal target admits an efficient approximation: we propose a simple, purely autoregressive parallel sampling algorithm that approximately samples from the sharpened answer marginal, eliciting stronger performance than standard power sampling on mathematics and coding benchmarks while being orders of magnitude faster.

</details>


### [348] [Sequential Neural Probabilistic Amplitude Shaping: Learning the Channel's Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28143)
*Mohammad Taha Askari,Lutz Lampe,Amirhossein Ghazisaeidi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural probabilistic amplitude shaping with a block-less sequential autoregressive encoder compatible with arithmetic distribution matching, outperforming existing PAS by accounting for implementation losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve shaping efficiency by reducing rate loss and increasing achievable information rate under practical implementation losses while maintaining implementability.

Method: Introduce a block-less sequential autoregressive encoder for neural probabilistic amplitude shaping that is compatible with arithmetic distribution matching; train to approximate target distributions and integrate with ADM to realize the shaping; explicitly account for implementation losses in optimization.

Result: Outperforms existing PAS methods when all implementation losses are included; yields reduced rate loss and higher achievable information rates.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical and effective neural PAS approach with a sequential autoregressive encoder that aligns with ADM, providing gains in rate efficiency and AIR in realistic settings.

Abstract: We present the first neural probabilistic amplitude shaping that outperforms existing methods while accounting for all implementation losses, using a block-less, easily implementable sequential autoregressive encoder compatible with arithmetic distribution matching, yielding reduced rate loss and higher achievable information rates.

</details>


### [349] [Sign-Aware Gated Sparse Autoencoders: Modeling Anticorrelated Features with Bi-Jump-ReLU Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28149)
*Bartosz Wieciech,Zmnako Awrahman,Marcin Czelej,Victor Hugo Jaramillo Velasquez,Wioletta Stobieniecka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sign-Aware Gated SAE (SA-GSAE) introduces two-sided gated sparsity with signed magnitude and auxiliary supervision to enable bipolar sharing of latent representations in LLM activations, achieving sign-aware, parameter-efficient representations and avoiding reconstruction collapse; it outperforms a full-width Gated SAE on several metrics and reduces dead units substantially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard sparse autoencoders often enforce non-negativity, forcing separate latents for opposite concepts and wasting capacity when features are anticorrelated. A sign-aware, efficient representation is needed for interpreting LLM activations with reduced dead units and better reconstruction.

Method: Two-sided polarity-sensitive gate selects support by sign; a signed-magnitude path avoids L1 shrinkage; an auxiliary reconstruction term prevents gate collapse; Bi-Jump-ReLU enables bipolar sharing (one latent encodes both signs along a shared direction); parameterization shows sign-awareness remains efficient even with rare anticorrelated pairs; symmetric variant r_i^+ = r_i^- considered as default.

Result: Empirical evaluation on real LLM activations across three mid-depth hookpoints on Pythia-1B and SmolLM3-3B (6 cells, 3 seeds). A half-width SA-GSAE at width H strictly Pareto-dominates a full-width Gated SAE at 2H over the full L0 sweep in 3 of 6 cells (MLP-output and resid-mid/Pythia-1B); in the other 3 cells, it matches R^2 within 0.025 (max gap -0.008) while reducing dead fraction by 0.35-0.62 absolute. Sweep-geomean dead fractions reduce ~100x-500x on MLP-output cells and Pythia-1B resid, ~2x-4x on attention cells and SmolLM3-3B resid. Ablations show the two-sided gate and auxiliary loss are critical (no auxiliary collapse; tying r_i^+ = r_i^- is indistinguishable). Full-width SA-GSAE collapses on SmolLM3-3B resid; half-width avoids collapse.

Conclusion: A symmetric variant of SA-GSAE with sign-aware gating is recommended as default due to strong Pareto improvements, parameter efficiency, and robust reconstruction without collapse; bipolar sharing concentrates on a small latent subset in attention and broad latents for MLP outputs, indicating effective, sign-aware representation of opposite concepts.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) extract interpretable features from Large Language Models, but standard variants enforce non-negativity, forcing separate latents for diametrically opposed concepts (e.g., "pressure too high" vs. "pressure too low") and wasting dictionary capacity when features are anticorrelated. We propose the Sign-Aware Gated SAE (SA-GSAE): two-sided gated sparsity with signed magnitude and auxiliary supervision. A polarity-sensitive gate selects support on either sign, a signed-magnitude path avoids L1 shrinkage, and an auxiliary reconstruction prevents gate collapse. Bipolar sharing - one latent encoding both signs along a shared direction - is realised via a new Bi-Jump-ReLU activation; parameter accounting shows sign-awareness stays parameter-efficient even when anticorrelated pairs are rare.
  On real LLM activations across three mid-depth hookpoints on Pythia-1B and SmolLM3-3B (6 cells, 3 seeds), a half-width SA-GSAE at width H strictly Pareto-dominates a full-width Gated SAE at 2H over the entire swept L0 overlap on 3 of 6 cells (both MLP-output hookpoints and resid-mid/Pythia-1B); on the remaining 3 it matches R^2 within 0.025 (max gap -0.008) while cutting dead fraction by 0.35-0.62 absolute. Sweep-geomean dead-fraction reductions are ~100x-500x on MLP-output cells and Pythia-1B resid, ~2x-4x on attention cells and SmolLM3-3B resid. Ablations show the two-sided gate and auxiliary loss are load-bearing (no auxiliary collapses LR to 0.27, 98% dead); tying r_i^+ = r_i^- is indistinguishable (|Delta R^2| = 0.0015), and we recommend this symmetric variant as default. MLP-output gains come from most latents carrying both polarities; on attention, bipolar structure concentrates in a small set of top latents. Full-width SA-GSAE exhibits a reproducible reconstruction collapse at SmolLM3-3B resid that the half-width entirely avoids.

</details>


### [350] [Off-Policy Learning to Reason Works Because It Is More Pessimistic Than You Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28150)
*Otmane Sakhi,Aleksei Arzhantsev,Imad Aouali,Flavian Vasile*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Off-policy RL for LLMs by removing importance-weight corrections and embracing older data; frames updates via implicit pessimism and proposes a principled stabilization to control the induced target distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale RL for LLMs, data is collected under older policies; relying on PPO-like importance weights causes high variance and instability, hindering training and accelerating entropy collapse. A shift to fully off-policy objectives may yield stronger, more stable learning.

Method: Provide an intuitive construction of off-policy objectives that subsume existing off-policy methods and reveal implicit pessimism; analyze how target distributions become more conservative than nominal goals; propose a principled modification to stabilize the induced distribution for off-policy learning.

Result: The perspective explains stability gains from particular implementation choices that implicitly regulate the target distribution; the proposed modification stabilizes this distribution and improves off-policy learning performance.

Conclusion: Offers a unified, theoretically motivated framework for off-policy RL in large-scale settings and a practical stabilization technique that can outperform traditional PPO-based approaches by exploiting off-policy data more robustly.

Abstract: Large scale reinforcement learning has become a central tool for improving reasoning in large language models. At this scale, generation is often lagged or asynchronous, so updates are performed on data collected by older policies. This makes learning inherently off-policy. Most existing approaches nevertheless remain rooted in PPO-style trust-region objectives, treating training as approximately on-policy and using importance weights to correct distribution mismatch. These corrections can introduce high variance, destabilize optimization, and accelerate entropy collapse. Recent work suggests an alternative: rather than correcting the mismatch, one can embrace off-policy data and remove importance weights, often yielding stronger algorithms. In this paper, we provide an intuitive construction of off-policy objectives that include successful off-policy objectives and show that their effectiveness can be understood through implicit pessimism: they optimize toward target policies that are more conservative than their nominal objectives suggest. This perspective explains why some particular implementation choices improve stability: they implicitly control the effective target distribution. We then propose a principled modification that stabilize this induced distribution and improve off-policy learning.

</details>


### [351] [Temporal Hyperbolic Graph Representation Learning for Scale-Free Internet Routing and Delay Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28155)
*Yi-Ling Kuo,Hao-Yu Tien,Shih-Yu Tsai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HERMIT combines hyperbolic temporal graph learning with a Random Forest regressor to jointly predict RTTs and network links. It outperforms a baseline RF using only historical RTTs and prior hyperbolic TGNNs on real Internet data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RTT prediction is vital for routing, QoS, and traffic engineering but is hampered by long-term temporal dependencies, evolving routing dynamics, and heavy-tailed latency. Euclidean TGNNs inadequately capture the Internet's hierarchical/scale-free topology; hyperbolic geometry offers a more suitable representation.

Method: A hybrid framework (HERMIT) built on HMPTGN: a hyperbolic, manifold-preserving temporal GNN coupled with a learnable RTT-aware edge encoder, producing hyperbolic node representations that are combined with historical RTT statistics. A Random Forest regressor performs RTT prediction; jointly handles link prediction and RTT prediction.

Result: On a large-scale Internet dataset (2015–2024), HERMIT achieves ~6% RMSE improvement over a baseline Random Forest that uses only historical RTTs and reduces large-error instances on heavy-tailed samples. It also surpasses HMPTGN and HTGN in link prediction performance.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic temporal graph learning, when integrated with tree-based regression, provides a scalable, accurate approach for RTT and link prediction in real-world Internet topologies, leveraging hierarchical structure and temporal dynamics.

Abstract: Predicting Internet round-trip time (RTT) is critical for routing optimization, quality-of-service (QoS) provisioning, and traffic engineering, yet remains challenging due to long-term temporal dependencies, evolving routing dynamics, and heavy-tailed latency distributions. While Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) can model evolving network topologies, most existing approaches operate in Euclidean space, which poorly captures the hierarchical and scale-free structure of Internet routing graphs. Hyperbolic geometry provides a more suitable representation space.
  We propose HERMIT (Hyperbolic Edge-aware RTT Modeling via Integrated Topology), a hybrid framework combining a hyperbolic manifold-preserving temporal GNN with a Random Forest regressor for joint link prediction and RTT prediction. Built on HMPTGN, HERMIT introduces RTT-aware edge features and a learnable edge encoder to improve modeling of evolving link states and routing behavior. The resulting hyperbolic node representations are combined with historical RTT statistics for robust latency prediction.
  We evaluate HERMIT on a large-scale real Internet dataset spanning 2015-2024. HERMIT consistently outperforms a strong Random Forest baseline using only historical RTT statistics, achieving a 6% RMSE improvement while reducing large errors on heavy-tailed samples. It also surpasses prior hyperbolic TGNN models, including HMPTGN and HTGN, in link prediction performance. These results demonstrate that combining hyperbolic temporal graph learning with tree-based regression provides a scalable solution for RTT prediction in real-world Internet topologies.

</details>


### [352] [Unification and Optimization of Robust Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28165)
*Jonas Hanselle,Valentin Margraf,Clemens Damke,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified design space for robust learning that decomposes robustness into sequential stages and optimizes hyperparameters jointly; competitive with single-method baselines across tabular, image, and reward tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address multiple failure modes (distribution shift, label noise, finite-sample degeneracies) that are typically tackled separately, by providing a unifying framework and a practical training procedure.

Method: Organize existing robust methods along three axes and implement a sequential training procedure: reference distribution enrichment, input-space perturbation, label-space perturbation, and sample-level aggregation, each with pessimistic/neutral/optimistic stances; enable joint hyperparameter optimization to compose robustness strategies.

Result: Joint hyperparameter optimization is competitive with the best single-method baseline across tabular, image, and reward-modeling benchmarks, offering a robust default when the dominant failure mode is unknown.

Conclusion: A unified robustness design space and joint optimization approach allow practitioners to flexibly compose robustness strategies suited to the task, avoiding premature commitment to a single failure mode.

Abstract: The literature has proposed various robust alternatives to empirical risk minimisation to address failure modes such as distribution shift, label noise and finite-sample degeneracies. Examples include distributionally robust optimization, label smoothing, vicinal risk minimization, and Mixup. However, such approaches are typically developed in isolation, forcing practitioners to commit a priori to a single failure mode even when the dominant mode for the task is unclear. To address this, we organize a broad class of existing methods along three common design axes and derive a tractable training procedure that decomposes robust learning into sequential stages (reference distribution enrichment, input-space perturbation, label-space perturbation, and sample-level aggregation), each with a choice of stance (pessimistic, neutral, or optimistic). This results in a unified design space in which joint hyperparameter optimization can compose and configure robustness strategies suited to the task at hand. Across tabular, image, and reward modeling benchmarks, joint hyperparameter optimization is competitive with the best single-method baseline in each setting, offering a reliable default for practitioners who do not know a priori which failure mode dominates their task.

</details>


### [353] [QuITE: Query-Based Irregular Time Series Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28166)
*JungHoon Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QuITE is a plug-and-play input embedding for irregular multivariate time series that uses learnable query tokens and a single self-attention layer to aggregate irregular observations, producing backbone-ready latent representations without data imputation or architecture changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irregular sampling in IMTS hinders effective modeling and existing solutions either require specialized backbones or interpolate data on regular grids, which can distort dynamics. A universal, lightweight embedding could unlock compatibility with standard MTS models.

Method: Introduce QuITE (Query-Based Irregular Time Series Embedding): uses learnable query tokens to attend over irregular observations via a single self-attention layer, aggregating them into latent representations compatible with existing MTS backbones; avoids imputation and architectural modification.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show consistent improvement of MTS models, with average relative gains up to 54.7% in forecasting and 15.8% in classification across diverse datasets and backbones; code available on GitHub.

Conclusion: QuITE provides a simple, effective plug-in embedding that addresses irregular sampling bottlenecks, enabling backbone-compatible representations and improving performance without artificial value generation.

Abstract: Irregular Multivariate Time Series (IMTS) are common in practice, yet their irregular sampling complicates effective modeling. Existing approaches typically either (i) design specialized architectures that limit the reuse of proven Multivariate Time Series (MTS) models, or (ii) map IMTS onto regular temporal grids through interpolation, which may distort temporal dynamics by introducing artificial values. To address these limitations, we propose a new input-embedding-based approach. We identify that the key bottleneck lies not in the backbone architecture, but in conventional embedding layers that assume uniform sampling. In this work, we introduce QuITE (Query-Based Irregular Time Series Embedding), a simple yet effective plug-and-play embedding module for IMTS. QuITE employs learnable query tokens to aggregate irregular observations through a single self-attention layer, directly producing backbone-compatible latent representations without artificial value generation or architectural modification. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show that QuITE consistently improves MTS models, yielding average relative gains of up to $54.7\%$ in forecasting and $15.8\%$ in classification across diverse datasets and backbone architectures. Code is available at: https://github.com/Meaningfull9502/QuITE.

</details>


### [354] [Joint Training of Multi-Token Prediction in Reinforcement Learning via Optimal Coefficient Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28184)
*Zili Wang,Jiajun Chai,Lin Chen,Xiaohan Wang,Shiming Xiang,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposing the per-step impact of multi-token prediction (MTP) on RL objectives explains joint training dynamics with RL from verifiable rewards (RLVR); introduces Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC) using an online log-probability proxy; yields robust gains across six math-reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RLVR+MTP joint training often degrades performance when MTP gradients are shared. The work seeks an optimization-centered understanding to enable effective joint training and improve reasoning capabilities in LLMs.

Method: Analytically decompose the per-step MTP effect on the RL objective into a first-order correlation term and a second-order perturbation penalty. Show how this unifies Detach, Cross-Entropy, and Policy loss training regimes. Analyze policy loss to explain residual degradation. Propose OCC: an adaptive scheme that online-tracks the optimal coefficient via a log-probability proxy at negligible cost.

Result: Empirical evaluation across six competition-level mathematical reasoning benchmarks shows that OCC consistently matches or exceeds the detach baseline and improves joint MTP-RL training performance.

Conclusion: The decomposition clarifies when joint MTP-RL training helps or hurts and identifies a practical calibration method. OCC provides a lightweight, online solution that stabilizes joint training and can generalize to other RL-enhanced pretraining settings.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as the standard paradigm for improving reasoning capability of large language models, while Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has been a widely adopted module in pretraining. Combining them is a natural approach, yet current RL practices detach MTP gradients because joint training degrades the performance. We revisit this failure from an optimization perspective. We show that the per-step effect of MTP on the RL objective can be decomposed into two terms: a first-order correlation and a second-order perturbation penalty. This decomposition unifies three MTP training regimes: Detach, Cross-Entropy loss, and Policy loss, and explains why each succeeds or fails. Further analysis of policy loss reveals that, although it aligns with intuition, performance still degrades: the correlation term decays while the quadratic penalty persists. Guided by the analysis, we propose Optimal Coefficient Calibration (OCC), an adaptive scheme that tracks the optimal coefficient online via a log-probability proxy at negligible cost. Across six competition-level mathematical reasoning benchmarks, OCC consistently matches or exceeds the detach baseline, delivering improved joint MTP-RL training performance.

</details>


### [355] [Hierarchical Synthetic Tabular Data Generation: A Hybrid Top-Down and Bottom-Up Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28198)
*Junfeng Nie,Alvin Jin,Xiaohui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical hybrid framework (H-TDBU) for synthetic tabular data that separates semantic structure from stochastic texture via top-down semantic constraints and bottom-up statistical learning, integrated through an iterative synthesis engine. It improves train-synthetic-test-real performance on weak multimodal financial benchmarks while preserving semantic consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data heterogeneity, logical consistency, rare-event coverage, and robustness in low-data regimes where purely generative models and LLM-based approaches struggle. Need controllability, semantic coherence, and fidelity in synthetic tabular data, especially in multimodal settings.

Method: Introduce H-TDBU: decouple semantic structures from stochastic texture. Top-down path creates structure-driven logical constraints and cross-modal alignment rules. Bottom-up path uses lightweight tabular generators to learn local statistical patterns from real data. An iterative feedback loop consolidates both paths into a unified synthesis engine for data generation.

Result: Evaluated on weak multimodal financial benchmarks combining tabular and sentiment-text data. H-TDBU outperforms neural baseline methods in train-synthetic-test-real performance and maintains semantic consistency.

Conclusion: Hierarchical rule-guided synthesis is effective for balancing controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation.

Abstract: Existing approaches for synthetic tabular data generation are based on either purely generative models or LLMs, both of which struggle with data heterogeneity, logical consistency, rare-event coverage, and robustness in low-data regimes. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical hybrid top-down and bottom-up (H-TDBU) framework that decouples semantic structures from stochastic texture. In the top-down path, structure-driven logical constraints and cross-modal alignment rules are constructed, while in the bottom-up path, lightweight tabular generators are used to learn local statistical patterns from real data. The two paths are consolidated in a unified synthesis engine with an iterative feedback loop. We evaluate the framework on weak multimodal financial benchmarks combining tabular and sentiment-text data. Experimental results show that our H-TDBU approach improves train-synthetic-test-real performance over neural baseline methods while preserving semantic consistency. Our results suggest that hierarchical rule-guided synthesis provides an effective mechanism for combining controllability, semantic coherence, and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation.

</details>


### [356] [Geometry-First Generative Spatial Single-Cell Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28200)
*Ehtesamul Azim,Muhtasim Noor Alif,Tae Hyun Hwang,Yanjie Fu,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEARS is a geometry-first framework that reconstructs intrinsic 2D spatial geometry from scRNA-seq with ST guidance without requiring cell-type labels or cell-to-spot mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current integration methods rely on fixed spot grids or cell-to-spot mappings, which constrain unpaired or grid-constrained reconstructions and can misalign spatial context. GEARS aims to recover the true spatial geometry guided by ST while remaining label-free and grid-free.

Method:  GEARS learns a domain-invariant encoder to align ST spots and dissociated cells; trains a permutation-equivariant generator with a diffusion-based refiner using EDM-style preconditioning to generate local spatial geometries under pose-invariant supervision from ST coordinates; at inference, reconstructs geometry on overlapping scRNA-seq subsets, aggregates pairwise distances, and solves a global distance-geometry problem to obtain canonical 2D coordinates and a dense distance matrix.

Result: GEARS shows improved global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial distribution alignment over strong baselines, with cross-section generalization and robust performance in unpaired settings.

Conclusion: GEARS provides a scalable, grid-free, label-free geometry-reconstruction framework that integrates ST with scRNA-seq to recover meaningful spatial layouts and outperforms existing deconvolution and spot-mapping approaches.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) profiles large numbers of cells but loses spatial context, whereas spatial transcriptomics (ST) preserves partial spatial structure at lower resolution. Most existing integration methods either deconvolve spot mixtures or map cells onto a measured spot lattice, which ties reconstructions to a fixed grid and slide-specific coordinate systems, a limitation that is especially problematic in unpaired settings. We propose GEARS, a geometry-first framework that reconstructs an intrinsic single-cell spatial geometry guided by ST, without relying on cell-type labels, histological images, or cell-to-spot assignment. GEARS first learns a domain-invariant expression encoder that aligns ST spots and dissociated cells, and then trains a permutation-equivariant generator with a diffusion-based refiner with EDM-style preconditioning to generate local spatial geometries under pose-invariant supervision derived from ST coordinates. At inference, GEARS reconstructs geometry on many overlapping subsets of scRNA-seq cells, aggregates predicted pairwise distances across subsets, and solves a global distance-geometry problem to obtain canonical two-dimensional coordinates and a dense distance matrix. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments, including cross-section generalization, show that GEARS consistently improves global distance preservation, local neighborhood fidelity, and spatial distribution alignment compared to strong spatial mapping and deconvolution baselines.

</details>


### [357] [Refining Multidimensional Video Reward Models via Disentangled Influence Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28203)
*Muyao Wang,Zeke Xie,Hideki Nakayama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a dimension-disentangled framework to address dimensional heterogeneity in Multidimensional Video Reward Models for Text-to-Video, with pruning and reweighting strategies that improve ground-truth alignment over global filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global scalar filtering fails for T2V MVRMs because sample reliability varies across evaluation dimensions; dimension-specific supervision risk must be estimated to enable reliable training.

Method: Introduce a disentangled influence framework to estimate dimension-specific supervision risk, followed by two refinement strategies: (1) Dimension-Disentangled Pruning (remove extreme high-risk samples) and (2) Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting (softly down-weight high-risk supervision).

Result: Experiments show the proposed disentangled strategies outperform global filtering baselines, yielding reward models with superior alignment to ground truth.

Conclusion: Dimensional heterogeneity is a critical issue in T2V evaluation; addressing it via dimension-disentangled sampling refinements improves MVRM reliability and alignment, suggesting a new direction for training robust reward models in complex video tasks.

Abstract: As Text-to-Video (T2V) generation models continue to evolve, the complexity of video evaluation necessitates a fine-grained assessment across various axes. To address this, recent works have focused on developing Multidimensional Video Reward Models (MVRMs), which decompose the evaluation process to better align with the multifaceted nature of human visual perception. However, training effective MVRMs is fundamentally challenged by the complex nature of video data. In this work, we identify a critical phenomenon termed Dimensional Heterogeneity: the reliability of a training sample can vary substantially across evaluation dimensions, meaning that a sample may provide reliable supervision for one objective while inducing high supervision risk for another. Consequently, prevailing data-centric methods that filter based on global scalar metrics are ill-posed for T2V tasks. To address this, we propose a disentangled influence framework that that efficiently estimates dimension-specific supervision risk. Leveraging this framework, we introduce two dimension-disentangled refinement strategies: Dimension-Disentangled Pruning, which removes extreme high-risk samples, and Dimension-Disentangled Reweighting, which softly down-weights high-risk supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our disentangled strategies significantly outperform global filtering baselines, yielding reward models with superior alignment to ground truth.

</details>


### [358] [Robust Contrastive Graph Clustering with Adaptive Local-Global Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28209)
*Lei Zhang,Fubo Sun,Haipeng Yang,Zhong Guan,Likang Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a contrastive graph clustering framework that combines multi-scale local structure from multi-depth GNNs with global semantics via attention-guided prototypes; uses a dual-view objective (instance-level and structure-aware) to improve robustness and discrimination, achieving competitive results on eight real-world graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current self-supervised graph clustering struggles to flexibly model high-order local structures and global semantics, leading to suboptimal representations in fragmented or ambiguous graphs.

Method: At local level: adaptively fuse multi-depth GNN topological signals via attention to capture multi-scale neighborhoods. At global level: evolve cluster-centroid-derived prototypes and aggregate them via attention to guide node representations and enhance inter-cluster separability. Train with dual-view contrastive loss combining instance-level and structure-aware components.

Result: Empirical evaluation on eight real-world graph datasets shows competitive clustering performance; code released.

Conclusion: The proposed attention-based fusion of local and global signals within a dual-view contrastive framework improves node representations for clustering in complex graphs, validating the approach.

Abstract: Graph clustering is essential in graph analysis for revealing structural patterns and node communities. Despite recent advances in self-supervised contrastive learning that have improved clustering via structural and attribute signals, existing methods still struggle to flexibly capture high-order local structures and often overlook global semantics in complex graphs. These limitations lead to suboptimal node representations, especially in real-world graphs with fragmented structures and ambiguous cluster boundaries. To address these limitations, a contrastive graph clustering framework is proposed to jointly integrate multi-scale local structures with global semantics via attention mechanisms. At the local level, GNN-based topological signals extracted from multiple propagation depths are adaptively fused through attention-based weighting to capture multi-scale neighborhood features. At the global level, semantic prototypes derived from dynamically evolving cluster centers are adaptively aggregated through attention to guide node representations and enhance inter-cluster separability. The model is trained under a dual-view contrastive learning paradigm with a hybrid objective that combines instance-level and structure-aware losses to improve representation robustness and discrimination. Experiments on eight real-world graph datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive clustering performance. Code is available at https://github.com/vege12138/w2.

</details>


### [359] [PhAME: Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing via Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28226)
*Łukasz Janisiów,Sebastian Musiał,Bartosz Zieliński,Dawid Rymarczyk,Tomasz Danel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhAME enables phenotype-aware molecular editing by editing in a pretrained graph-VAE latent space with dual-scale classifier-free guidance, balancing phenotypic alignment and structural similarity to a seed to achieve state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simultaneously optimize multiple properties guided by high-dimensional phenotypic signatures (e.g., cell morphology, transcriptomics) while preserving proximity to a known hit, addressing limitations of existing generative methods.

Method: Latent diffusion in the latent space of a pretrained graph-based VAE; compositional classifier-free guidance with two independent scales (phenotype-conditioning and similarity-to-seed) to control the trade-off; molecular edits in latent space.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation benchmarks; maintained high chemical validity and novelty.

Conclusion: Phenotype-aware molecular editing via PhAME provides controllable, high-quality optimization that respects both target phenotypes and chemical similarity, enabling more reliable and interpretable design guided by biological signatures.

Abstract: Small-molecule drug discovery requires simultaneous optimization of numerous properties of candidate molecules. These properties can be investigated through the analysis of high-dimensional biological signatures, such as cell morphology and transcriptomic perturbations, which provide a rich perspective on the underlying biological mechanisms. However, existing generative methods, which use those signatures for optimization, fail to meet two key requirements: providing precise guidance toward desired phenotypic signatures while maintaining structural proximity to a known hit. We introduce PhAME (Phenotype-Aware Molecular Editing), a latent diffusion framework that overcomes this challenge by recasting molecular optimization as editing in the latent space of a pretrained graph-based VAE. Our central contribution is a compositional classifier-free guidance scheme with two independent scales, one for the phenotype-conditioning and one for similarity to the seed structure, allowing practitioners to control the tradeoff between these two objectives. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks, including docking score optimization and multimodal phenotypic generation, demonstrate that PhAME achieves state-of-the-art results while maintaining high chemical validity and novelty.

</details>


### [360] [IRDS: Interpretable RLVR Data Selection via Verifier-Coupled Sparse Autoencoder Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28247)
*Yuhan Li,Mingxu Zhang,Dazhong Shen,Ying Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IRDS introduces interpretable data selection for RLVR via sparse autoencoder clusters and a verifier-coupled coverage objective, optimized by greedy log-determinant maximization, achieving higher accuracy at lower cost on multiple models and math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data inefficiency in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for LLM reasoning. Existing methods fail to achieve full subset-level coverage, utilize verifier signals effectively, or offer interpretability.

Method: Train a sparse autoencoder (SAE) to derive a cluster basis; select training instances that the model struggles with but can still learn from, based on a verifier-coupled coverage objective on the SAE basis. Solve via greedy log-determinant (D-opt) maximization to ensure diverse, informative selections with auditable problem motifs.

Result: IRDS attains the highest overall accuracy among evaluated methods, improving by +3.9/+4.0 pp on two Qwen models and +0.5 pp on Llama-3.1-8B, while being an order of magnitude cheaper than trajectory-based RLVR baselines, across three instruction-tuned models and six math benchmarks.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that interpretable, motif-aware data selection combined with verifier signaling enhances RLVR data efficiency and model performance, with substantial cost reductions and auditable reasoning patterns.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a key technique for en- hancing LLM reasoning, yet its data ineffi- ciency remains a major bottleneck. Existing methods address this problem only partially, each missing at least one of subset-level cov- erage, verifier signal use, or interpretability. To address this gap, we present IRDS (Inter- pretable RLVR Data Selection), which selects RLVR training instances on a sparse autoen- coder (SAE) cluster basis so the selection itself is auditable on recognizable problem motifs. To select instances the model both fails on and can still learn from, we introduce a verifier- coupled coverage objective on the SAE basis and solve it by greedy log-determinant max- imization. Experiments on three instruction- tuned models and six math reasoning bench- marks show that IRDS achieves the highest overall accuracy, exceeding the strongest base- line by +3.9/+4.0 pp on the two Qwen models and by +0.5 pp on Llama-3.1-8B, while run- ning an order of magnitude cheaper than the trajectory-based baseline.

</details>


### [361] [Parameter-Efficient Generative Modeling with Controlled Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28267)
*Peyman Morteza*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A continuous-time generative model that builds expressive flows from a fixed set of vector fields modulated by learned scalar controls, leveraging the Chow-Rashevskii bracket-generating condition for expressivity; it provides a parameter-efficient, interpretable alternative to unconstrained vector fields and is trained via continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood on synthetic distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce parameter count and enhance interpretability of continuous flows by exploiting geometric control theory. By using a small set of fixed vector fields and learned scalars, the model achieves expressive transport without learning a high-dimensional vector field. The Chow-Rashevskii framework ensures potential reachability of the ambient space.

Method: Define velocity as a modulation of fixed vector fields by learned scalar control functions. If the fixed fields are bracket-generating, their Lie algebra spans the ambient space, enabling expressive transport with few learned channels. The design decouples the number of output channels from ambient dimension. An expressivity principle is formulated under controllability and well-posedness assumptions. Train using a continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood objective. Conduct proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions.

Result: Proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions demonstrate the model's ability to transport a source distribution to a target distribution using a small set of controlled, fixed vector fields, validating the expressivity and parameter efficiency of the approach.

Conclusion: The approach provides a structured, interpretable geometric alternative to standard vector-field parameterizations.
