With bracket-generating fixed fields and appropriate assumptions, controlled flows can transport distributions between targets, and the number of learned channels can be chosen independently of ambient dimension; this lays groundwork for further empirical evaluation and extensions.

Abstract: We introduce a continuous-time generative modeling framework, motivated by the Chow-Rashevskii theorem, that builds expressive flows from a small set of fixed vector fields and learned scalar controls. Instead of learning an unconstrained high-dimensional vector field, our framework constructs the velocity by modulating fixed vector fields with learned scalar control functions. When the fixed fields are bracket-generating, their Lie algebra spans the ambient space, providing a mechanism for expressive transport with only a small number of learned control channels and offering a parameter-efficient geometric alternative to standard vector-field parameterizations. This decoupled formulation yields a structured and interpretable generative model in which the number of learned scalar output channels can be chosen independently of the ambient dimension. We formulate an expressivity principle showing that, under suitable controllability and well-posedness assumptions, such controlled flows can transport a source distribution to a target distribution. We train the resulting model using a continuous-normalizing-flow likelihood objective and present proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic distributions.

</details>


### [362] [Dynamic Topic Modeling with a Higher-Order Hypergraphical Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28269)
*Hanjia Gao,Hanwen Ye,Qing Nie,Annie Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hypergraph-based dynamic topic model that represents documents as hyperedges over co-occurring words with repetition encoded as node weights, introducing a nonlinear normalization and a hypergraph-based multinomial likelihood. It uses structured low-rank factorization with temporal regularization to model evolving topics, with convergence guarantees and non-asymptotic error bounds, showing improvements on synthetic data and the ICLR corpus over traditional multinomial topic models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of traditional topic models that tie word occurrence and repetition into a single multinomial vector, which constrains higher-order word interactions and dynamic evolution, especially in corpora with overlapping semantics.

Method: Represent each document as a hyperedge connecting all co-occurring words; encode repetition as node weights. Derive a hypergraph-based multinomial distribution with a nonlinear normalization dependent on the observed word set. Develop a dynamic topic model using structured low-rank factorization with explicit temporal regularization on topic-word profiles, and provide convergence guarantees with non-asymptotic error bounds despite nonconvex bilinear factorization and document-specific normalization.

Result: Numerical experiments on synthetic data and the ICLR corpus show consistent improvements over existing multinomial-based topic models.

Conclusion: A hypergraph-based dynamic topic model enhances capturing higher-order word interactions and word repetition dynamics, offering theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over conventional multinomial-topic approaches.

Abstract: Dynamic topic modeling is widely used to analyze evolving trends in scientific literature, medical records, and social media. Traditional topic models represent each topic through a single probability vector on the multinomial simplex and implicitly couple word occurrence and repetition within one probabilistic mechanism. However, this formulation restricts the dependence structure among words and overlooks informative higher-order interactions, particularly in dynamic corpora with overlapping semantics. To address these limitations, we introduce a hypergraph representation of text where each document is modeled as a hyperedge connecting all co-occurring words, with repetition intensities encoded as node weights. This representation naturally separates word occurrence from repetition and induces a novel hypergraph-based multinomial distribution with a nonlinear normalization depending on the observed word set of each document. Building on this likelihood, we develop a dynamic topic modeling framework via structured low-rank factorizations with explicit temporal regularization on topic-word profiles. Moreover, we establish local convergence guarantees and derive non-asymptotic error bounds despite the intrinsic nonconvexity induced by bilinear factorization and document-specific nonlinear normalization. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and an application to the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) corpus demonstrate consistent improvements over existing multinomial-based topic models.

</details>


### [363] [Commit to the Bit: Reactive Reinforcement Learning Done Right](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28276)
*Onno Eberhard,Claire Vernade,Michael Muehlebach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Committed Q-learning for finite, partially observable environments with deterministic observations, proving almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under a weaker rewire-robustness assumption, via a variant of Q-learning with a committed action per feature and resampling only on feature changes; introduces quasi-Markov environments for analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between real-world non-Markovian environments and classical RL assuming Markov property, by enabling convergence guarantees under weaker realizability conditions and deterministic observation settings.

Method: Propose Committed Q-learning, a Q-learning variant where the behavior policy commits to a single action when entering a feature and only resamples when the observed feature changes; analyze using the concept of quasi-Markov environments and the rewire-robustness assumption.

Result: Proves almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under rewire-robustness, and characterizes the algorithm's behavior in quasi-Markov environments.

Conclusion: Extends RL theory beyond strict Markov/q*-realizability by providing convergence guarantees for reactive policies in deterministic observation settings, aided by the quasi-Markov framework and the weaker rewire-robustness condition.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms are commonly analyzed (and designed) under the Markov assumption. This is unrealistic, as most environments encountered in practice are either partially observable, or require function approximation that restricts the agent to access non-Markovian state features. We consider the problem of learning an optimal reactive policy in a finite environment with deterministic observations (or equivalently, hard state aggregation). We introduce a new algorithm, Committed Q-learning, and prove almost-sure convergence to the optimal reactive policy under an intuitive assumption we call rewire-robustness. This assumption is strictly weaker than the $q_\star$-realizability condition used in prior work. Our algorithm is a variant of classical Q-learning in which the behavior policy commits to a single action upon entering a feature, and only resamples actions when the observed feature changes. A crucial part of our analysis is the introduction of quasi-Markov environments.

</details>


### [364] [AtomComposer: Discovering Chemical Space from First Principles with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28287)
*Bjarke Hastrup,Francois Cornet,Tejs Vegge,Arghya Bhowmik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Autonomous reinforcement-learning agent (AtomComposer) discovers valid 3D isomers under stoichiometric constraints without pretraining, enabling broad generalization across formulas and outperforming single-composition baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current molecular generative models rely on curated pretraining data, which biases exploration and limits discovery of novel chemistry. There is a need for online, from-scratch exploration of vast chemical spaces.

Method: A self-guided agent constructs valid 3D molecular isomers under stoichiometric constraints, trained entirely online with reinforcement learning. It uses a multi-composition training scheme with energy- and validity-based rewards to generalize across diverse chemistry, instead of per-step energy rewards tied to a single formula.

Result: The agent discovers up to an order of magnitude more valid isomers on unseen test formulas than existing single-composition RL baselines, demonstrating scalable, from-scratch exploration of chemical configuration space.

Conclusion: Online reinforcement learning is a powerful paradigm for scalable exploration of chemical configuration space, and AtomComposer demonstrates broad generalization and effective discovery of valid molecular isomers without pretraining.

Abstract: Discovering novel stable molecules without training data remains a grand scientific challenge. Current molecular generative models are trained on large, pre-curated datasets, which introduce biases and limit exploration of novel chemistry. In contrast, we propose a new paradigm: autonomous, generalized agents capable of mapping vast, unknown chemical spaces without any pretraining. For the first time, we present AtomComposer, a self-guided agent that autonomously constructs valid 3D isomers under stoichiometric constraints and is trained exclusively online using reinforcement learning. Unlike existing approaches that generally overfit to a specific chemical formula, we establish a multi-composition training scheme that enables a broad generalization across diverse chemistry, guided by energy- and validity-based rewards. Our agent can discover up to an order of magnitude more valid isomers on unseen test formulas than existing single-composition reinforcement-learning baselines trained with per-step energy rewards. These results fulfill the promise of online reinforcement learning as a powerful paradigm for scalable, from-scratch exploration of chemical configuration space.

</details>


### [365] [Adaptive Bandit Algorithms for Contextual Matching Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28290)
*Shiyun Lin,Simon Mauras,Vianney Perchet,Nadav Merlis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces bandit learning in two-sided matching markets with contextual arms; proposes adaptive algorithms for stochastic and adversarial contexts, achieving instance-dependent poly-log regret in the stochastic case and instance-independent sublinear regret in the adversarial case, relative to stable matching benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small context shifts can drastically alter the stable matching benchmark, causing large regrets; need robust regret notions and adaptive algorithms for contextual matching markets.

Method: Two settings: stochastic contexts from latent distribution; adversarial contexts. For stochastic: define minimum preference gap; design fully adaptive algorithm; obtain instance-dependent poly-log regret upper bound; prove matching instance-independent upper and lower bounds under mild distributional assumptions. For adversarial: define tractable regret notion preserved under arbitrary contexts; develop adaptive algorithm achieving instance-independent sublinear regret.

Result: In the stochastic setting, the algorithm achieves an instance-dependent poly-logarithmic regret bound; under mild distributional assumptions, matching instance-independent upper and lower bounds are established. In the adversarial setting, the proposed notion yields instance-independent sublinear regret with an adaptive algorithm.

Conclusion: Provides rigorous regret guarantees for contextual bandit learning in matching markets across stochastic and adversarial contexts, introducing the minimum preference gap and a robust regret notion, and delivering adaptive algorithms with strong performance guarantees.

Abstract: We study bandit learning in matching markets, where players and arms constitute the two market sides, and the players' utilities are linear in the arm contexts. In each round, new arms arrive with observable contexts. Then, the algorithm matches them to players, aiming to minimize each player's regret against a stable matching benchmark. This contextual structure creates significant complexity: subtle context shifts can slightly alter one player's utility while completely reconfiguring the underlying benchmark, causing large regret spikes for others. We address this in two settings: stochastic contexts, drawn from a latent distribution, and adversarial contexts, which may be arbitrary. For the stochastic case, we introduce a novel minimum preference gap to capture learning difficulty and provide a fully adaptive algorithm with an instance-dependent poly-logarithmic regret upper bound. We also establish matching instance-independent regret upper and lower bounds under a mild distributional assumption. For the adversarial setting, we propose a tractable regret notion that remains valid under arbitrary contexts and achieves an instance-independent sublinear regret bound via an adaptive algorithm.

</details>


### [366] [ProRL: Effective Reinforcement Learning for Proactive Recommendation via Rectified Policy Gradient Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28293)
*Hongru Hou,Tiehua Mei,Denghui Geng,Jinhui Huang,Ao Xu,Hengrui Chen,Jiaqing Liang,Deqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProRL is an RL framework for proactive recommender systems that fixes gradient estimation deficiencies via Stepwise Reward Centering and Position-Specific Advantage Estimation, improving path-quality optimization and outperforming state-of-the-art PRSs on three datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two gradient estimation issues in policy-gradient-based PRSs: (i) length-dependent bias from decomposing path-level rewards into step-level rewards with positive mean, and (ii) high gradient variance from weighting steps by the full path reward despite decomposition.

Method: Introduce two mechanisms: (1) Stepwise Reward Centering to subtract expected rewards and neutralize length-dependent bias, ensuring zero expected gradient for path extension; (2) Position-Specific Advantage Estimation to compute step-dependent baselines using the reward decomposition, reducing gradient variance. These yield policy gradients that reflect true path quality.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three real-world datasets shows ProRL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art PRSs. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The proposed mechanisms produce unbiased, lower-variance policy gradients that more accurately optimize path quality in PRSs, yielding substantial empirical gains and practical release of code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Proactive Recommender Systems (PRSs) aim to guide user preference shift toward target items by generating paths of intermediate recommendations. Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for optimizing such sequential decision tasks, as path rewards can naturally capture both short-term acceptance and long-term guidance effectiveness. However, naively applying policy gradients to PRS results in deficient gradient estimation. We identify two deficiencies: (1) path-level rewards decompose into step-level rewards with positive mean, creating a length-dependent bias that causes gradients to favor path extension over meaningful exploration; (2) weighting each step by the entire path-level reward ignores the decomposition structure, leading to high gradient variance. To rectify these two deficiencies, we propose an effective RL framework ProRL with two novel mechanisms for proactive recommendation. First, Stepwise Reward Centering subtracts expected rewards to neutralize length-dependent bias, ensuring that path extension yields zero expected gradient signal. Second, Position-Specific Advantage Estimation leverages the reward decomposition structure to compute step-dependent baselines, reducing gradient variance. Together, these mechanisms yield policy gradients that precisely target path quality. Our experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that ProRL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art PRSs. Our code is available at https://github.com/hongruhou89/ProRL.

</details>


### [367] [Machine Learning methods for event classification and vertex reconstruction of the 12C + 12C reaction with the MATE-TPC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28296)
*Minghui Zhang,Xiaobin Li,Jie Chen,Ningtao Zhang,Fenhua Lu,Junrui Ma,Jiazhen Yan,Wanqin Tu,Xiaodong Tang,Bingshui Gao,Chengui Lu,Zhichao Zhang,Jinlong Zhang,Weiping Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML classification of 12C+12C reaction events in a TPC using ResNet and VGG CNNs achieving high accuracy on simulated data (≈97%) and lower on experimental data (≈90%), plus channel classification (≈95%) and a CNN-based vertex reconstruction; demonstrates ML effectiveness for event discrimination and vertex reconstruction in complex nuclear reaction data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of identifying specific nuclear reaction events in an active-target TPC data stream, where traditional methods struggle with complex, high-dimensional data and multiple reaction channels.

Method: Apply deep CNN architectures (ResNet-50, ResNet-34, ResNet-18, and VGG-19) to classify elastic scattering versus fusion events in 12C+12C data from MATE (trained on simulated data and tested on experimental data); extend to classify events from different fusion channels (simulation); develop a CNN model for reaction-vertex reconstruction.

Result: Classification accuracies: ~97% on simulated data and ~90% on experimental data for elastic vs fusion; ML models identify events misclassified by traditional methods; cross-channel fusion classification ~95% accuracy on simulated data; CNN-based vertex reconstruction offers an alternative strategy.

Conclusion: ML techniques can effectively classify events from different reaction channels and reconstruct the reaction vertex in complex nuclear reaction data, enabling more robust analyses and paving the way for future studies in nuclear reaction data analysis.

Abstract: In modern nuclear physics experiments, identifying events of interest is challenging for nuclear reaction studies with the active target Time Projection Chamber (TPC). In this work, machine learning techniques are employed to analyze the complex data of the 12C + 12C fusion reaction from a TPC named MATE (multi-purpose active-target time projection chamber for nuclear experiments). Specifically, we successfully applied Residual Neural Network (ResNet-50, ResNet-34 and ResNet-18) and Visual Geometry Group (VGG-19) to classify elastic scattering and fusion reaction events from the 12C + 12C reaction. The classification results of the four models are nearly identical, with accuracies of approximately 97% for the simulated data and 90% for the experimental data. Moreover, these approaches successfully identify some events that are misclassified by traditional methods. These models are also applied to classify events from different fusion reaction channels, with classification accuracies of approximately 95% on simulated data. In addition, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model is developed to reconstruct the reaction vertex, providing an alternative strategy for vertex reconstruction. These results indicate that machine learning techniques can effectively classify reaction events from different channels and reconstruct the reaction vertex, thereby paving the way for future analyses of complex nuclear reaction data.

</details>


### [368] [T-GINEE: A Tensor-Based Multilayer Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28300)
*Maolin Wang,Ziting Mai,Xuhui Chen,Zhiqi Li,Tianshuo Wei,Yutian Xiao,Wenlin Zhang,Wanyu Wang,Ruocheng Guo,Haoxuan Li,Zenglin Xu,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tensor-based regularization framework (T-GINEE) for multilayer networks that explicitly models cross-layer correlations using CP decomposition and generalized estimating equations, with a flexible link function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional single-layer analyses fail to capture inter-layer dependencies in multilayer networks; there is a need to explicitly model cross-network correlations rather than treating layers independently or aggregating them.

Method: Use CP tensor decomposition to capture shared latent factors across layers; integrate generalized estimating equations to model inter-layer correlations via working covariance matrices; employ a flexible link function to handle sparsity and other characteristics; optimize with a task-specific loss plus regularization.

Result: Proves consistency and asymptotic normality under mild conditions; empirical validation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows improved performance for multilayer network analysis.

Conclusion: T-GINEE provides a theoretically sound and empirically effective framework for analyzing multilayer networks by jointly modeling cross-layer dependencies and network structure.

Abstract: Traditional network analysis focuses on single-layer networks, real-world systems often form multilayer networks with multiple relationship types. However, existing methods typically fail to capture complex inter-layer dependencies by treating layers independently or aggregating them. To address this, we propose T-GINEE (Tensor-Based Generalized Multilayer-graph Estimating Equation), a statistical regularization framework combining tensor-based generalized estimating equations with task-specific loss to model cross-network correlations explicitly. Key innovations include: (1) CP tensor decomposition capturing structural dependencies via shared latent factors; (2) a generalized estimating equation framework modeling inter-layer correlations through working covariance matrices; and (3) a flexible link function accommodating characteristics like sparsity. Our theoretical analysis establishes consistency and asymptotic normality under mild conditions. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate T-GINEE's effectiveness for multilayer network analysis.

</details>


### [369] [How Far Can Disaggregation Go? A Design-Space Exploration of Attention-FFN Disaggregation for Efficient MoE LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28302)
*Hanjiang Wu,Abhimanyu Rajeshkumar Bambhaniya,Sarbartha Banerjee,Tuhin Khare,Sudarshan Srinivasan,Suvinay Subramanian,Souvik Kundu,Madhu Kumar,Midhilesh Elavazhagan,William Won,Amir Yazdanbakhsh,Tushar Krishna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Disaggregation levels for MoE inference (chunked-prefill, P/D, Attention-FFN Disaggregation) are systematically evaluated; AFD enables feasible high-throughput MoE inference under strict latency objectives, achieving ~4k tokens/s on DeepSeek-V3.2, with non-AFD approaches infeasible under those constraints; the work provides practical guidelines for partitioning attention and FFN across GPUs and insights for rack- and cluster-scale disaggregated AI infra.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing model sizes and stringent TTFT/TPOT SLOs necessitate understanding when every level of disaggregation is worth the added scheduling complexity, especially for mixture-of-experts where heterogeneous bottlenecks (memory-bound attention, compute-heavy FFNs, and MoE dispatch/communication) interact across hardware.

Method: Systematic characterization of MoE inference across workloads using chunked-prefill and P/D baselines, plus an Attention-FFN Disaggregation framework. The evaluation fuses on-device kernel measurements with high-fidelity network simulation to study throughput and latency trade-offs across input/output lengths, prefix-KV reuse, and per-user latency constraints. Experiments on DeepSeek-V3.2 simulate real workloads (chat, coding, agentic-coding) to assess scalability and feasibility.

Result: AFD sustains ~4k tokens/s system throughput under strict TTFT/TPOT SLOs; non-AFD deployments are infeasible under these SLOs. The study yields concrete takeaways for jointly optimizing throughput and interactivity and specifies how to partition attention and FFN across GPUs as a function of workload and model architecture.

Conclusion: Disaggregation depth should be matched to workload characteristics and model architecture. AFD offers tangible throughput benefits under tight latency constraints, and the paper provides design principles for partitioning attention and FFN across GPUs, with guidance applicable to current rack- and cluster-scale deployments and future disaggregated AI infrastructure.

Abstract: Modern large language model (LLM) inference has progressively disaggregated to keep pace with growing model sizes and tight TTFT and TPOT service-level objectives: from chunked-prefill aggregation, to prefill-decode (P/D) disaggregation, and most recently to operator-level Attention-FFN Disaggregation (AFD). This trend is especially important for mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, where memory-bound attention, compute-intensive expert FFNs, and MoE dispatch/combine communication create distinct resource demands. AFD further exposes this heterogeneity by placing attention and MoE-FFN execution on separate GPU groups. Each level of disaggregation deepens the scheduling design space across workload characteristics, resource allocation, and interconnect topology, raising the central question: when does each level actually pay off? We systematically characterize this trade-off for MoE inference across realistic workloads spanning input/output sequence lengths, prefix-KV reuse, and per-user latency constraints. Using chunked-prefill and P/D disaggregation as baselines, we study the benefits and limits of AFD at scale through a framework that fuses on-device kernel measurements with high-fidelity network simulation. Under strict TTFT/TPOT SLOs, AFD sustains around 4k tokens/s of system throughput on DeepSeek-V3.2 across chat, coding, and agentic-coding workloads, where non-AFD deployments are infeasible. We distill concrete takeaways for jointly optimizing throughput and interactivity, including how to partition attention and FFN across GPUs as a function of workload and model architecture, providing design principles for current rack- and cluster-scale deployments as well as future disaggregated AI infrastructure.

</details>


### [370] [Compositional Generalization in Autoregressive Models via Logit Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28304)
*Aakash Kumar,Maria Sofia Bucarelli,Emanuele Natale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled composition framework for autoregressive models that ensures component control over disjoint output subspaces, is robust to smooth output-space transforms, and preserves length-generalization under uniform factorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive models must merge skills across tasks without interference; existing heuristics lack theoretical guarantees. The work draws on diffusion-model composition to establish principled conditions for safe composition.

Method: Impose a factorized-conditionals assumption and define a composition that preserves each component's designated subspace; prove projectivity; extend results to smooth reparameterizations of the output space (feature-space theorem); analyze length-generalization under uniform factorization guarantees at the target length.

Result: Under the factorized-conditionals assumption, the composed model is projective (no cross-model interference in each component's subspace); this property persists under smooth reparameterizations of the output space (feature-space theorem); composition preserves length-generalization when factorization assumptions and component guarantees hold uniformly at the target length.

Conclusion: Provides principled criteria for when composing or merging autoregressive models will be stable and effective, identifying conditions under which interactions between components remain well-behaved.

Abstract: Composing autoregressive models remains a core challenge in understanding how large language models can combine behaviors or skills learned across tasks. We introduce a new and principled composition strategy for autoregressive systems, inspired by composition methods developed for diffusion models. Under a factorized-conditionals assumption, we show that the resulting composition is projective: each component model preserves control over its own designated subspace of the output distribution avoiding interference between models. This property is further preserved under smooth reparameterizations of the output space, yielding a feature-space theorem. Finally, we show that composition preserves length-generalizing behavior when the factorization assumptions and component guarantees hold uniformly at the target length. These results provide a principled understanding of when model composition and merging succeed in autoregressive systems and identify conditions under which their interactions remain stable.

</details>


### [371] [Learning to Assess the Reliability of Number-of-Runs Estimation in Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28309)
*Sara Gjorgjieva,Eva Tuba,Tome Eftimov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learning-based prediction can determine when run-number estimates in large-scale stochastic optimization benchmarking are reliable, using 132k Nevergrad-COCO runs; achieves high minority-class recall within-configuration, but is limited by data diversity across configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce unnecessary computational cost by stopping data collection once sufficient evidence of reliability is obtained, while avoiding incorrect reliability estimates.

Method: Train classifiers on 23 features (statistical, energy-free, shape and stability) using annotated outcomes from 132k Nevergrad runs on COCO; evaluation is within-configuration: train and test on data sharing the same optimizer; optimize for minority-class recall to detect unreliable estimates.

Result: Run-number reliability can be learned within-configuration, enabling detection of unreliable estimates with high minority-class recall; overall performance limited by restricted data diversity within fixed configurations.

Conclusion: Learning-based reliability prediction is feasible in this setting but generalization is constrained by configuration-specific data diversity; broader cross-configuration data may be required for robust, generalizable predictions.

Abstract: In large-scale benchmarking of stochastic optimization algorithms, the key challenge is no longer whether repeated runs are needed for reliability, but how to determine when sufficient evidence has been collected without incurring unnecessary computational cost. We study a learning-based extension of a recent empirical online heuristic that adaptively estimates the required number of runs using outlier handling and skewness-based symmetry checks. Using annotated outcomes from 132{,}000 Nevergrad runs on COCO (24 problems in 20 dimensions, 10 instances each, 11 optimizers), we train classifiers on 23 statistical, energy-free, and shape and stability features to predict whether a run-number estimate is reliable, prioritizing detection of incorrect estimates via minority-class recall. We evaluate reliability prediction using a within-configuration learning setup, where models are trained and tested on data sharing the same optimizer. The results show that run-number reliability can be learned in a within-configuration scenario, enabling detection of unreliable estimates with high minority-class recall, although performance remains limited by the restricted data diversity within fixed configurations.

</details>


### [372] [Hybrid Neural World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28317)
*Pranav Lakshmanan,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single horizon-conditioned neural surrogate predicts any future state in one forward pass, with an implicit per-trajectory error map that detects shocks, fronts, and contacts; provides large speedups and robust gating across multiple physical dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural surrogates speed up physical dynamics but fail silently at sharp dynamical events (shocks, fronts, contacts). A robust, calibration-free surrogate is needed that encodes discontinuities without explicit supervision.

Method: Train one network with continuous horizon conditioning to predict state at horizon T directly from current state; supervise against textbook reference solvers; no explicit supervision of discontinuity location; derive an error map from forward passes that highlights discontinuities; two operating modes: Mode 1 surrogate-only for maximum throughput; Mode 2 gating the surrogate with a reference solver to defer uncertain trajectories; no calibration set or governing-equation knowledge required; applicable across reaction-diffusion, compressible Euler, and rigid-body collisions.

Result: Mode 1 achieves 26x–72x CPU speedups over textbook solvers on PDE environments; the per-trajectory error map is competitive with or better than baselines such as deep ensembles, learned error heads, gradient-magnitude indicators, and locally-adaptive conformal prediction, while using a single network and no calibration data; Mode 2 uses the error map to gate a reference solver, halving the surrogate’s residual error at the default operating point; the approach generalizes across the listed dynamics without modification.

Conclusion: The paper presents a practical recipe for training and deploying multi-horizon neural surrogates that implicitly encode discontinuities via a per-trajectory error map, enabling high-throughput predictions with robust uncertainty gating across diverse physical dynamics without requiring calibration data or governing equations.

Abstract: Neural surrogates promise large speedups over classical solvers for physical dynamics but fail silently at sharp dynamical events such as shocks, fronts, and contact. We present hybrid neural world models for physical dynamics: a recipe for training and deploying multi-horizon surrogates in physical state space, where a single network with continuous horizon conditioning is trained with direct supervision against textbook reference solvers to predict any future state at horizon T in one forward pass. Although no part of the training data, loss function, or architecture supervises discontinuity location, the trained surrogate encodes it implicitly, recoverable from its forward passes alone as a per-trajectory error map that concentrates on shocks, fronts, and contacts, and stays small elsewhere. The map is competitive with or better than standard label-free baselines including deep ensembles, learned error heads, gradient-magnitude indicators, and locally-adaptive conformal prediction, while using only a single trained network and requiring no calibration set or governing-equation knowledge. The recipe supports two operating points. Mode 1 runs the surrogate alone for maximum throughput, with same-hardware CPU speedups of 26x to 72x against textbook solvers on the PDE environments. Mode 2 uses the error map to gate a reference-solver fallback, deferring uncertain trajectories and roughly halving the surrogate's residual error at the default operating point. The recipe applies without modification across reaction-diffusion, compressible Euler, and rigid-body collision dynamics.

</details>


### [373] [Learning the Error Patterns of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28328)
*Jinwoo Kim,Taylor Berg-KirkPatrick,Loris D'Antoni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learn domain- and model-specific error patterns as 'prefix filters' and optimize their learning with the Palla algorithm to constrain LLM output, improving domain validity (e.g., compile success) with a sizable boost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often produce small, targeted errors when operating within domain validity constraints. A compact, learnable representation of these errors enables both analysis and constraint-based generation.

Method: Introduce per-domain-and-LLM symbolic 'prefix filters' to capture error patterns. Propose Palla, an efficient algorithm to learn these filters in practice, and implement constrained sampling to enforce them during generation.

Result: Prefix filters learned by Palla enable quantitative analysis of LLM error patterns and, when used in constrained sampling, significantly improve task performance. For TypeScript generation, Palla boosts compile rates for Qwen2.5-1.5B by over 60%, matching the unconstrained performance of a larger model (Llama3.1-8B).

Conclusion: Prefix filters plus the Palla learning algorithm provide a scalable approach to diagnosing and mitigating domain-specific generation errors in LLMs, with practical gains in domain validity and competitive performance relative to larger models.

Abstract: When generating outputs for domains with specific validity constraints (e.g., a program should compile), LLMs often fail in a small number of focused ways: for example, by using Python function names when generating TypeScript. We observe that these error patterns can be represented using a small number of constraints that can be learned in practice. We propose \emph{prefix filters}, which are per-domain-and-LLM symbolic functions, as objects to capture the error patterns, Palla as an algorithm to learn prefix filters efficiently in practice, and implement Palla. Prefix filters learned by Palla i) help us quantitatively analyze the error patterns of LLMs, and ii) can be used to constrain the outputs of a model via constrained sampling algorithms. For example, Palla boosts compile rates for Qwen2.5-1.5B on TypeScript generation, by over 60%, allowing Qwen2.5-1.5B to achieve similar performance to Llama3.1-8B unconstrained.

</details>


### [374] [Dimensionality Reduction for Robust Federated Learning: A Theoretical Analysis and Convergence Guarantee](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28335)
*Shiyuan Zuo,Jiashuo Li,Rongfei Fan,Han Hu,Jie Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PDR accelerates robust, byzantine-resilient FL by compressing gradients with sparse random projections, achieving O(Mp) server complexity and preserving convergence under standard FL assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning with Byzantine-robust aggregation incurs high computational cost on high-dimensional gradients. A fast, scalable solution is needed that preserves convergence guarantees.

Method: Apply sparse random projection to compress gradients into a small subspace, enabling efficient computation of reliability weights for vector-level distance-based robust aggregators; integrate with existing aggregators; leverage Subspace Embedding Theorem to preserve essential information.

Result: Achieves an optimal server-side complexity of O(Mp), matching the data-reading lower bound; retains known convergence rates—O(1/√T) for non-convex and O(1/T) for strongly convex functions; Byzantiness error floor inflates by factor (1+ε)/(1−ε); experimental results show substantial time-speedups with competitive convergence.

Conclusion: PDR provides a universal, near-free acceleration for robust FL aggregators by dimensionality reduction, maintaining convergence guarantees while significantly reducing computation for large models; the slight increase in the Byzantine error floor is tunable and predictable.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train models without sharing raw data, but it is highly vulnerable to Byzantine attacks. Existing robust approaches can neutralize these threats but incur substantial computational overhead during high-dimensional gradient aggregation, an overhead that scales poorly with model size and increasingly dominates the training cost as modern models grow larger. To address this computational bottleneck, we propose Projected Dimensionality Reduction (PDR), a universal acceleration framework for vector-level distance-based robust aggregators, which performs robust aggregation by compressing gradients into a drastically smaller subspace via sparse random projection to efficiently compute reliability weights. This approach reduces the server computational complexity to an optimal $ \mathcal{O}(Mp) $, where $ M $ is the number of clients and $ p $ is the model dimension, matching the theoretical lower bound required merely to read the gradients. We establish convergence guarantees under standard FL assumptions in prior Byzantine-robust FL analyses. By leveraging the Subspace Embedding Theorem, we show that PDR achieves optimal convergence rates of $ \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T}) $ for non-convex functions and $ \mathcal{O}(1/T) $ for strongly convex functions, where $ T $ denotes the number of iterations. Crucially, we mathematically demonstrate that this massive acceleration comes almost for free, merely inflating the inherent Byzantine error floor by a bounded, tunable factor of $ \frac{1+ε}{1-ε} $. Experimental results on benchmark datasets confirm that integrating PDR with existing aggregators yields orders of magnitude speedups in time efficiency while maintaining highly competitive convergence performance.

</details>


### [375] [Detecting Diffusion-Generated Time Series Under Generator Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28355)
*Zhi Wen Soi,Aditya Shankar,Gert Lek,Abele Mălan,Daniel Neider,Jian-Jia Chen,Lydia Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A black-box classifier on raw time-series signals outperforms a white-box reconstruction-based detector in distinguishing diffusion-generated time series, challenging the assumption that image-domain detection methods transfer to time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As diffusion-generated time series become harder to distinguish from real data, and white-box detectors degrade under generator shift, there is a need to systematically compare white-box and black-box detection approaches and quantify their performance.

Method: Systematic comparison between a white-box, reconstruction-based detector adapted from the image domain, and a black-box classifier operating on the raw time-series signal; evaluation in-distribution and under generator shift; report F1 and TPR@FPR metrics.

Result: The black-box detector achieves an average F1 of 79.2 and a TPR@1%FPR of 57.2, representing a 22.1% relative improvement over the white-box detector, which fails under generator shift.

Conclusion: Diffusion-generated time-series detection does not trivially transfer from the image domain; the study highlights the need for tailored detection strategies and identifies open directions for future work.

Abstract: The boundary between real and diffusion-generated time series is becoming increasingly difficult to draw, yet detection in this domain remains underexplored, especially when the generator is unknown. We compare white-box detection, which requires access to the generator, against black-box detection, which operates on the raw signal alone. The white-box approach, a reconstruction-based detector adapted from the image domain, works well in in-distribution but breaks down under generator shift: reconstruction-based detection in images succeeds because large generic generators provide a near-universal reconstruction prior, and no analogous generator exists for time series. In contrast, a simple off-the-shelf classifier used as a black-box detector performs remarkably well, achieving an average F1 of 79.2, a 22.1% relative improvement over the white-box approach, and a TPR@1%FPR of 57.2. Diffusion-generated time series detection is therefore not a direct transfer of the image domain problem. This work provides the first systematic exploration of white-box and black-box detection for diffusion-generated time series. We close by identifying several open and promising directions.

</details>


### [376] [Score Based Error Correcting Code Decoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28358)
*Alon Helvits,Eliya Nachmani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A score-based, continuous-time denoiser decodes codes via a probability-flow ODE, achieving strong BER across 42 code/SNR settings with modest SNR gains and faster decoding when using a DPM solver instead of Euler.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft decoding remains challenging across code families and block lengths; a scalable, SNR-agnostic method enabling low-latency, accurate decoding is needed.

Method: Train a neural denoiser that defines a probability-flow ODE updating the noisy channel observation toward a valid codeword under parity constraints. The model uses raw signed channel observations as input, is trained across noise levels without SNR conditioning, and allows an inference-time latency-accuracy trade-off via the ODE solver budget. Compare solvers (Euler vs DPM).

Result: SB-ECC achieves best BER in 39/42 code/SNR settings, with an average SNR gain of 0.17 dB and a maximum gain of 0.46 dB over the strongest baseline. Switching the solver from Euler to DPM preserves -ln(BER) while reducing end-to-end decoding time by 8.86% on average (up to 12.82%).

Conclusion: A robust, SNR-agnostic score-based denoising approach yields near-state-of-the-art BER across diverse settings and enables a tunable latency-accuracy trade-off; solver choice can significantly impact runtime without BER degradation.

Abstract: Error-correcting codes enable reliable communication, yet practical soft decoding remains challenging across code families and block lengths. We propose SB-ECC, a score-based decoder that casts decoding as continuous-time denoising. A neural denoiser defines a probability-flow ordinary differential equation (ODE) that iteratively updates the noisy channel observation toward a valid codeword, guided by parity constraints. The model is trained across noise levels without time/SNR conditioning, enabling inference without SNR estimation and supporting a direct latency accuracy trade off controlled by the ODE solver budget. We use the raw signed channel observation as input for learning a continuous denoising field. Across 42 code/SNR settings, SB-ECC achieves the best BER in 39/42 entries, with an average SNR gain of 0.17dB and a maximum gain of 0.46dB over the strongest competing baseline, we showed that swapping the solver from Euler to DPM preserves -ln(BER) while reducing end-to-end decoding time by 8.86% on average (up to 12.82%).

</details>


### [377] [LEIA: Learned Environment for Interactive Architected Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28368)
*Haiqian Yang,Yuan Cao,Markus J. Buehler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEIA is a learned world model for interactive architected materials, enabling real-time deformation and stress predictions on large 3D meshes and supporting rapid, FE-ground-truth–validated candidate design via a MicroPlate benchmark with explicit and implicit microstructure regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable engineers to interactively explore material behavior with accurate, history-dependent, multiscale constitutive dynamics beyond existing slow or simplified models.

Method: Introduce LEIA, a autoregressive world model that handles large unstructured meshes and user-specified loading; develop MicroPlate benchmark with two regimes: explicit microstructure (3D geometry) and implicit internal DOF homogenization; compare LEIA to four baselines; demonstrate surrogate-guided de novo design ranking with FE validation.

Result: LEIA delivers real-time deformation and stress-field predictions under boundary conditions; supports efficient candidate generation and ranking for materials design; FE ground truth validates the stress rankings; MicroPlate provides a rigorous testbed across regimes.

Conclusion: LEIA (i) enables interactive engineering workflows for architected materials, (ii) supports large-scale, multiscale, nonlinear material modeling in real time, and (iii) offers a benchmark (MicroPlate) for evaluating modeling fidelity across explicit and implicit microstructure regimes, paving the way for fast, surrogate-driven material design.

Abstract: World models have enabled interactive exploration of game environments and robotic manipulation, but physical engineering remains beyond their reach: real materials exhibit nonlinear constitutive laws, carry history-dependent internal state, undergo inertial dynamics, and may possess hierarchical structures spanning multiple length scales. We present LEIA (Learned Environment for Interactive Architected materials), a world model that lets engineers apply boundary conditions step by step and observe the resulting deformation and stress fields in real time. LEIA handles large three-dimensional unstructured meshes and generates autoregressive responses to user-specified loading. We introduce MicroPlate, a benchmark of architected plates spanning two regimes of microstructure modeling: architected lattices that resolve microstructure explicitly through three-dimensional geometry, and a homogeneous plate where microstructural change is modeled implicitly through internal degrees of freedom. MicroPlate is used to assess LEIA alongside four baseline methods across both regimes. Finally, we demonstrate that LEIA enables efficient candidate generation and ranking for fast surrogate-guided search for de novo designs of architected materials, with stress-accurate candidate ranking validated by finite element ground truth.

</details>


### [378] [Meta-Attention: Bayesian Per-Token Routing for Efficient Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28384)
*Alan Ferrari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bayesian meta-attention that dynamically routes tokens to full softmax, linear, or local attention via a compute-aware Dirichlet prior; Phase 1 results show substantial compute savings and reduced routing entropy, avoiding routing collapse with a principled ELBO-based training objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of a single uniform attention mechanism across tokens by enabling per-token selection of attention strategy that adapts to local context and compute budget, while avoiding ad-hoc routing regularizers and routing collapse.

Method: Introduce Meta-Attention with a Bayesian Meta-Controller. Per-token mechanism selection is treated as posterior inference under a compute-aware Dirichlet prior. The routing weights come from an amortised variational posterior q(alpha | x_t; phi) trained with an ELBO objective that balances task performance and attention-cost. Phase-1 experiments on a Tiny LM benchmark validate forward-pass correctness, posterior diversity, and ablate against prior-free baselines, with PyTorch prototype provided.

Result: The approach yields a projected FLOP cost of 25.1% under hard routing vs. 59.3% for the prior-free baseline (-34.2 pp). Routing entropy decreases from 55.8% to 43.3% (-12.5 pp), indicating reduced routing collapse. Phase-1 results confirm the Dirichlet prior stabilizes routing, while the non-Bayesian model defaults to full attention. Codebase available at the given link, and a Phase-1 prototype validates core properties.

Conclusion: A principled Bayesian routing mechanism yields better compute-performance trade-offs than deterministic or prior-free routing, with minimal overhead and explicit uncertainty driving the soft-to-hard routing transition. The Dirichlet prior mitigates routing collapse and provides a scalable path for adaptive attention in transformers.

Abstract: Standard transformer architectures apply a single attention mechanism uniformly across all tokens and sequence positions, irrespective of local context or computational budget. We propose Meta-Attention, a framework that dynamically routes each token to the most appropriate attention strategy -- full softmax attention, linear (kernel) attention, or sliding-window local attention -- via a Bayesian Meta-Controller. Unlike prior routing approaches that use deterministic or prior-free learned routing, the Meta-Controller treats per-token mechanism selection as posterior inference under a compute-aware Dirichlet prior: routing weights are the output of an amortised variational posterior q(alpha | x_t; phi) trained with an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) objective that jointly encodes task performance and attention-mechanism cost. This design produces principled routing uncertainty estimates that govern the soft-to-hard routing transition, mitigates routing collapse without ad hoc load-balancing losses, and yields better compute-performance trade-offs than deterministic or prior-free learned routing at negligible overhead. Phase 1 empirical results on a Tiny LM benchmark confirm core predictions: the Bayesian controller's learned routing distribution implies a projected normalised FLOP cost of 25.1% under hard routing, vs. 59.3% for the prior-free baseline (-34.2 pp), and reduces routing entropy from 55.8% to 43.3% (-12.5 pp), demonstrating that the Dirichlet prior prevents routing collapse while the non-Bayesian model defaults to full attention. We present the Bayesian architecture, ELBO training objective, and a Phase 1 PyTorch prototype validating forward-pass correctness, posterior diversity, and a controlled ablation against a prior-free baseline. Code available at: https://github.com/KFEAL/meta-attention

</details>


### [379] [CLANE: Continual Learning of Actions on Neuromorphic Hardware from Event Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28387)
*Elvin Hajizada,Michael Neumeier,Edward Paxon Frady,Yulia Sandamirskaya,Axel von Arnim,Bing Li,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: End-to-end continual on-device action recognition for event cameras on neuromorphic hardware (Loihi 2). CLANE combines a spiking CNN with a learning head (CLP-SNN) and novel on-chip modules to achieve 70.4% continual-learning accuracy on THU E-ACT-50, with >100x energy reduction and 16x lower latency vs a CNN+GRU+CLP GPU baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable private, low-latency adaptation for AR/VR and robotics using event-based sensing and neuromorphic hardware. Prior work lacked an end-to-end, on-device continual learning pipeline for event-based action recognition on neuromorphic platforms.

Method: Developed CLANE: a spiking 2D CNN for spatiotemporal feature extraction on Loihi 2, with an on-chip CLP-SNN learning head. Augmented to action clips via a Temporal Aggregation Layer and a Fixed-point Normalization Layer (novel Loihi 2 modules). Trained/evaluated on THU E-ACT-50 (50-class real-world dataset). Performed iso-algorithm cross-platform benchmarking across three evaluation levels, comparing against a sequential CNN+GRU+CLP edge GPU baseline.

Result: Achieved 70.4% accuracy in continual learning on the 50-class dataset. Demonstrated energy efficiency >100× and latency reduction of ~16× compared to the GPU baseline, validated through cross-platform benchmarking across three levels.

Conclusion:  Shows the feasibility and benefits of end-to-end continual on-device learning for event-based action recognition on neuromorphic hardware. The approach yields substantial energy and latency gains and preserves privacy by processing on-device. Future work could explore scalability, robustness, and comparisons with additional baselines and datasets.

Abstract: Recognizing and continuously learning novel human actions without forgetting prior classes is a requirement for emerging AR/VR and robotics applications. For these applications, both on-device processing and learning are essential for privacy and low-latency adaptation. Event cameras address the efficiency of visual sensing with sparse, asynchronous output that is naturally compatible with neuromorphic processing. Yet no prior system has deployed a continual on-device learning pipeline for event-based action recognition using neuromorphic hardware. We present CLANE, Continual Learning of Actions on Neuromorphic Hardware from Event Cameras, deployed end-to-end on Intel Loihi 2. CLANE combines a spiking 2D CNN for spatiotemporal feature extraction with CLP-SNN as its on-chip learning head, extended to action clips via a Temporal Aggregation Layer and a fixed-point Normalization Layer, both novel Loihi 2 modules. On THU E-ACT-50, a 50-class dataset captured under real-world conditions, CLANE achieves 70.4% accuracy in a continual learning task while delivering more than 100x energy reduction and 16x lower latency over a sequential CNN+GRU+CLP edge GPU baseline, validated through iso-algorithm cross-platform benchmarking across three evaluation levels.

</details>


### [380] [ADWIN: Adaptive Windows for Horizon-Aware On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28396)
*Kun Liang,Chenming Tang,Clive Bai,Weijie Liu,Saiyong Yang,Yunfang Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADWIN adapts rollout horizon in on-policy distillation by training on short teacher-anchored prefixes and auditing with delayed full-rollout probes, achieving similar/better accuracy at up to 4.1x training cost reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the inefficiency of full-rollout supervised updates in on-policy distillation by avoiding over-allocating supervision to late, low-marginal-value positions and by aligning training with long-horizon directions.

Method: Introduce an adaptive-window framework that treats rollout length as an online decision. Train on short teacher-anchored prefixes; periodically perform delayed full-rollout probes to audit prefix–full alignment and adjust the next horizon with staleness control.

Result: Across math and code reasoning benchmarks (single-task, multi-task, strong-to-weak), ADWIN achieves comparable or better accuracy with up to 4.1× reduction in end-to-end training cost compared to full-rollout OPD and prefix baselines.

Conclusion: ADWIN offers a dynamic, horizon-aware OPD framework that maintains long-horizon alignment while cutting unnecessary supervision, achieving a favorable accuracy–compute trade-off.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) transfers reasoning behavior by training a student on teacher feedback along student-generated trajectories, but standard full-rollout training ties every update to a costly completion and can over-allocate supervision to late positions with low marginal value for the current student. We revisit this assumption through the useful supervision horizon: student-induced rollouts can drift from teacher-preferred continuations, while aligned prefixes may already preserve the long-horizon OPD update direction. We propose ADWIN, an adaptive-window framework for OPD that treats rollout length as an online admissibility decision, training on short teacher-anchored prefixes while using delayed full-rollout probes to audit prefix--full alignment and adapt the next horizon with staleness control. Across math and code reasoning benchmarks in single-task, multi-task, and strong-to-weak settings, ADWIN improves the accuracy--compute trade-off over full-rollout OPD and prefix-based baselines, reducing end-to-end training cost by up to 4.1 times while achieving comparable or better accuracy.

</details>


### [381] [Revisiting Metafeatures to Explain Model Differences on Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28418)
*Markus Herre,Andrej Tschalzev,Sascha Marton,Christian Bartelt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Global dataset meta-features do not robustly explain performance gaps among tabular model families on TabArena's 51 datasets; meta-features underperform baseline in leave-one-dataset-out evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether dataset meta-features can account for performance differences between model families (NN vs tree; non-foundation vs foundation; TabICLv2 vs TabPFN-2.6) on tabular prediction tasks using TabArena results.

Method: Compute dataset-level performance gaps from TabArena; relate to model-agnostic descriptors; apply false discovery rate controlled statistics; conduct leave-one-dataset-out predictive analysis; compare meta-feature predictors against a baseline.

Result: (1) For neural network vs. tree gaps, no meta-feature survives false discovery control. (2) For non-foundation vs. foundation gaps, one association is robust but does not generalize in leave-one-dataset-out prediction. (3) For TabICLv2 vs. TabPFN-2.6, one robust association also improves held-out prediction. Leave-one-dataset-out analysis shows meta-feature predictors fail to meaningfully outperform a simple baseline. Overall, meta-features fail to robustly explain performance gaps across 51 TabArena datasets.

Conclusion: Dataset heterogeneity is high and global meta-feature approaches are not robust explanations for cross-model performance gaps on TabArena; meta-features are insufficient for reliable model selection guidance on tabular data.

Abstract: With the rise of tabular foundation models alongside traditional models still performing well on many tasks, choosing the right model for a tabular dataset remains difficult. We investigate whether dataset meta-features can explain performance gaps between model families on tabular prediction tasks. Using the TabArena benchmark results, we analyze dataset-level performance gaps and relate them to model-agnostic dataset descriptors. After strict statistical tests with false discovery control, we find that (1) for neural network vs. tree gaps, no meta-feature survives false discovery control, (2) for non-foundation vs. foundation model gaps, one association is robust but does not generalize when tested in leave-one-dataset-out prediction, and (3) for TabICLv2 vs. TabPFN-2.6, one robust association also improves held-out prediction. Furthermore, we conduct a leave-one-dataset-out analysis and find that meta-feature predictors fail to improve meaningfully over a simple baseline. Overall, our results show the heterogeneity of tabular datasets and that global meta-feature approaches are not robust enough to offer explanations on the 51 TabArena datasets.

</details>


### [382] [Conveyance: A Versatile Framework for Learning in Structured Class Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28420)
*Yasser Taha,Grégoire Montavon,Nils Körber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Conveyance, a loss function for structured class spaces that leverages graph-like class relations without requiring explicit joint distributions or utility matrices, achieving competitive results across hierarchical classification, ordinal regression, and multiple instance learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cross-entropy is structure-agnostic and cannot exploit relationships between classes, especially under structured noise; a loss that encodes class relations without manual joint distributions is desirable.

Method: Propose Conveyance that maximizes two margins over distinct class partitions while preserving monotonicity and partial convexity; enables encoding graph-like class relations and is applicable to hierarchical classification, ordinal regression, and multiple instance learning.

Result: Empirically matches or surpasses specialized baselines across the evaluated tasks, providing a unified solution for structured class spaces.

Conclusion: Conveyance offers a versatile, unified framework for structured class spaces, reducing the need for joint distributions or manually tuned utility matrices while maintaining favorable optimization properties.

Abstract: While machine learning (ML) architectures have evolved rapidly to account for complex data, loss functions like cross-entropy remain mostly structure-agnostic in many real-world applications. However, the `class-symmetric' nature of these standard losses fundamentally limits the ability of ML models to exploit structural relationships between classes, particularly when facing structured noise. We propose \textsc{Conveyance}, a new classification approach and associated loss function tailored to structured class spaces. It allows users to encode graph-like relations between classes without having to define complex joint distributions or manually tune utility matrices.Technically, our loss function operates by maximizing two separate margins over distinct class partitions, while preserving formal properties such as monotonicity and partial convexity. We demonstrate the versatility and effectiveness of our method by applying it to hierarchical classification, ordinal regression, and multiple instance learning. Across these tasks, \textsc{Conveyance} either matches or exceeds the performance of specialized baselines, thereby offering a unified solution for structured class spaces.

</details>


### [383] [Latent Diffusion for Missing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28427)
*Alberte Heering Estad,Ignacio Peis,Jes Frellsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent diffusion for missing-data imputation improves robustness and quality by performing diffusion in a learned latent space via a robust VAE-based imputer, outperforming pixel-space diffusion under MCAR missingness, stable up to 50% missingness, and yielding better downstream imputation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address degradation of pixel-space diffusion under heavy incomplete data; investigate whether latent-space diffusion offers robustness when training data are heavily MCAR.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) robust VAE-based imputer learns compact latent representations from incomplete observations; (2) a diffusion model is trained in the latent space; comparative study against pixel-space diffusion under identical incomplete-data setup across varying missing rates.

Result: Latent diffusion maintains high sample quality and remains stable up to 50% missingness; pixel-space diffusion degrades with increasing missingness; latent diffusion also yields better downstream imputation performance; latent-space modeling reduces artifact amplification from zero imputation and provides a stronger prior for incomplete data.

Conclusion: Latent diffusion is a strong, practically useful alternative to pixel-space diffusion for missing-data problems.

Abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful generative approaches for missing-data imputation, yet most existing methods operate directly in data space and degrade when training data are heavily incomplete. We investigate whether shifting diffusion to a learned latent representation improves robustness under missing-completely-at-random (MCAR) corruption. To this end, we propose a two-stage framework: a robust VAE-based imputer first learns compact semantic features from incomplete observations, and a diffusion model is then trained in the resulting latent space. Across training missing rates, we perform a controlled comparison against pixel-space diffusion models under the same incomplete-data setting. The latent diffusion model maintains high sample quality and remains stable up to 50\% missingness, while pixel-space diffusion degrades progressively as missingness increases. For downstream imputation, latent diffusion also achieves consistently better performance than pixel-space diffusion. These findings indicate that latent-space modeling mitigates artifact amplification from zero-imputed inputs and provides a more robust generative prior for incomplete-data learning. Overall, our results support latent diffusion as a strong and practically useful alternative to pixel-space diffusion for missing-data problems.

</details>


### [384] [Bilinear Coordinate Alignment for Training-Free Task-Vector Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28444)
*Jungyong Son,Jinwook Jung,Minhee Park,Sungyong Baik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BiCo is a training-free method that transfers fine-tuning expertise across model versions by aligning bilinear task vectors in input and output spaces, achieving competitive performance without parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained models yields version-specific parameterizations; reusing fine-tuned expertise on new model versions is inefficient. Prior transfer methods rely on partial matches of activations or gradients and fall short of direct fine-tuning.

Method: Reframe task vectors as accumulated bilinear interactions between input activations and output-side gradients. Formulate transfer as dual-space alignment and apply Bilinear Coordinate (BiCo) alignment by estimating orthogonal Procrustes mappings in both input and gradient spaces using a single forward-backward pass on a small calibration set, with no parameter updates.

Result: Across extensive CV and NLP benchmarks, BiCo consistently outperforms existing transfer methods across diverse model widths, depths, and pre-training configurations.

Conclusion: Task-vector transfer can be effectively achieved via dual-space bilinear alignment, enabling training-free cross-model adaptation that approaches or matches the performance of direct fine-tuning while avoiding re-tuning for new model versions.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large-scale pre-trained models is a recent prevalent paradigm for adapting general representations to specialized tasks. However, when a new version of a pre-trained model becomes available, expertise acquired through fine-tuning cannot be directly reused because it is tied to the parameterization of the original model, requiring another costly fine-tuning. To address this inefficiency, recent work uses task vectors, defined as the parameter difference between a fine-tuned model and its base model, to transfer expertise across models. While existing methods bridge disparate models by matching activations or gradients, a significant performance gap remains relative to direct fine-tuning, suggesting that these partial correspondences are insufficient. In this work, instead of viewing a task vector merely as a parameter offset, we revisit the formation of task vectors and show that they can be derived as accumulated bilinear interactions between input-side activations and output-side gradients. Motivated by this observation, we formulate task-vector transfer as a dual-space alignment problem and propose BiCo, a training-free framework for transferring task vectors through Bilinear Coordinate alignment. BiCo estimates orthogonal Procrustes mappings in both spaces using a single forward-backward pass on a small calibration set, without any parameter update. Across extensive computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks, BiCo consistently outperforms existing transfer methods across models that differ in width, depth, and pre-training configuration.

</details>


### [385] [Mitigating Adaptive Attacks against Reasoning Models with Activation Consistency Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28467)
*Avidan Shah,Jannik Brinkmann,Rico Angell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation-level consistency training (ACT) aligns internal representations between clean prompts and adversarial rewrites, yielding robust defense against jailbreaks/prompt injections in reasoning LLMs, with competitive performance and interpretable linear shifts in activation space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance safety of reasoning LLMs by supervising internal representations to resist adversarial prompts and jailbreaks, extending beyond output-only defenses to activation-level supervision and improving interpretability.

Method: Contrast two training objectives: output-level BCT and activation-level ACT. Train with self-supervised pairs of clean and wrapped prompts. Evaluate across five reasoning models in jailbreak and prompt-injection scenarios. Analyze activation-space dynamics and robustness to adaptive attacks; test with compliant traces and base-model-only jailbreaks.

Result: ACT is competitive with other training-based defenses, and more robust against adaptive attacks. Mechanistic evidence shows a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary. A single steering direction can be recovered to induce refusals on reasoning models with minimal impact on benign inputs. ACT remains robust even when the chain-of-thought is replaced by a compliant trace from the undefended base model.

Conclusion: Supervising internal representations via ACT is an effective and interpretable safety-training approach for reasoning models, offering robust defense against jailbreaks and prompt injections and enabling controllable refusals with minimal collateral impact.

Abstract: As LLMs gain stronger reasoning capabilities, their extended chain-of-thought introduces new degrees of complexity for defending against adversarial jailbreaks and prompt injection. We study consistency training, a family of fine-tuning objectives that enforce identical behavior on clean prompts and adversarial rewrites, and evaluate its two main variants, output-level (BCT) and activation-level (ACT), across five reasoning models. We formulate both methods as a prompt injection defense and find ACT to be competitive with other training-based defenses while requiring only self-supervised pairs of clean and wrapped prompts. Our experiments also generalize both techniques within the jailbreak setting, demonstrating that ACT remains more robust to adaptive attacks. We also provide mechanistic evidence that ACT's defense against jailbreaks is encoded as a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary. After ACT training, we can recover a single steering direction that controls refusal on reasoning models with minimal effect on benign inputs. We find that ACT remains robust even when the model's chain-of-thought is replaced with a compliant trace from the undefended base model, pivoting to refuse prefilled jailbreaks. Together, these results suggest that supervising internal representations is a surprisingly effective and interpretable approach to various forms of safety training in reasoning models.

</details>


### [386] [Fitting Unknown Number of Hyperplanes with Manifold Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28501)
*Zhiqin Cheng,Yu Zhan,Mingjin Zhang,Lingbo Liu,Liang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A manifold-optimization framework for fitting an unknown number of hyperplanes via a two-stage Riemannian EM on the unit sphere, with a projected density initialization, achieving improved geometric accuracy and robustness over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address non-convexity, non-differentiability, and unknown model order in hyperplane fitting; improve geometric consistency and robustness to intersecting hyperplanes.

Method: Reformulate as unsupervised learning on S^{d-1}; use two-stage optimization: Phase I Riemannian EM with heavy-tailed kernel to estimate posterior probabilities; Phase II hard matching; initialization via projected density estimation.

Result: Experiments show improvements in geometric accuracy and robustness over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed manifold optimization framework effectively handles the challenges and yields precise local optima with good initialization; robust to ambiguities between intersecting hyperplanes.

Abstract: Fitting an unknown number of hyperplanes to data is a fundamental yet challenging problem in machine learning, characterized by its non-convexity, non-differentiability, and unknown model order. Existing approaches often struggle with local optima or lack geometric consistency. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework based on Manifold Optimization. We reformulate the problem as an unsupervised learning task on the unit sphere manifold $\mathcal{S}^{\textbf{dim}-1}$. This formulation effectively handles the non-convex constraints and linearizes the distance measurement, rendering the gradient descent tractable. We propose a Two-Stage Manifold Optimization algorithm. In Phase I, we employ a Riemannian Expectation-Maximization process with a heavy-tailed kernel to robustly estimate posterior probabilities, effectively resolving the ambiguities of point distribution between intersecting hyperplanes. In Phase II, upon convergence of the soft estimates, the probabilistic weights degenerate into hard matching, generating a precise local optimum that strictly satisfies the geometric definition. Furthermore, we introduce a projected density estimation strategy for initialization to facilitate global convergence by significantly reducing the feature description space and search complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both geometric accuracy and robustness.

</details>


### [387] [Comparative Analysis of Liquid Neural Networks and LSTM for Sequential Pattern Recognition: Robustness, Efficiency, and Clinical Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27467)
*Ye Kyaw Thu,Thazin Myint Oo,Thepchai Supnithi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CfC-based Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs) model hidden states as continuous-time dynamics, achieving better parameter efficiency and robustness across temporally rich and sparse data compared to traditional RNN/LSTM baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate the practicality and robustness of CfC LNNs for continuous-time sequence modeling across diverse modalities (neuromorphic events, sketching, handwriting, physiological time-series) and under data sparsity via temporal dropout.

Method: Benchmark LNNs against conventional recurrent architectures on four datasets (N-MNIST, QuickDraw, IAM, PhysioNet Sepsis-3), and perform a temporal dropout stress test to assess robustness; include extended background and appendix on implementation and experimental settings.

Result: LNNs demonstrate superior parameter efficiency and significantly higher robustness in native temporal domains and clinical environments with sparse data across all tested modalities.

Conclusion: CfC LNNs are effective for modeling real-world continuous-time processes and confer robustness to missing data, making them advantageous for temporally sparse or continuous-time tasks; the extended preprint enriches context and reproducibility via detailed appendices.

Abstract: Traditional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units operate on discrete time steps, often failing to capture the fluid temporal dynamics of real-world physical processes. Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs), specifically Closed-form Continuous-time (CfC) networks, address this by modeling the hidden state evolution as a continuous differential equation. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive benchmarking study across four distinct sequential modalities: neuromorphic event-based data (N-MNIST), stroke-based drawing (QuickDraw), visual handwriting (IAM), and physiological time-series (PhysioNet Sepsis-3). Furthermore, we perform a rigorous stress test using temporal dropout to evaluate model robustness against missing data. Our findings reveal that LNNs consistently provide superior parameter efficiency and significantly higher robustness in natively temporal domains and clinical environments where data sparsity is prevalent.
