This extended preprint provides additional background on related datasets and the LNN theoretical lineage, supplemented with a detailed appendix documenting our full implementation and experimental settings.

</details>


### [388] [Learning Theory of the SVRG: Generalization and Convergence Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28513)
*Yunwen Lei,Zimeng Wang,Xiaoming Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides non-vacuous, data-dependent generalization bounds for SVRG (and extends to SAGA) via algorithmic stability. It decomposes SVRG into an SGD-like step plus a zero-mean correction, uses Lyapunov functions to absorb extra gradient terms, and yields optimal excess risk bounds in convex and strongly convex settings, with a framework applicable to other VR methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between convergence analyses and generalization behavior of variance-reduced methods; a non-vacuous, data-dependent stability analysis for SVRG is needed to understand optimization-generalization interplay.

Method: Perform algorithmic stability analysis by decomposing the SVRG update into an SGD-like component and a zero-mean correction term; introduce novel Lyapunov functions to absorb gradient terms arising from reference points; derive sharp, data-dependent stability bounds in convex and strongly convex regimes; extend the framework to SAGA.

Result: Sharp stability bounds are obtained for SVRG in both convex and strongly convex settings, leading to optimal excess population risk bounds that are data-dependent; the approach highlights the interplay between optimization and generalization and can be generalized to other variance-reduced methods, demonstrated for SAGA.

Conclusion: The proposed stability-based framework clarifies how VR methods generalize, providing non-vacuous generalization guarantees and a path to extend the analysis to other VR algorithms beyond SVRG/SAGA.

Abstract: Variance reduction (VR) methods employ stochastic gradients with decreasing variance, and they have been widely applied to solve large-scale optimization problems in machine learning because of their efficiency. Existing theoretical studies of VR methods are mainly focused on the convergence analysis, leaving the generalization behavior largely unexplored. In this paper, we bridge this gap by developing the first non-vacuous generalization analysis of the representative VR method: Stochastic Variance Reduced Gradient (SVRG), through the lens of algorithmic stability. In particular, we establish sharp stability bounds of the SVRG in both convex and strongly convex settings by exploiting its algorithmic structure. The obtained bounds are data-dependent, because the training errors are incorporated along the trajectory. Our analysis clarifies the interplay between optimization and generalization, leading to optimal excess population risk bounds in both settings. Our approach differs substantially from existing analyses of stochastic algorithms in the sense that we decompose the SVRG update as an SGD-like step plus a zero-mean correction term and then introduce novel Lyapunov functions to absorb the additional gradient terms induced by the reference points. Our analytical framework can be generalized to other VR methods, and we demonstrate the generalization by the well-known Stochastic Average Gradient Accelerated (SAGA) method.

</details>


### [389] [Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum is Algorithmically Stable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28517)
*Yunwen Lei,Zimeng Wang,Xiaoming Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGDM generalization is analyzed via algorithmic stability, presenting a unified momentum framework (Polyak/Nesterov), tight on-average stability bounds for smooth convex problems without Lipschitz loss assumptions, along with optimization bounds and excess risk results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in understanding SGDM generalization: momentum speeds up training but may hurt generalization. A stability-based, unified analysis without Lipschitz loss assumptions is needed to characterize how momentum affects generalization across Polyak and Nesterov schemes.

Method: Introduce a generalized SGDM framework that encompasses Polyak’s and Nesterov’s momentum. Derive tight on-average model stability bounds for smooth convex problems, allowing any momentum parameter in [0,1). Do not require loss functions to be Lipschitz. Derive optimization error bounds along the trajectory and combine with stability results to obtain excess population risk bounds for SGDM with both momentum schemes.

Result: Obtained tight on-average stability bounds for generalized SGDM that do not rely on Lipschitzness and hold for any momentum in [0,1). Established optimization error bounds along the trajectory and, by combining these with stability analysis, derived optimal excess population risk bounds for SGDM under both Polyak’s and Nesterov’s momentum.

Conclusion: The work provides a comprehensive generalization analysis of SGDM via algorithmic stability, unifying momentum schemes, showing momentum can maintain good generalization under mild assumptions, and delivering optimal excess risk bounds for both Polyak and Nesterov momentum.

Abstract: Stochastic gradient descent with momentum (SGDM) is one of the most widely used optimization algorithms in machine learning. While optimization properties of SGDM have been extensively studied in the literature, it remains insufficiently understood whether and when SGDM can generalize well to unseen data. In particular, it has been conjectured that while momentum accelerates training, it may degrade generalization. In this paper, we close this gap by developing a comprehensive generalization analysis of SGDM through the lens of algorithmic stability. More specifically, we introduce a generalized SGDM framework that encompasses both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum schemes, and establish tight on-average model stability bounds for smooth and convex problems. Notably, the obtained bounds exploit small optimization error bounds along the trajectory, apply to any momentum parameter in the interval $[0, 1)$, and do not require the commonly assumed Lipschitzness of loss functions. We further derive optimization error bounds for the generalized SGDM, and combine them with our generalization analyses to obtain optimal excess population risk bounds for SGDM with both Polyak's and Nesterov's momentum.

</details>


### [390] [Stabilizing distribution-free probabilistic forecasts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28531)
*Jente Van Belle,Honglin Wen,Wouter Verbeke,Pierre Pinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a unified framework to trade off forecast stability and quality in distribution-free probabilistic time-series forecasting by learning stabilized conditional quantile functions via neural nets and regression splines, enabling targeted stabilization of distribution regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecast updates improve short-horizon accuracy but induce instability with downstream costs; need to balance accuracy and stability, and allow region-specific stabilization for downstream tasks.

Method: Use regression splines parameterized by a neural network to model conditional quantile functions; jointly optimize for forecast quality and stability by penalizing forecast-update dissimilarities; enable weighting across distribution regions (e.g., central vs tails).

Result: Empirically evaluated on two datasets with differing statistics; shows reduced forecast instability with limited loss in accuracy; demonstrates the ability to target stabilization to specific distribution regions.

Conclusion: Integrating stability into training yields controllable trade-offs, improves trust in probabilistic forecasts, and supports downstream decision making by prioritizing stabilization where it matters most.

Abstract: Multi-step-ahead forecasts are often updated as new observations become available, since shorter forecast horizons typically improve forecast quality. However, such improvements come at the cost of forecast instability, i.e., variability in forecasts for the same target period. This instability can trigger costly changes to plans formulated based on the forecasts and may erode trust in the forecasting system. In this work, we integrate forecast stability alongside forecast quality into the training of distribution-free probabilistic time-series forecasting models, allowing us to control this trade-off. We propose a method for generating stabilized forecasted conditional quantile functions using regression splines parameterized by a neural network. This approach enables joint optimization of quality and stability, as it allows us to directly penalize dissimilarities arising from forecast updates. Furthermore, it allows assigning varying importance to stabilizing different parts of the forecast distributions (e.g., central parts vs. tails) to focus on the parts most relevant for the intended downstream use (e.g., the upper tail for inventory management). We empirically evaluate the proposed method on two datasets with different statistical properties and show that it can effectively reduce forecast instability without a substantial loss in forecast quality, and that it can target stabilization effort toward specific parts of the forecast distributions.

</details>


### [391] [Semi-Supervised Hypothesis Testing by Betting on Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28533)
*Yaniv Tenzer,Elad Tolochinsky,Yaniv Romano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A testing-by-betting framework uses unlabeled X data to boost sequential hypothesis testing via an e-statistic, achieving anytime-valid tests under label or concept shift with power for binary outcomes and robustness to predictor errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited labeled data hinders inference about Y and Y|X; abundant unlabeled X data can be leveraged to improve hypothesis testing about the joint distribution and conditional distribution.

Method: Introduce an e-statistic derived from predictions on unlabeled X, embed it into a sequential testing procedure, prove anytime validity under label/concept shift, analyze power for binary Y, and demonstrate robustness to prediction inaccuracies; validate with simulations and large-language-model evaluation.

Result: The proposed test is anytime valid under standard shift models, the e-statistic has non-trivial power for binary data, and the approach yields power gains over baselines (including prediction-powered inference) that persist with limited unlabeled data and weak X–Y correlation.

Conclusion: Unlabeled data can meaningfully enhance sequential hypothesis testing under distributional shifts without requiring perfect predictions, with demonstrated gains in simulations and LLM evaluations; opens avenues for broader application and extensions to other Y-types and shift models.

Abstract: We introduce a testing-by-betting framework that leverages predictions on unlabeled data to enhance the power of sequential hypothesis testing. Given limited samples from the joint distribution of $(X,Y)$, and additional unlabeled samples from the marginal of $X$, we ask how unlabeled data can be used to hypothesize about the distribution of $Y$, and the conditional distribution of $Y\mid X$. We introduce an e-statistic and use it to construct a sequential test. Under standard distributional assumptions -- label shift or concept shift -- we establish that the test is anytime valid. Furthermore, we show that for binary data, the e-statistic has non-trivial power. Crucially, our approach retains these properties even when the underlying predictions are inaccurate. Through simulations and applications to large language models evaluation, we demonstrate power gains over baseline approaches, including prediction-powered inference. These gains persist even with relatively limited unlabeled data and when predictions have low accuracy due to weak correlation between $X$ and $Y$.

</details>


### [392] [High Performance, Low Reliability: Uncertainty Benchmarking for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28554)
*José Lucas De Melo Costa,Fabrice Popineau,Arpad Rimmel,Bich-Liên Doan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFMs achieve top AUC on TALENT datasets but show weaker conditional coverage under conformal prediction than GBDTs; reveals a performance-uncertainty trade-off and an open challenge in reliable uncertainty calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trustworthiness gap in Tabular Foundation Models by systematically comparing TFMs, GBDTs, and classical baselines on a large TALENT benchmark to understand accuracy–uncertainty trade-offs.

Method: Empirical study over 112 TALENT datasets, comparing TFMs, GBDTs, and classical baselines. Metrics include predictive performance (AUC) and uncertainty via conformal prediction (SSCS). Complementary experiments on synthetic data. Code released at the provided GitHub repository.

Result: TFMs achieve the highest AUC but exhibit lower conditional coverage (SSCS) under conformal prediction compared to GBDTs. A performance–uncertainty trade-off is observed, with effects resonating in certain data regimes; results supported by synthetic data analyses.

Conclusion: TFMs advance predictive performance but calibrated uncertainty remains a major open challenge for their reliable adoption; methodological work is needed to improve uncertainty quantification in TFMs.

Abstract: Recent Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) have demonstrated state-of-the-art predictive performance, often surpassing Gradient-Boosted Decision Trees (GBDTs). However, the trustworthiness of these models, particularly their uncertainty quantification, has been largely overlooked. We investigate this gap through an extensive study comparing TFMs, GBDTs, and classical baselines on the 112 datasets of the TALENT benchmark. Our results reveal a performance-uncertainty trade-off: although TFMs achieve the highest predictive performance, measured by AUC, they exhibit lower conditional coverage under conformal prediction, measured by SSCS, compared to GBDTs. Complementary experiments on synthetic datasets further characterize the regimes in which this effect intensifies. We conclude that while TFMs advance predictive frontiers, achieving well-calibrated uncertainty remains a major open challenge for their reliable adoption. Code is available at: https://github.com/jose-melo/high-performance-low-reliability

</details>


### [393] [A Multi-dimensional Framework for Evaluating Generalization in EEG Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28563)
*Aditya Kommineni,Emily Zhou,Kleanthis Avramidis,Tiantian Feng,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-dimensional evaluation framework assesses EEG foundation models under low-resource, realistic constraints. Foundation models excel on long-context tasks but offer limited robustness and give mixed gains on short-window EEG tasks, compared to supervised models with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current EEG foundation models are typically evaluated with full fine-tuning on curated datasets, which ignores practical biomedical constraints such as limited labeled data, reduced sensor coverage, and the need for parameter-efficient adaptation. A low-resource, realistic evaluation is needed to gauge transferability and practical utility.

Method: Empirical evaluation across six EEG datasets comparing supervised EEG models and foundation models (LaBraM, CSBrain, CBraMod) under a proposed multi-dimensional, low-resource framework. Analyses distinguish long-context tasks (e.g., sleep stage prediction, mental health state classification) from short-window, BCI-like tasks, and assess robustness under channel-constrained conditions.

Result: Foundation models consistently improve performance on long-context tasks (sleep staging, mental health state classification). For short-window BCI-like tasks, supervised models—though smaller in parameter count—achieve comparable performance. Additional analyses reveal limited robustness of foundation models in short-window and channel-constrained scenarios.

Conclusion: Multi-dimensional, realistic evaluation protocols are essential to characterize EEG model behavior under practical constraints. The results support using such protocols for assessing transferability and guiding deployment of EEG models in neurotechnology and clinical settings.

Abstract: Evaluating foundation models under appropriate adaptation settings is essential for understanding the quality and transferability of the learned representations. Recent EEG foundation models have demonstrated promising transfer capabilities across tasks and datasets, motivating their growing use in neurotechnology and clinical applications. However, these models are typically evaluated under full fine-tuning on well-curated downstream datasets, a setting that does not reflect biomedical domain constraints such as limited labeled data, reduced sensor coverage, or parameter-efficient adaptation. In this work, we propose a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for assessing EEG models under realistic low-resource conditions. Empirical analysis of both supervised EEG models and recent EEG foundation models, including LaBraM, CSBrain, and CBraMod, across 6 different datasets is performed under the proposed multi-dimensional evaluation framework. We find that EEG foundation models consistently provide performance gains on long-context tasks such as sleep stage prediction and mental health state classification. In contrast, for short-window Brain Computer Interface style tasks, supervised models achieve comparable despite having substantially fewer parameters. Additional analyses demonstrate that current foundation models provide limited robustness to short-window tasks and channel constrained settings. Together, these findings motivate the use of multi-dimensional evaluation protocols that characterize model behavior under realistic use constraints.

</details>


### [394] [Semantic Optimal Transport for Sparse Autoencoder Feature Matching and Circuit Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28567)
*Tue M. Cao,Nguyen Do,My T. Thai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributional semantic distance framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that unifies cross-layer feature matching and circuit compression by representing each feature as an activation-weighted distribution over hidden states and comparing distributions with Wasserstein distance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale semantic matching across SAE layers and compress large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes; existing approaches treat these as separate problems and rely on decoder vectors or external models.

Method: Represent each SAE feature as an activation-weighted distribution over hidden states rather than a single decoder vector; project these distributions into a shared reference space; compare them using Wasserstein distance to obtain a unified semantic metric; prove invariance to activation rescaling, stability under perturbations, and recovery under finite-sample margin conditions.

Result: Empirically, the method outperforms decoder-vector and LLM-based baselines, captures subtle functional distinctions between related features, and enables automatic compression of large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes.

Conclusion: Introduces a unified, robust semantic metric for cross-layer SAE analysis that simultaneously addresses feature matching across layers and automatic circuit compression into interpretable structures.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a central tool for interpreting language models. However, two key SAE analyses that remain difficult to scale are (1) matching semantically similar features across multi-layers and (2) compressing large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes. Although these have been treated as separate problems, we show that both are instances of a more fundamental challenge, which we frame as the estimation of semantic distances between SAE features that lie on different activation manifolds. We introduce a distributional framework for this problem, in which each feature is represented not by a single decoder vector like in the literature, but by an activation-weighted distribution over the hidden states that express it. By projecting these distributions into a shared reference space and comparing them with Wasserstein distance, our method provides a unified semantic metric for cross-layer feature comparison. We prove that our representation is invariant to activation rescaling, stable under perturbations, and recovers true matches under finite-sample margin conditions. Empirically, our method outperforms decoder-vector and LLM-based baselines and captures subtle functional distinctions between related features. Notably, our method compresses large feature circuits into interpretable supernodes automatically.

</details>


### [395] [Efficient Pre-Training of LLMs through Truncated SVD Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28573)
*Kaivan Kamali,Kajetan Schweighofer,Hormoz Shahrzad,Olivier Francon,Babak Hodjat,Risto Miikkulainen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSVD introduces a low-rank, strictly orthonormal parameterization for LLM pretraining, using a spectral-energy heuristic for adaptive rank and a caching mechanism to enforce orthonormality, achieving compute savings while matching or surpassing full-parameter baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretraining LLMs is extremely costly; there is a need for methods that reduce parameter count and computation without sacrificing performance; existing methods either use static ranks or fail to enforce orthonormality due to cost.

Method: Propose TSVD framework with adaptive rank selection via spectral energy-based heuristic; persistent low-rank factorization with orthonormal constraints; caching mechanism to maintain orthonormality during updates; theoretical analysis of pretraining dynamics; empirical evaluation across multiple model scales.

Result: Experiments show TSVD matches or exceeds full-parameter baselines with significantly reduced compute; demonstrates scalability and empirical effectiveness.

Conclusion: TSVD provides a principled, scalable approach for efficient high-performance LLM pretraining, combining adaptive rank control and enforced orthonormality to reduce cost without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: The massive scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has made pretraining increasingly cost-prohibitive. While low-rank representation and orthonormal weight matrices could in principle reduce parameter counts and computational overhead, most existing methods rely on static rank selection and do not enforce weight orthonormality due to high computational cost. This paper introduces TSVD, a framework that maintains low rank and strict orthonormality throughout the training process. It utilizes a spectral energy-based heuristic for adaptive rank selection, and a caching mechanisms to maintain orthonormality. Theoretical analysis justifies the advantage of the approach in pretraining dynamics and experiments across various model scales demonstrate that it is effective empirically. TSVD matches or exceeds the performance of full-parameter baselines while significantly reducing compute requirements. The approach thus offers a well-founded, practical, and scalable path toward efficient high-performance LLM pretraining.

</details>


### [396] [A Generalized Tikhonov Layer for Interpretable-by-design Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28578)
*Nicolas Tremblay,Benjamin Ricaud,Filippo Maria Bianchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Tikhonov layer for graph neural networks, an interpretable layer where the propagation operator R = (p(L)+Q)^{-1} Q is derived from a learnable polynomial p and a diagonal node-importance matrix Q. Rx is the exact minimizer of a generalized graph Tikhonov objective, yielding built-in explanations via q_i and p. A deep Q-network generates Q to preserve expressivity, while the layer remains transparent. It provides a global receptive field and mitigates oversmoothing/oversquashing; empirically competitive on graph classification with faithful explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph models are powerful but largely opaque. There is a need for interpretable mechanisms that reveal whether predictions rely on node features or local topology, and what spectral/topological regimes (homophily/heterophily/band-pass) are exploited, without sacrificing performance.

Method: Define L as the normalized Laplacian, Q = diag(q_1,...,q_n) with positive entries, and p(·) a learnable polynomial. The layer computes R = (p(L)+Q)^{-1} Q and outputs Rx, which is the exact minimizer of a generalized graph Tikhonov objective balancing data fidelity and topology-driven regularization. Node explanations come from the learned {q_i} and the shape of p; Q is produced by a dedicated arbitrarily deep Q-network to maintain expressivity while keeping the Tikhonov layer interpretable. A theorem asserts that distinct Q yield distinct R, linking explanation and computation.

Result: On standard graph classification benchmarks, the model achieves performance comparable to or better than opaque baselines while delivering interpretable, faithful explanations.

Conclusion: The Tikhonov layer delivers a global receptive field in a single layer, mitigating oversmoothing and oversquashing. It couples explainability directly to the computation and preserves expressivity via a separate Q-network, enabling practical, interpretable graph modeling.

Abstract: We propose the Tikhonov layer, a graph neural network layer that is interpretable by design: once trained, its learned parameters directly reveal which node features and which aspects of the graph topology were leveraged for prediction. In practice, the layer's propagation matrix takes the closed-form $R = (p(L)+Q)^{-1} Q$, where $L$ is the normalized graph Laplacian, $Q = diag(q_1,...,q_n)$ a learnable diagonal matrix of positive node-importance scores, and $p(\cdot)$ a learnable polynomial. For any input feature $x$, the layer output $Rx$ is the exact minimizer of a generalized graph Tikhonov problem that trades off node-level data fidelity against a topology-driven regularization penalty. The learned pair $\{\{q_i\},p\}$ constitutes a built-in explanation: large $q_i$ indicates that node $i$'s own features drive the prediction, while small $q_i$ signals reliance on the local graph topology; the shape of $p$ reveals whether homophily, heterophily, or a band-pass response is exploited. Expressivity is preserved by routing complexity through a dedicated, arbitrarily deep Q-network that produces the importance scores, while the Tikhonov layer itself remains transparent. We prove that distinct node-importance matrices yield distinct propagation operators, structurally coupling the explanation to the computation. Additionally, the Tikhonov layer provides, in a single layer, a global receptive field, mitigating both oversmoothing and oversquashing. Experiments on standard graph classification benchmarks confirm that the model matches (and sometimes outperforms) opaque baselines while producing interpretable and faithful explanations.

</details>


### [397] [Outer-Momentum Restarting in High-Dimensional Two-Phase Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28585)
*Kristi Topollai,Allan Ma,Tolga Dimlioglu,Sui Jiet Tay,Anna Choromanska*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Periodic restarting of the outer momentum in DiLoCo-like distributed optimizers stabilizes training by exploiting phase cancellation, widening the stable learning-rate and momentum range across communication rounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stabilize and make robust the outer momentum in communication-efficient distributed optimization by controlling outer memory through restarts, enabling more aggressive local updates and better cross-round stability.

Method: Theoretical analysis on a linearized squared-loss model under the empirical NTK, deriving a mode-wise restart contraction that shows resets discard stale momentum while preserving inner-loop progress; complemented by toy experiments and language-model pretraining demonstrations.

Result: A contraction bound demonstrating phase-cancellation benefits of periodic restarts; empirical evidence that restart schedules widen the stable region for outer learning rates and momentum across communication periods.

Conclusion: Periodic restarting of outer momentum serves as an effective, simple complement to inner-loop updates, expanding hyperparameter stability and enabling more efficient, communication-efficient distributed optimization.

Abstract: Communication-efficient distributed optimizers such as DiLoCo reduce synchronization costs by letting workers perform many local updates before aggregating their progress with an outer momentum optimizer. Recent theory suggests that the outer optimizer acts on an effective spectrum induced by the inner optimization loop, and that the choice of outer momentum controls how progress from local updates is accumulated across communication rounds. We study periodic restarting of the outer momentum as a simple complementary mechanism for controlling this outer memory. In a linearized squared-loss model where prediction-space residuals evolve under the empirical NTK, we derive a mode-wise restart contraction showing that resets exploit phase cancellation by discarding stale momentum while preserving inner-loop progress. Toy experiments verify the predicted contraction behavior, and language-model pretraining experiments show that periodic restarts widen the stable range of outer learning rates and momentum values across communication periods.

</details>


### [398] [Thinned Mean Field Langevin Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28589)
*Zonghao Chen,Heishiro Kanagawa,François-Xavier Briol,Chris J. Oates,Lester Mackey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KT-MFLD reduces MFLD's O(N^2) particle interaction cost to O(N^{3/2}) by using kernel thinning to interact with a core set of size O(N^{1/2}); it achieves the same convergence guarantees (up to log factors) under mild regularity assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Eliminate the quadratic cost of simulating interacting particle systems in entropy-regularized mean-field Langevin dynamics while preserving convergence guarantees for a wide class of learning tasks.

Method: Introduce KT-MFLD, where each particle interacts only with a thinned coreset of size O(N^{1/2}) via kernel thinning. Provide complexity analysis and convergence guarantees (up to log factors) under mild regularity. Empirically validate on tasks including training student-teacher networks, MMQ/maximum mean discrepancy-based quantization, and post-Bayesian optimized posteriors.

Result: Computational complexity reduced to O(N^{3/2}). Maintains convergence guarantees up to logarithmic factors; empirical experiments corroborate performance on stated tasks.

Conclusion: KT-MFLD offers a scalable alternative to MFLD with similar asymptotic convergence behavior, enabling entropy-regularized learning tasks at larger scales. Further work may explore broader kernels, coreset constructions, and tighter constants.

Abstract: Several important learning tasks can be formulated as minimizing an entropy-regularized objective over an appropriate space of probability distributions. Mean-field Langevin dynamics (MFLD) facilitate computation in this general context, casting the minimizer as the invariant distribution of a McKean--Vlasov process, which can be numerically discretized using $N$ particles and thus simulated. However, simulating this interacting particle system has computational complexity of order $N^2$. Motivated by recent research into \emph{kernel thinning}, we propose \texttt{KT-MFLD}, in which each particle interacts only with a thinned particle coreset of size $\mathcal{O}(N^{\frac{1}{2}})$. \texttt{KT-MFLD} thus reduces the computational complexity to order $N^{\frac{3}{2}}$ while, under mild regularity conditions, achieving the same convergence guarantees (up to logarithmic factors) as MFLD. Our theoretical analysis is empirically confirmed on tasks including the training of student-teacher neural networks, quantization with maximum mean discrepancy, and computation of predictively-oriented posteriors in a post-Bayesian framework.

</details>


### [399] [PLS in the Mirror of Self-Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28592)
*Jiangsheng,You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Casting PLS as linearized self-attention provides a conceptual link between dimensionality reduction and attention in networks, suggesting self-attention may implicitly normalize dimensions similar to PLS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify Partial Least Squares (PLS) dimensionality reduction and predictor selection with neural attention mechanisms; understand how PLS insights translate to attention-based models.

Method: Conceptual note: interpret PLS as a linearized self-attention, and analyze how PLS’s dimensionality reduction and predictor selection relate to the normalization effects in self-attention.

Result: Identifies a parallel between PLS reduction/selection and attention dimensionality normalization; proposes that self-attention may include a degree of dimensionality normalization that benefits learning.

Conclusion: Stimulates further theoretical and empirical exploration of PLS-inspired attention mechanisms and their impact on neural network learning.

Abstract: This note provides an interesting observation on casting partial least square (PLS) as a linearized self-attention so that PLS may be studied within the neural network paradigm. On the other hand, the dimensionality reduction and selection of predictors in PLS may indicate that self-attention includes certain degree of dimensionality normalization toward improved learning.

</details>


### [400] [Transformers Provably Learn to Internalize Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28600)
*Yixiao Huang,Hanlin Zhu,Zixuan Wang,Jiantao Jiao,Stuart Russell,Somayeh Sojoudi,Song Mei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides the first theoretical guarantees for Log-ICoT in multi-layer transformers, showing polynomial sample complexity for k-parity with L = log2 k stages, matching explicit CoT's efficiency while eliminating inference overhead; experiments corroborate theory and illustrate progressive internalization of reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Establish a theoretical foundation for implicit Chain-of-Thought (ICoT), addressing the gap between empirical gains and formal understanding, particularly for parity tasks and deep architectures.

Method: The authors analyze L-layer transformers trained with a Log-ICoT curriculum, proving that the model learns k-parity with polynomial sample complexity and logarithmic training stages; they compare against standard ICoT and extend results from one-layer to multi-layer networks; experiments validate the theory and visualize reasoning absorption across layers.

Result: Polynomial sample complexity poly(n) for k-parity with L = log2 k training stages; Log-ICoT achieves sample efficiency matching explicit CoT while avoiding inference-time reasoning tokens; experiments confirm theoretical predictions and demonstrate progressive embedding of reasoning into deeper layers.

Conclusion: Log-ICoT reduces the number of training stages to logarithmic in k by grouping reasoning steps, provides a rigorous bedrock for ICoT, and offers practical benefits for training efficiency and scaling of deep transformers; suggests deeper layers eventually internalize reasoning without explicit prompts.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting substantially improves the sample efficiency of transformers, reducing the complexity of tasks like parity learning from exponential to polynomial in the input length. However, generating explicit reasoning steps at inference is computationally expensive. Implicit Chain-of-Thought (ICoT) has emerged as a promising empirical remedy that trains models to internalize intermediate steps within their hidden states, but its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. We give the first theoretical analysis of ICoT, proving that an $L$-layer transformer trained under our proposed Log-ICoT curriculum learns $k$-parity with $\mathsf{poly}(n)$ samples and $L = \log_2 k$ training stages. This matches the sample efficiency of explicit CoT while eliminating its inference overhead, and extends prior one-layer parity guarantees to multi-layer architectures. Compared to standard ICoT, which removes thinking tokens one at a time, Log-ICoT removes them in geometric chunks, reducing the number of stages from linear in $k$ to logarithmic. Experiments on multi-layer transformers confirm the theory and visualize how reasoning is progressively absorbed into deeper layers.

</details>


### [401] [Online Irregular Multivariate Time Series Forecasting via Uncertainty-Driven Dual-Expert Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28603)
*Haonan Wen,Hanyang Chen,Songhe Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Under-Cali is an online irregular multivariate time series forecasting framework that uses an uncertainty-driven dual-expert calibration with adaptive routing to selectively calibrate challenging samples while keeping the base model frozen, enabling efficient online adaptation with low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irregularly sampled multivariate time series with dynamically evolving missingness patterns break temporal regularities and degrade online performance. There is a need for online learning methods that adapt without full fine-tuning of the forecasting model.

Method: An uncertainty estimator guides a dual-expert calibration module with an adaptive routing mechanism. Incoming batches are assessed for uncertainty; high-uncertainty samples go to an unreliable expert for calibration, while low-uncertainty samples stay with a reliable expert. The reliable expert and the uncertainty estimator are updated using well-calibrated reliable samples, and the unreliable expert is updated with challenging samples. The base forecasting model is kept frozen; adaptation occurs via a lightweight, model-agnostic calibration module.

Result: Extensive experiments on IMTS benchmarks show consistent forecasting improvements with low computational cost and available code at the provided repository.

Conclusion: The proposed Under-Cali framework enables stable, efficient online adaptation for irregular multivariate time series by combining uncertainty-driven routing with dual-expert calibration, while preserving a frozen base model to minimize overhead.

Abstract: Irregular multivariate time series forecasting is critical in many real-world applications, where time series are irregularly sampled and exhibit dynamically evolving missingness patterns. Although existing methods perform well in offline settings, they often suffer from significant performance degradation when deployed online due to dynamic shifts in data distribution. Maintaining forecasting capability in such dynamic scenarios typically necessitates online adaptation techniques. Since irregular sampling fundamentally undermines temporal continuity and periodicity, we cannot leverage these widely studied characteristics from regular MTS for online learning. To this end, we study the problem of online IMTS forecasting and propose Under-Cali, an uncertainty-driven dual-expert calibration framework consisting of three core components: an uncertainty estimator, a dual-expert calibration module, and an adaptive routing module. We design an uncertainty estimator that serves as the core control signal to jointly manage inference and adaptation processes. In our framework, the uncertainty estimator first assesses uncertainty for each incoming batch. The adaptive routing module then directs samples with high uncertainty to the unreliable expert for calibration, while low uncertainty samples remain with the reliable expert. Subsequently, the system updates the reliable expert and the uncertainty estimator using well-calibrated reliable samples, and updates the unreliable expert with challenging samples, enabling stable and efficient online learning. Under-Cali keeps the source forecasting model frozen and performs adaptation only through a lightweight, model-agnostic calibration module, enabling efficient adaptation. Extensive experiments on IMTS benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements with low computational cost. Our code is available at https://github.com/HaonanWen/Under-Cali.

</details>


### [402] [Learning High-Dimensional Parity Functions with Product Networks using Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28612)
*Guillaume Larue,Louis-Adrien Dufrène,Quentin Lampin,Hadi Ghauch,Ghaya Rekaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compact product-based neural architectures with Bernoulli data sparsity enable efficient learning of high-dimensional parity functions, with convergence guarantees and polynomial-time scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning high-dimensional parity is challenging for standard networks; parity is a fundamental Boolean function with broad relevance in ML, cryptography, and error correction.

Method: Propose compact product-based architectures; leverage stochastic data sparsity p_e ≤ 1/N; perform theoretical analysis proving convergence; tune hyperparameters (p_e, learning rate α); validate with experiments up to N=100,000.

Result: Theoretical convergence guarantees; polynomial scaling of sample/compute with N; empirical identification of optimal p_e and α; successful learning of parity for very large N.

Conclusion: Highlights the role of architectural inductive bias and data sparsity in enabling efficient learning; implications for neural arithmetic, structured reasoning, binary networks, and automated protocol discovery.

Abstract: Parity functions are fundamental Boolean operations with critical applications across machine learning, cryptography, and error correction. Yet, learning high-dimensional parity functions poses significant challenges: in a general setting, standard neural network architectures typically require exponential sample complexity, making gradient-based optimization intractable for large number of inputs $N$. We demonstrate that compact product-based neural architectures combined with stochastic data sparsity (Bernoulli inputs with $p_e \leq 1/N$) and appropriate hyperparameter choice enable efficient parity learning, with theoretical guarantees of convergence. Experiments validate our theory across dimensions up to $N = 100{,}000$, with empirical evidence showing optimal hyperparameter choices for $p_e$ and learning rate $α$, as well as polynomial complexity scaling laws. This work establishes fundamental connections between architectural inductive bias and data sparsity, opening new possibilities for neural arithmetic, structured reasoning, binary neural networks, and machine learning applied to automated protocol discovery.

</details>


### [403] [Random Process Flow Matching: Generative Implicit Representations of Multivariate Random Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28625)
*Julien Lalanne,David Picard,Lionel Boillot,Lina-María Guayacán-Carrillo,Leon Barens,Jean-Michel Pereira*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A flow-matching framework with neural implicit signals to reconstruct sparse fields, using Random Fourier Features and a GP-based Bayesian posterior to yield calibrated samples and uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses uncertainty and data-sparsity in generative reconstruction: enabling queryable, high-frequency signals from limited observations with quantified uncertainty.

Method: RP Flow treats the vector field as a neural implicit function via flow matching. It uses Random Fourier Features for implicit signal representation, ensembles for uncertainty, and constructs a Bayesian posterior by GP regression in source space to generate samples.

Result: Produces realistic samples with calibrated uncertainty estimates under challenging conditions like high frequency, high sparsity, and high dimensionality.

Conclusion: RP Flow advances generative modeling for reconstruction tasks with scarce data, offering traceable uncertainty as a core capability.

Abstract: Generative modeling provides a powerful framework for learning data distributions. These models initially relied on probabilistic methods such as Gaussian Processes (GP) for uncertainty-aware predictions and shifted towards larger trainable models to learn more complex distributions. In this work, we introduce Random Process (RP) Flow, a Flow Matching-based framework that represents the vector field as a neural implicit function. Unlike modern generative methods, our setting involves a single observed field, from which only sparse measurements are available. RP Flow uses Random Fourier Features to learn an implicit signal representation that can be queried at any arbitrary location from a limited set of observations, while encoding uncertainty through ensemble sampling. We propose constructing a Bayesian posterior by GP regression in the source space to generate high-quality samples. Our empirical results demonstrate that this framework generates realistic samples along with calibrated uncertainty estimates, even under challenging conditions such as high frequency, high sparsity, or high dimensionality. These findings position RP Flow as a milestone towards generative models for reconstruction tasks where data is scarce and uncertainty must remain traceable.

</details>


### [404] [When Interpretability Is Unequally Distributed: Fairness in Hybrid Interpretable Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28626)
*Ziba Jabbar Zare,Ulrich Aïvodji,Julien Ferry,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICD quantifies demographic disparities in how hybrid interpretable models route individuals to interpretable vs. black-box components; authors analyze ICD across methods/datasets, show substantial ICD in intermediate regimes, and that simple coverage constraints can reduce ICD with little accuracy/sparsity cost, sometimes improving standard fairness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hybrid interpretable models mix transparent and black-box components, raising a unique procedural fairness concern: some groups may systematically receive interpretable decisions while others are routed to the black box. ICD formalizes this concern to enable audit and mitigation.

Method: Define Interpretability Coverage Disparity (ICD) as a predictive-parity-style measure on the routing decision. Empirically evaluate ICD across four hybrid interpretable learning methods, three standard fairness benchmarks, and multiple sensitive attributes using predictive multiplicity tools. Investigate ICD in various transparency regimes and assess mitigation via coverage-disparity constraints in exact hybrid learning methods.

Result: ICD is substantial in intermediate transparency regimes where both components are used. Applying coverage-disparity constraints reduces ICD significantly with only marginal impact on accuracy and sparsity. In several settings, ICD mitigation also improves standard algorithmic fairness metrics.

Conclusion: Hybrid interpretable models should be audited for fairness not only in predictions but also in how interpretability is allocated across individuals and groups. Simple mitigation can reduce ICD with minimal losses, and ICD mitigation can improve broader fairness metrics.

Abstract: Hybrid interpretable models combine a transparent component with a black-box model by assigning some examples to the former and deferring the rest to the latter. While this design enables flexible tradeoffs between accuracy and interpretability, it also raises a distinct procedural fairness concern: some demographic groups may systematically receive interpretable decisions, while others are disproportionately routed to a black box.
  We formalize this issue as Interpretability Coverage Disparity (ICD), a demographic-parity-style measure applied to the routing decision of hybrid interpretable models. Using tools from predictive multiplicity, we study ICD across four hybrid interpretable learning methods, three standard fairness benchmark datasets, and multiple sensitive attributes. Our experiments reveal substantial ICD in intermediate transparency regimes, where both the interpretable and black-box components are actively used. We further show that simple coverage-disparity constraints can significantly reduce ICD in exact hybrid learning methods, with marginal impact on accuracy and sparsity. In several settings, ICD mitigation also improves standard algorithmic fairness metrics. These results show that hybrid interpretable models should be audited not only for predictive fairness, but also for how they allocate interpretability across individuals and groups.

</details>


### [405] [Single-Rollout Hidden-State Dynamics for Training-Free RLVR Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28631)
*Jianghao Wu,Jianfei Cai,Weiqiang Wang,Jin Ye,Daniel F. Schmidt,Yasmeen George*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHIFT is a training-free, one-shot data selector for RLVR that uses inference-time hidden-state dynamics (RIRS) to choose diverse, high-utility candidates, enabling strong performance under ultra-low budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data selection for RLVR is bottlenecked by reliance on labels/rewards and expensive evaluation; a training-free, pre-training selector is desirable, especially in specialized domains.

Method: For each candidate, SHIFT runs a single deterministic reasoning rollout, computes RIRS as the start-to-end hidden-state delta, uses magnitude as a utility proxy, and applies a quality-weighted farthest-first CoreSet in an RIRS-augmented feature space to select a compact subset from large unlabeled pools.

Result: In mathematical reasoning and medical QA benchmarks under ultra-low budgets, SHIFT outperforms training-free diversity and difficulty/uncertainty baselines, improving in-domain accuracy and transfer to harder tasks; ablations show RIRS-based coverage and quality weighting are complementary and not reducible to input/output lengths.

Conclusion: SHIFT provides an effective, scalable data-selection mechanism for RLVR in label-scarce settings and can enable more robust reasoning with minimal supervision; code is available at github.com/JianghaoWu/SHIFT.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) can yield large reasoning gains from very few training instances, yet its strong sensitivity to which instances are used makes data selection a central bottleneck. Most existing selection pipelines rely on training-time optimization signals and/or require access to verifiable rewards or ground-truth answers over large candidate pools, which is costly and often infeasible in specialized domains. We study RLVR data selection in a setting where selection must be performed before any RL training and without labels or reward evaluation on the full pool. We propose SHIFT, a one-shot, training-free selector based solely on inference-time hidden-state dynamics. For each candidate instance, SHIFT runs a single deterministic reasoning rollout and computes a reasoning-induced representation shift (RIRS) as the start-to-end hidden-state delta. SHIFT uses the RIRS magnitude as a lightweight proxy for instance utility and enforces coverage via a quality-weighted farthest-first CoreSet procedure in an RIRS-augmented feature space, producing compact subsets that scale to large unlabeled pools. Across mathematical reasoning and medical QA benchmarks under ultra-low budgets, SHIFT consistently outperforms training-free diversity and difficulty/uncertainty baselines, improving both in-domain accuracy and transfer to harder evaluation settings. Ablations show that RIRS-based coverage and quality-weighting contribute complementary gains, and analyses indicate that RIRS is not explained by simple input/output length statistics. Code is available at github.com/JianghaoWu/SHIFT.

</details>


### [406] [Augmenting Attention with Exponentially Decaying Memory Improves Query-Aware KV Sparsity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28640)
*Xiuying Wei,Caglar Gulcehre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RAT+ memory yields consistent improvements for query-aware sparse inference (Quest, MoBA, SnapKV) across eight needle-in-a-haystack tasks; validated on RAT+ checkpoints and OLMo2-7B pretraining; two hypotheses with targeted experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient inference for long-context LMs; attention and KV-cache dominate cost. Investigate whether exponentially decaying memory in RAT+ can enhance sparse inference performance.

Method: Empirical evaluation of RAT+ memory with Quest, MoBA, SnapKV under sparse budgets on eight tasks; replication on published RAT+ checkpoints; additional pretraining of OLMo2-7B with memory module for 10B tokens; propose two hypotheses and design experiments.

Result: RAT+ consistently improves accuracy over standard attention across sparse budgets; gains observed on both RAT+ checkpoints and OLMo2-7B experiments; memory module benefits query-aware sparse inference.

Conclusion: Memory-augmented recurrence backbones can boost sparse inference; provide hypotheses and targeted experiments to explain the gains; suggests design guidance for memory-based inference in long-context LMs.

Abstract: Efficient inference is critical for long-context language models, where attention computation and KV-cache access dominate the cost. Recent work RAT+, introduces a recurrence-augmented attention backbone that enables flexible dilated attention at inference time. In this paper, we investigate whether this exponentially decaying memory can also improve existing query-aware sparse inference methods. Using representative methods including Quest, MoBA, and SnapKV, we show that RAT+ consistently improves accuracy over standard attention across sparse budgets on eight needle-in-a-haystack tasks. We validate these gains both on the released checkpoints from the RAT+ paper and on OLMo2-7B, which we continue pretraining with the added memory module for 10B tokens. Finally, we propose two hypotheses explaining why this memory module benefits query-aware sparse inference and design targeted experiments to support them.

</details>


### [407] [Interpretability-Guided Layer Selection over Subspace Projection: SAEs as Stethoscopes, Not Scalpels, for Raw Task Vector Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28649)
*Li Lei,Madalina Ciobanu,Qingqing Mao,Ritankar Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAE-guided editing for LLMs fails due to an information bottleneck from projecting task vectors onto SAE feature subspaces; reframes SAE as a diagnostic tool (stethoscope) rather than a filter; injecting raw task vectors into SAE-identified layers improves math performance on Minerva Math.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficient, domain-specific model editing without full fine-tuning and without catastrophic forgetting; diagnose why SAE-based filtering underperforms and provide an interpretable alternative.

Method: Evaluate SAE-guided editing on Gemma-3-4B-IT, identify a geometric misalignment between activation-space SAE directions and weight-space task vectors; show failure (≈97% loss of modification energy). Propose SAE-as-diagnostic approach (stethoscope): inject unfiltered task vectors into layers selected by SAE specificity scores; assess on Minerva Math benchmark (Number Theory; 7 subjects).

Result: The projection approach acts as an information bottleneck, discarding ~97% of modification energy and yielding no significant gains across seven math subjects. The diagnostic approach yields a meaningful improvement: Number Theory accuracy from 29.6% to 39.4% (z=+3.41, p=0.0007); 5 of 7 subjects improve, none degrade. Method is deterministic and adds no inference cost.

Conclusion: SAEs are more effective as diagnostic tools (layer-level assessment) rather than feature-level filters. Injecting raw task vectors into SAE-identified layers, based on a specificity score, offers a principled, interpretable editing workflow with tangible performance gains.

Abstract: LLMs increasingly require surgical model editing to enhance domain-specific capabilities without incurring the computational cost or catastrophic forgetting associated with full fine-tuning. Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a promising tool in this setting, in principle allowing for feature-level identification of where to intervene. In this work, we rigorously evaluate an SAE-guided editing pipeline for mathematical reasoning on Gemma-3-4B-IT and uncover a fundamental failure mode: the intuitively appealing approach of projecting task vectors onto SAE feature subspaces acts as an information bottleneck that discards approximately 97% of the modification energy, yielding no statistically significant improvements across seven math subjects. We show that this failure stems from a geometric misalignment between activation-space SAE directions and weight-space task vectors. We then propose a shift in perspective: SAE as a Stethoscope, Not a Scalpel, where SAEs are used for layer-level diagnosis rather than intervention-level filtering. By injecting unfiltered raw task vectors only into layers identified by an SAE-derived specificity score, we improve Number Theory accuracy from 29.6% to 39.4% (z=+3.41, p=0.0007) on the Minerva Math benchmark; 5 of 7 math subjects significantly improved and none significantly degraded. Our method is fully deterministic, requires no additional inference cost, and provides a principled framework for interpretability-guided model editing.

</details>


### [408] [Applications of temporal graph learning for predicting the dynamics of biological systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28659)
*Manuel Dileo,Andrea Sottoriva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A temporal graph-learning framework for single-cell biology using pseudotime-resolved gene regulatory networks to model evolving cellular states; it uses temporal graph networks to forecast gene expression and predict regulatory links and centrality, outperforming static foundation models on developmental datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capture dynamic cellular programs that evolve over developmental pseudotime, rather than rely on static representations, by explicitly modeling changing regulatory interactions across developmental snapshots.

Method: From scRNA-seq data, infer pseudotime trajectories, segment cells into developmental snapshots, reconstruct one gene regulatory network per snapshot, and apply temporal graph neural networks to predict future states. Evaluate on three tasks (gene-expression forecasting, link prediction, out-degree centrality), using two mouse developmental datasets (erythroid gastrulation, pancreatic endocrinogenesis).

Result: Temporal graph models outperform established foundation-model baselines (scGPT, scFoundation) on the tasks, indicating that evolving regulatory structure provides information beyond static pretrained representations. Temporal dynamics enable identification of temporally important gene hubs via link and centrality predictions.

Conclusion: Temporal graph learning is a promising and complementary approach for modeling dynamic biological systems in single-cell biology, offering insights into regulatory dynamics that static models may miss.

Abstract: Biological foundation models have shown strong performance in single-cell representation learning by applying transformer architectures directly to gene-expression matrices. However, these approaches predominantly operate in static settings and do not explicitly model the temporal evolution of developmental programs in the cell. Modeling such dynamics is important for understanding how cellular states progressively emerge, differentiate, and reorganize during development or disease progression. In this work-in-progress paper, we investigate an alternative temporal graph-based perspective in which cellular states are represented through pseudotime-resolved gene regulatory networks and modeled as evolving graph structures over persistent gene identities. Starting from single-cell transcriptomic data, we infer pseudotime trajectories, discretize cells into developmental snapshots, reconstruct one gene regulatory network per snapshot, and apply temporal graph neural networks to forecast biological states. We evaluate this framework on two publicly available mouse developmental datasets, erythroid gastrulation and pancreatic endocrinogenesis, considering three complementary tasks: gene-expression forecasting, link prediction, and out-degree centrality prediction. Our results show that graph-based models outperform well-known foundation-model such as scGPT and scFoundation, suggesting that explicitly modeling evolving regulatory structure provides useful information beyond static pretrained representations. For link prediction and centrality forecasting, temporal graph learning captures non-trivial regulatory dynamics and enables the identification of temporally important gene hubs. Overall, our findings support temporal graph learning as a promising direction for modeling dynamic biological systems and as a complementary paradigm to current foundation model approaches in single-cell biology.

</details>


### [409] [Activation Steering for Synthetic Data Generation: The Role of Diversity in Downstream Safety Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28664)
*Vijeta Deshpande,Tootiya Giyahchi,Veena Padmanabhan,Leman Akoglu,Anna Rumshisky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AS can generate highly aligned, semantically targeted outputs for safety data, but diversity declines with stronger steering; downstream utility exists in a narrow regime requiring balance among success, coherence, and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scarcity of HHH-violating examples to train robust safety detectors; evaluate whether Activation Steering can provide effective synthetic data for downstream classifiers.

Method: Two-fold intrinsic/extrinsic evaluation across 4 concepts × 2 models × 4 steering methods. Intrinsic axes: concept alignment, coherence, plus newly defined sample- and set-level diversity. Extrinsic: replace HHH-violating training data with AS-generated outputs to train detectors; compare to prompting-generated data.

Result: AS data shows better detector performance in 3/4 concepts; overall only 41/136 AS configurations beat prompting. Diversity decreases with stronger steering; harmonic mean of success, coherence, diversity correlates with AUROC more consistently than any two individually. Practical heuristic for hyperparameters derived.

Conclusion: AS has potential for synthetic data generation for safety detection; diversity is a critical, previously overlooked axis; practitioners should optimize a harmonic mix of alignment, coherence, and diversity to maximize downstream utility.

Abstract: Safety detection models require examples of HHH (Helpful, Harmless, Honest)-violating outputs for robust generalization, however such examples are scarce. Activation Steering (AS) has emerged as a data-efficient method for generating target-concept-aligned responses. We investigate whether AS can generate high-quality training datasets for downstream classifiers, a question that remains untested. We present a two-fold study with intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation across $4$ concepts $\times\,2$ models $\times\,4$ steering methods. Intrinsically, beyond the field-standard rubric of steering success (concept alignment) and coherence, we introduce sample- and set-level diversity as a quality axis previously absent from the literature, and find that increasing steering strength reduces response diversity. Extrinsically, we replace HHH-violating examples in the available training data with steered generations and fine-tune detection classifiers. AS-generated data results in a better classifier than the prompting-generated data on $3$ of $4$ concepts. However, only $41$ of $136$ AS configurations outperform prompting, indicating that downstream utility lies in a narrow regime that jointly satisfies success, coherence, and diversity. The harmonic mean of these three axes correlates with downstream AUROC more consistently across concepts than success and coherence alone, providing a practical heuristic target for practitioners tuning AS hyperparameters. Together, our results highlight the potential of AS in synthetic data generation for improving safety detection and identify diversity as a critical, previously overlooked axis for tuning AS.

</details>


### [410] [Optimal Data Acquisition for Reinforcement Learning: A Large Deviations Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28675)
*Mingjie Hu,Jian-Qiang Hu,Enlu Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified large deviations framework for data acquisition in infinite-horizon RL, introducing an exponential decay rate as a data-efficiency metric, with a variational LD-based characterization leading to a tractable convex relaxation and a lazy projected subgradient method to produce near-robustly optimal data acquisition policies; extended to linear function approximation with positive experimental results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data acquisition in RL can be costly and slow, especially in business and healthcare settings with human-in-the-loop interactions. A principled efficiency metric and scalable algorithms are needed to maximize information gain while ensuring strong performance guarantees.

Method: Develop a large deviations variational characterization of the exponential decay rate of policy-selection error for Markov decision processes. Formulate a nested optimization problem, propose a convex relaxation with explicit constraints, and solve it with a lazy one-step projected subgradient method to build an adaptive data acquisition policy. Extend the framework to linear function approximation for scalability.

Result: The approach yields a policy that is near-robustly optimal under the proposed optimality criterion, up to a constant factor. The convex relaxation is tractable, and the algorithm provides data-acquisition policies with performance guarantees. Extensions to linear function approximation improve scalability, with numerical experiments supporting effectiveness.

Conclusion: The paper delivers a principled, scalable framework for data acquisition in infinite-horizon RL, unifying large deviations theory with practical optimization and adaptive data collection, and demonstrating robustness and scalability through theory and experiments.

Abstract: Data acquisition efficiency is a central challenge in deploying reinforcement learning in business and healthcare operations, where interactions are costly, slow, and often involve humans in the loop. This paper develops a unified large deviations framework for data acquisition in infinite-horizon reinforcement learning. We introduce the exponential decay rate of the policy-selection error probability as a principled efficiency metric and derive a variational characterization of this rate via large deviations theory for Markov chains, yielding a nested optimization problem. Based on this characterization, we formalize two complementary notions of optimality in terms of the optimal solution of the nested problem. Because the resulting program is implicit and generally intractable, we propose a tractable convex relaxation with explicit constraints. We then develop a lazy one-step projected subgradient method to solve the relaxed problem and use its iterates to construct an adaptive data acquisition policy. We prove that the resulting reinforcement learning algorithm is near-robustly optimal under our optimality criterion, up to a constant factor. Finally, we extend the framework to linear function approximation to improve scalability, and numerical experiments support the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

</details>


### [411] [Optimal ridge regularization revisited](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28679)
*Jack Timmermans,Sergio A. Alvarez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An iterative, data-informed approach computes the optimal ridge penalty from generative parameters in fixed-X linear regression, with proven convergence under low-noise and demonstrated near-optimal generalization in synthetic tests at modest computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ridge regularization strength is crucial for generalization; deriving an efficient, parameter-driven method to select alpha in the fixed-design setting can improve performance without extensive cross-validation.

Method: Introduce an iterative procedure that updates the regularization strength using known generative parameters (bounded covariance, isotropic noise with finite variance) in the fixed-X regime; prove convergence under limited noise; augment with sample-based estimates to apply to random-X generalization.

Result: The algorithm converges for the stated regime; experimental results on synthetic data show near-optimal random-X generalization across diverse sample sizes, aspect ratios, and noise levels; additional cost is equivalent to one ridge regression (underparameterized) or two (overparameterized).

Conclusion: A computationally efficient, theoretically grounded method to set the ridge penalty from generative parameters yields robust generalization in fixed-X and extrapolates well to random-X scenarios, at modest extra computation.

Abstract: We consider $L^2$-regularized linear (ridge) regression over a finite data sample $X$ with bounded covariance and linear prediction targets $y$ with additive isotropic noise of finite variance. We present an iterative procedure to compute the optimal regularization strength numerically from the generative parameters in the fixed-$X$ setting and prove its convergence at limited noise levels. Our experimental evaluation over synthetic data shows that the proposed procedure combined with sample-based parameter estimates attains near-optimal random-$X$ generalization across a wide range of sample sizes, aspect ratios, and noise levels, at an added computational cost equivalent to one preliminary ridge regression in the underparameterized regime and two in the overparameterized case.

</details>


### [412] [History-aware adaptive reduced-order models via incremental singular value decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28684)
*Amirpasha Hedayat,Ali Mohaghegh,Laura Balzano,Cheng Huang,Karthik Duraisamy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive projection-based ROM using incremental SVD (iSVD) with occasional full-order corrections yields history-aware online basis updates, yielding improved accuracy and efficiency across Burgers, Sod, and RDE problems and extending predictive horizons beyond training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maintain ROM accuracy when online dynamics deviate from offline training data, enabling online basis adaptation with low overhead.

Method: Projection-based ROM updated by incremental SVD with correction snapshots from full-order evaluations; the basis, reduced operators, and hyper-reduction stay fully parameterized, and updates propagate through the reduced system; the method preserves history via evolving singular structure.

Result: iSVD outperforms instantaneous-update baselines, with strongest overall performance across test problems; in the RDE case it surpasses state-of-the-art Direct adaptive ROM in accuracy and efficiency; online cost is dominated by obtaining correction snapshots from the FOM, while the iSVD update itself is negligible.

Conclusion: iSVD provides an effective online-learning mechanism for reduced subspaces, enabling ROMs that remain predictive over horizons orders of magnitude longer than the initial training window.

Abstract: Reduced-order models (ROMs) can accelerate high-dimensional dynamical simulations, but their accuracy often deteriorates when online dynamics leave the regime represented by offline training data. We develop a projection-based adaptive ROM framework based on incremental singular value decomposition (iSVD), in which occasional full-order operator evaluations provide correction snapshots for online basis updates. The intrusive ROMs considered here are fully parameterized by the basis, so each update naturally propagates to reduced operators and hyper-reduction machinery. Through its evolving singular structure, iSVD retains an encoded history of the observed dynamics and is history-aware in this sense. We study the method on three nonlinear problems of increasing complexity: the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation, the Sod shock tube, and a stiff one-dimensional ten-species rotating detonation engine (RDE). The Burgers problem is used to analyze the method and compare iSVD with alternative basis adaptation rules, showing that history-aware updates outperform instantaneous updates and that iSVD gives the strongest overall performance. The Sod and RDE cases demonstrate that these advantages persist in more challenging compressible-flow settings. For the RDE problem, the iSVD adaptive ROM improves upon the current state-of-the-art Direct adaptive ROM baseline in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. A cost analysis shows that the dominant online cost comes from interacting with the full-order model to obtain correction snapshots, while the iSVD update itself is negligible. These results identify iSVD as an effective mechanism for online learning of reduced subspaces and suggest a path toward ROMs that remain predictive over horizons several orders of magnitude longer than their initial training window.

</details>


### [413] [Expressive Power of Floating-Point Neural Networks with Arbitrary Reduction Orders and Inexact Activation Implementations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28704)
*Yeachan Park,Geonho Hwang,Wonyeol Lee,Sejun Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distinguishability-based framework for floating-point neural networks under generalized execution semantics is developed to characterize universal representability, showing necessary and sufficient conditions for exact function representation and proving universality for many common activations (e.g., Sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, ELU, SeLU, GeLU, Swish, Mish, sin) under realistic finite-precision models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current expressivity results assume exact arithmetic or overly simplified FP models. Real-world FP computation uses arbitrary reduction orders and inexact activation implementations with bounded ulp errors, motivating a unified theory of FP neural expressivity.

Method: Introduce a general distinguishability framework. Prove that being able to distinguish every pair of distinct inputs in the first layer is a necessary condition for universal representability; under mild activation-implementation assumptions, this condition becomes sufficient. Apply the framework to a broad set of activations and FP models to establish universality results.

Result: Characterizes when FP neural networks can represent arbitrary functions exactly; identifies activation implementations that fail universality; establishes sufficiency under mild conditions; proves universal representability for a broad class of practical activations (Sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, ELU, SeLU, GeLU, Swish, Mish, sin) under more realistic FP execution models than previously known.

Conclusion: The framework provides a broad, practically relevant criterion for FP network universality, extending prior isolated examples and confirming universal expressivity for many common activations under realistic floating-point semantics, with potential guidance for FP-NN design and analysis.

Abstract: Most existing expressivity theories for neural networks assume exact real arithmetic, whereas practical neural networks are executed under finite-precision floating-point arithmetic with implementation-dependent execution semantics. Recent works have begun studying the expressive power of floating-point neural networks, but existing results are limited to highly restricted activation functions and idealized assumptions such as fixed left-to-right reduction orders and correctly rounded activation implementations. In this work, we study the expressive power of floating-point neural networks under generalized floating-point execution semantics, including arbitrary reduction orders and inexact activation implementations with bounded ulp errors. We investigate when floating-point neural networks can represent arbitrary functions between floating-point domains exactly. To this end, we introduce a general distinguishability framework and show that the ability to distinguish every pair of distinct inputs in the first layer is necessary for universal representability. This characterization yields broad classes of activation implementations that are not universal representators, extending previous isolated counterexamples such as the correctly rounded cosine activation. We further prove that a suitable form of distinguishability is also sufficient for universal representability under mild conditions on the activation implementation. Using this framework, we establish universal representability results for a broad class of practical activation functions, including implementations of $\mathrm{Sigmoid}$, $\tanh$, $\mathrm{ReLU}$, $\mathrm{ELU}$, $\mathrm{SeLU}$, $\mathrm{GeLU}$, $\mathrm{Swish}$, $\mathrm{Mish}$, and $\sin$, under significantly more realistic floating-point execution models than previously known.

</details>


### [414] [Understanding Generalization and Forgetting in In-Context Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28705)
*Guangyu Li,Meng Ding,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical framework for in-context continual learning (ICL-CL) analyzing how prompts with sequential tasks are processed by pretrained Transformers, revealing intertask interference and limits of attention-based continual inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world prompts often contain sequences of heterogeneous tasks. Understanding how ICL handles sequential tasks and whether continual learning occurs during inference is critical for robustness and transfer.

Method: Model the pretrained Transformer with linear and masked linear self-attention; derive prediction error expressions for sequential task prompts; perform bias-variance-interference decomposition; analyze generalization and forgetting and order sensitivity.

Result: Attention mechanisms induce intertask interference by aggregating historical contexts, causing systematic bias. A bias-variance-interference decomposition clarifies when historical in-context information yields positive or negative transfer. The work reveals fundamental limits of attention-based continual inference and explains order sensitivity and performance degradation in long prompts.
